question_html: Prove that the line through A (0, -1, -1) and B (4, 5,1) intersects the line through C (3,9,4) and D (-4, 4, 4).
question_text: Prove that the line through A (0, -1, -1) and B (4, 5,1) intersects the line through C (3,9,4) and D (-4, 4, 4).
explanation: The equation of line through {tex} A (0,-1,-1)\ and\ B (4, 5,1){/tex} is {tex}\frac { x - 0 } { 4 - 0 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 5 + 1 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 1 + 1 }{/tex} i.e. {tex}\frac { x } { 4 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 6 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 2 }{/tex} .....(i) Equation of line through {tex}C (3,9,4)\ and\ D (-4,4,4){/tex} is {tex}\frac { x - 3 } { - 4 - 3 } = \frac { y - 9 } { 4 - 9 } = \frac { z - 4 } { 0 }{/tex} i.e., {tex}\frac { x - 3 } { - 7 } = \frac { y - 9 } { - 5 } = \frac { z - 4 } { 0 }{/tex} ....(ii) We know that, the lines {tex}\frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a _ { 1 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b _ { 1 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c _ { 1 } }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { x - x _ { 2 } } { a _ { 2 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 2 } } { b _ { 2 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 2 } } { c _ { 2 } }{/tex} will intersect, if {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } } & { y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } } & { z _ { 2 } - z _ { 1 } } \\ { a _ { 1 } } & { b _ { 1 } } & { c _ { 1 } } \\ { a _ { 2 } } & { b _ { 2 } } & { c _ { 2 } } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} :. The given lines will intersect, if {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 3 - 0 } & { 9 - ( - 1 ) } & { 4 - ( - 1 ) } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 2 } \\ { - 7 } & { - 5 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} Now, {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 3 - 0 } & { 9 - ( - 1 ) } & { 4 - ( - 1 ) } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 2 } \\ { - 7 } & { - 5 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right| = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 3 } & { 10 } & { 5 } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 2 } \\ { - 7 } & { - 5 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= 3(0+ 10 -10 (0+ 14) + 5 (-20+ 42){/tex} {tex}= 30-140 + 110 =0{/tex} Hence, the given lines intersect.
solution: The equation of line through {tex} A (0,-1,-1)\ and\ B (4, 5,1){/tex} is {tex}\frac { x - 0 } { 4 - 0 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 5 + 1 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 1 + 1 }{/tex} i.e. {tex}\frac { x } { 4 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 6 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 2 }{/tex} .....(i) Equation of line through {tex}C (3,9,4)\ and\ D (-4,4,4){/tex} is {tex}\frac { x - 3 } { - 4 - 3 } = \frac { y - 9 } { 4 - 9 } = \frac { z - 4 } { 0 }{/tex} i.e., {tex}\frac { x - 3 } { - 7 } = \frac { y - 9 } { - 5 } = \frac { z - 4 } { 0 }{/tex} ....(ii) We know that, the lines {tex}\frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a _ { 1 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b _ { 1 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c _ { 1 } }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { x - x _ { 2 } } { a _ { 2 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 2 } } { b _ { 2 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 2 } } { c _ { 2 } }{/tex} will intersect, if {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } } & { y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } } & { z _ { 2 } - z _ { 1 } } \\ { a _ { 1 } } & { b _ { 1 } } & { c _ { 1 } } \\ { a _ { 2 } } & { b _ { 2 } } & { c _ { 2 } } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} :. The given lines will intersect, if {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 3 - 0 } & { 9 - ( - 1 ) } & { 4 - ( - 1 ) } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 2 } \\ { - 7 } & { - 5 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex} Now, {tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 3 - 0 } & { 9 - ( - 1 ) } & { 4 - ( - 1 ) } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 2 } \\ { - 7 } & { - 5 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right| = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 3 } & { 10 } & { 5 } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 2 } \\ { - 7 } & { - 5 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= 3(0+ 10 -10 (0+ 14) + 5 (-20+ 42){/tex} {tex}= 30-140 + 110 =0{/tex} Hence, the given lines intersect.
solution_html:
The equation of line through{tex} A (0,-1,-1)\ and\ B (4, 5,1){/tex} is
{tex}\frac { x - 0 } { 4 - 0 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 5 + 1 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 1 + 1 }{/tex}
i.e. {tex}\frac { x } { 4 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 6 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 2 }{/tex}.....(i)
Equation of line through {tex}C (3,9,4)\ and\ D (-4,4,4){/tex} is
{tex}\frac { x - 3 } { - 4 - 3 } = \frac { y - 9 } { 4 - 9 } = \frac { z - 4 } { 0 }{/tex}
i.e., {tex}\frac { x - 3 } { - 7 } = \frac { y - 9 } { - 5 } = \frac { z - 4 } { 0 }{/tex} ....(ii)
We know that, the lines
{tex}\frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a _ { 1 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b _ { 1 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c _ { 1 } }{/tex}and
{tex}\frac { x - x _ { 2 } } { a _ { 2 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 2 } } { b _ { 2 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 2 } } { c _ { 2 } }{/tex}will intersect,
if{tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } } & { y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } } & { z _ { 2 } - z _ { 1 } } \\ { a _ { 1 } } & { b _ { 1 } } & { c _ { 1 } } \\ { a _ { 2 } } & { b _ { 2 } } & { c _ { 2 } } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex}
:. The given lines will intersect, if
{tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 3 - 0 } & { 9 - ( - 1 ) } & { 4 - ( - 1 ) } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 2 } \\ { - 7 } & { - 5 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right| = 0{/tex}
Now,
{tex}\left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 3 - 0 } & { 9 - ( - 1 ) } & { 4 - ( - 1 ) } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 2 } \\ { - 7 } & { - 5 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right| = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { 3 } & { 10 } & { 5 } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 2 } \\ { - 7 } & { - 5 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex}= 3(0+ 10 -10 (0+ 14) + 5 (-20+ 42){/tex}
{tex}= 30-140 + 110 =0{/tex}
Hence, the given lines intersect.

question_html: Find the length shortest distance between the lines: {tex}\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{z-9}{-3}{/tex}  and {tex}\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y+15}{-7}=\frac{z-9}{5}{/tex}
question_text: Find the length shortest distance between the lines: {tex}\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{z-9}{-3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y+15}{-7}=\frac{z-9}{5}{/tex}
explanation: Here,it is given equations of lines {tex}\mathrm{L}_1: \frac{\mathrm{x}+1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-9}{-3}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{L}_2: \frac{\mathrm{x}-3}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+15}{-7}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-9}{5}{/tex} Direction ratios of L 1 and L 2 are (2, 1, -3) are (2, -7, 5) respectively. Suppose, general point on line L 1 is P = (x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ) x 1 = 2s - 1, y 1 = s + 1, z 1 = -3s + 9 and let general point on line L 2 is Q = (x 2 , y 2, z 2) x 2 = 2t + 3, y 2 = -7t - 15, z 2 = 5t + 9 then ,we get {tex}\therefore \vec{P Q}=\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right) \hat{\imath}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right) \hat{\jmath}{/tex} {tex}+\left(z_{2}-z_{1}\right) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=(5 t+9-2 s+1) \hat{i}+(-7 t-15{/tex} {tex}-s-1) \hat{\jmath}+(5 t+9+3 s-9) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{PQ}}=(5 t-2 s+10) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+{/tex} {tex}(-7 t-s-16) \hat{\jmath}+(5 t+3 s) \hat{k}{/tex} Direction ratios of {tex}\vec{\mathrm{PQ}}{/tex} are ((5t - 2s + 10), (-7t - s - 16), (5t + 3s)) PQ will be the shortest distance if it is perpendicular to both the given lines Thus, by the condition of perpendicularity, 2(5t - 2s + 10) + 1(-7t - s - 16) - 3(5t - 3s) = 0 and 2(5t - 2s + 10) -7(-7t - s - 16) + 5(5t + 3s) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10t - 4s + 20 - 7t - s - 16 - 15t - 9s = 0 and 10t - 4s + 20 + 49t + 7s + 112 + 25t + 15s = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -12t - 14s = -4 and 84t + 18s = -132 Solving above two equations, we obtain t = -2 and s = 2 thus, P = (3, 3, 3) and Q = (-1, -1, -1) Now, distance between points P and Q is {tex}d=\sqrt{(3+1)^{2}+(3+1)^{2}+(3+1)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{(4)^{2}+(4)^{2}+(4)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{16+16+16}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{48}{/tex} {tex}=4 \sqrt{3}{/tex} Thus, the shortest distance between two given lines is {tex}d=4 \sqrt{3}{/tex} units Now, equation of line passing through points P and Q is {tex}\frac{x-x_{1}}{x_{1}-x_{2}}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{y_{1}-y_{2}}=\frac{z-z_{1}}{z_{1}-z_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{3+1}=\frac{y-3}{3+1}=\frac{z-3}{3+1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{4}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} x - 3 = y - 3 = z - 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y = z Therefore, equation of line of shortest distance between two given lines is x = y = z
solution: Here,it is given equations of lines {tex}\mathrm{L}_1: \frac{\mathrm{x}+1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-9}{-3}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{L}_2: \frac{\mathrm{x}-3}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+15}{-7}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-9}{5}{/tex} Direction ratios of L 1 and L 2 are (2, 1, -3) are (2, -7, 5) respectively. Suppose, general point on line L 1 is P = (x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ) x 1 = 2s - 1, y 1 = s + 1, z 1 = -3s + 9 and let general point on line L 2 is Q = (x 2 , y 2, z 2) x 2 = 2t + 3, y 2 = -7t - 15, z 2 = 5t + 9 then ,we get {tex}\therefore \vec{P Q}=\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right) \hat{\imath}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right) \hat{\jmath}{/tex} {tex}+\left(z_{2}-z_{1}\right) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}=(5 t+9-2 s+1) \hat{i}+(-7 t-15{/tex} {tex}-s-1) \hat{\jmath}+(5 t+9+3 s-9) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{PQ}}=(5 t-2 s+10) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+{/tex} {tex}(-7 t-s-16) \hat{\jmath}+(5 t+3 s) \hat{k}{/tex} Direction ratios of {tex}\vec{\mathrm{PQ}}{/tex} are ((5t - 2s + 10), (-7t - s - 16), (5t + 3s)) PQ will be the shortest distance if it is perpendicular to both the given lines Thus, by the condition of perpendicularity, 2(5t - 2s + 10) + 1(-7t - s - 16) - 3(5t - 3s) = 0 and 2(5t - 2s + 10) -7(-7t - s - 16) + 5(5t + 3s) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10t - 4s + 20 - 7t - s - 16 - 15t - 9s = 0 and 10t - 4s + 20 + 49t + 7s + 112 + 25t + 15s = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -12t - 14s = -4 and 84t + 18s = -132 Solving above two equations, we obtain t = -2 and s = 2 thus, P = (3, 3, 3) and Q = (-1, -1, -1) Now, distance between points P and Q is {tex}d=\sqrt{(3+1)^{2}+(3+1)^{2}+(3+1)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{(4)^{2}+(4)^{2}+(4)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{16+16+16}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{48}{/tex} {tex}=4 \sqrt{3}{/tex} Thus, the shortest distance between two given lines is {tex}d=4 \sqrt{3}{/tex} units Now, equation of line passing through points P and Q is {tex}\frac{x-x_{1}}{x_{1}-x_{2}}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{y_{1}-y_{2}}=\frac{z-z_{1}}{z_{1}-z_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{3+1}=\frac{y-3}{3+1}=\frac{z-3}{3+1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{4}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} x - 3 = y - 3 = z - 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y = z Therefore, equation of line of shortest distance between two given lines is x = y = z
solution_html:
Here,it is given equations of lines
{tex}\mathrm{L}_1: \frac{\mathrm{x}+1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-9}{-3}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{L}_2: \frac{\mathrm{x}-3}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+15}{-7}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-9}{5}{/tex}
Direction ratios of L1 and L2 are (2, 1, -3) are (2, -7, 5) respectively.
Suppose, general point on line L1 is P = (x1, y1, z1)
x1 = 2s - 1, y1 = s + 1, z1 = -3s + 9
and let general point on line L2 is Q = (x2, y2, z2)
x2 = 2t + 3, y2 = -7t - 15, z2 = 5t + 9 then ,we get
{tex}\therefore \vec{P Q}=\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right) \hat{\imath}+\left(y_{2}-y_{1}\right) \hat{\jmath}{/tex}{tex}+\left(z_{2}-z_{1}\right) \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}=(5 t+9-2 s+1) \hat{i}+(-7 t-15{/tex}{tex}-s-1) \hat{\jmath}+(5 t+9+3 s-9) \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \vec{\mathrm{PQ}}=(5 t-2 s+10) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+{/tex}{tex}(-7 t-s-16) \hat{\jmath}+(5 t+3 s) \hat{k}{/tex}
Direction ratios of {tex}\vec{\mathrm{PQ}}{/tex} are ((5t - 2s + 10), (-7t - s - 16), (5t + 3s))
PQ will be the shortest distance if it is perpendicular to both the given lines
Thus, by the condition of perpendicularity,
2(5t - 2s + 10) + 1(-7t - s - 16) - 3(5t - 3s) = 0 and
2(5t - 2s + 10) -7(-7t - s - 16) + 5(5t + 3s) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10t - 4s + 20 - 7t - s - 16 - 15t - 9s = 0 and
10t - 4s + 20 + 49t + 7s + 112 + 25t + 15s = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -12t - 14s = -4 and 84t + 18s = -132
Solving above two equations, we obtain
t = -2 and s = 2
thus,
P = (3, 3, 3) and Q = (-1, -1, -1)
Now, distance between points P and Q is
{tex}d=\sqrt{(3+1)^{2}+(3+1)^{2}+(3+1)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{(4)^{2}+(4)^{2}+(4)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{16+16+16}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{48}{/tex}
{tex}=4 \sqrt{3}{/tex}
Thus, the shortest distance between two given lines is
{tex}d=4 \sqrt{3}{/tex} units
Now, equation of line passing through points P and Q is
{tex}\frac{x-x_{1}}{x_{1}-x_{2}}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{y_{1}-y_{2}}=\frac{z-z_{1}}{z_{1}-z_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{3+1}=\frac{y-3}{3+1}=\frac{z-3}{3+1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{x-3}{4}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x - 3 = y - 3 = z - 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y = z
Therefore, equation of line of shortest distance between two given lines is x = y = z

question_html: Find the distance of a point (2, 4, –1) from the line {tex}\frac{{x + 5}}{1} = \frac{{y + 3}}{4} = \frac{{z - 6}}{{ - 9}}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the distance of a point (2, 4, –1) from the line {tex}\frac{{x + 5}}{1} = \frac{{y + 3}}{4} = \frac{{z - 6}}{{ - 9}}{/tex} .
explanation: We have equation of the line as {tex}\frac{{x + 5}}{1} = \frac{{y + 3}}{4} = \frac{{z - 6}}{{ - 9}} = \lambda {/tex} . {tex} \Rightarrow x = \lambda - 5,y = 4\lambda - 3,z = 6 - 9\lambda {/tex} Let the coordinates of L be {tex}\left( {\lambda - 5,4\lambda - 3,6 - 9\lambda } \right){/tex} , then Dr’s of PL are {tex}\left( {\lambda - 7,4\lambda - 7,7 - 9\lambda } \right){/tex} . Also, the direction ratios of given line are proportional to 1, 4, -9. Since, P L is perpendicular to the given line. {tex}\therefore \left( {\lambda - 7} \right) \cdot 1 + (4\lambda - 7) \cdot 4 + (7 - 9\lambda ) \cdot ( - 9) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda - 7 + 16\lambda - 28 + 81\lambda - 63 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 98\lambda = 98 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex} So, the coordinates of L are (-4, 1, -3). {tex}\therefore {/tex} Required distance, PL {tex} = \sqrt {{{( - 4 - 2)}^2} + {{(1 - 4)}^2} + {{( - 3 + 1)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {36 + 9 + 4} = 7\;units{/tex}
solution: We have equation of the line as {tex}\frac{{x + 5}}{1} = \frac{{y + 3}}{4} = \frac{{z - 6}}{{ - 9}} = \lambda {/tex} . {tex} \Rightarrow x = \lambda - 5,y = 4\lambda - 3,z = 6 - 9\lambda {/tex} Let the coordinates of L be {tex}\left( {\lambda - 5,4\lambda - 3,6 - 9\lambda } \right){/tex} , then Dr’s of PL are {tex}\left( {\lambda - 7,4\lambda - 7,7 - 9\lambda } \right){/tex} . Also, the direction ratios of given line are proportional to 1, 4, -9. Since, P L is perpendicular to the given line. {tex}\therefore \left( {\lambda - 7} \right) \cdot 1 + (4\lambda - 7) \cdot 4 + (7 - 9\lambda ) \cdot ( - 9) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda - 7 + 16\lambda - 28 + 81\lambda - 63 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 98\lambda = 98 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex} So, the coordinates of L are (-4, 1, -3). {tex}\therefore {/tex} Required distance, PL {tex} = \sqrt {{{( - 4 - 2)}^2} + {{(1 - 4)}^2} + {{( - 3 + 1)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {36 + 9 + 4} = 7\;units{/tex}
solution_html:
We have equation of the line as {tex}\frac{{x + 5}}{1} = \frac{{y + 3}}{4} = \frac{{z - 6}}{{ - 9}} = \lambda {/tex}.
{tex} \Rightarrow x = \lambda - 5,y = 4\lambda - 3,z = 6 - 9\lambda {/tex}
Let the coordinates of L be {tex}\left( {\lambda - 5,4\lambda - 3,6 - 9\lambda } \right){/tex}, then Dr’s of PL are {tex}\left( {\lambda - 7,4\lambda - 7,7 - 9\lambda } \right){/tex}.
Also, the direction ratios of given line are proportional to 1, 4, -9.
Since, P L is perpendicular to the given line.
{tex}\therefore \left( {\lambda - 7} \right) \cdot 1 + (4\lambda - 7) \cdot 4 + (7 - 9\lambda ) \cdot ( - 9) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \lambda - 7 + 16\lambda - 28 + 81\lambda - 63 = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 98\lambda = 98 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex}
So, the coordinates of L are (-4, 1, -3).
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Required distance, PL{tex} = \sqrt {{{( - 4 - 2)}^2} + {{(1 - 4)}^2} + {{( - 3 + 1)}^2}} {/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {36 + 9 + 4} = 7\;units{/tex}

question_html: Show that the lines {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=z{/tex} intersect. Also, find their point of intersection.
question_text: Show that the lines {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=z{/tex} intersect. Also, find their point of intersection.
explanation: We have lines {tex}L_{1}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\lambda{/tex} and {tex}L_{2}: \frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=z=\mu{/tex} Any point on the line L 1 is {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3){/tex} Any point on the line L 2 is {tex}(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex} If lines intersect then there exists a value of {tex}\lambda, \mu{/tex} for which {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3)=(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda+1=5 \mu+4,3 \lambda+2=2 \mu+1{/tex} and {tex}4 \lambda+3=\mu{/tex} Solving first two equations we get {tex}\lambda=-1, \mu=-1{/tex} These values of {tex}\lambda=-1, \mu=-1{/tex} also satisfy the third equation. Thus lines intersect. Also the point of intersections is (-1, -1, -1).
solution: We have lines {tex}L_{1}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\lambda{/tex} and {tex}L_{2}: \frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=z=\mu{/tex} Any point on the line L 1 is {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3){/tex} Any point on the line L 2 is {tex}(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex} If lines intersect then there exists a value of {tex}\lambda, \mu{/tex} for which {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3)=(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda+1=5 \mu+4,3 \lambda+2=2 \mu+1{/tex} and {tex}4 \lambda+3=\mu{/tex} Solving first two equations we get {tex}\lambda=-1, \mu=-1{/tex} These values of {tex}\lambda=-1, \mu=-1{/tex} also satisfy the third equation. Thus lines intersect. Also the point of intersections is (-1, -1, -1).
solution_html:
We have lines
{tex}L_{1}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\lambda{/tex}
and {tex}L_{2}: \frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=z=\mu{/tex}
Any point on the line L1 is {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3){/tex}
Any point on the line L2 is {tex}(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex}
If lines intersect then there exists a value of {tex}\lambda, \mu{/tex} for which {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3)=(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda+1=5 \mu+4,3 \lambda+2=2 \mu+1{/tex} and {tex}4 \lambda+3=\mu{/tex}
Solving first two equations we get {tex}\lambda=-1, \mu=-1{/tex}
These values of {tex}\lambda=-1, \mu=-1{/tex} also satisfy the third equation.
Thus lines intersect.
Also the point of intersections is (-1, -1, -1).

question_html: Find the foot of the perpendicular drawn from point {tex}(2,-1,5){/tex} to the line {tex}\frac{{x}-11}{10}=\frac{{y}+2}{-4}=\frac{{z}+8}{-11}{/tex}. Also, find the length of the perpendicular.
question_text: Find the foot of the perpendicular drawn from point {tex}(2,-1,5){/tex} to the line {tex}\frac{{x}-11}{10}=\frac{{y}+2}{-4}=\frac{{z}+8}{-11}{/tex} . Also, find the length of the perpendicular.
explanation: Let: {tex}\frac{x-11}{10}=\frac{y+2}{-4}=\frac{z+8}{-11}=\lambda{/tex} So the foot of the perpendicular is {tex}(10 \lambda+11,-4 \lambda-2,-11 \lambda-8){/tex} Direction ratio of the lines is 10 : -4 : 11 Direction ratio of the perpendicular is {tex}\Rightarrow(10 \lambda+11-2):(-4 \lambda-2+1){/tex} {tex}:(-11 \lambda-8-5){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(10 \lambda+9):(-4 \lambda-1){/tex} {tex}:(-11 \lambda-13){/tex} Since this is perpendicular to the line, {tex}10(10 \lambda+9)-4(-4 \lambda-1){/tex} {tex}-11(-11 \lambda-13)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 100 \lambda+90+16 \lambda{/tex} {tex}+4+121 \lambda+143=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 237 \lambda=-237{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=-1{/tex} So the foot of the perpendicular is (1, 2, 3) Distance {tex}=\sqrt{(1-2)^{2}+(2+1)^{2}+(3-5)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{1+9+4}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{14}{/tex} units Therefore, the foot of the perpendicular is (1, 2, 3) and length of perpendicular is {tex}\sqrt{14}{/tex} units.
solution: Let: {tex}\frac{x-11}{10}=\frac{y+2}{-4}=\frac{z+8}{-11}=\lambda{/tex} So the foot of the perpendicular is {tex}(10 \lambda+11,-4 \lambda-2,-11 \lambda-8){/tex} Direction ratio of the lines is 10 : -4 : 11 Direction ratio of the perpendicular is {tex}\Rightarrow(10 \lambda+11-2):(-4 \lambda-2+1){/tex} {tex}:(-11 \lambda-8-5){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(10 \lambda+9):(-4 \lambda-1){/tex} {tex}:(-11 \lambda-13){/tex} Since this is perpendicular to the line, {tex}10(10 \lambda+9)-4(-4 \lambda-1){/tex} {tex}-11(-11 \lambda-13)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 100 \lambda+90+16 \lambda{/tex} {tex}+4+121 \lambda+143=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 237 \lambda=-237{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=-1{/tex} So the foot of the perpendicular is (1, 2, 3) Distance {tex}=\sqrt{(1-2)^{2}+(2+1)^{2}+(3-5)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{1+9+4}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{14}{/tex} units Therefore, the foot of the perpendicular is (1, 2, 3) and length of perpendicular is {tex}\sqrt{14}{/tex} units.
solution_html:
Let: 
{tex}\frac{x-11}{10}=\frac{y+2}{-4}=\frac{z+8}{-11}=\lambda{/tex}
So the foot of the perpendicular is {tex}(10 \lambda+11,-4 \lambda-2,-11 \lambda-8){/tex}
Direction ratio of the lines is 10 : -4 : 11
Direction ratio of the perpendicular is
{tex}\Rightarrow(10 \lambda+11-2):(-4 \lambda-2+1){/tex}{tex}:(-11 \lambda-8-5){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(10 \lambda+9):(-4 \lambda-1){/tex}{tex}:(-11 \lambda-13){/tex}
Since this is perpendicular to the line,
{tex}10(10 \lambda+9)-4(-4 \lambda-1){/tex}{tex}-11(-11 \lambda-13)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 100 \lambda+90+16 \lambda{/tex}{tex}+4+121 \lambda+143=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 237 \lambda=-237{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=-1{/tex}
So the foot of the perpendicular is (1, 2, 3)
Distance {tex}=\sqrt{(1-2)^{2}+(2+1)^{2}+(3-5)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{1+9+4}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{14}{/tex} units
Therefore, the foot of the perpendicular is (1, 2, 3) and length of perpendicular is {tex}\sqrt{14}{/tex} units.

question_html: Find the image {tex}{A}^{\prime}{/tex} of the point {tex}{A}(1,6,3){/tex} in the line {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{{y}-1}{2}=\frac{{z}-2}{3}{/tex}. Also, find the equation of the line joining A and {tex}{A}^{\prime}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the image {tex}{A}^{\prime}{/tex} of the point {tex}{A}(1,6,3){/tex} in the line {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{{y}-1}{2}=\frac{{z}-2}{3}{/tex} . Also, find the equation of the line joining A and {tex}{A}^{\prime}{/tex} .
explanation: The equation of given line is {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{3}=\lambda{/tex} Any arbitrary point on the line is {tex}M(\lambda, 2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2){/tex} dr's of {tex}A M{/tex} are {tex} \lt \lambda-1,2 \lambda-5,3 \lambda-1 \gt {/tex} {tex}\operatorname{Here} 1(\lambda-1)+2(2 \lambda-5)+3(3 \lambda-1)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore M(1,3,5){/tex} is the foot perpendicular of the point A to the given line. Let image of point A in the line be {tex}A^{\prime}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma){/tex} Since {tex}M{/tex} is the mid-point of {tex}A A^{\prime}{/tex} , so {tex}M\left(\frac{1+\alpha}{2}, \frac{6+\beta}{2}, \frac{3+\gamma}{2}\right)=M(1,3,5){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A^{\prime}(1,0,7){/tex} is the image of A. Also, Equation of {tex}A A^{\prime}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{x-1}{0}=\frac{y-6}{-3}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex}
solution: The equation of given line is {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{3}=\lambda{/tex} Any arbitrary point on the line is {tex}M(\lambda, 2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2){/tex} dr's of {tex}A M{/tex} are {tex} \lt \lambda-1,2 \lambda-5,3 \lambda-1 \gt {/tex} {tex}\operatorname{Here} 1(\lambda-1)+2(2 \lambda-5)+3(3 \lambda-1)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore M(1,3,5){/tex} is the foot perpendicular of the point A to the given line. Let image of point A in the line be {tex}A^{\prime}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma){/tex} Since {tex}M{/tex} is the mid-point of {tex}A A^{\prime}{/tex} , so {tex}M\left(\frac{1+\alpha}{2}, \frac{6+\beta}{2}, \frac{3+\gamma}{2}\right)=M(1,3,5){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow A^{\prime}(1,0,7){/tex} is the image of A. Also, Equation of {tex}A A^{\prime}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{x-1}{0}=\frac{y-6}{-3}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
The equation of given line is {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{3}=\lambda{/tex}
Any arbitrary point on the line is {tex}M(\lambda, 2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2){/tex}
dr's of {tex}A M{/tex} are {tex} \lt \lambda-1,2 \lambda-5,3 \lambda-1 \gt {/tex}
{tex}\operatorname{Here} 1(\lambda-1)+2(2 \lambda-5)+3(3 \lambda-1)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore M(1,3,5){/tex} is the foot perpendicular of the point A to the given line.
Let image of point A in the line be {tex}A^{\prime}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma){/tex}
Since {tex}M{/tex} is the mid-point of {tex}A A^{\prime}{/tex}, so {tex}M\left(\frac{1+\alpha}{2}, \frac{6+\beta}{2}, \frac{3+\gamma}{2}\right)=M(1,3,5){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow A^{\prime}(1,0,7){/tex} is the image of A.
Also, Equation of {tex}A A^{\prime}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{x-1}{0}=\frac{y-6}{-3}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex}

question_html: Verify that lines given by {tex}\vec{r}=(1-\lambda) \hat{i}+(\lambda-2) \hat{j}+(3-2 \lambda) \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(\mu+1) \hat{i}+(2 \mu-1) \hat{j}-(2 \mu+1) \hat{k}{/tex} are skew lines. Hence, find shortest distance between the lines.
question_text: Verify that lines given by {tex}\vec{r}=(1-\lambda) \hat{i}+(\lambda-2) \hat{j}+(3-2 \lambda) \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(\mu+1) \hat{i}+(2 \mu-1) \hat{j}-(2 \mu+1) \hat{k}{/tex} are skew lines. Hence, find shortest distance between the lines.
explanation: Rewriting the lines, we get {tex} \vec{r}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(-\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) \text { and } {/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\mu(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) {/tex} Let {tex}\vec{a}_1=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_2=\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k},{/tex} {tex} \vec{b}_1=-\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_2=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} Note that the dr's of given lines are not proportional so, they are not parallel lines. The lines will be skew if they do not intersect each other also. {tex} \operatorname{Here} \vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1=\hat{j}-4 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2{/tex} {tex}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -1 & 1 & -2 \\ 1 & 2 & -2 \end{array}\right|=2 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k} {/tex} Consider {tex}\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right){/tex} {tex} =(\hat{j}-4 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})=8 \neq 0 {/tex} Hence lines will not intersect. So the lines are skew. {tex} \text { Shortest Distance } =\frac{\left|\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right)\right|}{\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right|} {/tex} {tex} = \frac{8}{\sqrt{4+16+9}}=\frac{8}{\sqrt{29}} {/tex}
solution: Rewriting the lines, we get {tex} \vec{r}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(-\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) \text { and } {/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\mu(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) {/tex} Let {tex}\vec{a}_1=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_2=\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k},{/tex} {tex} \vec{b}_1=-\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_2=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} Note that the dr's of given lines are not proportional so, they are not parallel lines. The lines will be skew if they do not intersect each other also. {tex} \operatorname{Here} \vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1=\hat{j}-4 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2{/tex} {tex}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -1 & 1 & -2 \\ 1 & 2 & -2 \end{array}\right|=2 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k} {/tex} Consider {tex}\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right){/tex} {tex} =(\hat{j}-4 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})=8 \neq 0 {/tex} Hence lines will not intersect. So the lines are skew. {tex} \text { Shortest Distance } =\frac{\left|\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right)\right|}{\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right|} {/tex} {tex} = \frac{8}{\sqrt{4+16+9}}=\frac{8}{\sqrt{29}} {/tex}
solution_html:
Rewriting the lines, we get
{tex} \vec{r}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(-\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) \text { and } {/tex}{tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\mu(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) {/tex}
Let {tex}\vec{a}_1=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_2=\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k},{/tex} {tex} \vec{b}_1=-\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_2=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}
Note that the dr's of given lines are not proportional so, they are not parallel lines.
The lines will be skew if they do not intersect each other also.
{tex} \operatorname{Here} \vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1=\hat{j}-4 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2{/tex} {tex}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -1 & 1 & -2 \\ 1 & 2 & -2 \end{array}\right|=2 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k} {/tex}
Consider {tex}\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right){/tex}
{tex} =(\hat{j}-4 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})=8 \neq 0 {/tex}
Hence lines will not intersect. So the lines are skew.
{tex} \text { Shortest Distance } =\frac{\left|\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right)\right|}{\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right|} {/tex}
{tex} = \frac{8}{\sqrt{4+16+9}}=\frac{8}{\sqrt{29}} {/tex}

question_html: Vector {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} is inclined at equal angles to the three axes {tex}x, y{/tex} and z. If magnitude of {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} is {tex}5 \sqrt{3}{/tex} units, then find {tex}\vec{r}{/tex}.
question_text: Vector {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} is inclined at equal angles to the three axes {tex}x, y{/tex} and z. If magnitude of {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} is {tex}5 \sqrt{3}{/tex} units, then find {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} .
explanation: Unit vector equally inclined along coordinate axes is {tex}\frac{\hat{i}}{\sqrt{3}}+\frac{\hat{j}}{\sqrt{3}}+\frac{\hat{k}}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=5 \sqrt{3}\left(\frac{\hat{i}}{\sqrt{3}}+\frac{\hat{j}}{\sqrt{3}}+\frac{\hat{k}}{\sqrt{3}}\right)=5 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} (or {tex}-5 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}){/tex}
solution: Unit vector equally inclined along coordinate axes is {tex}\frac{\hat{i}}{\sqrt{3}}+\frac{\hat{j}}{\sqrt{3}}+\frac{\hat{k}}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=5 \sqrt{3}\left(\frac{\hat{i}}{\sqrt{3}}+\frac{\hat{j}}{\sqrt{3}}+\frac{\hat{k}}{\sqrt{3}}\right)=5 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} (or {tex}-5 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}){/tex}
solution_html:
Unit vector equally inclined along coordinate axes is {tex}\frac{\hat{i}}{\sqrt{3}}+\frac{\hat{j}}{\sqrt{3}}+\frac{\hat{k}}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{r}=5 \sqrt{3}\left(\frac{\hat{i}}{\sqrt{3}}+\frac{\hat{j}}{\sqrt{3}}+\frac{\hat{k}}{\sqrt{3}}\right)=5 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} (or {tex}-5 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}){/tex}

question_html: Find the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point {tex}(1,1,4){/tex} on the line {tex}\frac{{x}+2}{5}=\frac{{y}+1}{2}=\frac{-{z}+4}{-3}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point {tex}(1,1,4){/tex} on the line {tex}\frac{{x}+2}{5}=\frac{{y}+1}{2}=\frac{-{z}+4}{-3}{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}\frac{x+2}{5}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z-4}{3}=\lambda{/tex} Coordinate of general point on the given line are {tex}M(5 \lambda-2,2 \lambda-1,3 \lambda+4){/tex} Direction Ratios of PM vector are {tex} \lt 5 \lambda-3,2 \lambda-2,3 \lambda \gt {/tex} Since, {tex}{P M} \perp \boldsymbol{l}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 5(5 \lambda-3)+2(2 \lambda-2)+3(3 \lambda)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Hence, coordinates of {tex}M{/tex} are {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}, 0, \frac{11}{2}\right){/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\frac{x+2}{5}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z-4}{3}=\lambda{/tex} Coordinate of general point on the given line are {tex}M(5 \lambda-2,2 \lambda-1,3 \lambda+4){/tex} Direction Ratios of PM vector are {tex} \lt 5 \lambda-3,2 \lambda-2,3 \lambda \gt {/tex} Since, {tex}{P M} \perp \boldsymbol{l}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 5(5 \lambda-3)+2(2 \lambda-2)+3(3 \lambda)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Hence, coordinates of {tex}M{/tex} are {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}, 0, \frac{11}{2}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\frac{x+2}{5}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z-4}{3}=\lambda{/tex}
Coordinate of general point on the given line are {tex}M(5 \lambda-2,2 \lambda-1,3 \lambda+4){/tex}

Direction Ratios of PM vector are {tex} \lt 5 \lambda-3,2 \lambda-2,3 \lambda \gt {/tex}
Since, {tex}{P M} \perp \boldsymbol{l}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 5(5 \lambda-3)+2(2 \lambda-2)+3(3 \lambda)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Hence, coordinates of {tex}M{/tex} are {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}, 0, \frac{11}{2}\right){/tex}

question_html: Find the image {tex}{A}^{\prime}{/tex} of the point {tex}{A}(2,1,2){/tex} in the line {tex}l: \overrightarrow{{r}}=4 \hat{{i}}+2 \hat{{j}}+2 \hat{{k}}+\lambda(\hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}}){/tex}. Also, find the equation of line joining {tex}{AA}^{\prime}{/tex}. Find the foot of perpendicular from point A on the line {tex}l{/tex}.
question_text: Find the image {tex}{A}^{\prime}{/tex} of the point {tex}{A}(2,1,2){/tex} in the line {tex}l: \overrightarrow{{r}}=4 \hat{{i}}+2 \hat{{j}}+2 \hat{{k}}+\lambda(\hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}}){/tex} . Also, find the equation of line joining {tex}{AA}^{\prime}{/tex} . Find the foot of perpendicular from point A on the line {tex}l{/tex} .
explanation: Let the image of A in the line be {tex}A^{\prime}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma){/tex} The point P , which is the point of intersection of the lines {tex}l{/tex} and {tex}A A^{\prime}{/tex} , will have coordinates ( {tex}\lambda+4,-\lambda+2,-\lambda+2{/tex} ) for some {tex}\lambda{/tex} . Drs of AP are {tex} \lt \lambda+2,-\lambda+1,-\lambda \gt {/tex} {tex}A P \perp l{/tex} {tex}(\lambda+2)-(-\lambda+1)-(-\lambda)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=-\frac{1}{3}{/tex} Therefore, the coordinates of P are {tex}\left(\frac{11}{3}, \frac{7}{3}, \frac{7}{3}\right){/tex} P is the mid-point of {tex}A A^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2+\alpha}{2}=\frac{11}{3}, \frac{1+\beta}{2}=\frac{7}{3}, \frac{2+\gamma}{2}=\frac{7}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{16}{3}, \beta=\frac{11}{3}, \gamma=\frac{8}{3}{/tex} The coordinates of the image are {tex}\left(\frac{16}{3}, \frac{11}{3}, \frac{8}{3}\right){/tex} The equation of {tex}A A^{\prime}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{x-2}{\frac{10}{3}}=\frac{y-1}{\frac{8}{3}}=\frac{z-2}{\frac{2}{3}}{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{3(x-2)}{5}=\frac{3(y-1)}{4}=\frac{3(z-2)}{1}{/tex}
solution: Let the image of A in the line be {tex}A^{\prime}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma){/tex} The point P , which is the point of intersection of the lines {tex}l{/tex} and {tex}A A^{\prime}{/tex} , will have coordinates ( {tex}\lambda+4,-\lambda+2,-\lambda+2{/tex} ) for some {tex}\lambda{/tex} . Drs of AP are {tex} \lt \lambda+2,-\lambda+1,-\lambda \gt {/tex} {tex}A P \perp l{/tex} {tex}(\lambda+2)-(-\lambda+1)-(-\lambda)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=-\frac{1}{3}{/tex} Therefore, the coordinates of P are {tex}\left(\frac{11}{3}, \frac{7}{3}, \frac{7}{3}\right){/tex} P is the mid-point of {tex}A A^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2+\alpha}{2}=\frac{11}{3}, \frac{1+\beta}{2}=\frac{7}{3}, \frac{2+\gamma}{2}=\frac{7}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{16}{3}, \beta=\frac{11}{3}, \gamma=\frac{8}{3}{/tex} The coordinates of the image are {tex}\left(\frac{16}{3}, \frac{11}{3}, \frac{8}{3}\right){/tex} The equation of {tex}A A^{\prime}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{x-2}{\frac{10}{3}}=\frac{y-1}{\frac{8}{3}}=\frac{z-2}{\frac{2}{3}}{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{3(x-2)}{5}=\frac{3(y-1)}{4}=\frac{3(z-2)}{1}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let the image of A in the line be {tex}A^{\prime}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma){/tex}
The point P , which is the point of intersection of the lines {tex}l{/tex} and {tex}A A^{\prime}{/tex}, will have coordinates ({tex}\lambda+4,-\lambda+2,-\lambda+2{/tex}) for some {tex}\lambda{/tex}.
Drs of AP are {tex} \lt \lambda+2,-\lambda+1,-\lambda \gt {/tex}
{tex}A P \perp l{/tex}
{tex}(\lambda+2)-(-\lambda+1)-(-\lambda)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=-\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
Therefore, the coordinates of P are {tex}\left(\frac{11}{3}, \frac{7}{3}, \frac{7}{3}\right){/tex}
P is the mid-point of {tex}A A^{\prime}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2+\alpha}{2}=\frac{11}{3}, \frac{1+\beta}{2}=\frac{7}{3}, \frac{2+\gamma}{2}=\frac{7}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{16}{3}, \beta=\frac{11}{3}, \gamma=\frac{8}{3}{/tex}
The coordinates of the image are {tex}\left(\frac{16}{3}, \frac{11}{3}, \frac{8}{3}\right){/tex}
The equation of {tex}A A^{\prime}{/tex} is
{tex}\frac{x-2}{\frac{10}{3}}=\frac{y-1}{\frac{8}{3}}=\frac{z-2}{\frac{2}{3}}{/tex}
or,
{tex}\frac{3(x-2)}{5}=\frac{3(y-1)}{4}=\frac{3(z-2)}{1}{/tex}

question_html: A vector {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} makes equal angles with all the three axes. If the magnitude of the vector is {tex}5 \sqrt{3}{/tex} units, then find {tex}\vec{a}{/tex}.
question_text: A vector {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} makes equal angles with all the three axes. If the magnitude of the vector is {tex}5 \sqrt{3}{/tex} units, then find {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} be the angle which the vector {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} makes with all the three axes. Then {tex}3 \cos ^{2} \alpha=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \alpha=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} The unit vector along the vector {tex}\vec{a}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(\hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{a}=5(\hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}){/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} be the angle which the vector {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} makes with all the three axes. Then {tex}3 \cos ^{2} \alpha=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \alpha=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex} The unit vector along the vector {tex}\vec{a}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(\hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{a}=5(\hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\alpha{/tex} be the angle which the vector {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} makes with all the three axes.
Then {tex}3 \cos ^{2} \alpha=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos \alpha=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}{/tex}
The unit vector along the vector {tex}\vec{a}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(\hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{a}=5(\hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Determine the vector equation of a line passing through the point {tex}(1,2,-3){/tex} and perpendicular to both the given lines
{tex}\frac{x-8}{3}=\frac{y+16}{-16}=\frac{x-10}{7} \text { and }{/tex} {tex} \frac{x-15}{3}=\frac{y-29}{-8}=\frac{z-5}{-5}{/tex}.
question_text: Determine the vector equation of a line passing through the point {tex}(1,2,-3){/tex} and perpendicular to both the given lines {tex}\frac{x-8}{3}=\frac{y+16}{-16}=\frac{x-10}{7} \text { and }{/tex} {tex} \frac{x-15}{3}=\frac{y-29}{-8}=\frac{z-5}{-5}{/tex} .
explanation: Let direction ratios of required line be {tex}{a}, {b}, {c}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}3 a-16 b+7 c=0{/tex} {tex}3 a-8 b-5 c=0{/tex} Solving we get {tex}\frac{a}{136}=\frac{b}{36}=\frac{c}{24}{/tex} DRs are {tex}136,36,24{/tex} or {tex}34,9,6{/tex} Equation is {tex}\frac{x-1}{136}=\frac{y-2}{36}=\frac{z+3}{24}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{x-1}{34}=\frac{y-2}{9}=\frac{z+3}{6}{/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=\hat{{i}}+2 \hat{{j}}-3 \hat{{k}}+\lambda(34 \hat{{i}}+9 \hat{{j}}+6 \hat{{k}}){/tex}
solution: Let direction ratios of required line be {tex}{a}, {b}, {c}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}3 a-16 b+7 c=0{/tex} {tex}3 a-8 b-5 c=0{/tex} Solving we get {tex}\frac{a}{136}=\frac{b}{36}=\frac{c}{24}{/tex} DRs are {tex}136,36,24{/tex} or {tex}34,9,6{/tex} Equation is {tex}\frac{x-1}{136}=\frac{y-2}{36}=\frac{z+3}{24}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{x-1}{34}=\frac{y-2}{9}=\frac{z+3}{6}{/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=\hat{{i}}+2 \hat{{j}}-3 \hat{{k}}+\lambda(34 \hat{{i}}+9 \hat{{j}}+6 \hat{{k}}){/tex}
solution_html:
Let direction ratios of required line be {tex}{a}, {b}, {c}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}3 a-16 b+7 c=0{/tex}
{tex}3 a-8 b-5 c=0{/tex}
Solving we get {tex}\frac{a}{136}=\frac{b}{36}=\frac{c}{24}{/tex}
DRs are {tex}136,36,24{/tex} or {tex}34,9,6{/tex}
Equation is {tex}\frac{x-1}{136}=\frac{y-2}{36}=\frac{z+3}{24}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{x-1}{34}=\frac{y-2}{9}=\frac{z+3}{6}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{r}=\hat{{i}}+2 \hat{{j}}-3 \hat{{k}}+\lambda(34 \hat{{i}}+9 \hat{{j}}+6 \hat{{k}}){/tex}

question_html:
Find the angle at which the given lines are inclined to each other:
{tex}l_{1}: \frac{{x}-5}{2}=\frac{{y}+3}{1}=\frac{{z}-1}{-3}{/tex}
{tex}l_{2}: \frac{{x}}{3}=\frac{{y}-1}{2}=\frac{{z}+5}{-1}{/tex}
question_text: Find the angle at which the given lines are inclined to each other: {tex}l_{1}: \frac{{x}-5}{2}=\frac{{y}+3}{1}=\frac{{z}-1}{-3}{/tex} {tex}l_{2}: \frac{{x}}{3}=\frac{{y}-1}{2}=\frac{{z}+5}{-1}{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between given lines {tex} \therefore \cos \theta=\frac{(2 \times 3)+(1 \times 2)+(-3 \times-1)}{\sqrt{14} \sqrt{14}} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{11}{14} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{11}{14}\right) {/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between given lines {tex} \therefore \cos \theta=\frac{(2 \times 3)+(1 \times 2)+(-3 \times-1)}{\sqrt{14} \sqrt{14}} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{11}{14} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{11}{14}\right) {/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between given lines
{tex} \therefore \cos \theta=\frac{(2 \times 3)+(1 \times 2)+(-3 \times-1)}{\sqrt{14} \sqrt{14}} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{11}{14} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{11}{14}\right) {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} if the following lines are perpendicular to each other:
{tex}l_{1}: \frac{1-{x}}{-3}=\frac{3 {y}-2}{2 \lambda}=\frac{{z}-3}{3}{/tex}
{tex}l_{2}: \frac{{x}-1}{3 \lambda}=\frac{1-{y}}{1}=\frac{2 {z}-5}{3}{/tex}
question_text: Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} if the following lines are perpendicular to each other: {tex}l_{1}: \frac{1-{x}}{-3}=\frac{3 {y}-2}{2 \lambda}=\frac{{z}-3}{3}{/tex} {tex}l_{2}: \frac{{x}-1}{3 \lambda}=\frac{1-{y}}{1}=\frac{2 {z}-5}{3}{/tex}
explanation: {tex}l_{1}: \frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y-\frac{2}{3}}{\frac{2}{3} \lambda}=\frac{z-3}{3}{/tex} {tex}{l}_{2}: \frac{{x}-1}{3 \lambda}=\frac{{y}-1}{-1}=\frac{{z}-\frac{5}{2}}{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} lines are perpendicular {tex}\Rightarrow 3(3 \lambda)+\frac{2}{3} \lambda(-1)+3 \cdot \frac{3}{2}=0{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{-27}{50}{/tex}
solution: {tex}l_{1}: \frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y-\frac{2}{3}}{\frac{2}{3} \lambda}=\frac{z-3}{3}{/tex} {tex}{l}_{2}: \frac{{x}-1}{3 \lambda}=\frac{{y}-1}{-1}=\frac{{z}-\frac{5}{2}}{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} lines are perpendicular {tex}\Rightarrow 3(3 \lambda)+\frac{2}{3} \lambda(-1)+3 \cdot \frac{3}{2}=0{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{-27}{50}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}l_{1}: \frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y-\frac{2}{3}}{\frac{2}{3} \lambda}=\frac{z-3}{3}{/tex}
{tex}{l}_{2}: \frac{{x}-1}{3 \lambda}=\frac{{y}-1}{-1}=\frac{{z}-\frac{5}{2}}{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex}
lines are perpendicular {tex}\Rightarrow 3(3 \lambda)+\frac{2}{3} \lambda(-1)+3 \cdot \frac{3}{2}=0{/tex}
{tex}\lambda=\frac{-27}{50}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
An engineer is designing a new metro rail network in a city.

Initially, two metro lines, Line A and Line B, each consisting of multiple stations are designed. The track for Line A is represented by {tex}l_{1}: \frac{{x}-2}{3}=\frac{{y}+1}{-2}=\frac{{z}-3}{4}{/tex}, while the track for Line B is represented by {tex}l_{2}: \frac{{x}-1}{2}=\frac{{y}-3}{1}=\frac{{z}+2}{-3}{/tex}.
Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Find whether the two metro tracks are parallel. (1)
  2. Solar panels are to be installed on the rooftop of the metro stations. Determine the equation of the line representing the placement of solar panels on the rooftop of Line A's stations, given that panels are to be positioned parallel to Line A's track ({tex}l_{1}{/tex}) and pass through the point {tex}(1,-2,-3){/tex}. (1)
    1. To connect the stations, a pedestrian pathway perpendicular to the two metro lines is to be constructed which passes through point ( {tex}3,2,1{/tex} ). Determine the equation of the pedestrian walkway. (2)

      OR

    2. Find the shortest distance between Line A and Line B. (2)
question_text: An engineer is designing a new metro rail network in a city. Initially, two metro lines, Line A and Line B, each consisting of multiple stations are designed. The track for Line A is represented by {tex}l_{1}: \frac{{x}-2}{3}=\frac{{y}+1}{-2}=\frac{{z}-3}{4}{/tex} , while the track for Line B is represented by {tex}l_{2}: \frac{{x}-1}{2}=\frac{{y}-3}{1}=\frac{{z}+2}{-3}{/tex} . Based on the above information, answer the following questions: Find whether the two metro tracks are parallel. (1) Solar panels are to be installed on the rooftop of the metro stations. Determine the equation of the line representing the placement of solar panels on the rooftop of Line A's stations, given that panels are to be positioned parallel to Line A's track ( {tex}l_{1}{/tex} ) and pass through the point {tex}(1,-2,-3){/tex} . (1) To connect the stations, a pedestrian pathway perpendicular to the two metro lines is to be constructed which passes through point ( {tex}3,2,1{/tex} ). Determine the equation of the pedestrian walkway. (2) OR Find the shortest distance between Line A and Line B. (2)
explanation: {tex}l_{1}: \frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y+1}{-2}=\frac{z-3}{4} ; {/tex} {tex}l_{2}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-3}{1}=\frac{z+2}{-3}{/tex} Drs of {tex}l_{1}{/tex} are {tex}\langle 3,-2,4\rangle{/tex} , drs of {tex}l_{2}{/tex} are {tex}\langle 2,1,-3\rangle{/tex} as drs are not proportional, hence {tex}l_{1}{/tex} is not parallel to {tex}l_{2}{/tex} . Equations of line parallel to {tex}l_{1}{/tex} and passing through {tex}(1,-2,-3){/tex} is {tex}\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+2}{-2}=\frac{z+3}{4} \text { or } \vec{r}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} The pathway is perpendicular to {tex}l_{1}{/tex} and {tex}l_{2} \ldots{/tex} . It is parallel to {tex}\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -2 & 4 \\ 2 & 1 & -3\end{array}\right|=2 \hat{i}+17 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of pathway is {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+17 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}){/tex} OR {tex}\vec{a}_{1}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_{2}=\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}_{1}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_{2}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}d=\left|\frac{\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right)}{\left|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right|}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\left|\begin{array}{c}(-\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}+17 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}) \\ \sqrt{4+289+49}\end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\frac{31}{\sqrt{342}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}l_{1}: \frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y+1}{-2}=\frac{z-3}{4} ; {/tex} {tex}l_{2}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-3}{1}=\frac{z+2}{-3}{/tex} Drs of {tex}l_{1}{/tex} are {tex}\langle 3,-2,4\rangle{/tex} , drs of {tex}l_{2}{/tex} are {tex}\langle 2,1,-3\rangle{/tex} as drs are not proportional, hence {tex}l_{1}{/tex} is not parallel to {tex}l_{2}{/tex} . Equations of line parallel to {tex}l_{1}{/tex} and passing through {tex}(1,-2,-3){/tex} is {tex}\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+2}{-2}=\frac{z+3}{4} \text { or } \vec{r}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} The pathway is perpendicular to {tex}l_{1}{/tex} and {tex}l_{2} \ldots{/tex} . It is parallel to {tex}\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -2 & 4 \\ 2 & 1 & -3\end{array}\right|=2 \hat{i}+17 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of pathway is {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+17 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}){/tex} OR {tex}\vec{a}_{1}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_{2}=\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}_{1}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_{2}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}d=\left|\frac{\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right)}{\left|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right|}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\left|\begin{array}{c}(-\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}+17 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}) \\ \sqrt{4+289+49}\end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=\frac{31}{\sqrt{342}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}l_{1}: \frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y+1}{-2}=\frac{z-3}{4} ; {/tex} {tex}l_{2}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-3}{1}=\frac{z+2}{-3}{/tex}

  1. Drs of {tex}l_{1}{/tex} are {tex}\langle 3,-2,4\rangle{/tex}, drs of {tex}l_{2}{/tex} are {tex}\langle 2,1,-3\rangle{/tex} as drs are not proportional, hence {tex}l_{1}{/tex} is not parallel to {tex}l_{2}{/tex}.
  2. Equations of line parallel to {tex}l_{1}{/tex} and passing through {tex}(1,-2,-3){/tex} is
    {tex}\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+2}{-2}=\frac{z+3}{4} \text { or } \vec{r}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex}
    1. The pathway is perpendicular to {tex}l_{1}{/tex} and {tex}l_{2} \ldots{/tex}. It is parallel to {tex}\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}{/tex}
      {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -2 & 4 \\ 2 & 1 & -3\end{array}\right|=2 \hat{i}+17 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex}
      {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of pathway is {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+17 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}){/tex}

      OR

    2. {tex}\vec{a}_{1}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_{2}=\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}
      {tex}\vec{b}_{1}=3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_{2}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex}
      {tex}d=\left|\frac{\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right)}{\left|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right|}\right|{/tex}
      {tex}=\left|\begin{array}{c}(-\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}+17 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}) \\ \sqrt{4+289+49}\end{array}\right|{/tex}
      {tex}=\frac{31}{\sqrt{342}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the equation of a line in vector and cartesian form which passes through the point ({tex}1,2,-4{/tex}) and is perpendicular to the lines {tex}\frac{{x}-8}{3}=\frac{{y}+19}{-16}=\frac{{z}-10}{7}{/tex}, and {tex}\vec{r}=15 \hat{i}+29 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}+\mu(3 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}) .{/tex}
question_text: Find the equation of a line in vector and cartesian form which passes through the point ( {tex}1,2,-4{/tex} ) and is perpendicular to the lines {tex}\frac{{x}-8}{3}=\frac{{y}+19}{-16}=\frac{{z}-10}{7}{/tex} , and {tex}\vec{r}=15 \hat{i}+29 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}+\mu(3 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}) .{/tex}
explanation: Given lines are {tex}\frac{x-8}{3}=\frac{y+19}{-16}=\frac{z-10}{7}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(15 \hat{i}+29 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})+\mu(3 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}){/tex} The first line in vector form is {tex}\vec{r}=(8 \hat{i}-19 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k})+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex} \vec{a}_1=8 \hat{i}-19 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_2=15 \hat{i}+29 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k} {/tex} {tex} \vec{b}_1=3 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_2=3 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k} {/tex} {tex}\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -16 & 7 \\ 3 & 8 & -5\end{array}\right|=24 \hat{i}+36 \hat{j}+72 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of line passing through {tex}(1,2,-4){/tex} and parallel to {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} is {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})+t(24 \hat{i}+36 \hat{j}+72 \hat{k}){/tex} or {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})+t^{\prime}(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} Cartesian form of line is {tex}\frac{x-1}{24}=\frac{y-2}{36}=\frac{z+4}{72}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z+4}{6}{/tex}
solution: Given lines are {tex}\frac{x-8}{3}=\frac{y+19}{-16}=\frac{z-10}{7}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(15 \hat{i}+29 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})+\mu(3 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}){/tex} The first line in vector form is {tex}\vec{r}=(8 \hat{i}-19 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k})+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex} \vec{a}_1=8 \hat{i}-19 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_2=15 \hat{i}+29 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k} {/tex} {tex} \vec{b}_1=3 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_2=3 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k} {/tex} {tex}\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -16 & 7 \\ 3 & 8 & -5\end{array}\right|=24 \hat{i}+36 \hat{j}+72 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of line passing through {tex}(1,2,-4){/tex} and parallel to {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} is {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})+t(24 \hat{i}+36 \hat{j}+72 \hat{k}){/tex} or {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})+t^{\prime}(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} Cartesian form of line is {tex}\frac{x-1}{24}=\frac{y-2}{36}=\frac{z+4}{72}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z+4}{6}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given lines are {tex}\frac{x-8}{3}=\frac{y+19}{-16}=\frac{z-10}{7}{/tex}
and {tex}\vec{r}=(15 \hat{i}+29 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})+\mu(3 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}){/tex}
The first line in vector form is {tex}\vec{r}=(8 \hat{i}-19 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k})+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex} \vec{a}_1=8 \hat{i}-19 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_2=15 \hat{i}+29 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k} {/tex}
{tex} \vec{b}_1=3 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_2=3 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k} {/tex}
{tex}\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -16 & 7 \\ 3 & 8 & -5\end{array}\right|=24 \hat{i}+36 \hat{j}+72 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of line passing through {tex}(1,2,-4){/tex} and parallel to {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} is
{tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})+t(24 \hat{i}+36 \hat{j}+72 \hat{k}){/tex} or {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})+t^{\prime}(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex}
Cartesian form of line is {tex}\frac{x-1}{24}=\frac{y-2}{36}=\frac{z+4}{72}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z+4}{6}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the shortest distance between the lines {tex}l_{1}{/tex} and {tex}l_{2}{/tex} given by:
{tex}l_{1}: \vec{{r}} =\hat{{i}}+2 \hat{{j}}-4 \hat{{k}}+\lambda(4 \hat{{i}}+6 \hat{{j}}+12 \hat{{k}}){/tex} {tex}\text { and } l_{2}: \vec{{r}} =3 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}-5 \hat{{k}}+\mu(6 \hat{{i}}+9 \hat{{j}}+18 \hat{{k}}){/tex}
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the lines {tex}l_{1}{/tex} and {tex}l_{2}{/tex} given by: {tex}l_{1}: \vec{{r}} =\hat{{i}}+2 \hat{{j}}-4 \hat{{k}}+\lambda(4 \hat{{i}}+6 \hat{{j}}+12 \hat{{k}}){/tex} {tex}\text { and } l_{2}: \vec{{r}} =3 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}-5 \hat{{k}}+\mu(6 \hat{{i}}+9 \hat{{j}}+18 \hat{{k}}){/tex}
explanation: Given lines are: {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})+2 \lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})+3 \mu(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} Clearly, the given lines are parallel. Here, {tex}\vec{a}_{1}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_{2}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 2 & 3 & 6\end{array}\right|=9 \hat{i}-14 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore\left|\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \times \vec{b}\right|=\sqrt{81+196+16}=\sqrt{293}{/tex} Also, {tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{4+9+36}=7{/tex} S.D. {tex}=\frac{\left|\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \times \vec{b}\right|}{|\vec{b}|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{293}}{7}{/tex}
solution: Given lines are: {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})+2 \lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})+3 \mu(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} Clearly, the given lines are parallel. Here, {tex}\vec{a}_{1}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_{2}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 2 & 3 & 6\end{array}\right|=9 \hat{i}-14 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore\left|\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \times \vec{b}\right|=\sqrt{81+196+16}=\sqrt{293}{/tex} Also, {tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{4+9+36}=7{/tex} S.D. {tex}=\frac{\left|\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \times \vec{b}\right|}{|\vec{b}|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{293}}{7}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given lines are: {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})+2 \lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex}
and {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k})+3 \mu(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex}
Clearly, the given lines are parallel.
Here, {tex}\vec{a}_{1}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_{2}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 2 & 3 & 6\end{array}\right|=9 \hat{i}-14 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore\left|\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \times \vec{b}\right|=\sqrt{81+196+16}=\sqrt{293}{/tex}
Also, {tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{4+9+36}=7{/tex}
S.D. {tex}=\frac{\left|\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \times \vec{b}\right|}{|\vec{b}|}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\sqrt{293}}{7}{/tex}

question_html: Find the distance of the point {tex}(-1,-5,-10){/tex} from the point of intersection of the lines {tex}\frac{{x}-1}{2}=\frac{{y}-2}{3}=\frac{{z}-3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{x}-4}{5}=\frac{{y}-1}{2}={z}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the distance of the point {tex}(-1,-5,-10){/tex} from the point of intersection of the lines {tex}\frac{{x}-1}{2}=\frac{{y}-2}{3}=\frac{{z}-3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{x}-4}{5}=\frac{{y}-1}{2}={z}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}l_{1}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\lambda{/tex} Any point on {tex}l_{1}{/tex} is {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3){/tex} {tex}l_{2}: \frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-0}{1}=\mu{/tex} Any point on {tex}l_{2}{/tex} is {tex}(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex} For point of intersection, {tex}2 \lambda+1=5 \mu+4,3 \lambda+2=2 \mu+1{/tex} Solving, {tex}\lambda=\mu=-1{/tex} Since, {tex}\lambda=\mu=-1{/tex} satisfy {tex}4 \lambda+3=\mu{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Point of intersection is {tex}(-1,-1,-1){/tex} Now distance of {tex}(-1,-5,-10){/tex} from {tex}(-1,-1,-1){/tex} is: {tex}\sqrt{(-1+1)^{2}+(-1+5)^{2}+(-1+10)^{2}}=\sqrt{97}{/tex} units
solution: {tex}l_{1}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\lambda{/tex} Any point on {tex}l_{1}{/tex} is {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3){/tex} {tex}l_{2}: \frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-0}{1}=\mu{/tex} Any point on {tex}l_{2}{/tex} is {tex}(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex} For point of intersection, {tex}2 \lambda+1=5 \mu+4,3 \lambda+2=2 \mu+1{/tex} Solving, {tex}\lambda=\mu=-1{/tex} Since, {tex}\lambda=\mu=-1{/tex} satisfy {tex}4 \lambda+3=\mu{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Point of intersection is {tex}(-1,-1,-1){/tex} Now distance of {tex}(-1,-5,-10){/tex} from {tex}(-1,-1,-1){/tex} is: {tex}\sqrt{(-1+1)^{2}+(-1+5)^{2}+(-1+10)^{2}}=\sqrt{97}{/tex} units
solution_html:
{tex}l_{1}: \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}=\lambda{/tex}
Any point on {tex}l_{1}{/tex} is {tex}(2 \lambda+1,3 \lambda+2,4 \lambda+3){/tex}
{tex}l_{2}: \frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-0}{1}=\mu{/tex}
Any point on {tex}l_{2}{/tex} is {tex}(5 \mu+4,2 \mu+1, \mu){/tex}
For point of intersection,
{tex}2 \lambda+1=5 \mu+4,3 \lambda+2=2 \mu+1{/tex}
Solving, {tex}\lambda=\mu=-1{/tex}
Since, {tex}\lambda=\mu=-1{/tex} satisfy {tex}4 \lambda+3=\mu{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Point of intersection is {tex}(-1,-1,-1){/tex}
Now distance of {tex}(-1,-5,-10){/tex} from {tex}(-1,-1,-1){/tex} is:
{tex}\sqrt{(-1+1)^{2}+(-1+5)^{2}+(-1+10)^{2}}=\sqrt{97}{/tex} units

question_text: Let the position vectors of the points {tex}A, B{/tex} and {tex}C{/tex} be {tex}3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} , {tex}\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively. Find the angle between the lines
{tex}\vec{r}=(3+2 \lambda) \hat{i}-(2-2 \lambda) \hat{j}+(6+2 \lambda) \hat{k} \text { and }{/tex}
{tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{{j}}-5 \hat{{k}})+\mu(6 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}+2 \hat{{k}}){/tex}.
question_text: Find the angle between the lines {tex}\vec{r}=(3+2 \lambda) \hat{i}-(2-2 \lambda) \hat{j}+(6+2 \lambda) \hat{k} \text { and }{/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{{j}}-5 \hat{{k}})+\mu(6 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}+2 \hat{{k}}){/tex} .
explanation: Given lines are: {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})+\mu(6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between these two lines. {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{2(6)+2(3)+2(2)}{\sqrt{4+4+4} \sqrt{36+9+4}}=\frac{22}{2 \sqrt{3} \times 7}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{11}{21} \sqrt{3} \Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{11}{21} \sqrt{3}\right){/tex}
solution: Given lines are: {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})+\mu(6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between these two lines. {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{2(6)+2(3)+2(2)}{\sqrt{4+4+4} \sqrt{36+9+4}}=\frac{22}{2 \sqrt{3} \times 7}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{11}{21} \sqrt{3} \Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{11}{21} \sqrt{3}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Given lines are: {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}
and {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})+\mu(6 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}
Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between these two lines.
{tex}\cos \theta=\frac{2(6)+2(3)+2(2)}{\sqrt{4+4+4} \sqrt{36+9+4}}=\frac{22}{2 \sqrt{3} \times 7}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{11}{21} \sqrt{3} \Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{11}{21} \sqrt{3}\right){/tex}

question_text: Determine if the lines {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-\hat{j}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(4 \hat{i}-\hat{k})+\mu(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} intersect with each other.
explanation: {tex} \vec{b}_1=3 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}, \vec{b}_2=2 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{k}}, {/tex} {tex}\vec {{a}}_2=4 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{k}}, \vec{a}_1=\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}} {/tex} {tex} \left(\vec{{a}_2}-\vec{a}_1\right)=3 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}} {/tex} {tex} \vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2=-3 \hat{{i}}-9 \hat{{j}}+2 \hat{{k}} {/tex} {tex}\left(\overrightarrow{{a}_2}-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right){/tex} {tex}=(3 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}) \cdot(-3 \hat{{i}}-9 \hat{{j}}+2 \hat{{k}})=0{/tex} , hence lines intersect
solution: {tex} \vec{b}_1=3 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}, \vec{b}_2=2 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{k}}, {/tex} {tex}\vec {{a}}_2=4 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{k}}, \vec{a}_1=\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}} {/tex} {tex} \left(\vec{{a}_2}-\vec{a}_1\right)=3 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}} {/tex} {tex} \vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2=-3 \hat{{i}}-9 \hat{{j}}+2 \hat{{k}} {/tex} {tex}\left(\overrightarrow{{a}_2}-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right){/tex} {tex}=(3 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}) \cdot(-3 \hat{{i}}-9 \hat{{j}}+2 \hat{{k}})=0{/tex} , hence lines intersect
solution_html:
{tex} \vec{b}_1=3 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}, \vec{b}_2=2 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{k}}, {/tex}{tex}\vec {{a}}_2=4 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{k}}, \vec{a}_1=\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}} {/tex}
{tex} \left(\vec{{a}_2}-\vec{a}_1\right)=3 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}} {/tex}

{tex}\left(\overrightarrow{{a}_2}-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right){/tex} {tex}=(3 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}) \cdot(-3 \hat{{i}}-9 \hat{{j}}+2 \hat{{k}})=0{/tex}, hence lines intersect

question_html: Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex}, if the points {tex}(-1,-1,2),(2,8, \lambda){/tex} and {tex}(3,11,6){/tex} are collinear.
question_text: Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} , if the points {tex}(-1,-1,2),(2,8, \lambda){/tex} and {tex}(3,11,6){/tex} are collinear.
explanation: {tex}A(-1,-1,2), B(2,8, \lambda), C(3,11,6){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=3 \hat{\imath}+9 \hat{\jmath}+(\lambda-2) \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{B C}=\hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+(6-\lambda) \hat{k}{/tex} Since {tex}A, B{/tex} and {tex}C{/tex} are collinear, {tex}\frac{3}{1}=\frac{9}{3}=\frac{\lambda-2}{6-\lambda}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=5{/tex}
solution: {tex}A(-1,-1,2), B(2,8, \lambda), C(3,11,6){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=3 \hat{\imath}+9 \hat{\jmath}+(\lambda-2) \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{B C}=\hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+(6-\lambda) \hat{k}{/tex} Since {tex}A, B{/tex} and {tex}C{/tex} are collinear, {tex}\frac{3}{1}=\frac{9}{3}=\frac{\lambda-2}{6-\lambda}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=5{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}A(-1,-1,2), B(2,8, \lambda), C(3,11,6){/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{A B}=3 \hat{\imath}+9 \hat{\jmath}+(\lambda-2) \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{B C}=\hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+(6-\lambda) \hat{k}{/tex}
Since {tex}A, B{/tex} and {tex}C{/tex} are collinear, {tex}\frac{3}{1}=\frac{9}{3}=\frac{\lambda-2}{6-\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=5{/tex}

question_html: Find the image of the point {tex}(-1,5,2){/tex} in the line {tex}\frac{2 {x}-4}{2}=\frac{{y}}{2}=\frac{2-{z}}{3}{/tex}. Find the length of the line segment joining the points (given point and the image point).
question_text: Find the image of the point {tex}(-1,5,2){/tex} in the line {tex}\frac{2 {x}-4}{2}=\frac{{y}}{2}=\frac{2-{z}}{3}{/tex} . Find the length of the line segment joining the points (given point and the image point).
explanation: Let {tex}{A}^{\prime}({a}, {b}, {c}){/tex} be the image of the point {tex}{A}(-{1 , 5 , 2}){/tex} in the given line, also assume ' {tex}M{/tex} ' as the point of intersection of {tex}A A^{\prime}{/tex} with the given line, then ' {tex}M{/tex} ' is the mid-point of the line segment {tex}{A A}^{\prime}{/tex} The Line in the standard form is: {tex}\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z-2}{-3}{/tex} , then {tex}M{/tex} is the point {tex}(\lambda+2,2 \lambda,-3 \lambda+2){/tex} , for some {tex}\lambda \in R{/tex} Direction Ratios of AM are {tex}\lambda+3,2 \lambda-5,-3 \lambda{/tex} AM {tex}\perp{/tex} Line, {tex}\therefore 1(\lambda+3)+2(2 \lambda-5)-3(-3 \lambda)=0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{1}{2} {M}\left(\frac{5}{2}, 1, \frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}={M}\left(\frac{{a}-1}{2}, \frac{{~b}+5}{2}, \frac{{c}+2}{2}\right) \Rightarrow {a}=6, {~b}=-3, {c}=-1{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The Image of {tex}A{/tex} in the line is {tex}A^{\prime}(6,-3,-1){/tex} {tex}\text { And, } A A^{\prime}=\sqrt{49+64+9}=\sqrt{122}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}{A}^{\prime}({a}, {b}, {c}){/tex} be the image of the point {tex}{A}(-{1 , 5 , 2}){/tex} in the given line, also assume ' {tex}M{/tex} ' as the point of intersection of {tex}A A^{\prime}{/tex} with the given line, then ' {tex}M{/tex} ' is the mid-point of the line segment {tex}{A A}^{\prime}{/tex} The Line in the standard form is: {tex}\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z-2}{-3}{/tex} , then {tex}M{/tex} is the point {tex}(\lambda+2,2 \lambda,-3 \lambda+2){/tex} , for some {tex}\lambda \in R{/tex} Direction Ratios of AM are {tex}\lambda+3,2 \lambda-5,-3 \lambda{/tex} AM {tex}\perp{/tex} Line, {tex}\therefore 1(\lambda+3)+2(2 \lambda-5)-3(-3 \lambda)=0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{1}{2} {M}\left(\frac{5}{2}, 1, \frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}={M}\left(\frac{{a}-1}{2}, \frac{{~b}+5}{2}, \frac{{c}+2}{2}\right) \Rightarrow {a}=6, {~b}=-3, {c}=-1{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The Image of {tex}A{/tex} in the line is {tex}A^{\prime}(6,-3,-1){/tex} {tex}\text { And, } A A^{\prime}=\sqrt{49+64+9}=\sqrt{122}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}{A}^{\prime}({a}, {b}, {c}){/tex} be the image of the point {tex}{A}(-{1 , 5 , 2}){/tex} in the given line, also assume ' {tex}M{/tex} ' as the point of intersection of {tex}A A^{\prime}{/tex} with the given line, then ' {tex}M{/tex} ' is the mid-point of the line segment {tex}{A A}^{\prime}{/tex}

The Line in the standard form is: {tex}\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z-2}{-3}{/tex}, then {tex}M{/tex} is the point {tex}(\lambda+2,2 \lambda,-3 \lambda+2){/tex}, for some {tex}\lambda \in R{/tex}
Direction Ratios of AM are {tex}\lambda+3,2 \lambda-5,-3 \lambda{/tex}
AM {tex}\perp{/tex} Line,
{tex}\therefore 1(\lambda+3)+2(2 \lambda-5)-3(-3 \lambda)=0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{1}{2} {M}\left(\frac{5}{2}, 1, \frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}={M}\left(\frac{{a}-1}{2}, \frac{{~b}+5}{2}, \frac{{c}+2}{2}\right) \Rightarrow {a}=6, {~b}=-3, {c}=-1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The Image of {tex}A{/tex} in the line is {tex}A^{\prime}(6,-3,-1){/tex}
{tex}\text { And, } A A^{\prime}=\sqrt{49+64+9}=\sqrt{122}{/tex}

question_html:
Find the shortest distance between the lines:
{tex}\vec{r} =(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{r} =(\hat{i}+4 \hat{k})+\mu(3 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+9 \hat{k}){/tex}
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the lines: {tex}\vec{r} =(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{r} =(\hat{i}+4 \hat{k})+\mu(3 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+9 \hat{k}){/tex}
explanation: The two given lines are parallel with, {tex} \vec{a}_1=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_2=\hat{i}+4 \hat{k} {/tex} Then {tex}\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1=-\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and the parallel vector is {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex} \vec{b} \times\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & -2 & 3 \\ -1 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right|=-5 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-\hat{k} {/tex} Shortest Distance {tex}=\frac{\left|\vec{b} \times\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right)\right|}{|\vec{b}|}=\frac{\sqrt{42}}{\sqrt{14}}=\sqrt{3}{/tex}
solution: The two given lines are parallel with, {tex} \vec{a}_1=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_2=\hat{i}+4 \hat{k} {/tex} Then {tex}\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1=-\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and the parallel vector is {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex} \vec{b} \times\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & -2 & 3 \\ -1 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right|=-5 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-\hat{k} {/tex} Shortest Distance {tex}=\frac{\left|\vec{b} \times\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right)\right|}{|\vec{b}|}=\frac{\sqrt{42}}{\sqrt{14}}=\sqrt{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
The two given lines are parallel with,
{tex} \vec{a}_1=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_2=\hat{i}+4 \hat{k} {/tex}
Then {tex}\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1=-\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and the parallel vector is {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex} \vec{b} \times\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & -2 & 3 \\ -1 & 1 & 1 \end{array}\right|=-5 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-\hat{k} {/tex}
Shortest Distance {tex}=\frac{\left|\vec{b} \times\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right)\right|}{|\vec{b}|}=\frac{\sqrt{42}}{\sqrt{14}}=\sqrt{3}{/tex}

question_html: If a line makes an angle of 30o with the positive direction of x-axis, 120o with the positive direction of y-axis, then the angle which it makes with the positive direction of z-axis is:
question_text: If a line makes an angle of 30 o with the positive direction of x-axis, 120 o with the positive direction of y-axis, then the angle which it makes with the positive direction of z-axis is:

options.explanation: To find the angle with the z-axis, we use the relation: {tex} \cos ^2 \alpha+\cos ^2 \beta+\cos ^2 \gamma=1{/tex} where {tex}\alpha=30^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\beta=120^{\circ}{/tex} . Calculating, we get: {tex} \cos ^2 30^{\circ}=\frac{3}{4}, \cos ^2 120^{\circ}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} Thus, {tex} \frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{4}+\cos ^2 \gamma=1 \Longrightarrow \cos ^2 \gamma=0 \Longrightarrow \gamma=90^{\circ} .{/tex} So, the angle with the z-axis is 90 o .

explanation: To find the angle with the z-axis, we use the relation: {tex} \cos ^2 \alpha+\cos ^2 \beta+\cos ^2 \gamma=1{/tex} where {tex}\alpha=30^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\beta=120^{\circ}{/tex} . Calculating, we get: {tex} \cos ^2 30^{\circ}=\frac{3}{4}, \cos ^2 120^{\circ}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} Thus, {tex} \frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{4}+\cos ^2 \gamma=1 \Longrightarrow \cos ^2 \gamma=0 \Longrightarrow \gamma=90^{\circ} .{/tex} So, the angle with the z-axis is 90 o .

question_html:
Equations of sides of a parallelogram ABCD are as follows:
{tex}\mathrm{AB}: \quad \frac{\mathrm{x}+1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-2}{-2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}B C: \quad \frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+2}{-5}=\frac{z-5}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{CD}: \frac{\mathrm{x}-4}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+7}{-2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-8}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\text { DA: } \frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y+3}{-5}=\frac{z-4}{3}{/tex}
Find the equation of diagonal BD.
question_text: Equations of sides of a parallelogram ABCD are as follows: {tex}\mathrm{AB}: \quad \frac{\mathrm{x}+1}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-2}{-2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-1}{2}{/tex} {tex}B C: \quad \frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+2}{-5}=\frac{z-5}{3}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{CD}: \frac{\mathrm{x}-4}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+7}{-2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-8}{2}{/tex} {tex}\text { DA: } \frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y+3}{-5}=\frac{z-4}{3}{/tex} Find the equation of diagonal BD.
explanation: Let coordinates of B (from AB) = {tex}(\lambda-1,2-2 \lambda, 2 \lambda+1){/tex} Also coordinates of B (from BC) = {tex}(3 \mu+1,-5 \mu-2,3 \mu+5){/tex} Solving we get {tex}\lambda=2, \mu=0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Point of intersection is B(1, -2, 5) Similarly, point of intersection from CD and DA is D (2, -3, 4) Direction ratios of BD are 2 - 1, -3 + 2, 4 - 5 i.e. 1, -1, -1 Equation of BD is {tex}\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y+2}{-1}=\frac{z-5}{-1}{/tex}
solution: Let coordinates of B (from AB) = {tex}(\lambda-1,2-2 \lambda, 2 \lambda+1){/tex} Also coordinates of B (from BC) = {tex}(3 \mu+1,-5 \mu-2,3 \mu+5){/tex} Solving we get {tex}\lambda=2, \mu=0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Point of intersection is B(1, -2, 5) Similarly, point of intersection from CD and DA is D (2, -3, 4) Direction ratios of BD are 2 - 1, -3 + 2, 4 - 5 i.e. 1, -1, -1 Equation of BD is {tex}\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y+2}{-1}=\frac{z-5}{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let coordinates of B (from AB) = {tex}(\lambda-1,2-2 \lambda, 2 \lambda+1){/tex}
Also coordinates of B (from BC) = {tex}(3 \mu+1,-5 \mu-2,3 \mu+5){/tex}
Solving we get {tex}\lambda=2, \mu=0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Point of intersection is B(1, -2, 5)
Similarly, point of intersection from CD and DA is D (2, -3, 4)
Direction ratios of BD are 2 - 1, -3 + 2, 4 - 5 i.e. 1, -1, -1
Equation of BD is
{tex}\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y+2}{-1}=\frac{z-5}{-1}{/tex}

question_html:
Find the co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point {tex}(2,3,-8){/tex} to the line {tex}\frac{4-x}{2}=\frac{y}{6}=\frac{1-z}{3}{/tex}.
Also, find the perpendicular distance of the given point from the line.
question_text: Find the co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point {tex}(2,3,-8){/tex} to the line {tex}\frac{4-x}{2}=\frac{y}{6}=\frac{1-z}{3}{/tex} . Also, find the perpendicular distance of the given point from the line.
explanation: We have equation of line is {tex}\frac{{4 - x}}{2} = \frac{y}{6} = \frac{{1 - z}}{3}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 4}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{y}{6} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 3}} = \lambda {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = - 2\lambda + 4,y = 6\lambda {/tex} and {tex}z = - 3\lambda + 1{/tex} Let the coordinates of L be {tex}(4 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda ,1 - 3\lambda ){/tex} , then, direction ratios of PL are proportional to {tex}(4 - 2\lambda - 2,6\lambda - 3,1 - 3\lambda + 8){/tex} i.e., {tex}(2 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda - 3,9 - 3\lambda ){/tex} . Also, direction ratios are proportional to -2, 6, -3. Since, PL is perpendicular to give line. {tex}\therefore - 2(2 - 2\lambda ) + 6(6\lambda - 3) - 3(9 - 3\lambda ) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow - 4 + 4\lambda + 36\lambda - 18 - 27 + 9\lambda = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 49\lambda = 49 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex} So, the coordinates of L are {tex}\left( {4 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda ,1 - 3\lambda } \right){/tex} i.e., (2, 6, -2). Also, length of PL {tex} = \sqrt {{{(2 - 2)}^2} + {{(6 - 3)}^2} + {{( - 2 + 8)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {0 + 9 + 36} = 3\sqrt 5 units{/tex}
solution: We have equation of line is {tex}\frac{{4 - x}}{2} = \frac{y}{6} = \frac{{1 - z}}{3}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 4}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{y}{6} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 3}} = \lambda {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = - 2\lambda + 4,y = 6\lambda {/tex} and {tex}z = - 3\lambda + 1{/tex} Let the coordinates of L be {tex}(4 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda ,1 - 3\lambda ){/tex} , then, direction ratios of PL are proportional to {tex}(4 - 2\lambda - 2,6\lambda - 3,1 - 3\lambda + 8){/tex} i.e., {tex}(2 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda - 3,9 - 3\lambda ){/tex} . Also, direction ratios are proportional to -2, 6, -3. Since, PL is perpendicular to give line. {tex}\therefore - 2(2 - 2\lambda ) + 6(6\lambda - 3) - 3(9 - 3\lambda ) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow - 4 + 4\lambda + 36\lambda - 18 - 27 + 9\lambda = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 49\lambda = 49 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex} So, the coordinates of L are {tex}\left( {4 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda ,1 - 3\lambda } \right){/tex} i.e., (2, 6, -2). Also, length of PL {tex} = \sqrt {{{(2 - 2)}^2} + {{(6 - 3)}^2} + {{( - 2 + 8)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {0 + 9 + 36} = 3\sqrt 5 units{/tex}
solution_html:
We have equation of line is {tex}\frac{{4 - x}}{2} = \frac{y}{6} = \frac{{1 - z}}{3}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 4}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{y}{6} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 3}} = \lambda {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x = - 2\lambda + 4,y = 6\lambda {/tex} and {tex}z = - 3\lambda + 1{/tex}

Let the coordinates of L be {tex}(4 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda ,1 - 3\lambda ){/tex}, then, direction ratios of PL are proportional to {tex}(4 - 2\lambda - 2,6\lambda - 3,1 - 3\lambda + 8){/tex} i.e., {tex}(2 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda - 3,9 - 3\lambda ){/tex}.
Also, direction ratios are proportional to -2, 6, -3. Since, PL is perpendicular to give line.
{tex}\therefore - 2(2 - 2\lambda ) + 6(6\lambda - 3) - 3(9 - 3\lambda ) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow - 4 + 4\lambda + 36\lambda - 18 - 27 + 9\lambda = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 49\lambda = 49 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex}
So, the coordinates of L are {tex}\left( {4 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda ,1 - 3\lambda } \right){/tex} i.e., (2, 6, -2).

Also, length of PL {tex} = \sqrt {{{(2 - 2)}^2} + {{(6 - 3)}^2} + {{( - 2 + 8)}^2}} {/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {0 + 9 + 36} = 3\sqrt 5 units{/tex}
