question_html: Two vertices of the parallelogram ABCD are given as {tex}{A}(-1,2,1){/tex} and {tex}{B}(1,-2,5){/tex}. If the equation of the line passing through C and D is {tex}\frac{{x}-4}{1}=\frac{{y}+7}{-2}=\frac{{z}-8}{2}{/tex}, then find the distance between sides AB and CD. Hence, find the area of parallelogram ABCD.
question_text: Two vertices of the parallelogram ABCD are given as {tex}{A}(-1,2,1){/tex} and {tex}{B}(1,-2,5){/tex} . If the equation of the line passing through C and D is {tex}\frac{{x}-4}{1}=\frac{{y}+7}{-2}=\frac{{z}-8}{2}{/tex} , then find the distance between sides AB and CD. Hence, find the area of parallelogram ABCD.
explanation: d.r's of CD are <1, -2, 2> {tex}\therefore{/tex} d.r's of AB are <1, -2, 2> {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of AB is {tex}\frac{{x}+1}{1}=\frac{{y}-2}{-2}=\frac{{z}-1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of CD is {tex}\frac{{x}-4}{1}=\frac{{y}+7}{-2}=\frac{{z}-8}{2}{/tex} Let {tex}\overrightarrow{a_1}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k},{/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{a_2}=4 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k} {/tex} & {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}=5 \hat{i}-9 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex} \left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right) \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 5 & -9 & 7 \\ 1 & -2 & 2 \end{array}\right|=-4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-\hat{k} {/tex} Distance between AB and CD is given by {tex}d=\frac{\left|\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \times \vec{b}\right|}{|\vec{b}|}{/tex} {tex} d=\frac{\sqrt{16+9+1}}{\sqrt{1+4+4}}=\frac{\sqrt{26}}{3} {/tex} {tex} C D=\sqrt{2^2+(-4)^2+(4)^2}=6 {/tex} Area of parallelogram {tex}{ABCD}={b} \times {h}=6 \times \frac{\sqrt{26}}{3}=2 \sqrt{26}{/tex}
solution: d.r's of CD are <1, -2, 2> {tex}\therefore{/tex} d.r's of AB are <1, -2, 2> {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of AB is {tex}\frac{{x}+1}{1}=\frac{{y}-2}{-2}=\frac{{z}-1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of CD is {tex}\frac{{x}-4}{1}=\frac{{y}+7}{-2}=\frac{{z}-8}{2}{/tex} Let {tex}\overrightarrow{a_1}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k},{/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{a_2}=4 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k} {/tex} & {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}=5 \hat{i}-9 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex} \left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right) \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 5 & -9 & 7 \\ 1 & -2 & 2 \end{array}\right|=-4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-\hat{k} {/tex} Distance between AB and CD is given by {tex}d=\frac{\left|\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \times \vec{b}\right|}{|\vec{b}|}{/tex} {tex} d=\frac{\sqrt{16+9+1}}{\sqrt{1+4+4}}=\frac{\sqrt{26}}{3} {/tex} {tex} C D=\sqrt{2^2+(-4)^2+(4)^2}=6 {/tex} Area of parallelogram {tex}{ABCD}={b} \times {h}=6 \times \frac{\sqrt{26}}{3}=2 \sqrt{26}{/tex}
solution_html:
d.r's of CD are <1, -2, 2>
{tex}\therefore{/tex} d.r's of AB are <1, -2, 2>
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of AB is {tex}\frac{{x}+1}{1}=\frac{{y}-2}{-2}=\frac{{z}-1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of CD is {tex}\frac{{x}-4}{1}=\frac{{y}+7}{-2}=\frac{{z}-8}{2}{/tex}
Let {tex}\overrightarrow{a_1}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k},{/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{a_2}=4 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k} {/tex} & {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}=5 \hat{i}-9 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex} \left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right) \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 5 & -9 & 7 \\ 1 & -2 & 2 \end{array}\right|=-4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-\hat{k} {/tex}
Distance between AB and CD is given by {tex}d=\frac{\left|\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \times \vec{b}\right|}{|\vec{b}|}{/tex}
{tex} d=\frac{\sqrt{16+9+1}}{\sqrt{1+4+4}}=\frac{\sqrt{26}}{3} {/tex}
{tex} C D=\sqrt{2^2+(-4)^2+(4)^2}=6 {/tex}
Area of parallelogram {tex}{ABCD}={b} \times {h}=6 \times \frac{\sqrt{26}}{3}=2 \sqrt{26}{/tex}

question_html: Find the equation of the line passing through the point of intersection of the lines {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{0}=\frac{y}{-3}=\frac{z-7}{2}{/tex} and perpendicular to these given lines.
question_text: Find the equation of the line passing through the point of intersection of the lines {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{0}=\frac{y}{-3}=\frac{z-7}{2}{/tex} and perpendicular to these given lines.
explanation: The first line is given by {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{3}=\lambda{/tex} . The coordinates of any point on the first line are given by {tex}x=\lambda, y=2 \lambda+1{/tex} , and {tex}z=3 \lambda+2{/tex} . The second line is given by {tex}\frac{x-1}{0}=\frac{y}{-3}=\frac{z-7}{2}=\mu{/tex} . The coordinates of any point on the second line are given by {tex}x=1, y=-3 \mu{/tex} , and {tex}z=2 \mu+7{/tex} . To find the point of intersection, the coordinates must be equal. Equating the {tex}x{/tex} -coordinates gives {tex}\lambda=1{/tex} . Equating the {tex}y{/tex} -coordinates gives {tex}2 \lambda+1=-3 \mu{/tex} . Substituting {tex}\lambda=1{/tex} into the {tex}y{/tex} -coordinate equation gives {tex}2(1)+1=-3 \mu{/tex} , which simplifies to {tex}3=-3 \mu{/tex} . Solving for {tex}\mu{/tex} gives {tex}\mu=-1{/tex} . The {tex}z{/tex} -coordinates are checked for consistency. For the first line, {tex}z=3 \lambda+2=3(1)+2=5{/tex} . For the second line, {tex}z=2 \mu+7=2(-1)+7=5{/tex} . The {tex}z{/tex} -coordinates are equal, so the point of intersection is {tex}(1,3,5){/tex} . Direction Ratios of the Required Line The direction ratios of the first line are {tex}(1,2,3){/tex} . The direction ratios of the second line are {tex}(0,-3,2){/tex} . The required line is perpendicular to both given lines. The direction vector of the required line is the cross product of the direction vectors of the two given lines. The cross product is calculated as {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}{i} & {j} & {k} \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & -3 & 2\end{array}\right|{/tex} . The cross product is {tex}{i}(2 \cdot 2-3 \cdot(-3))-{j}(1 \cdot 2-3 \cdot 0){/tex} {tex}+{k}(1 \cdot(-3)-2 \cdot 0){/tex} . This simplifies to {tex}{i}(4+9)-{j}(2-0)+{k}(-3-0){/tex} . The direction vector is {tex}13 {i}-2 {j}-3 {k}{/tex} . The direction ratios of the required line are {tex}(13,-2,-3){/tex} . Equation of the Line The equation of a line passing through a point {tex}\left(x_1, y_1, z_1\right){/tex} with direction ratios {tex}(a, b, c){/tex} is given by {tex}\frac{x-x_1}{a}=\frac{y-y_1}{b}=\frac{z-z_1}{c}{/tex} . The line passes through the point of intersection {tex}(1,3,5){/tex} . The direction ratios of the line are {tex}(13,-2,-3){/tex} . The equation of the line is {tex}\frac{x-1}{13}=\frac{y-3}{-2}=\frac{z-5}{-3}{/tex} .

question_html: Find the Cartesian equation of the line passing through the origin, perpendicular to y -axis and making equal acute angles with x and z axes.
question_text: Find the Cartesian equation of the line passing through the origin, perpendicular to y -axis and making equal acute angles with x and z axes.
explanation: Let the line makes angle {tex}\alpha{/tex} with the x and z axes respectively. {tex} \therefore \cos ^2 \alpha+\cos ^2 \pi / {/tex} {tex}2+\cos ^2 \alpha=1 \Rightarrow \cos \alpha= \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{x}{1 / \sqrt{2}}=\frac{y}{0}=\frac{z}{1 / \sqrt{2}} \text { or } \frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{0}=\frac{z}{1} {/tex}
solution: Let the line makes angle {tex}\alpha{/tex} with the x and z axes respectively. {tex} \therefore \cos ^2 \alpha+\cos ^2 \pi / {/tex} {tex}2+\cos ^2 \alpha=1 \Rightarrow \cos \alpha= \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{x}{1 / \sqrt{2}}=\frac{y}{0}=\frac{z}{1 / \sqrt{2}} \text { or } \frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{0}=\frac{z}{1} {/tex}
solution_html:
Let the line makes angle {tex}\alpha{/tex} with the x and z axes respectively.
{tex} \therefore \cos ^2 \alpha+\cos ^2 \pi / {/tex}{tex}2+\cos ^2 \alpha=1 \Rightarrow \cos \alpha= \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{x}{1 / \sqrt{2}}=\frac{y}{0}=\frac{z}{1 / \sqrt{2}} \text { or } \frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{0}=\frac{z}{1} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the shortest distance between the lines {tex}{L}_{1} \& {~L}_{2}{/tex} given below :
{tex}{L}_{1}{/tex} : The line passing through {tex}(2,-1,1){/tex} and parallel to {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{{y}}{1}=\frac{{z}}{3}{/tex}
{tex}{L}_{2}: \overrightarrow{{r}}=\hat{{i}}+(2 \mu+1) \hat{{j}}-(\mu+2) \hat{{k}}{/tex}
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the lines {tex}{L}_{1} \& {~L}_{2}{/tex} given below : {tex}{L}_{1}{/tex} : The line passing through {tex}(2,-1,1){/tex} and parallel to {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{{y}}{1}=\frac{{z}}{3}{/tex} {tex}{L}_{2}: \overrightarrow{{r}}=\hat{{i}}+(2 \mu+1) \hat{{j}}-(\mu+2) \hat{{k}}{/tex}
explanation: Equation of {tex}{L}_{1}: \overrightarrow{{r}}=2 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}+\hat{{k}}+\lambda(\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}){/tex} Equation of {tex}{L}_{2}: \overrightarrow{{r}}=(\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}-2 \hat{{k}})+\mu(2 \hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}}){/tex} Taking {tex}\vec{a}_{1}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{b}_{1}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}_{2}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}_{,} \vec{b}_{2}=2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}=-7 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\text { Shortest Distance }=\frac{\left\|\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right)\right\|}{\left\|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right\|}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}{/tex}
solution: Equation of {tex}{L}_{1}: \overrightarrow{{r}}=2 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}+\hat{{k}}+\lambda(\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}){/tex} Equation of {tex}{L}_{2}: \overrightarrow{{r}}=(\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}-2 \hat{{k}})+\mu(2 \hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}}){/tex} Taking {tex}\vec{a}_{1}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{b}_{1}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}_{2}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}_{,} \vec{b}_{2}=2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}=-7 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\text { Shortest Distance }=\frac{\left\|\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right)\right\|}{\left\|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right\|}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Equation of {tex}{L}_{1}: \overrightarrow{{r}}=2 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}+\hat{{k}}+\lambda(\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}){/tex}
Equation of {tex}{L}_{2}: \overrightarrow{{r}}=(\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}-2 \hat{{k}})+\mu(2 \hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}}){/tex}
Taking {tex}\vec{a}_{1}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{b}_{1}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a}_{2}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}_{,} \vec{b}_{2}=2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}=-7 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Shortest Distance }=\frac{\left\|\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right)\right\|}{\left\|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right\|}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}{/tex}

question_html: If the lines {tex}\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{{y}-2}{2 {k}}=\frac{{z}-3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{3 {k}}=\frac{{y}-1}{1}=\frac{{z}-6}{-7}{/tex} are perpendicular to each other, find the value of k and hence write the vector equation of a line perpendicular to these two lines and passing through the point {tex}(3,-4,7){/tex}.
question_text: If the lines {tex}\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{{y}-2}{2 {k}}=\frac{{z}-3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{3 {k}}=\frac{{y}-1}{1}=\frac{{z}-6}{-7}{/tex} are perpendicular to each other, find the value of k and hence write the vector equation of a line perpendicular to these two lines and passing through the point {tex}(3,-4,7){/tex} .
explanation: {tex}{L}_{1}: \frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{2 k}=\frac{z-3}{2} \Rightarrow{/tex} direction ratio's of {tex}{L}_1=\langle-3,2 k, 2\rangle{/tex} {tex}{L}_{2}: \frac{x-1}{3 k}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{z-6}{-7} \Rightarrow{/tex} direction ratio's of {tex}{L}_{2}=\langle 3 k, 1,-7\rangle{/tex} Since {tex}{L}_{1} \perp {~L}_{2}{/tex} , {tex}-9 k+2 k-14=0 \Rightarrow k=-2{/tex} Thus, d.r.'s of {tex}{L}_{1}= \lt -3,-4,2 \gt {/tex} , d.r.'s of {tex}{L}_{2}= \lt -6,1,-7 \gt {/tex} Now the vector perpendicular to both {tex}{L}_{1} \& {~L}_{2}{/tex} is given by {tex}\vec{b}=\left\|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -3 & -4 & 2 \\ -6 & 1 & -7\end{array}\right\|=26 \hat{i}-33 \hat{j}-27 \hat{k}{/tex} Thus, Equation of the required line is {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k})+\lambda(26 \hat{i}-33 \hat{j}-27 \hat{k}){/tex}
solution: {tex}{L}_{1}: \frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{2 k}=\frac{z-3}{2} \Rightarrow{/tex} direction ratio's of {tex}{L}_1=\langle-3,2 k, 2\rangle{/tex} {tex}{L}_{2}: \frac{x-1}{3 k}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{z-6}{-7} \Rightarrow{/tex} direction ratio's of {tex}{L}_{2}=\langle 3 k, 1,-7\rangle{/tex} Since {tex}{L}_{1} \perp {~L}_{2}{/tex} , {tex}-9 k+2 k-14=0 \Rightarrow k=-2{/tex} Thus, d.r.'s of {tex}{L}_{1}= \lt -3,-4,2 \gt {/tex} , d.r.'s of {tex}{L}_{2}= \lt -6,1,-7 \gt {/tex} Now the vector perpendicular to both {tex}{L}_{1} \& {~L}_{2}{/tex} is given by {tex}\vec{b}=\left\|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -3 & -4 & 2 \\ -6 & 1 & -7\end{array}\right\|=26 \hat{i}-33 \hat{j}-27 \hat{k}{/tex} Thus, Equation of the required line is {tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k})+\lambda(26 \hat{i}-33 \hat{j}-27 \hat{k}){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{L}_{1}: \frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{2 k}=\frac{z-3}{2} \Rightarrow{/tex} direction ratio's of {tex}{L}_1=\langle-3,2 k, 2\rangle{/tex}
{tex}{L}_{2}: \frac{x-1}{3 k}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{z-6}{-7} \Rightarrow{/tex} direction ratio's of {tex}{L}_{2}=\langle 3 k, 1,-7\rangle{/tex}
Since {tex}{L}_{1} \perp {~L}_{2}{/tex},
{tex}-9 k+2 k-14=0 \Rightarrow k=-2{/tex}
Thus, d.r.'s of {tex}{L}_{1}= \lt -3,-4,2 \gt {/tex}, d.r.'s of {tex}{L}_{2}= \lt -6,1,-7 \gt {/tex}
Now the vector perpendicular to both {tex}{L}_{1} \& {~L}_{2}{/tex} is given by
{tex}\vec{b}=\left\|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -3 & -4 & 2 \\ -6 & 1 & -7\end{array}\right\|=26 \hat{i}-33 \hat{j}-27 \hat{k}{/tex}
Thus, Equation of the required line is
{tex}\vec{r}=(3 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k})+\lambda(26 \hat{i}-33 \hat{j}-27 \hat{k}){/tex}

question_html: Find the value of k so that the lines joining the points {tex}(1,-1,2){/tex} and {tex}(3,4, {k}){/tex} is perpendicular to the line joining the points {tex}(0,3,2){/tex} and {tex}(3,5,6){/tex}.
question_text: Find the value of k so that the lines joining the points {tex}(1,-1,2){/tex} and {tex}(3,4, {k}){/tex} is perpendicular to the line joining the points {tex}(0,3,2){/tex} and {tex}(3,5,6){/tex} .
explanation: The direction ratios of the two lines are: {tex}2,5, {k}-2{/tex} and {tex}3,2,4{/tex} respectively. The lines are perpendicular. {tex}\therefore 2(3)+5(2)+4(k-2){/tex} {tex}=0 \Rightarrow k=-2{/tex}
solution: The direction ratios of the two lines are: {tex}2,5, {k}-2{/tex} and {tex}3,2,4{/tex} respectively. The lines are perpendicular. {tex}\therefore 2(3)+5(2)+4(k-2){/tex} {tex}=0 \Rightarrow k=-2{/tex}
solution_html:
The direction ratios of the two lines are: {tex}2,5, {k}-2{/tex} and {tex}3,2,4{/tex} respectively.
The lines are perpendicular.
{tex}\therefore 2(3)+5(2)+4(k-2){/tex}{tex}=0 \Rightarrow k=-2{/tex}

question_html: Find the distance between the line {tex}\frac{x}{2}=\frac{2 y-6}{4}=\frac{1-z}{-1}{/tex} and another line parallel to it passing through the point {tex}(4,0,-5){/tex}.
question_text: Find the distance between the line {tex}\frac{x}{2}=\frac{2 y-6}{4}=\frac{1-z}{-1}{/tex} and another line parallel to it passing through the point {tex}(4,0,-5){/tex} .
explanation: Equation of the given line in standard form is {tex}{L}_{1}: \frac{x}{2}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z-1}{1}{/tex} Equation of the line parallel to {tex}{L}_{1}{/tex} & passing through ( {tex}4,0,-5{/tex} ) is {tex}{L}_{2}: \frac{x-4}{2}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z+5}{1}{/tex} Vector Equation of Lines are {tex}{L}_{1}: \vec{r}=(0 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}{L}_{2}: \vec{r}=(4 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})+\mu(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{a_{2}}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}=(4 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})-(0 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=(4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right) \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & -3 & -6 \\ 2 & 2 & 1\end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=9 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+14 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{4+4+1}=3{/tex} Thus, distance between the lines is {tex}\text {S.D.}=\frac{\left|\left(\overrightarrow{a_{2}}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}\right) \times \vec{b}\right|}{\\vec{b}|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{81+256+196}}{3}=\frac{\sqrt{533}}{3} \text {units}{/tex}
solution: Equation of the given line in standard form is {tex}{L}_{1}: \frac{x}{2}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z-1}{1}{/tex} Equation of the line parallel to {tex}{L}_{1}{/tex} & passing through ( {tex}4,0,-5{/tex} ) is {tex}{L}_{2}: \frac{x-4}{2}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z+5}{1}{/tex} Vector Equation of Lines are {tex}{L}_{1}: \vec{r}=(0 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}{L}_{2}: \vec{r}=(4 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})+\mu(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{a_{2}}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}=(4 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})-(0 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=(4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right) \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & -3 & -6 \\ 2 & 2 & 1\end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=9 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+14 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{4+4+1}=3{/tex} Thus, distance between the lines is {tex}\text {S.D.}=\frac{\left|\left(\overrightarrow{a_{2}}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}\right) \times \vec{b}\right|}{\\vec{b}|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{81+256+196}}{3}=\frac{\sqrt{533}}{3} \text {units}{/tex}
solution_html:
Equation of the given line in standard form is
{tex}{L}_{1}: \frac{x}{2}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z-1}{1}{/tex}
Equation of the line parallel to {tex}{L}_{1}{/tex} & passing through ({tex}4,0,-5{/tex}) is {tex}{L}_{2}: \frac{x-4}{2}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z+5}{1}{/tex}
Vector Equation of Lines are {tex}{L}_{1}: \vec{r}=(0 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}{L}_{2}: \vec{r}=(4 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})+\mu(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}
Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{a_{2}}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}=(4 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k})-(0 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=(4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right) \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & -3 & -6 \\ 2 & 2 & 1\end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=9 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+14 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{4+4+1}=3{/tex}
Thus, distance between the lines is
{tex}\text {S.D.}=\frac{\left|\left(\overrightarrow{a_{2}}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}\right) \times \vec{b}\right|}{\\vec{b}|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{81+256+196}}{3}=\frac{\sqrt{533}}{3} \text {units}{/tex}

question_html: Find the vector equation of the line passing through the point {tex}(2,3,-5){/tex} and making equal angles with the co-ordinate axes.
question_text: Find the vector equation of the line passing through the point {tex}(2,3,-5){/tex} and making equal angles with the co-ordinate axes.
explanation: {tex}\cos \alpha=\cos \beta=\cos \gamma=l \Rightarrow l^{2}+l^{2}+l^{2}=1 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 l^{2}=1, \therefore l=1 / \sqrt{3}{/tex} Direction cosines of the line are {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow{/tex} the direction ratios are 1,1,1 ∴ Vector equation of the line is: {tex}\overrightarrow{{r}}=2 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}-5 \hat{{k}}+\lambda(\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}+\hat{{k}}){/tex}
solution: {tex}\cos \alpha=\cos \beta=\cos \gamma=l \Rightarrow l^{2}+l^{2}+l^{2}=1 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 l^{2}=1, \therefore l=1 / \sqrt{3}{/tex} Direction cosines of the line are {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow{/tex} the direction ratios are 1,1,1 ∴ Vector equation of the line is: {tex}\overrightarrow{{r}}=2 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}-5 \hat{{k}}+\lambda(\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}+\hat{{k}}){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\cos \alpha=\cos \beta=\cos \gamma=l \Rightarrow l^{2}+l^{2}+l^{2}=1 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 l^{2}=1, \therefore l=1 / \sqrt{3}{/tex}
Direction cosines of the line are {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow{/tex} the direction ratios are 1,1,1
∴ Vector equation of the line is: {tex}\overrightarrow{{r}}=2 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}-5 \hat{{k}}+\lambda(\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}+\hat{{k}}){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the point of intersection of the lines
{tex} \vec{r}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+6 \hat{k}+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-\hat{k}) \text {, and } {/tex}
{tex} \vec{r}=-3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}+\mu(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) {/tex}
Also, find the vector equation of the line passing through the point of intersection of the given lines and perpendicular to both the lines.
question_text: Find the point of intersection of the lines {tex} \vec{r}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+6 \hat{k}+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-\hat{k}) \text {, and } {/tex} {tex} \vec{r}=-3 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}+\mu(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) {/tex} Also, find the vector equation of the line passing through the point of intersection of the given lines and perpendicular to both the lines.
explanation: If the given lines intersect then, {tex} \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}+6 \hat{{k}}+\lambda(3 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{k}}){/tex} {tex}=-3 \hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}+\mu(\hat{{i}}+2 \hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}}) {/tex} Solving the equations, {tex}1+3 \lambda=\mu,-1=2 \mu-3{/tex} , we get {tex}\lambda=0, \mu=1{/tex} which do not satisfy the equation, {tex}6-\lambda=3-\mu{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} The lines do not intersect, hence no point of intersection
solution: If the given lines intersect then, {tex} \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}+6 \hat{{k}}+\lambda(3 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{k}}){/tex} {tex}=-3 \hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}+\mu(\hat{{i}}+2 \hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}}) {/tex} Solving the equations, {tex}1+3 \lambda=\mu,-1=2 \mu-3{/tex} , we get {tex}\lambda=0, \mu=1{/tex} which do not satisfy the equation, {tex}6-\lambda=3-\mu{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} The lines do not intersect, hence no point of intersection
solution_html:
If the given lines intersect then,
{tex} \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}+6 \hat{{k}}+\lambda(3 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{k}}){/tex}{tex}=-3 \hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}+\mu(\hat{{i}}+2 \hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}}) {/tex}
Solving the equations, {tex}1+3 \lambda=\mu,-1=2 \mu-3{/tex}, we get {tex}\lambda=0, \mu=1{/tex}
which do not satisfy the equation, {tex}6-\lambda=3-\mu{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The lines do not intersect, hence no point of intersection

question_html: Find the shortest distance between the lines {tex}\frac{x-8}{3}=\frac{y+9}{-16}=\frac{z-10}{7}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{x}-15}{3}=\frac{{y}-29}{8}=\frac{{z}-5}{-5}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the lines {tex}\frac{x-8}{3}=\frac{y+9}{-16}=\frac{z-10}{7}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{x}-15}{3}=\frac{{y}-29}{8}=\frac{{z}-5}{-5}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} \left.\begin{array}{l} \vec{r}=(8 \hat{i}-9 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k})+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}) \\ \vec{r}=(15 \hat{i}+29 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})+\mu(3 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}) \end{array}\right\} {/tex} We have, {tex}\vec{a}_1=8 \hat{i}-9 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_1{/tex} {tex}=3 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_2{/tex} {tex}=15 \hat{i}+29 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_2{/tex} {tex}=3 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex} {r} \vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1=7 \hat{i}+38 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2={/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -16 & 7 \\ 3 & 8 & -5 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=24 \hat{i}+36 \hat{j}+72 \hat{k} {/tex} {tex}\text { Shortest Distance }={/tex} {tex}\left|\frac{\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right) \cdot\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right)}{\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right|}\right|=\frac{98}{7}=14 {/tex}
solution: {tex} \left.\begin{array}{l} \vec{r}=(8 \hat{i}-9 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k})+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}) \\ \vec{r}=(15 \hat{i}+29 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})+\mu(3 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}) \end{array}\right\} {/tex} We have, {tex}\vec{a}_1=8 \hat{i}-9 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_1{/tex} {tex}=3 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_2{/tex} {tex}=15 \hat{i}+29 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_2{/tex} {tex}=3 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex} {r} \vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1=7 \hat{i}+38 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2={/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -16 & 7 \\ 3 & 8 & -5 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}=24 \hat{i}+36 \hat{j}+72 \hat{k} {/tex} {tex}\text { Shortest Distance }={/tex} {tex}\left|\frac{\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right) \cdot\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right)}{\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right|}\right|=\frac{98}{7}=14 {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \left.\begin{array}{l} \vec{r}=(8 \hat{i}-9 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k})+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}) \\ \vec{r}=(15 \hat{i}+29 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})+\mu(3 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}) \end{array}\right\} {/tex}
We have, {tex}\vec{a}_1=8 \hat{i}-9 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_1{/tex}{tex}=3 \hat{i}-16 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}, \vec{a}_2{/tex}{tex}=15 \hat{i}+29 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_2{/tex}{tex}=3 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex} {r} \vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1=7 \hat{i}+38 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2={/tex}
{tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & -16 & 7 \\ 3 & 8 & -5 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=24 \hat{i}+36 \hat{j}+72 \hat{k} {/tex}
{tex}\text { Shortest Distance }={/tex}{tex}\left|\frac{\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right) \cdot\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right)}{\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right|}\right|=\frac{98}{7}=14 {/tex}

question_html:
Find the angle between the lines
{tex}\frac{5-x}{-7}=\frac{y+2}{-5}=\frac{z}{1} \text { and } \frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{3}{/tex}
question_text: Find the angle between the lines {tex}\frac{5-x}{-7}=\frac{y+2}{-5}=\frac{z}{1} \text { and } \frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{3}{/tex}
explanation: The direction ratios of the two lines are: 7, -5, 1 and 1, 2, 3 respectively. {tex}\therefore{/tex} The angle between the two lines is given by: {tex}\theta =\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{7(1)-5(2)+1(3)}{\sqrt{49+25+1} \sqrt{1+4+9}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \theta =\cos ^{-1} 0=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
solution: The direction ratios of the two lines are: 7, -5, 1 and 1, 2, 3 respectively. {tex}\therefore{/tex} The angle between the two lines is given by: {tex}\theta =\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{7(1)-5(2)+1(3)}{\sqrt{49+25+1} \sqrt{1+4+9}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \theta =\cos ^{-1} 0=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
The direction ratios of the two lines are: 7, -5, 1 and 1, 2, 3 respectively.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The angle between the two lines is given by: {tex}\theta =\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{7(1)-5(2)+1(3)}{\sqrt{49+25+1} \sqrt{1+4+9}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \theta =\cos ^{-1} 0=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}

question_html: Show that the lines {tex} \frac { x + 1 } { 3 } = \frac { y + 3 } { 5 } = \frac { z + 5 } { 7 }{/tex} and {tex} \frac { x - 2 } { 1 } = \frac { y - 4 } { 3 } = \frac { z - 6 } { 5 }{/tex} intersect. Also, find their point of intersection.
question_text: Show that the lines {tex} \frac { x + 1 } { 3 } = \frac { y + 3 } { 5 } = \frac { z + 5 } { 7 }{/tex} and {tex} \frac { x - 2 } { 1 } = \frac { y - 4 } { 3 } = \frac { z - 6 } { 5 }{/tex} intersect. Also, find their point of intersection.
explanation: The given lines are {tex} \frac { x + 1 } { 3 } = \frac { y + 3 } { 5 } = \frac { z + 5 } { 7 } = \lambda{/tex} (let) ...(i) and {tex} \frac { x - 2 } { 1 } = \frac { y - 4 } { 3 } = \frac { z - 6 } { 5 } = \mu ( \text { let } ){/tex} ...(ii) Then, any point on line (i) is of the form {tex} P ( 3 \lambda - 1,5 \lambda - 3,7 \lambda - 5 ){/tex} ...(iii) and any point on line (ii) is of the form {tex} Q ( \mu + 2,3 \mu + 4,5 \mu + 6 ){/tex} ...(iv) If lines (i) and (ii) intersect, then these points must coincide for some {tex} \lambda{/tex} and {tex} \mu{/tex} . Consider, {tex} 3 \lambda - 1 = \mu + 2{/tex} {tex} 5 \lambda - 3 = 3 \mu + 4{/tex} and {tex}7 \lambda - 5 = 5 \mu + 6{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 3 \mathrm { \lambda } - \mu = 3{/tex} ...(v) {tex}5 \lambda - 3 \mu = 7{/tex} ...(vi) and {tex}7 \lambda - 5 \mu = 11{/tex} ...(vii) On multiplying Eq. (v) by 3 and then subtracting Eq. (vi), we get, {tex} 9 \lambda - 3 u - 5 \lambda + 3 u = 9 - 7{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 4 \lambda = 2{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} On putting the value of {tex} \lambda{/tex} in Eq. (v), we get {tex} 3 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } - \mu = 3{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 3 } { 2 } - \mu = 3{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mu = - \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex} On putting the values of {tex} \lambda{/tex} and µ in Eq. (vii), we get {tex} 7 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } - 5 \left( - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \right) = 11{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 7 } { 2 } + \frac { 15 } { 2 } = 11 \Rightarrow \frac { 22 } { 2 } = 11{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 11 = 11, which is true. Hence, lines ( i) and (ii) intersect and their point of intersection is {tex} P \left( 3 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } - 1,5 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } - 3,7 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } - 5 \right){/tex} [put {tex}\lambda = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (iii)] i.e. {tex} \ P \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } , - \frac { 1 } { 2 } , - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \right){/tex}
solution: The given lines are {tex} \frac { x + 1 } { 3 } = \frac { y + 3 } { 5 } = \frac { z + 5 } { 7 } = \lambda{/tex} (let) ...(i) and {tex} \frac { x - 2 } { 1 } = \frac { y - 4 } { 3 } = \frac { z - 6 } { 5 } = \mu ( \text { let } ){/tex} ...(ii) Then, any point on line (i) is of the form {tex} P ( 3 \lambda - 1,5 \lambda - 3,7 \lambda - 5 ){/tex} ...(iii) and any point on line (ii) is of the form {tex} Q ( \mu + 2,3 \mu + 4,5 \mu + 6 ){/tex} ...(iv) If lines (i) and (ii) intersect, then these points must coincide for some {tex} \lambda{/tex} and {tex} \mu{/tex} . Consider, {tex} 3 \lambda - 1 = \mu + 2{/tex} {tex} 5 \lambda - 3 = 3 \mu + 4{/tex} and {tex}7 \lambda - 5 = 5 \mu + 6{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 3 \mathrm { \lambda } - \mu = 3{/tex} ...(v) {tex}5 \lambda - 3 \mu = 7{/tex} ...(vi) and {tex}7 \lambda - 5 \mu = 11{/tex} ...(vii) On multiplying Eq. (v) by 3 and then subtracting Eq. (vi), we get, {tex} 9 \lambda - 3 u - 5 \lambda + 3 u = 9 - 7{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 4 \lambda = 2{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} On putting the value of {tex} \lambda{/tex} in Eq. (v), we get {tex} 3 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } - \mu = 3{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 3 } { 2 } - \mu = 3{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mu = - \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex} On putting the values of {tex} \lambda{/tex} and µ in Eq. (vii), we get {tex} 7 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } - 5 \left( - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \right) = 11{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 7 } { 2 } + \frac { 15 } { 2 } = 11 \Rightarrow \frac { 22 } { 2 } = 11{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 11 = 11, which is true. Hence, lines ( i) and (ii) intersect and their point of intersection is {tex} P \left( 3 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } - 1,5 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } - 3,7 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } - 5 \right){/tex} [put {tex}\lambda = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (iii)] i.e. {tex} \ P \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } , - \frac { 1 } { 2 } , - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \right){/tex}
solution_html:
The given lines are
{tex} \frac { x + 1 } { 3 } = \frac { y + 3 } { 5 } = \frac { z + 5 } { 7 } = \lambda{/tex}(let) ...(i)
and {tex} \frac { x - 2 } { 1 } = \frac { y - 4 } { 3 } = \frac { z - 6 } { 5 } = \mu ( \text { let } ){/tex} ...(ii)
Then, any point on line (i) is of the form
{tex} P ( 3 \lambda - 1,5 \lambda - 3,7 \lambda - 5 ){/tex} ...(iii)
and any point on line (ii) is of the form
{tex} Q ( \mu + 2,3 \mu + 4,5 \mu + 6 ){/tex} ...(iv)
If lines (i) and (ii) intersect, then these points must coincide for some {tex} \lambda{/tex} and {tex} \mu{/tex}.
Consider, {tex} 3 \lambda - 1 = \mu + 2{/tex}
{tex} 5 \lambda - 3 = 3 \mu + 4{/tex}
and {tex}7 \lambda - 5 = 5 \mu + 6{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 3 \mathrm { \lambda } - \mu = 3{/tex} ...(v)
{tex}5 \lambda - 3 \mu = 7{/tex} ...(vi)
and {tex}7 \lambda - 5 \mu = 11{/tex} ...(vii)
On multiplying Eq. (v) by 3 and then subtracting Eq. (vi), we get,
{tex} 9 \lambda - 3 u - 5 \lambda + 3 u = 9 - 7{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 4 \lambda = 2{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
On putting the value of {tex} \lambda{/tex}  in Eq. (v), we get
{tex} 3 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } - \mu = 3{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 3 } { 2 } - \mu = 3{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mu = - \frac { 3 } { 2 }{/tex}
On putting the values of {tex} \lambda{/tex} and µ in Eq. (vii), we get
{tex} 7 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } - 5 \left( - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \right) = 11{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 7 } { 2 } + \frac { 15 } { 2 } = 11 \Rightarrow \frac { 22 } { 2 } = 11{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 11 = 11, which is true.
Hence, lines ( i) and (ii) intersect and their point of intersection is
{tex} P \left( 3 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } - 1,5 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } - 3,7 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } - 5 \right){/tex}
[put {tex}\lambda = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} in Eq. (iii)]
i.e. {tex} \ P \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } , - \frac { 1 } { 2 } , - \frac { 3 } { 2 } \right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the vector equation of the line passing through the point (2, 1, 3) and perpendicular to both the lines {tex}\frac {\text x - 1 } { 1 } = \frac {\text y - 2 } { 2 } = \frac {\text z - 3 } { 3 }{/tex}; {tex}\frac {\text x } { - 3 } = \frac {\text y } { 2 } = \frac {\text z } { 5 }{/tex}.
question_text: Find the vector equation of the line passing through the point (2, 1, 3) and perpendicular to both the lines {tex}\frac {\text x - 1 } { 1 } = \frac {\text y - 2 } { 2 } = \frac {\text z - 3 } { 3 }{/tex} ; {tex}\frac {\text x } { - 3 } = \frac {\text y } { 2 } = \frac {\text z } { 5 }{/tex} .
explanation: We have to find the vector and cartesian equations of the line passing through the point (2,1,3) and perpendicular to the lines {tex}\frac { x - 1 } { 1 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 3 }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { x } { - 3 } = \frac { y } { 2 } = \frac { z } { 5 }.{/tex} We know that any line through the point (2, 1, 3) can be written as {tex}\frac { x - 2 } { a } = \frac { y - 1 } { b } = \frac { z - 3 } { c }{/tex} ........................(i) where, a, b and c are the direction ratios of line (i). {tex}\frac { x - 1 } { 1 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 3 }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { x - 0 } { - 3 } = \frac { y - 0 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 0 } { 5 }{/tex} Direction ratios of these two lines are (1,2,3) and (-3, 2,5), respectively. We know that, if two lines are perpendicular, then {tex}a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad a + 2 b + 3 c = 0{/tex} ....................(ii) and {tex}\quad - 3 a + 2 b + 5 c = 0{/tex} ................(iii) In Equations. (ii) and (iii), by cross-multiplication, we get {tex}\frac { a } { 10 - 6 }=\frac { b } { - 9 - 5 } = \frac { c } { 2 + 6 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { a } { 4 } = \frac { b } { - 14 } = \frac { c } { 8 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { a } { 2 } = \frac { b } { - 7 } = \frac { c } { 4 } = \lambda ( \text { say } ){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad a = 2 \lambda , b = - 7 \lambda{/tex} and {tex}c = 4 \lambda{/tex} On substituting the values of a, band c in Equation(i), we get , {tex}\frac { x - 2 } { 2 \lambda } = \frac { y - 1 } { - 7 \lambda } = \frac { z - 3 } { 4 \lambda } \Rightarrow \frac { x - 2 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { - 7 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 4 }{/tex} which is the required cartesian equation of the line. The vector equation of line which passes through (2,1,3) and parallel to the vector {tex}2 \hat { i } - 7 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} is {tex}\vec { r } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } + \lambda ( 2 \hat { i } - 7 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ){/tex} which is the required vector equation of the line.
solution: We have to find the vector and cartesian equations of the line passing through the point (2,1,3) and perpendicular to the lines {tex}\frac { x - 1 } { 1 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 3 }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { x } { - 3 } = \frac { y } { 2 } = \frac { z } { 5 }.{/tex} We know that any line through the point (2, 1, 3) can be written as {tex}\frac { x - 2 } { a } = \frac { y - 1 } { b } = \frac { z - 3 } { c }{/tex} ........................(i) where, a, b and c are the direction ratios of line (i). {tex}\frac { x - 1 } { 1 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 3 }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { x - 0 } { - 3 } = \frac { y - 0 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 0 } { 5 }{/tex} Direction ratios of these two lines are (1,2,3) and (-3, 2,5), respectively. We know that, if two lines are perpendicular, then {tex}a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad a + 2 b + 3 c = 0{/tex} ....................(ii) and {tex}\quad - 3 a + 2 b + 5 c = 0{/tex} ................(iii) In Equations. (ii) and (iii), by cross-multiplication, we get {tex}\frac { a } { 10 - 6 }=\frac { b } { - 9 - 5 } = \frac { c } { 2 + 6 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { a } { 4 } = \frac { b } { - 14 } = \frac { c } { 8 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { a } { 2 } = \frac { b } { - 7 } = \frac { c } { 4 } = \lambda ( \text { say } ){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad a = 2 \lambda , b = - 7 \lambda{/tex} and {tex}c = 4 \lambda{/tex} On substituting the values of a, band c in Equation(i), we get , {tex}\frac { x - 2 } { 2 \lambda } = \frac { y - 1 } { - 7 \lambda } = \frac { z - 3 } { 4 \lambda } \Rightarrow \frac { x - 2 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { - 7 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 4 }{/tex} which is the required cartesian equation of the line. The vector equation of line which passes through (2,1,3) and parallel to the vector {tex}2 \hat { i } - 7 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} is {tex}\vec { r } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } + \lambda ( 2 \hat { i } - 7 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ){/tex} which is the required vector equation of the line.
solution_html:
We have to find the vector and cartesian equations of the line passing through the point (2,1,3) and perpendicular to the lines {tex}\frac { x - 1 } { 1 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 3 }{/tex}and {tex}\frac { x } { - 3 } = \frac { y } { 2 } = \frac { z } { 5 }.{/tex}

We know that any line through the point (2, 1, 3) can be written as
{tex}\frac { x - 2 } { a } = \frac { y - 1 } { b } = \frac { z - 3 } { c }{/tex}........................(i)
where, a, b and c are the direction ratios of line (i).
{tex}\frac { x - 1 } { 1 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 3 }{/tex}
and {tex}\frac { x - 0 } { - 3 } = \frac { y - 0 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 0 } { 5 }{/tex}
Direction ratios of these two lines are (1,2,3) and (-3, 2,5), respectively.
We know that, if two lines are perpendicular,
then
{tex}a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad a + 2 b + 3 c = 0{/tex} ....................(ii)
and {tex}\quad - 3 a + 2 b + 5 c = 0{/tex} ................(iii)
In Equations. (ii) and (iii), by cross-multiplication,
we get
{tex}\frac { a } { 10 - 6 }=\frac { b } { - 9 - 5 } = \frac { c } { 2 + 6 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { a } { 4 } = \frac { b } { - 14 } = \frac { c } { 8 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { a } { 2 } = \frac { b } { - 7 } = \frac { c } { 4 } = \lambda ( \text { say } ){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad a = 2 \lambda , b = - 7 \lambda{/tex}
and {tex}c = 4 \lambda{/tex}
On substituting the values of a, band c in Equation(i),
we get ,
{tex}\frac { x - 2 } { 2 \lambda } = \frac { y - 1 } { - 7 \lambda } = \frac { z - 3 } { 4 \lambda } \Rightarrow \frac { x - 2 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { - 7 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 4 }{/tex}
which is the required cartesian equation of the line.
The vector equation of line which passes through (2,1,3) and parallel to the vector {tex}2 \hat { i } - 7 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k }{/tex} is 
{tex}\vec { r } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } + \lambda ( 2 \hat { i } - 7 \hat { j } + 4 \hat { k } ){/tex}
which is the required vector equation of the line.

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the angle between the lines
2x = 3y = -z and 6x = -y = -4z.
question_text: Find the angle between the lines 2x = 3y = -z and 6x = -y = -4z.
explanation: Given that, 2x = 3y = -z 6x = -y = -4z The given lines can be re-written as {tex}\frac{x}{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{y}{\frac{1}{3}}=\frac{z}{-1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x}{\frac{1}{6}}=\frac{y}{-1}=\frac{z}{-\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x}{3}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{-6}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{-12}=\frac{z}{-3}{/tex} These lines are parallel to vectors {tex}\vec{b_{1}}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b_{2}}=2 \hat{i}-12 \mathrm{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between these lines. Then we know that {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{\vec{b_{1}} \cdot \vec{b_{2}}}{|\vec{b_{1}}||\vec{b_{2}}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}-12 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{3^{2}+2^{2}+(-6)^{2}} \sqrt{2^{2}+(-12)^{2}+(-3)^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6-24+18}{\sqrt{9+4+36} \sqrt{4+144+9}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
solution: Given that, 2x = 3y = -z 6x = -y = -4z The given lines can be re-written as {tex}\frac{x}{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{y}{\frac{1}{3}}=\frac{z}{-1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x}{\frac{1}{6}}=\frac{y}{-1}=\frac{z}{-\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x}{3}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{-6}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{-12}=\frac{z}{-3}{/tex} These lines are parallel to vectors {tex}\vec{b_{1}}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b_{2}}=2 \hat{i}-12 \mathrm{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between these lines. Then we know that {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{\vec{b_{1}} \cdot \vec{b_{2}}}{|\vec{b_{1}}||\vec{b_{2}}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}-12 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{3^{2}+2^{2}+(-6)^{2}} \sqrt{2^{2}+(-12)^{2}+(-3)^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6-24+18}{\sqrt{9+4+36} \sqrt{4+144+9}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that,
2x = 3y = -z
6x = -y = -4z
The given lines can be re-written as
{tex}\frac{x}{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{y}{\frac{1}{3}}=\frac{z}{-1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x}{\frac{1}{6}}=\frac{y}{-1}=\frac{z}{-\frac{1}{4}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x}{3}=\frac{y}{2}=\frac{z}{-6}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x}{2}=\frac{y}{-12}=\frac{z}{-3}{/tex} 
These lines are parallel to vectors
{tex}\vec{b_{1}}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b_{2}}=2 \hat{i}-12 \mathrm{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} 
Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between these lines.
 Then we know that
{tex}\cos \theta=\frac{\vec{b_{1}} \cdot \vec{b_{2}}}{|\vec{b_{1}}||\vec{b_{2}}|}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}) \cdot(2 \hat{i}-12 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{3^{2}+2^{2}+(-6)^{2}} \sqrt{2^{2}+(-12)^{2}+(-3)^{2}}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{6-24+18}{\sqrt{9+4+36} \sqrt{4+144+9}}{/tex} 
= 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Show that the following lines do not intersect each other:
{tex}\frac{\text x-1}{3}=\frac{\text y+1}{2}=\frac{\text z-1}{5}{/tex}; {tex}\frac{\text x+2}{4}=\frac{\text y-1}{3}=\frac{\text z+1}{-2}{/tex}.
question_text: Show that the following lines do not intersect each other: {tex}\frac{\text x-1}{3}=\frac{\text y+1}{2}=\frac{\text z-1}{5}{/tex} ; {tex}\frac{\text x+2}{4}=\frac{\text y-1}{3}=\frac{\text z+1}{-2}{/tex} .
explanation: The coordinates of any point on the first line are given by {tex}\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z-1}{5}=\lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 {tex}\lambda{/tex} + 1 y = 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} - 1 z = 5 {tex}\lambda{/tex} + 1 The coordinates of a general point on the first line are {tex}(3 \lambda+1,2 \lambda-1,5 \lambda+1){/tex} The coordinates of any point on the second line are given by {tex}\frac{x+2}{4}=\frac{y-1}{3}=\frac{z+1}{-2}=\mu{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 4 {tex}\mu{/tex} - 2 y = 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} + 1 z = -2 {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1 The coordinates of a general point on the second line are {tex}(4 \mu-2,3 \mu+1,-2 \mu-1){/tex} If the lines intersect, then they have a common point. So, for some values of {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}\mu{/tex} . we must have {tex}3 \lambda+1=4 \mu-2,2 \lambda-1=3 \mu+1,5 \lambda+1=-2 \mu-1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 \lambda-4 \mu=-3{/tex} .....(i) {tex}2 \lambda-3 \mu=2{/tex} .....(ii) {tex}5 \lambda+2 \mu=-2{/tex} .....(iii) Solving (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -17 {tex}\mu{/tex} = -12 Substituting {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -17 and {tex}\mu{/tex} = -12 in (iii), we get LHS = {tex}3 \lambda+2 \mu{/tex} = 3(-17) + 2(-12) = -75 {tex}\ne{/tex} -2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} LHS {tex}\ne{/tex} RHS Hence, the given lines do not intersect.
solution: The coordinates of any point on the first line are given by {tex}\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z-1}{5}=\lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 {tex}\lambda{/tex} + 1 y = 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} - 1 z = 5 {tex}\lambda{/tex} + 1 The coordinates of a general point on the first line are {tex}(3 \lambda+1,2 \lambda-1,5 \lambda+1){/tex} The coordinates of any point on the second line are given by {tex}\frac{x+2}{4}=\frac{y-1}{3}=\frac{z+1}{-2}=\mu{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 4 {tex}\mu{/tex} - 2 y = 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} + 1 z = -2 {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1 The coordinates of a general point on the second line are {tex}(4 \mu-2,3 \mu+1,-2 \mu-1){/tex} If the lines intersect, then they have a common point. So, for some values of {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}\mu{/tex} . we must have {tex}3 \lambda+1=4 \mu-2,2 \lambda-1=3 \mu+1,5 \lambda+1=-2 \mu-1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 \lambda-4 \mu=-3{/tex} .....(i) {tex}2 \lambda-3 \mu=2{/tex} .....(ii) {tex}5 \lambda+2 \mu=-2{/tex} .....(iii) Solving (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -17 {tex}\mu{/tex} = -12 Substituting {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -17 and {tex}\mu{/tex} = -12 in (iii), we get LHS = {tex}3 \lambda+2 \mu{/tex} = 3(-17) + 2(-12) = -75 {tex}\ne{/tex} -2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} LHS {tex}\ne{/tex} RHS Hence, the given lines do not intersect.
solution_html:
The coordinates of any point on the first line are given by
{tex}\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y+1}{2}=\frac{z-1}{5}=\lambda{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3{tex}\lambda{/tex}  + 1
y = 2{tex}\lambda{/tex} - 1
z = 5{tex}\lambda{/tex} + 1
The coordinates of a general point on the first line are {tex}(3 \lambda+1,2 \lambda-1,5 \lambda+1){/tex} 
The coordinates of any point on the second line are given by 
{tex}\frac{x+2}{4}=\frac{y-1}{3}=\frac{z+1}{-2}=\mu{/tex} 
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 4{tex}\mu{/tex} - 2
y = 3{tex}\mu{/tex} + 1
z = -2{tex}\mu{/tex} - 1
The coordinates of a general point on the second line are {tex}(4 \mu-2,3 \mu+1,-2 \mu-1){/tex} 
If the lines intersect, then they have a common point. So, for some values of {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}\mu{/tex}.
we must have
{tex}3 \lambda+1=4 \mu-2,2 \lambda-1=3 \mu+1,5 \lambda+1=-2 \mu-1{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 3 \lambda-4 \mu=-3{/tex} .....(i)
{tex}2 \lambda-3 \mu=2{/tex} .....(ii)
{tex}5 \lambda+2 \mu=-2{/tex} .....(iii)
Solving (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = -17
{tex}\mu{/tex} = -12
Substituting {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -17 and {tex}\mu{/tex} = -12 in (iii), we get
LHS = {tex}3 \lambda+2 \mu{/tex} 
= 3(-17) + 2(-12)
= -75 {tex}\ne{/tex} -2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} LHS {tex}\ne{/tex} RHS
Hence, the given lines do not intersect.

question_text: If the angle between the lines {tex}\frac{\text x-5}{\alpha}=\frac{\text y+2}{-5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text z+\frac{24}{5}}{\beta}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{\text x}{1}=\frac{\text y}{0}=\frac{\text z}{1}{/tex} is {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex} , find the relation between {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} .
explanation: cos {tex} \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{|\alpha \cdot 1+0+\beta|}{\sqrt{\alpha^2+\beta^2+25} \sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{|\alpha+\beta|}{\sqrt{\alpha^2+\beta^2+25} \sqrt{2}}{/tex} Squaring both sides, we get {tex}\alpha^2+\beta^2+2 \alpha \beta{/tex} = {tex}\alpha^2+\beta^2+25{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{25}{2}{/tex}
solution: cos {tex} \frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{|\alpha \cdot 1+0+\beta|}{\sqrt{\alpha^2+\beta^2+25} \sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{|\alpha+\beta|}{\sqrt{\alpha^2+\beta^2+25} \sqrt{2}}{/tex} Squaring both sides, we get {tex}\alpha^2+\beta^2+2 \alpha \beta{/tex} = {tex}\alpha^2+\beta^2+25{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\alpha \beta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{25}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:

Squaring both sides, we get
{tex}\alpha^2+\beta^2+2 \alpha \beta{/tex} = {tex}\alpha^2+\beta^2+25{/tex}

explanation: The given line is {tex}\frac{\text x-5}{\frac{1}{5}}=\frac{\text y-2}{-\frac{1}{7}}=\frac{\text z}{\frac{1}{35}}{/tex} , or {tex}\frac{\text x-5}{7}=\frac{\text y-2}{-5}=\frac{\text z}{1}{/tex} So, the required vector equation of the line passing through(1, 2, -1) is {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} = {tex}(\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-\hat{k}){/tex} + {tex}\lambda(7 \hat{\imath}-5 \hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}){/tex} Cartesian equation of the line is {tex}\frac{\text x-1}{7}=\frac{\text y-2}{-5}=\frac{\text z+1}{1}{/tex}

question_html: If a line makes angles {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} and {tex}\gamma{/tex} with x-axis, y-axis and z-axis respectively, then prove that sin2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + sin2 {tex}\beta{/tex} + sin2 {tex}\gamma{/tex} = 2.
question_text: If a line makes angles {tex}\alpha{/tex} , {tex}\beta{/tex} and {tex}\gamma{/tex} with x-axis, y-axis and z-axis respectively, then prove that sin 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + sin 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} + sin 2 {tex}\gamma{/tex} = 2.
explanation: d.c. are cos {tex}\alpha{/tex} , cos {tex}\beta{/tex} , cos {tex}\gamma{/tex} cos 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + cos 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} + cos 2 {tex}\gamma{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 - sin 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) + (1 - sin 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} ) + (1 - sin 2 {tex}\gamma{/tex} ) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + sin 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} + sin 2 {tex}\gamma{/tex} = 2
solution: d.c. are cos {tex}\alpha{/tex} , cos {tex}\beta{/tex} , cos {tex}\gamma{/tex} cos 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + cos 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} + cos 2 {tex}\gamma{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 - sin 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} ) + (1 - sin 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} ) + (1 - sin 2 {tex}\gamma{/tex} ) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin 2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + sin 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} + sin 2 {tex}\gamma{/tex} = 2
solution_html:
d.c. are cos {tex}\alpha{/tex}, cos {tex}\beta{/tex}, cos {tex}\gamma{/tex}
cos2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + cos2 {tex}\beta{/tex} + cos2 {tex}\gamma{/tex} = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 - sin2 {tex}\alpha{/tex}) + (1 - sin2 {tex}\beta{/tex}) + (1 - sin2 {tex}\gamma{/tex}) = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin2 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + sin2 {tex}\beta{/tex} + sin2 {tex}\gamma{/tex} = 2

question_html: Find the shortest distance between the pair of lines {tex}\frac{\text x-1}{2}=\frac{\text y+1}{3}{/tex} = z and {tex}\frac{\text x+1}{5}=\frac{\text y-2}{1}{/tex}; z = 2.
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the pair of lines {tex}\frac{\text x-1}{2}=\frac{\text y+1}{3}{/tex} = z and {tex}\frac{\text x+1}{5}=\frac{\text y-2}{1}{/tex} ; z = 2.
explanation: Given lines are {tex}\frac{\text x-1}{2}=\frac{\text y+1}{3}=\frac{\text z-0}{1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{\text x+1}{5}=\frac{\text y-2}{1}=\frac{\text z-2}{0}{/tex} In vector form, lines are {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}){/tex} + {tex}\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} = {tex}\vec{a}_1+\lambda \vec{b}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} + {tex}\mu(5 \hat{i}+\hat{j}){/tex} = {tex}\vec{a}_2+\lambda \vec{b}_2{/tex} now, {tex}\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1{/tex} = {tex}-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 5 & 1 & 0 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}-\hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-13 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right|=\sqrt{195}{/tex} S.D. = {tex}\left|\frac{\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right)}{\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right|}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\frac{2+15-26}{\sqrt{195}}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{9}{\sqrt{195}}{/tex}
solution: Given lines are {tex}\frac{\text x-1}{2}=\frac{\text y+1}{3}=\frac{\text z-0}{1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{\text x+1}{5}=\frac{\text y-2}{1}=\frac{\text z-2}{0}{/tex} In vector form, lines are {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}){/tex} + {tex}\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} = {tex}\vec{a}_1+\lambda \vec{b}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} + {tex}\mu(5 \hat{i}+\hat{j}){/tex} = {tex}\vec{a}_2+\lambda \vec{b}_2{/tex} now, {tex}\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1{/tex} = {tex}-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 5 & 1 & 0 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}-\hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-13 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right|=\sqrt{195}{/tex} S.D. = {tex}\left|\frac{\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right)}{\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right|}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\frac{2+15-26}{\sqrt{195}}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{9}{\sqrt{195}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given lines are {tex}\frac{\text x-1}{2}=\frac{\text y+1}{3}=\frac{\text z-0}{1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{\text x+1}{5}=\frac{\text y-2}{1}=\frac{\text z-2}{0}{/tex}
In vector form, lines are
{tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}){/tex} + {tex}\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} = {tex}\vec{a}_1+\lambda \vec{b}_1{/tex} and
{tex}\vec{r}=(-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} + {tex}\mu(5 \hat{i}+\hat{j}){/tex} = {tex}\vec{a}_2+\lambda \vec{b}_2{/tex}
now, {tex}\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1{/tex} = {tex}-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 5 & 1 & 0 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}-\hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-13 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right|=\sqrt{195}{/tex}
S.D. = {tex}\left|\frac{\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right)}{\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right|}\right|{/tex}
= {tex}\left|\frac{2+15-26}{\sqrt{195}}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{9}{\sqrt{195}}{/tex}

question_html: The equations of a line are 5x - 3 = 15y + 7 = 3 - 10z. Write the direction cosines of the line and find the coordinates of a point through which it passes.
question_text: The equations of a line are 5x - 3 = 15y + 7 = 3 - 10z. Write the direction cosines of the line and find the coordinates of a point through which it passes.
explanation: The equation of the given line is {tex}\frac{\text x-\frac 3 5}{\frac 1 5}=\frac{\text y+\frac {7}{15}}{\frac {1}{15}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text z-\frac {3}{10}}{\frac {-1}{10}}{/tex} Its direction ratios are. {tex}\left\langle\frac{1}{5}, \frac{1}{15},-\frac{1}{10}\right\rangle{/tex} or 〈6, 2, -3〉 Direction cosines are {tex}\left\langle \pm \frac{6}{7}, \pm \frac{2}{7}, \mp \frac{3}{7}\right\rangle{/tex} The point through which it passes is {tex}\left(\frac{3}{5}, \frac{-7}{15}, \frac{3}{10}\right){/tex}
solution: The equation of the given line is {tex}\frac{\text x-\frac 3 5}{\frac 1 5}=\frac{\text y+\frac {7}{15}}{\frac {1}{15}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text z-\frac {3}{10}}{\frac {-1}{10}}{/tex} Its direction ratios are. {tex}\left\langle\frac{1}{5}, \frac{1}{15},-\frac{1}{10}\right\rangle{/tex} or 〈6, 2, -3〉 Direction cosines are {tex}\left\langle \pm \frac{6}{7}, \pm \frac{2}{7}, \mp \frac{3}{7}\right\rangle{/tex} The point through which it passes is {tex}\left(\frac{3}{5}, \frac{-7}{15}, \frac{3}{10}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
The equation of the given line is
{tex}\frac{\text x-\frac 3 5}{\frac 1 5}=\frac{\text y+\frac {7}{15}}{\frac {1}{15}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text z-\frac {3}{10}}{\frac {-1}{10}}{/tex}
Its direction ratios are.
{tex}\left\langle\frac{1}{5}, \frac{1}{15},-\frac{1}{10}\right\rangle{/tex} or 〈6, 2, -3〉
Direction cosines are {tex}\left\langle \pm \frac{6}{7}, \pm \frac{2}{7}, \mp \frac{3}{7}\right\rangle{/tex}
The point through which it passes is {tex}\left(\frac{3}{5}, \frac{-7}{15}, \frac{3}{10}\right){/tex}

question_html: Find the points on the line {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { 3 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 2 }{/tex} at a distance of 5 units from the point  P(1,3,3).
question_text: Find the points on the line {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { 3 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 2 }{/tex} at a distance of 5 units from the point P(1,3,3).
explanation: Given equation of line is: {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { 3 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 2 } = \lambda ( \text { say } ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { x + 2 } { 3 } = \lambda , \frac { y + 1 } { 2 } = \lambda , \frac { z - 3 } { 2 } = \lambda{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 3 \lambda - 2 ,y = 2 \lambda - 1 , z = 2 \lambda + 3{/tex} So, we have a point on the line is {tex} Q ( 3 \lambda - 2,2 \lambda - 1,2 \lambda + 3 ){/tex} ....(i) Now, given that distance between two points P(1, 3, 3) and {tex}Q ( 3 \lambda - 2,2 \lambda - 1,2 \lambda + 3 ){/tex} is 5 Units, i.e. PQ=5 {tex} \Rightarrow \sqrt {\left[ {{{(3\lambda - 2 - 1)}^2} + {{(2\lambda - 1 - 3)}^2} + {{(2\lambda + 3 - 3)}^2}} \right]} = 5{/tex} {tex}\left[ {{\text{distance }} = \sqrt {{{\left( {{x_2} - {x_1}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {{y_2} - {y_1}} \right)}^2} + {{({z_2} - {z_1})}^2}} } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \sqrt { ( 3 \lambda - 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 \lambda - 4 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 \lambda ) ^ { 2 } } = 5{/tex} Therefore,on squaring both sides, we get, {tex} ( 3 \lambda - 3 )^ 2 + ( 2 \lambda - 4 )^ 2 + ( 2 \lambda )^ 2 = 25{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 9 \lambda^ { 2 } + 9 - 18 \lambda + 4 \lambda ^ { 2 } + 16 - 16 \lambda + 4 \lambda ^{ 2 } = 25{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 17 \lambda ^ { 2 } - 34 \lambda = 0 \Rightarrow 17 \lambda ( \lambda - 2 ) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \text { Either } 17 \lambda = 0 \text { or } \lambda - 2 = 0{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \lambda = 0 \text { or } 2{/tex} On putting {tex} \lambda{/tex} = 0 and {tex} \lambda{/tex} = 2 in Eq. (i). we get the required points as (-2,-1,3) or (4,3,7).
solution: Given equation of line is: {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { 3 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 2 } = \lambda ( \text { say } ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { x + 2 } { 3 } = \lambda , \frac { y + 1 } { 2 } = \lambda , \frac { z - 3 } { 2 } = \lambda{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 3 \lambda - 2 ,y = 2 \lambda - 1 , z = 2 \lambda + 3{/tex} So, we have a point on the line is {tex} Q ( 3 \lambda - 2,2 \lambda - 1,2 \lambda + 3 ){/tex} ....(i) Now, given that distance between two points P(1, 3, 3) and {tex}Q ( 3 \lambda - 2,2 \lambda - 1,2 \lambda + 3 ){/tex} is 5 Units, i.e. PQ=5 {tex} \Rightarrow \sqrt {\left[ {{{(3\lambda - 2 - 1)}^2} + {{(2\lambda - 1 - 3)}^2} + {{(2\lambda + 3 - 3)}^2}} \right]} = 5{/tex} {tex}\left[ {{\text{distance }} = \sqrt {{{\left( {{x_2} - {x_1}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {{y_2} - {y_1}} \right)}^2} + {{({z_2} - {z_1})}^2}} } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \sqrt { ( 3 \lambda - 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 \lambda - 4 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 \lambda ) ^ { 2 } } = 5{/tex} Therefore,on squaring both sides, we get, {tex} ( 3 \lambda - 3 )^ 2 + ( 2 \lambda - 4 )^ 2 + ( 2 \lambda )^ 2 = 25{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 9 \lambda^ { 2 } + 9 - 18 \lambda + 4 \lambda ^ { 2 } + 16 - 16 \lambda + 4 \lambda ^{ 2 } = 25{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 17 \lambda ^ { 2 } - 34 \lambda = 0 \Rightarrow 17 \lambda ( \lambda - 2 ) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \text { Either } 17 \lambda = 0 \text { or } \lambda - 2 = 0{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \lambda = 0 \text { or } 2{/tex} On putting {tex} \lambda{/tex} = 0 and {tex} \lambda{/tex} = 2 in Eq. (i). we get the required points as (-2,-1,3) or (4,3,7).
solution_html:
Given equation of line is:
{tex} \frac { x + 2 } { 3 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 2 } = \lambda ( \text { say } ){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { x + 2 } { 3 } = \lambda , \frac { y + 1 } { 2 } = \lambda , \frac { z - 3 } { 2 } = \lambda{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 3 \lambda - 2 ,y = 2 \lambda - 1 , z = 2 \lambda + 3{/tex}
So, we have a point on the line is
{tex} Q ( 3 \lambda - 2,2 \lambda - 1,2 \lambda + 3 ){/tex}....(i)
Now, given that distance between two points
P(1, 3, 3) and{tex}Q ( 3 \lambda - 2,2 \lambda - 1,2 \lambda + 3 ){/tex}is
5 Units, i.e. PQ=5
{tex} \Rightarrow \sqrt {\left[ {{{(3\lambda - 2 - 1)}^2} + {{(2\lambda - 1 - 3)}^2} + {{(2\lambda + 3 - 3)}^2}} \right]} = 5{/tex}
{tex}\left[ {{\text{distance }} = \sqrt {{{\left( {{x_2} - {x_1}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {{y_2} - {y_1}} \right)}^2} + {{({z_2} - {z_1})}^2}} } \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \sqrt { ( 3 \lambda - 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 \lambda - 4 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 \lambda ) ^ { 2 } } = 5{/tex}
Therefore,on squaring both sides, we get,
{tex} ( 3 \lambda - 3 )^ 2 + ( 2 \lambda - 4 )^ 2 + ( 2 \lambda )^ 2 = 25{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 9 \lambda^ { 2 } + 9 - 18 \lambda + 4 \lambda ^ { 2 } + 16 - 16 \lambda + 4 \lambda ^{ 2 } = 25{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 17 \lambda ^ { 2 } - 34 \lambda = 0 \Rightarrow 17 \lambda ( \lambda - 2 ) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \text { Either } 17 \lambda = 0 \text { or } \lambda - 2 = 0{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad \lambda = 0 \text { or } 2{/tex}
On putting {tex} \lambda{/tex} = 0 and {tex} \lambda{/tex} = 2 in Eq. (i). we get the required points as (-2,-1,3) or (4,3,7).

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the vector equation of a line passing through a point with position vector {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and parallel to the line joining the points {tex}-\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the vector equation of a line passing through a point with position vector {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and parallel to the line joining the points {tex}-\hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} .
explanation: Let A = {tex}2\widehat i-\widehat j+\widehat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow B=-\widehat i+4\widehat j+\widehat k,\overrightarrow C=\widehat i+2\widehat j+2\widehat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{BC}=\overrightarrow C-\overrightarrow B{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{BC}=\widehat i+2\widehat j+2\widehat k+\widehat i-4\widehat j-\widehat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{BC}=2\widehat i-2\widehat j+\widehat k{/tex} We know that the equation of a line passing through a point vector a and parallel to b is r = a + {tex}\lambda{/tex} b. Here, vector a = 2i - j + k So, the equation of the required line {tex}\overrightarrow r=2\widehat i-\widehat j+\widehat k+\lambda\left(2\widehat i-2\widehat j+\widehat k\right){/tex}
solution: Let A = {tex}2\widehat i-\widehat j+\widehat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow B=-\widehat i+4\widehat j+\widehat k,\overrightarrow C=\widehat i+2\widehat j+2\widehat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{BC}=\overrightarrow C-\overrightarrow B{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{BC}=\widehat i+2\widehat j+2\widehat k+\widehat i-4\widehat j-\widehat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{BC}=2\widehat i-2\widehat j+\widehat k{/tex} We know that the equation of a line passing through a point vector a and parallel to b is r = a + {tex}\lambda{/tex} b. Here, vector a = 2i - j + k So, the equation of the required line {tex}\overrightarrow r=2\widehat i-\widehat j+\widehat k+\lambda\left(2\widehat i-2\widehat j+\widehat k\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let A = {tex}2\widehat i-\widehat j+\widehat k{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow B=-\widehat i+4\widehat j+\widehat k,\overrightarrow C=\widehat i+2\widehat j+2\widehat k{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{BC}=\overrightarrow C-\overrightarrow B{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{BC}=\widehat i+2\widehat j+2\widehat k+\widehat i-4\widehat j-\widehat k{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{BC}=2\widehat i-2\widehat j+\widehat k{/tex}
We know that the equation of a line passing through a point vector a and parallel to b is
r = a + {tex}\lambda{/tex}b.
Here, vector a = 2i - j + k
So, the equation of the required line
{tex}\overrightarrow r=2\widehat i-\widehat j+\widehat k+\lambda\left(2\widehat i-2\widehat j+\widehat k\right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The Cartesian equation of a line AB is: {tex}\frac{2 x-1}{12}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-3}{3}{/tex} Find the direction cosines of a line parallel to line AB.
question_text: The Cartesian equation of a line AB is: {tex}\frac{2 x-1}{12}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-3}{3}{/tex} Find the direction cosines of a line parallel to line AB.
explanation: We have, {tex}\frac{2 x-1}{12}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-3}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{x-{\frac12}}{\frac{12}2}=\frac{y+2}2=\frac{z-3}3{/tex} {tex}\frac{x-{\frac12}}6=\frac{y+2}2=\frac{z-3}3{/tex} The direction ratios of the line parallel to AB are proportional to {tex}\to{/tex} 6, 2, 3 The direction cosines of the line parallel to AB are proportional to {tex}\to{/tex} {tex}\frac6{\sqrt{36+4+9}}=\frac2{\sqrt{36+4+9}}=\frac3{\sqrt{36+4+9}}{/tex} {tex}\frac67=\frac27=\frac37{/tex}
solution: We have, {tex}\frac{2 x-1}{12}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-3}{3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{x-{\frac12}}{\frac{12}2}=\frac{y+2}2=\frac{z-3}3{/tex} {tex}\frac{x-{\frac12}}6=\frac{y+2}2=\frac{z-3}3{/tex} The direction ratios of the line parallel to AB are proportional to {tex}\to{/tex} 6, 2, 3 The direction cosines of the line parallel to AB are proportional to {tex}\to{/tex} {tex}\frac6{\sqrt{36+4+9}}=\frac2{\sqrt{36+4+9}}=\frac3{\sqrt{36+4+9}}{/tex} {tex}\frac67=\frac27=\frac37{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\frac{2 x-1}{12}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-3}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{x-{\frac12}}{\frac{12}2}=\frac{y+2}2=\frac{z-3}3{/tex}
{tex}\frac{x-{\frac12}}6=\frac{y+2}2=\frac{z-3}3{/tex}
The direction ratios of the line parallel to AB are proportional to {tex}\to{/tex} 6, 2, 3
The direction cosines of the line parallel to AB are proportional to {tex}\to{/tex} {tex}\frac6{\sqrt{36+4+9}}=\frac2{\sqrt{36+4+9}}=\frac3{\sqrt{36+4+9}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac67=\frac27=\frac37{/tex}

question_html:
Find the distance between the following parallel lines:
{tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\lambda(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\mu(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}
question_text: Find the distance between the following parallel lines: {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\lambda(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\mu(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}
explanation: {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\lambda(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\mu(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{a_1}=(2\widehat i+\widehat j-\widehat k){/tex} , {tex}\vec{a_2}=(\widehat i-2\widehat j+\widehat k){/tex} {tex}\vec b=\widehat i+\widehat j-\widehat k{/tex} Distance between two parallel lines {tex}d=\left|\frac{\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right)}{\left|\overrightarrow{b_1}\right|}\right|{/tex} ...(i) Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}=-\widehat i-3\widehat j+2\widehat k{/tex} , {tex}\left|\overrightarrow b\right|=\sqrt{1+1+1}=\sqrt3{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow b\times\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right)=\left(\widehat i+\widehat j-\widehat k\right)\left(-\widehat i-3\widehat j+2\widehat k\right){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow b\times\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right)=\begin{vmatrix}i&j&k\\1&1&-1\\-1&-3&2\end{vmatrix}=-\widehat i-\widehat j-2\widehat k{/tex} putting in eqn (i) {tex}d=\left|\frac{-\widehat i-\widehat j-2\widehat k}{\sqrt3}\right|{/tex} {tex}d=\frac{\widehat i+\widehat j+2\widehat k}{\sqrt3}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\lambda(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\mu(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{a_1}=(2\widehat i+\widehat j-\widehat k){/tex} , {tex}\vec{a_2}=(\widehat i-2\widehat j+\widehat k){/tex} {tex}\vec b=\widehat i+\widehat j-\widehat k{/tex} Distance between two parallel lines {tex}d=\left|\frac{\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right)}{\left|\overrightarrow{b_1}\right|}\right|{/tex} ...(i) Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}=-\widehat i-3\widehat j+2\widehat k{/tex} , {tex}\left|\overrightarrow b\right|=\sqrt{1+1+1}=\sqrt3{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow b\times\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right)=\left(\widehat i+\widehat j-\widehat k\right)\left(-\widehat i-3\widehat j+2\widehat k\right){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow b\times\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right)=\begin{vmatrix}i&j&k\\1&1&-1\\-1&-3&2\end{vmatrix}=-\widehat i-\widehat j-2\widehat k{/tex} putting in eqn (i) {tex}d=\left|\frac{-\widehat i-\widehat j-2\widehat k}{\sqrt3}\right|{/tex} {tex}d=\frac{\widehat i+\widehat j+2\widehat k}{\sqrt3}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\lambda(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}
and {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\mu(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{a_1}=(2\widehat i+\widehat j-\widehat k){/tex} , {tex}\vec{a_2}=(\widehat i-2\widehat j+\widehat k){/tex}
{tex}\vec b=\widehat i+\widehat j-\widehat k{/tex}
Distance between two parallel lines
{tex}d=\left|\frac{\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right)}{\left|\overrightarrow{b_1}\right|}\right|{/tex} ...(i)
Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}=-\widehat i-3\widehat j+2\widehat k{/tex}, {tex}\left|\overrightarrow b\right|=\sqrt{1+1+1}=\sqrt3{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow b\times\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right)=\left(\widehat i+\widehat j-\widehat k\right)\left(-\widehat i-3\widehat j+2\widehat k\right){/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow b\times\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right)=\begin{vmatrix}i&j&k\\1&1&-1\\-1&-3&2\end{vmatrix}=-\widehat i-\widehat j-2\widehat k{/tex} putting in eqn (i)
{tex}d=\left|\frac{-\widehat i-\widehat j-2\widehat k}{\sqrt3}\right|{/tex}
{tex}d=\frac{\widehat i+\widehat j+2\widehat k}{\sqrt3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the distance between the lines x = {tex}\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{3}{/tex} and x + 1 = {tex}\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-1}{3}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the distance between the lines x = {tex}\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{3}{/tex} and x + 1 = {tex}\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-1}{3}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{y-1}2=\frac{z-2}3{/tex} and {tex}x+1=\frac{y+2}2=\frac{z-1}3{/tex} ​​​​​​ {tex}\frac{x-0}1=\frac{y-1}2=\frac{z-2}3{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x+1}1=\frac{y+2}2=\frac{z-1}3 {/tex} As their direction cosines are same So lines are parallel Now, distance between points (0, 1, 2) and (-1, -2, 1) = {tex}\sqrt{\left(-1-0\right)^2+\left(-2-1\right)^2+\left(1-2\right)^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1+9+1}=\sqrt{11}{/tex} unit​​​​​​​
solution: {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{y-1}2=\frac{z-2}3{/tex} and {tex}x+1=\frac{y+2}2=\frac{z-1}3{/tex} ​​​​​​ {tex}\frac{x-0}1=\frac{y-1}2=\frac{z-2}3{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x+1}1=\frac{y+2}2=\frac{z-1}3 {/tex} As their direction cosines are same So lines are parallel Now, distance between points (0, 1, 2) and (-1, -2, 1) = {tex}\sqrt{\left(-1-0\right)^2+\left(-2-1\right)^2+\left(1-2\right)^2}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1+9+1}=\sqrt{11}{/tex} unit​​​​​​​
solution_html:
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{y-1}2=\frac{z-2}3{/tex} and {tex}x+1=\frac{y+2}2=\frac{z-1}3{/tex}​​​​​​
{tex}\frac{x-0}1=\frac{y-1}2=\frac{z-2}3{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x+1}1=\frac{y+2}2=\frac{z-1}3 {/tex}
As their direction cosines are same
So lines are parallel
Now, distance between points (0, 1, 2) and (-1, -2, 1) = {tex}\sqrt{\left(-1-0\right)^2+\left(-2-1\right)^2+\left(1-2\right)^2}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{1+9+1}=\sqrt{11}{/tex} unit​​​​​​​
