question_html: Find the shortest distance between the following lines : {tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}  {tex}+\lambda(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}}=(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} {tex}+\mu(7 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex}
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the following lines : {tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+5 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+7 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}+\lambda(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}}=(-\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} {tex}+\mu(7 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex}
explanation: Given {tex}\overrightarrow r=3\widehat i+5\widehat j+7\widehat k+\lambda\left(\widehat i-2\widehat j+\widehat k\right){/tex} And {tex}\overrightarrow r=-\widehat i-\widehat j-\widehat k+\mu\left(\widehat{7i}-6\widehat j+\widehat k\right){/tex} So a 1 = {tex}3\widehat i+5\widehat j+7\widehat k{/tex} , a 2 = {tex}-\widehat i-\widehat j-\widehat k{/tex} And b 1 = {tex}\widehat i-2\widehat j+\widehat k{/tex} , b 2 = {tex}7\widehat i-6\widehat j+\widehat k{/tex} {tex}d=\left|\frac{\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right).\left(\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right)}{\left|\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right|}\right|{/tex} {tex}\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right)=-4\widehat i-6\widehat j-8\widehat k{/tex} {tex}\left(\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right)=\begin{vmatrix}i&j&k\\1&-2&1\\7&-6&1\end{vmatrix}{/tex} {tex}=4\widehat i-6\widehat j+8\widehat k{/tex} {tex}\left|\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right|=\sqrt{16+36+64}=\sqrt{116}{/tex} {tex}d=\left|\frac{\left(-4\widehat i-6\widehat j-8\widehat k\right).\left(4\widehat i-6\widehat j+8\widehat k\right)}{\sqrt{116}}\right|{/tex} {tex}d=\left|\frac{-16+36-64}{\sqrt{116}}\right|=\frac{44}{\sqrt{116}}{/tex}
solution: Given {tex}\overrightarrow r=3\widehat i+5\widehat j+7\widehat k+\lambda\left(\widehat i-2\widehat j+\widehat k\right){/tex} And {tex}\overrightarrow r=-\widehat i-\widehat j-\widehat k+\mu\left(\widehat{7i}-6\widehat j+\widehat k\right){/tex} So a 1 = {tex}3\widehat i+5\widehat j+7\widehat k{/tex} , a 2 = {tex}-\widehat i-\widehat j-\widehat k{/tex} And b 1 = {tex}\widehat i-2\widehat j+\widehat k{/tex} , b 2 = {tex}7\widehat i-6\widehat j+\widehat k{/tex} {tex}d=\left|\frac{\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right).\left(\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right)}{\left|\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right|}\right|{/tex} {tex}\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right)=-4\widehat i-6\widehat j-8\widehat k{/tex} {tex}\left(\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right)=\begin{vmatrix}i&j&k\\1&-2&1\\7&-6&1\end{vmatrix}{/tex} {tex}=4\widehat i-6\widehat j+8\widehat k{/tex} {tex}\left|\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right|=\sqrt{16+36+64}=\sqrt{116}{/tex} {tex}d=\left|\frac{\left(-4\widehat i-6\widehat j-8\widehat k\right).\left(4\widehat i-6\widehat j+8\widehat k\right)}{\sqrt{116}}\right|{/tex} {tex}d=\left|\frac{-16+36-64}{\sqrt{116}}\right|=\frac{44}{\sqrt{116}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given {tex}\overrightarrow r=3\widehat i+5\widehat j+7\widehat k+\lambda\left(\widehat i-2\widehat j+\widehat k\right){/tex}
And {tex}\overrightarrow r=-\widehat i-\widehat j-\widehat k+\mu\left(\widehat{7i}-6\widehat j+\widehat k\right){/tex}
So a1 = {tex}3\widehat i+5\widehat j+7\widehat k{/tex} , a2 = {tex}-\widehat i-\widehat j-\widehat k{/tex}
And b1 = {tex}\widehat i-2\widehat j+\widehat k{/tex} , b2 = {tex}7\widehat i-6\widehat j+\widehat k{/tex}
{tex}d=\left|\frac{\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right).\left(\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right)}{\left|\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right|}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right)=-4\widehat i-6\widehat j-8\widehat k{/tex}
{tex}\left(\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right)=\begin{vmatrix}i&j&k\\1&-2&1\\7&-6&1\end{vmatrix}{/tex} {tex}=4\widehat i-6\widehat j+8\widehat k{/tex}
{tex}\left|\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right|=\sqrt{16+36+64}=\sqrt{116}{/tex}
{tex}d=\left|\frac{\left(-4\widehat i-6\widehat j-8\widehat k\right).\left(4\widehat i-6\widehat j+8\widehat k\right)}{\sqrt{116}}\right|{/tex}
{tex}d=\left|\frac{-16+36-64}{\sqrt{116}}\right|=\frac{44}{\sqrt{116}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If a line makes 60o and 45o angles with the positive directions of x-axis and z-axis respectively, then find the angle that it makes with the positive direction of y-axis. Hence, write the direction cosines of the line.
question_text: If a line makes 60 o and 45 o angles with the positive directions of x-axis and z-axis respectively, then find the angle that it makes with the positive direction of y-axis. Hence, write the direction cosines of the line.
explanation: let {tex}\alpha=\;60^\circ,\beta=?,\gamma=45^\circ{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\cos²\alpha+\cos²\beta+\cos²\gamma=1{/tex} {tex}\cos^260^\circ+\cos^2\beta+\cos^245^\circ=1{/tex} {tex}\frac14+\cos^2\beta+\frac12=1{/tex} {tex}\cos^2\beta=1-\frac34=\frac14{/tex} {tex}\cos\beta=\frac12{/tex} {tex}\beta=60^\circ{/tex} The angle made with y-axis is 60° Direction cosines = {tex}\left(\frac12,\frac12,\frac1{\sqrt2}\right){/tex}
solution: let {tex}\alpha=\;60^\circ,\beta=?,\gamma=45^\circ{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\cos²\alpha+\cos²\beta+\cos²\gamma=1{/tex} {tex}\cos^260^\circ+\cos^2\beta+\cos^245^\circ=1{/tex} {tex}\frac14+\cos^2\beta+\frac12=1{/tex} {tex}\cos^2\beta=1-\frac34=\frac14{/tex} {tex}\cos\beta=\frac12{/tex} {tex}\beta=60^\circ{/tex} The angle made with y-axis is 60° Direction cosines = {tex}\left(\frac12,\frac12,\frac1{\sqrt2}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
let {tex}\alpha=\;60^\circ,\beta=?,\gamma=45^\circ{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\cos²\alpha+\cos²\beta+\cos²\gamma=1{/tex}
{tex}\cos^260^\circ+\cos^2\beta+\cos^245^\circ=1{/tex}
{tex}\frac14+\cos^2\beta+\frac12=1{/tex}
{tex}\cos^2\beta=1-\frac34=\frac14{/tex}
{tex}\cos\beta=\frac12{/tex}
{tex}\beta=60^\circ{/tex}
The angle made with y-axis is 60°
Direction cosines = {tex}\left(\frac12,\frac12,\frac1{\sqrt2}\right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Check whether the lines {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}{/tex} = z are skew or not.
question_text: Check whether the lines {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-4}{5}=\frac{y-1}{2}{/tex} = z are skew or not.
explanation: Let {tex}\frac{x-1}2=\frac{y-2}3=\frac{z-3}4=k{/tex} {tex}\frac{x-4}5=\frac{y-1}2=z{/tex} ...(i) x = 2k + 1, y = 3k + 2, z = 4k + 3 Putting in eqn (i) {tex}\frac{2k-3}5=\frac{3k+1}2=4k+3{/tex} On compairing {tex}\frac{2k-3}5=\frac{3k+1}2{/tex} k = -1 And {tex}\frac{3k+1}2=4k+3{/tex} k = -1 Therefore the lines are not skew.
solution: Let {tex}\frac{x-1}2=\frac{y-2}3=\frac{z-3}4=k{/tex} {tex}\frac{x-4}5=\frac{y-1}2=z{/tex} ...(i) x = 2k + 1, y = 3k + 2, z = 4k + 3 Putting in eqn (i) {tex}\frac{2k-3}5=\frac{3k+1}2=4k+3{/tex} On compairing {tex}\frac{2k-3}5=\frac{3k+1}2{/tex} k = -1 And {tex}\frac{3k+1}2=4k+3{/tex} k = -1 Therefore the lines are not skew.
solution_html:
Let {tex}\frac{x-1}2=\frac{y-2}3=\frac{z-3}4=k{/tex}
{tex}\frac{x-4}5=\frac{y-1}2=z{/tex} ...(i)
x = 2k + 1, y = 3k + 2, z = 4k + 3
Putting in eqn (i)
{tex}\frac{2k-3}5=\frac{3k+1}2=4k+3{/tex}
On compairing {tex}\frac{2k-3}5=\frac{3k+1}2{/tex}
k = -1
And {tex}\frac{3k+1}2=4k+3{/tex}
k = -1
Therefore the lines are not skew.

question_html:
Find the shortest distance between the following lines:
{tex}\vec{r}=3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}+\lambda(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}
and {tex}\vec{r}=(-\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\mu(7 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the following lines: {tex}\vec{r}=3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}+\lambda(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(-\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\mu(7 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}
explanation: Given lines are: {tex}\vec{r}=3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}+7 \hat{k}+\lambda(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}) {/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(-\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\mu(7 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} Let the given lines be {tex}\vec{r}=\overrightarrow{a_1}+\lambda \overrightarrow{b_1}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\overrightarrow{a_2}+\lambda \overrightarrow{b_2}{/tex} Shortest distance between two lines {tex}d =\left|\frac{\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right)}{\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right|}\right|{/tex} {tex} \therefore \vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1 {/tex} {tex}=(-\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}-(3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=-4 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2 =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \\ 7 & -6 & 1 \end{array}\right| \\{/tex} {tex}=\hat{i}(-2+6)-\hat{j}(1-7){/tex} {tex}+\hat{k}(-6+14){/tex} {tex}=4 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right| =\sqrt{4^2+6^2+8^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{16+36+64}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{116}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}d=\left|\frac{(-4 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}) \cdot(4 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{116}}\right|{/tex} {tex}\left|\frac{-16-36-64}{\sqrt{116}}\right|{/tex} = {tex} \left|\frac{-116}{\sqrt{116}}\right|=\sqrt{116}{/tex} units
solution: Given lines are: {tex}\vec{r}=3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}+7 \hat{k}+\lambda(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}) {/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(-\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\mu(7 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} Let the given lines be {tex}\vec{r}=\overrightarrow{a_1}+\lambda \overrightarrow{b_1}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\overrightarrow{a_2}+\lambda \overrightarrow{b_2}{/tex} Shortest distance between two lines {tex}d =\left|\frac{\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right)}{\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right|}\right|{/tex} {tex} \therefore \vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1 {/tex} {tex}=(-\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}-(3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=-4 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2 =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \\ 7 & -6 & 1 \end{array}\right| \\{/tex} {tex}=\hat{i}(-2+6)-\hat{j}(1-7){/tex} {tex}+\hat{k}(-6+14){/tex} {tex}=4 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right| =\sqrt{4^2+6^2+8^2}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{16+36+64}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{116}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}d=\left|\frac{(-4 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}) \cdot(4 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{116}}\right|{/tex} {tex}\left|\frac{-16-36-64}{\sqrt{116}}\right|{/tex} = {tex} \left|\frac{-116}{\sqrt{116}}\right|=\sqrt{116}{/tex} units
solution_html:
Given lines are:
{tex}\vec{r}=3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}{/tex}{tex}+7 \hat{k}+\lambda(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}) {/tex}
and {tex}\vec{r}=(-\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}{tex}+\mu(7 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}
Let the given lines be {tex}\vec{r}=\overrightarrow{a_1}+\lambda \overrightarrow{b_1}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\overrightarrow{a_2}+\lambda \overrightarrow{b_2}{/tex}
Shortest distance between two lines
{tex}d =\left|\frac{\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right)}{\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right|}\right|{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1 {/tex} {tex}=(-\hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}{tex}-(3 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}=-4 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2 =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \\ 7 & -6 & 1 \end{array}\right| \\{/tex}
{tex}=\hat{i}(-2+6)-\hat{j}(1-7){/tex}{tex}+\hat{k}(-6+14){/tex}
{tex}=4 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\left|\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right| =\sqrt{4^2+6^2+8^2}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{16+36+64}{/tex}
{tex}=\sqrt{116}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}d=\left|\frac{(-4 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}) \cdot(4 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{116}}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\left|\frac{-16-36-64}{\sqrt{116}}\right|{/tex} = {tex} \left|\frac{-116}{\sqrt{116}}\right|=\sqrt{116}{/tex} units

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Electrical transmission wires which are laid down in winters are stretched tightly to accommodate expansion in summers.

Two such wires lie along the following lines:
l1 : {tex}\frac{x+1}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-3}{-2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+2}{-1}{/tex}
l2 : {tex}\frac{x}{-1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-7}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+7}{-2}{/tex}
Based on the given information, answer the following questions:

  1. Are the lines l1 and l2 coplanar? Justify your answer. (2)
  2. Find the point of intersection of the lines l1 and l2. (2)
question_text: Electrical transmission wires which are laid down in winters are stretched tightly to accommodate expansion in summers. Two such wires lie along the following lines: l 1 : {tex}\frac{x+1}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-3}{-2}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+2}{-1}{/tex} l 2 : {tex}\frac{x}{-1}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-7}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{z}+7}{-2}{/tex} Based on the given information, answer the following questions: Are the lines l 1 and l 2 coplanar? Justify your answer. (2) Find the point of intersection of the lines l 1 and l 2 . (2)
explanation: Consider = = 1(+7) - 4(-7) - 5(7) = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} lines l 1 and l 2 are coplanar Any point on {tex}l_1:(3 \lambda-1,-2 \lambda+3,-\lambda-2){/tex} Substituting in equation of {tex}l_2{/tex} {tex}\frac{3 \lambda-1}{-1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-2 \lambda+3-7}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} 9 {tex} \lambda-3{/tex} = 2 {tex}\lambda+4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} 7 {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 7 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1 Point is (2,1,-3)
solution: Consider = = 1(+7) - 4(-7) - 5(7) = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} lines l 1 and l 2 are coplanar Any point on {tex}l_1:(3 \lambda-1,-2 \lambda+3,-\lambda-2){/tex} Substituting in equation of {tex}l_2{/tex} {tex}\frac{3 \lambda-1}{-1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-2 \lambda+3-7}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} 9 {tex} \lambda-3{/tex} = 2 {tex}\lambda+4{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} 7 {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 7 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1 Point is (2,1,-3)

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Two motorcycles A and B are running at the speed more than the allowed speed on the roads represented by the lines {tex}\vec{r}=\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}}=(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}){/tex} {tex}+\mu(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} respectively.

  1. Find the shortest distance between the given lines. (2)
  2. Find the point at which the motorcycles may collide. (2)
question_text: Two motorcycles A and B are running at the speed more than the allowed speed on the roads represented by the lines {tex}\vec{r}=\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}}=(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}){/tex} {tex}+\mu(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} respectively. Find the shortest distance between the given lines. (2) Find the point at which the motorcycles may collide. (2)
explanation: Let {tex}\overrightarrow{a_1}=0,\overrightarrow{a_2}=3\widehat i+3\widehat j{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{b_1}=\widehat i+2\widehat j-\widehat k,\overrightarrow{b_2}=2\widehat i+\widehat j+\widehat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}=3\widehat i+3\widehat j{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}=\begin{vmatrix}\widehat i&\widehat j&\widehat k\\1&2&-1\\2&1&1\end{vmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}=3\widehat i-3\widehat j-3\widehat k{/tex} {tex}\left(a_2-a_1\right)\left(\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right)=\left(3\widehat i+3\widehat j\right)\left(3\widehat i-3\widehat j-3\widehat k\right){/tex} = 9 - 9 = 0 Shortest distance between line is {tex}d=\left|\frac{\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right).\left(\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right)}{\left|\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right|}\right|{/tex} {tex}d=\left|\frac0{3\sqrt3}\right|{/tex} = 0 units. From equation of both lines, we get {tex}\lambda\left(\widehat i+2\widehat j-\widehat k\right)=\left(3\widehat i+3\widehat j\right)+\mu\left(2\widehat i+\widehat j+\widehat k\right){/tex} {tex}\lambda\widehat i+2\lambda\widehat j-\lambda\widehat k=\left(3+2\mu\right)\widehat i+\left(3+\mu\right)\widehat j+\mu\widehat k{/tex} On compairing {tex}\lambda=3+2\mu{/tex} ...(i) {tex}2\lambda=3+\mu{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}-\lambda=\mu{/tex} ...(iii) From eqn (iii) and (i) {tex}\mu=-1{/tex} From eqn (ii) and (iii) {tex}\lambda=1{/tex} {tex}\lambda\left(\widehat i+2\widehat j-\widehat k\right)=\widehat i+2\widehat j-\widehat k{/tex} Hence, at point x = 1, y = 2 & z = -1 both motorcycle collides.
solution: Let {tex}\overrightarrow{a_1}=0,\overrightarrow{a_2}=3\widehat i+3\widehat j{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{b_1}=\widehat i+2\widehat j-\widehat k,\overrightarrow{b_2}=2\widehat i+\widehat j+\widehat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}=3\widehat i+3\widehat j{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}=\begin{vmatrix}\widehat i&\widehat j&\widehat k\\1&2&-1\\2&1&1\end{vmatrix}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}=3\widehat i-3\widehat j-3\widehat k{/tex} {tex}\left(a_2-a_1\right)\left(\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right)=\left(3\widehat i+3\widehat j\right)\left(3\widehat i-3\widehat j-3\widehat k\right){/tex} = 9 - 9 = 0 Shortest distance between line is {tex}d=\left|\frac{\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right).\left(\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right)}{\left|\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right|}\right|{/tex} {tex}d=\left|\frac0{3\sqrt3}\right|{/tex} = 0 units. From equation of both lines, we get {tex}\lambda\left(\widehat i+2\widehat j-\widehat k\right)=\left(3\widehat i+3\widehat j\right)+\mu\left(2\widehat i+\widehat j+\widehat k\right){/tex} {tex}\lambda\widehat i+2\lambda\widehat j-\lambda\widehat k=\left(3+2\mu\right)\widehat i+\left(3+\mu\right)\widehat j+\mu\widehat k{/tex} On compairing {tex}\lambda=3+2\mu{/tex} ...(i) {tex}2\lambda=3+\mu{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}-\lambda=\mu{/tex} ...(iii) From eqn (iii) and (i) {tex}\mu=-1{/tex} From eqn (ii) and (iii) {tex}\lambda=1{/tex} {tex}\lambda\left(\widehat i+2\widehat j-\widehat k\right)=\widehat i+2\widehat j-\widehat k{/tex} Hence, at point x = 1, y = 2 & z = -1 both motorcycle collides.
solution_html:
1. Let {tex}\overrightarrow{a_1}=0,\overrightarrow{a_2}=3\widehat i+3\widehat j{/tex}
    {tex}\overrightarrow{b_1}=\widehat i+2\widehat j-\widehat k,\overrightarrow{b_2}=2\widehat i+\widehat j+\widehat k{/tex}
    {tex}\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}=3\widehat i+3\widehat j{/tex}
    {tex}\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}=\begin{vmatrix}\widehat i&\widehat j&\widehat k\\1&2&-1\\2&1&1\end{vmatrix}{/tex}
    {tex}\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}=3\widehat i-3\widehat j-3\widehat k{/tex}
    {tex}\left(a_2-a_1\right)\left(\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right)=\left(3\widehat i+3\widehat j\right)\left(3\widehat i-3\widehat j-3\widehat k\right){/tex}
    = 9 - 9 = 0
    Shortest distance between line is
    {tex}d=\left|\frac{\left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right).\left(\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right)}{\left|\overrightarrow{b_1}\times\overrightarrow{b_2}\right|}\right|{/tex}
    {tex}d=\left|\frac0{3\sqrt3}\right|{/tex} = 0 units.

  2. From equation of both lines, we get
    {tex}\lambda\left(\widehat i+2\widehat j-\widehat k\right)=\left(3\widehat i+3\widehat j\right)+\mu\left(2\widehat i+\widehat j+\widehat k\right){/tex}
    {tex}\lambda\widehat i+2\lambda\widehat j-\lambda\widehat k=\left(3+2\mu\right)\widehat i+\left(3+\mu\right)\widehat j+\mu\widehat k{/tex}
    On compairing
    {tex}\lambda=3+2\mu{/tex} ...(i)
    {tex}2\lambda=3+\mu{/tex} ...(ii)
    {tex}-\lambda=\mu{/tex} ...(iii)
    From eqn (iii) and (i)
    {tex}\mu=-1{/tex}
    From eqn (ii) and (iii)
    {tex}\lambda=1{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda\left(\widehat i+2\widehat j-\widehat k\right)=\widehat i+2\widehat j-\widehat k{/tex}
    Hence, at point x = 1, y = 2 & z = -1 both motorcycle collides.

question_html: Find the shortest distance between the following lines: {tex}\frac{{x + 1}}{7} = \frac{{y + 1}}{{ - 6}} = \frac{{z + 1}}{1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{x - 3}}{1} = \frac{{y - 5}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - 7}}{1}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the following lines: {tex}\frac{{x + 1}}{7} = \frac{{y + 1}}{{ - 6}} = \frac{{z + 1}}{1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{x - 3}}{1} = \frac{{y - 5}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{{z - 7}}{1}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}{\vec a_1} = - \hat i - \hat j - \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} = 3\hat i + 5\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} = 7\hat i - 6\hat j + 1\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_2} = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} - {\vec a_1} = 4\hat i + 6\hat j + 8\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} \times {\vec b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 7&{ - 6}&1 \\ 1&{ - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = - 4\hat i - 6\hat j - 8\hat k{/tex} {tex}(\vec a_2-\vec a_1).(\vec b_1×\vec b_2)=(4\hat i+6\hat j+8\hat k)(-4\hat i-6\hat j-8\hat k)=-16-36-64=-116{/tex} {tex}\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( { - 4} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 6} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 8} \right)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {116} {/tex} {tex} = 2\sqrt {29} {/tex} {tex}d = \left| {\frac{{\left( {{{\vec a}_2} - {{\vec a}_1}} \right).\left( {{b_1} \times {b_2}} \right)}}{{\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left| {\frac{{ - 116}}{{2\sqrt {29} }}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = 2\sqrt {29} {/tex}
solution: {tex}{\vec a_1} = - \hat i - \hat j - \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} = 3\hat i + 5\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} = 7\hat i - 6\hat j + 1\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_2} = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} - {\vec a_1} = 4\hat i + 6\hat j + 8\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} \times {\vec b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 7&{ - 6}&1 \\ 1&{ - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = - 4\hat i - 6\hat j - 8\hat k{/tex} {tex}(\vec a_2-\vec a_1).(\vec b_1×\vec b_2)=(4\hat i+6\hat j+8\hat k)(-4\hat i-6\hat j-8\hat k)=-16-36-64=-116{/tex} {tex}\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( { - 4} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 6} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 8} \right)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {116} {/tex} {tex} = 2\sqrt {29} {/tex} {tex}d = \left| {\frac{{\left( {{{\vec a}_2} - {{\vec a}_1}} \right).\left( {{b_1} \times {b_2}} \right)}}{{\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left| {\frac{{ - 116}}{{2\sqrt {29} }}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = 2\sqrt {29} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{\vec a_1} = - \hat i - \hat j - \hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\vec a_2} = 3\hat i + 5\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\vec b_1} = 7\hat i - 6\hat j + 1\hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\vec b_2} = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\vec a_2} - {\vec a_1} = 4\hat i + 6\hat j + 8\hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\vec b_1} \times {\vec b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 7&{ - 6}&1 \\ 1&{ - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = - 4\hat i - 6\hat j - 8\hat k{/tex}
{tex}(\vec a_2-\vec a_1).(\vec b_1×\vec b_2)=(4\hat i+6\hat j+8\hat k)(-4\hat i-6\hat j-8\hat k)=-16-36-64=-116{/tex}
{tex}\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( { - 4} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 6} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 8} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {116} {/tex}
{tex} = 2\sqrt {29} {/tex}
{tex}d = \left| {\frac{{\left( {{{\vec a}_2} - {{\vec a}_1}} \right).\left( {{b_1} \times {b_2}} \right)}}{{\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right|}}} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \left| {\frac{{ - 116}}{{2\sqrt {29} }}} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = 2\sqrt {29} {/tex}

question_html: Find the value of k so that the lines x = - y = kz and x - 2 = 2y + 1 = - z + 1 are perpendicular to each other.
question_text: Find the value of k so that the lines x = - y = kz and x - 2 = 2y + 1 = - z + 1 are perpendicular to each other.
explanation: Given L1 = x = -y = kz {tex}\frac x1=\frac y{-1}=\frac z{\displaystyle\frac1k}{/tex} Direction Cosines of L1 = ( 1, -1 , {tex}\frac 1k{/tex} ) Also L2 = x - 2 = 2y + 1 = -z +1 {tex}\frac{x-2}1=\frac{y+\frac 12}{\frac 12}=\frac{z-1}{-1}{/tex} Two lines are perpendicular when dot product of their direction cosines is zero (DC)1 . (DC)2 = 0 {tex}\left(1,-1,\frac1k\right).\left(1,\frac12,-1\right)=0{/tex} {tex}1-\frac12-\frac1k=0{/tex} K = 2
solution: Given L1 = x = -y = kz {tex}\frac x1=\frac y{-1}=\frac z{\displaystyle\frac1k}{/tex} Direction Cosines of L1 = ( 1, -1 , {tex}\frac 1k{/tex} ) Also L2 = x - 2 = 2y + 1 = -z +1 {tex}\frac{x-2}1=\frac{y+\frac 12}{\frac 12}=\frac{z-1}{-1}{/tex} Two lines are perpendicular when dot product of their direction cosines is zero (DC)1 . (DC)2 = 0 {tex}\left(1,-1,\frac1k\right).\left(1,\frac12,-1\right)=0{/tex} {tex}1-\frac12-\frac1k=0{/tex} K = 2
solution_html:
Given L1 = x = -y = kz 
{tex}\frac x1=\frac y{-1}=\frac z{\displaystyle\frac1k}{/tex}
Direction Cosines of L1 = ( 1, -1 , {tex}\frac 1k{/tex})
Also L2 = x - 2 = 2y + 1 = -z +1
{tex}\frac{x-2}1=\frac{y+\frac 12}{\frac 12}=\frac{z-1}{-1}{/tex}
Two lines are perpendicular when dot product of their direction cosines is zero
(DC)1 . (DC)2 = 0
{tex}\left(1,-1,\frac1k\right).\left(1,\frac12,-1\right)=0{/tex}
{tex}1-\frac12-\frac1k=0{/tex}
K = 2

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the vector and cartesian equations of the line which is perpendicular to the lines with equations {tex}\frac{x+2}{1}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z+1}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} and passes through the point (1, 1, 1). Also find the angle between the given lines.
question_text: Find the vector and cartesian equations of the line which is perpendicular to the lines with equations {tex}\frac{x+2}{1}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z+1}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} and passes through the point (1, 1, 1). Also find the angle between the given lines.
explanation: Let equation of required line is {tex} \frac{x-1}{a}=\frac{y-1}{b}=\frac{z-1}{c} {/tex} ...(i) Since, the line is perpendicular to {tex} \frac{x+2}{1}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z+1}{4} {/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} , a + 2b + 4c = 0 ...(ii) 2a + 3b + 4c = 0 ...(iii) Solving (ii) and (iii), {tex} \frac{a}{-4}=\frac{b}{4}=\frac{c}{-1} {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} DR's of line in cartesian form is: -4, 4, -1 Equation of line in Cartesian form is: {tex} \frac{x-1}{-4}=\frac{y-1}{4}=\frac{z-1}{-1} {/tex} Vector form of line is {tex} \vec{r}=(\hat{i}+j+k)+\lambda(-4 \hat{i}+4 j-k) {/tex} Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between given lines. {tex}\cos \theta =\frac{1(2)+2(3)+4(4)}{\sqrt{1+4+16} \sqrt{4+9+16}} {/tex} {tex}=\frac{24}{\sqrt{21} \sqrt{29}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \theta =\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{24}{\sqrt{609}}\right){/tex}
solution: Let equation of required line is {tex} \frac{x-1}{a}=\frac{y-1}{b}=\frac{z-1}{c} {/tex} ...(i) Since, the line is perpendicular to {tex} \frac{x+2}{1}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z+1}{4} {/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} , a + 2b + 4c = 0 ...(ii) 2a + 3b + 4c = 0 ...(iii) Solving (ii) and (iii), {tex} \frac{a}{-4}=\frac{b}{4}=\frac{c}{-1} {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} DR's of line in cartesian form is: -4, 4, -1 Equation of line in Cartesian form is: {tex} \frac{x-1}{-4}=\frac{y-1}{4}=\frac{z-1}{-1} {/tex} Vector form of line is {tex} \vec{r}=(\hat{i}+j+k)+\lambda(-4 \hat{i}+4 j-k) {/tex} Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between given lines. {tex}\cos \theta =\frac{1(2)+2(3)+4(4)}{\sqrt{1+4+16} \sqrt{4+9+16}} {/tex} {tex}=\frac{24}{\sqrt{21} \sqrt{29}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \theta =\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{24}{\sqrt{609}}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let equation of required line is
{tex} \frac{x-1}{a}=\frac{y-1}{b}=\frac{z-1}{c} {/tex} ...(i)
Since, the line is perpendicular to
{tex} \frac{x+2}{1}=\frac{y-3}{2}=\frac{z+1}{4} {/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex},
a + 2b + 4c = 0 ...(ii)
2a + 3b + 4c = 0 ...(iii)
Solving (ii) and (iii),
{tex} \frac{a}{-4}=\frac{b}{4}=\frac{c}{-1} {/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} DR's of line in cartesian form is: -4, 4, -1
Equation of line in Cartesian form is:
{tex} \frac{x-1}{-4}=\frac{y-1}{4}=\frac{z-1}{-1} {/tex}
Vector form of line is
{tex} \vec{r}=(\hat{i}+j+k)+\lambda(-4 \hat{i}+4 j-k) {/tex}
Let {tex}\theta{/tex} be the angle between given lines.
{tex}\cos \theta =\frac{1(2)+2(3)+4(4)}{\sqrt{1+4+16} \sqrt{4+9+16}} {/tex}
{tex}=\frac{24}{\sqrt{21} \sqrt{29}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \theta =\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{24}{\sqrt{609}}\right){/tex}

question_html:
Find the shortest distance between the lines
{tex}\vec{r}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{r}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}+\mu(4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex}
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the lines {tex}\vec{r}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}+\lambda(3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}+\mu(4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}){/tex}
explanation: {tex} \vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1=\hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}-5 \hat{{k}} {/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{{~b}}_1 \times \overrightarrow{{b}}_2=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{{i}} & \hat{{j}} & \hat{{k}} \\ 3 & -2 & 5 \\ 4 & -1 & 3 \end{array}\right|=-\hat{{i}}+11 \hat{{j}}+5 \hat{{k}} {/tex} {tex} \text { Using, shortest distance }=\left|\frac{\left(\overrightarrow{{a}}_2-\overrightarrow{{a}}_1\right) \cdot\left(\overrightarrow{{b}}_1 \times \overrightarrow{{b}}_2\right)}{\left|\overrightarrow{{b}}_1 \times \overrightarrow{{b}}_2\right|}\right| {/tex} {tex}=\frac{7}{\sqrt{147}} \text { units or } \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \text { units }{/tex}
solution: {tex} \vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1=\hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}-5 \hat{{k}} {/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{{~b}}_1 \times \overrightarrow{{b}}_2=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{{i}} & \hat{{j}} & \hat{{k}} \\ 3 & -2 & 5 \\ 4 & -1 & 3 \end{array}\right|=-\hat{{i}}+11 \hat{{j}}+5 \hat{{k}} {/tex} {tex} \text { Using, shortest distance }=\left|\frac{\left(\overrightarrow{{a}}_2-\overrightarrow{{a}}_1\right) \cdot\left(\overrightarrow{{b}}_1 \times \overrightarrow{{b}}_2\right)}{\left|\overrightarrow{{b}}_1 \times \overrightarrow{{b}}_2\right|}\right| {/tex} {tex}=\frac{7}{\sqrt{147}} \text { units or } \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \text { units }{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1=\hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}-5 \hat{{k}} {/tex}
{tex} \overrightarrow{{~b}}_1 \times \overrightarrow{{b}}_2=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{{i}} & \hat{{j}} & \hat{{k}} \\ 3 & -2 & 5 \\ 4 & -1 & 3 \end{array}\right|=-\hat{{i}}+11 \hat{{j}}+5 \hat{{k}} {/tex}
{tex} \text { Using, shortest distance }=\left|\frac{\left(\overrightarrow{{a}}_2-\overrightarrow{{a}}_1\right) \cdot\left(\overrightarrow{{b}}_1 \times \overrightarrow{{b}}_2\right)}{\left|\overrightarrow{{b}}_1 \times \overrightarrow{{b}}_2\right|}\right| {/tex}
{tex}=\frac{7}{\sqrt{147}} \text { units or } \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \text { units }{/tex}

question_html: What are the direction cosines of a line which makes equal angles with the coordinate axes?
question_text: What are the direction cosines of a line which makes equal angles with the coordinate axes?
explanation: Given, line makes equal angles with coordinate axes. Let {tex} \alpha{/tex} , {tex}\beta{/tex} and {tex}\gamma{/tex} be the angles made by the lie with coordinate axes. {tex}\alpha = \beta = \gamma \Rightarrow \cos \alpha = \cos \beta = \cos \gamma{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}l = m = n{/tex} ....(i) {tex} [ \because l = \cos \alpha , m = \cos \beta , n = \cos \gamma ]{/tex} We know that, {tex}l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}l^2 + l^2 + l^2 = 1{/tex} [from Eq (1)] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 3 l ^ { 2 } = 1 \Rightarrow l ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \Rightarrow l = \pm \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} From Eq. ( i), direction cosines of the line are {tex} \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) \text { or } \left( \frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } , \frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } , \frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right){/tex}
solution: Given, line makes equal angles with coordinate axes. Let {tex} \alpha{/tex} , {tex}\beta{/tex} and {tex}\gamma{/tex} be the angles made by the lie with coordinate axes. {tex}\alpha = \beta = \gamma \Rightarrow \cos \alpha = \cos \beta = \cos \gamma{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}l = m = n{/tex} ....(i) {tex} [ \because l = \cos \alpha , m = \cos \beta , n = \cos \gamma ]{/tex} We know that, {tex}l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}l^2 + l^2 + l^2 = 1{/tex} [from Eq (1)] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 3 l ^ { 2 } = 1 \Rightarrow l ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \Rightarrow l = \pm \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex} From Eq. ( i), direction cosines of the line are {tex} \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) \text { or } \left( \frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } , \frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } , \frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right){/tex}
solution_html:
Given, line makes equal angles with coordinate axes. Let  {tex} \alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex} and {tex}\gamma{/tex} be the angles made by the lie with coordinate axes.
{tex}\alpha = \beta = \gamma \Rightarrow \cos \alpha = \cos \beta = \cos \gamma{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}l = m = n{/tex} ....(i)
{tex} [ \because l = \cos \alpha , m = \cos \beta , n = \cos \gamma ]{/tex}
We know that, {tex}l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} {tex}l^2 + l^2 + l^2 = 1{/tex} [from Eq (1)]
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 3 l ^ { 2 } = 1 \Rightarrow l ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \Rightarrow l = \pm \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex}
From Eq. ( i), direction cosines of the line are
{tex} \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right) \text { or } \left( \frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } , \frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } , \frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \right){/tex}

question_html: Find the cartesian equation of the line which passes through the point (-2, 4, -5) and is parallel to the line {tex}\frac { x + 3 } { 3 } = \frac { 4 - y } { 5 } = \frac { z + 8 } { 6 }{/tex} .
question_text: Find the cartesian equation of the line which passes through the point (-2, 4, -5) and is parallel to the line {tex}\frac { x + 3 } { 3 } = \frac { 4 - y } { 5 } = \frac { z + 8 } { 6 }{/tex} .
explanation: If two lines are parallel then they both have same direction ratios According to the question, the required line is parallel to the line {tex}\frac { x + 3 } { 3 } = \frac { 4 - y } { 5 } = \frac { z + 8 } { 6 } \ or \ \frac { x + 3 } { 3 } = \frac { y - 4 } { - 5 } = \frac { z + 8 } { 6 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction Ratios of both lines are proportional to each other. The required equation of the line passing through {tex}(-2, 4, - 5){/tex} having Direction ratios {tex}(3, - 5, 6){/tex} is {tex}\frac { x + 2 } { 3 } = \frac { y - 4 } { - 5 } = \frac { z + 5 } { 6 }{/tex}
solution: If two lines are parallel then they both have same direction ratios According to the question, the required line is parallel to the line {tex}\frac { x + 3 } { 3 } = \frac { 4 - y } { 5 } = \frac { z + 8 } { 6 } \ or \ \frac { x + 3 } { 3 } = \frac { y - 4 } { - 5 } = \frac { z + 8 } { 6 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction Ratios of both lines are proportional to each other. The required equation of the line passing through {tex}(-2, 4, - 5){/tex} having Direction ratios {tex}(3, - 5, 6){/tex} is {tex}\frac { x + 2 } { 3 } = \frac { y - 4 } { - 5 } = \frac { z + 5 } { 6 }{/tex}
solution_html:
If two lines are parallel then they both have same direction ratios
According to the question, the required line is parallel to the line
{tex}\frac { x + 3 } { 3 } = \frac { 4 - y } { 5 } = \frac { z + 8 } { 6 } \ or \ \frac { x + 3 } { 3 } = \frac { y - 4 } { - 5 } = \frac { z + 8 } { 6 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction Ratios of both lines are proportional to each other.
The required equation of the line passing through {tex}(-2, 4, - 5){/tex} having Direction ratios {tex}(3, - 5, 6){/tex} is
{tex}\frac { x + 2 } { 3 } = \frac { y - 4 } { - 5 } = \frac { z + 5 } { 6 }{/tex}

question_html: If a line makes angles {tex}{90^0},{135^0},{45^0}{/tex} with the x, y and z - axes respectively, find its direction cosines.
question_text: If a line makes angles {tex}{90^0},{135^0},{45^0}{/tex} with the x, y and z - axes respectively, find its direction cosines.
explanation: Here {tex}\alpha = {90^0},\beta = {135^0}{/tex} and {tex}\gamma = {45^0}{/tex} Since direction cosines of a line making angles {tex}\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma {/tex} with the x, y and z - axes respectively are {tex}\cos \alpha ,\cos \beta ,\cos \gamma {/tex} Therefore, the direction cosines of the required line are: {tex}\cos {90^0} = 0;\cos {135^0} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt 2 }},\cos {45^0} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \cos {{135}^0} = \cos \left( {{{180}^0} - {{45}^0}} \right) = - \cos {{45}^0} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right]{/tex}
solution: Here {tex}\alpha = {90^0},\beta = {135^0}{/tex} and {tex}\gamma = {45^0}{/tex} Since direction cosines of a line making angles {tex}\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma {/tex} with the x, y and z - axes respectively are {tex}\cos \alpha ,\cos \beta ,\cos \gamma {/tex} Therefore, the direction cosines of the required line are: {tex}\cos {90^0} = 0;\cos {135^0} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt 2 }},\cos {45^0} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \cos {{135}^0} = \cos \left( {{{180}^0} - {{45}^0}} \right) = - \cos {{45}^0} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Here {tex}\alpha = {90^0},\beta = {135^0}{/tex} and {tex}\gamma = {45^0}{/tex} 

Since direction cosines of a line making angles {tex}\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma {/tex} with the x, y and z - axes respectively are {tex}\cos \alpha ,\cos \beta ,\cos \gamma {/tex} 

Therefore, the direction cosines of the required line are:

{tex}\cos {90^0} = 0;\cos {135^0} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt 2 }},\cos {45^0} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex}

{tex}\left[ {\because \cos {{135}^0} = \cos \left( {{{180}^0} - {{45}^0}} \right) = - \cos {{45}^0} = \frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right]{/tex}

question_html: Find the direction cosines of a line which makes equal angles with the co-ordinate axes.
question_text: Find the direction cosines of a line which makes equal angles with the co-ordinate axes.
explanation: Let a line make equal angles {tex}\alpha ,\alpha ,\alpha {/tex} with the co-ordinate axes. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction cosines of the line are {tex}\cos \alpha ,\cos \alpha ,\cos \alpha {/tex} …(i) {tex}\therefore {\cos ^2}\alpha + {\cos ^2}\alpha + {\cos ^2}\alpha = 1\,\left[ {\because {{\cos }^2}\alpha + {{\cos }^2}\beta + {{\cos }^2}\gamma = 1} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 3{\cos ^2}\alpha = - 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}\alpha = \frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \alpha = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} Putting {tex}\cos \alpha = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} in eq. (i), direction cosines of the required line making equal angles with the co-ordinate axes are {tex}\pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}, \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}, \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} . {tex}{/tex}
solution: Let a line make equal angles {tex}\alpha ,\alpha ,\alpha {/tex} with the co-ordinate axes. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction cosines of the line are {tex}\cos \alpha ,\cos \alpha ,\cos \alpha {/tex} …(i) {tex}\therefore {\cos ^2}\alpha + {\cos ^2}\alpha + {\cos ^2}\alpha = 1\,\left[ {\because {{\cos }^2}\alpha + {{\cos }^2}\beta + {{\cos }^2}\gamma = 1} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 3{\cos ^2}\alpha = - 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}\alpha = \frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \alpha = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} Putting {tex}\cos \alpha = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} in eq. (i), direction cosines of the required line making equal angles with the co-ordinate axes are {tex}\pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}, \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}, \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} . {tex}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let a line make equal angles {tex}\alpha ,\alpha ,\alpha {/tex} with the co-ordinate axes.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}Direction cosines of the line are {tex}\cos \alpha ,\cos \alpha ,\cos \alpha {/tex}…(i)
{tex}\therefore {\cos ^2}\alpha + {\cos ^2}\alpha + {\cos ^2}\alpha = 1\,\left[ {\because {{\cos }^2}\alpha + {{\cos }^2}\beta + {{\cos }^2}\gamma = 1} \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 3{\cos ^2}\alpha = - 1{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {\cos ^2}\alpha = \frac{1}{3}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \cos \alpha = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex}
Putting {tex}\cos \alpha = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} in eq. (i), direction cosines of the required line making equal angles with the co-ordinate axes are {tex}\pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}, \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}, \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}{/tex} .

 {tex}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If a line has the direction ratios -18, 12, -4 then what are its direction cosines?
question_text: If a line has the direction ratios -18, 12, -4 then what are its direction cosines?
explanation: If a line has direction ratios of −18,12 and −4, then its direction cosines are {tex}\frac{-18}{\sqrt{(-18)^2+(12)^2+(-4)^2}}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{12}{\sqrt{(-18)^2+(12)^2+(-4)^2}}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{-4}{\sqrt{(-4)^2+(12)^2+(-4)^2}}{/tex} i.e., {tex}\frac{-18}{22}, \frac{12}{22}, \frac{-4}{22} \equiv \frac{9}{11}, \frac{6}{11}, \frac{-2}{11}{/tex} Thus, the direction cosines are {tex}-\frac{9}{11}, \frac{6}{11}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{-2}{11}{/tex}
solution: If a line has direction ratios of −18,12 and −4, then its direction cosines are {tex}\frac{-18}{\sqrt{(-18)^2+(12)^2+(-4)^2}}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{12}{\sqrt{(-18)^2+(12)^2+(-4)^2}}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{-4}{\sqrt{(-4)^2+(12)^2+(-4)^2}}{/tex} i.e., {tex}\frac{-18}{22}, \frac{12}{22}, \frac{-4}{22} \equiv \frac{9}{11}, \frac{6}{11}, \frac{-2}{11}{/tex} Thus, the direction cosines are {tex}-\frac{9}{11}, \frac{6}{11}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{-2}{11}{/tex}
solution_html:
If a line has direction ratios of −18,12 and −4, then its direction cosines are
{tex}\frac{-18}{\sqrt{(-18)^2+(12)^2+(-4)^2}}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{12}{\sqrt{(-18)^2+(12)^2+(-4)^2}}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{-4}{\sqrt{(-4)^2+(12)^2+(-4)^2}}{/tex}
i.e., {tex}\frac{-18}{22}, \frac{12}{22}, \frac{-4}{22} \equiv \frac{9}{11}, \frac{6}{11}, \frac{-2}{11}{/tex}
Thus, the direction cosines are {tex}-\frac{9}{11}, \frac{6}{11}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{-2}{11}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If the lines {tex}\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{2 \lambda}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{3 \lambda}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-6}{-5}{/tex} are perpendicular, find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex}. Hence find whether the lines are intersecting or not.
question_text: If the lines {tex}\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{2 \lambda}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{3 \lambda}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-6}{-5}{/tex} are perpendicular, find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} . Hence find whether the lines are intersecting or not.
explanation: The equation of the given lines are, {tex}\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{2 \lambda}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{3 \lambda}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-6}{-5}{/tex} If these lines are perpendicular, then a 1 a 2 + b 1 b 2 + c 1 c 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad-3 \times 3 \lambda+2 \lambda \times 2+2 \times(-5)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad-9 \lambda+4 \lambda-10=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad-5 \lambda=10{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda=-2 {/tex} Now, the lines are {tex}\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{-4}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{-6}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-6}{-5}{/tex} The co-ordinates of any point of first line are given by: {tex}\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{-4}=\frac{z-3}{2}=\alpha{/tex} or {tex}x-1=-3 \alpha \Rightarrow x=-3 \alpha+1{/tex} {tex}y-2=-4 \alpha \Rightarrow y=-4 \alpha+2{/tex} {tex}z-3=2 \alpha \Rightarrow z=2 \alpha+3{/tex} So, coordiantes of any point on this line are, {tex}(-3 \alpha+1,4 \alpha+2,2 \alpha+3){/tex} The coordinates of any point on second line are given by: {tex}\frac{x-1}{-6}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-6}{-5}=\beta{/tex} or {tex}x-1=-6 \beta \Rightarrow x=-6 \beta+1{/tex} {tex}y-1=2 \beta \Rightarrow y=2 \beta+1{/tex} {tex}z-6=-5 \beta \Rightarrow z=-5 \beta+6{/tex} So, co-ordinates of any point on second line are {tex}(-6 \beta+1,2 \beta+1,-5 \beta+6){/tex} If lines intersect then they have a common point. So, for some value of {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} , we have {tex}-3 \alpha+1=-6 \beta+1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad-3 \alpha=-6 \beta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=2 \beta{/tex} and {tex}-4 \alpha+2=2 \beta+1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad-4 \alpha+1=2 \beta{/tex} On solving, we have {tex}\alpha=\frac{1}{5}, and \;\beta=\frac{1}{10}{/tex} The values of {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} do not satisfy the third equation. Hence, lines do not intersect each other.
solution: The equation of the given lines are, {tex}\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{2 \lambda}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{3 \lambda}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-6}{-5}{/tex} If these lines are perpendicular, then a 1 a 2 + b 1 b 2 + c 1 c 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad-3 \times 3 \lambda+2 \lambda \times 2+2 \times(-5)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad-9 \lambda+4 \lambda-10=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad-5 \lambda=10{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda=-2 {/tex} Now, the lines are {tex}\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{-4}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{-6}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-6}{-5}{/tex} The co-ordinates of any point of first line are given by: {tex}\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{-4}=\frac{z-3}{2}=\alpha{/tex} or {tex}x-1=-3 \alpha \Rightarrow x=-3 \alpha+1{/tex} {tex}y-2=-4 \alpha \Rightarrow y=-4 \alpha+2{/tex} {tex}z-3=2 \alpha \Rightarrow z=2 \alpha+3{/tex} So, coordiantes of any point on this line are, {tex}(-3 \alpha+1,4 \alpha+2,2 \alpha+3){/tex} The coordinates of any point on second line are given by: {tex}\frac{x-1}{-6}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-6}{-5}=\beta{/tex} or {tex}x-1=-6 \beta \Rightarrow x=-6 \beta+1{/tex} {tex}y-1=2 \beta \Rightarrow y=2 \beta+1{/tex} {tex}z-6=-5 \beta \Rightarrow z=-5 \beta+6{/tex} So, co-ordinates of any point on second line are {tex}(-6 \beta+1,2 \beta+1,-5 \beta+6){/tex} If lines intersect then they have a common point. So, for some value of {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} , we have {tex}-3 \alpha+1=-6 \beta+1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad-3 \alpha=-6 \beta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=2 \beta{/tex} and {tex}-4 \alpha+2=2 \beta+1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad-4 \alpha+1=2 \beta{/tex} On solving, we have {tex}\alpha=\frac{1}{5}, and \;\beta=\frac{1}{10}{/tex} The values of {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} do not satisfy the third equation. Hence, lines do not intersect each other.
solution_html:
The equation of the given lines are,
{tex}\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{2 \lambda}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{3 \lambda}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-6}{-5}{/tex}
If these lines are perpendicular, then
a1a2 + b1b2 + c1c2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad-3 \times 3 \lambda+2 \lambda \times 2+2 \times(-5)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad-9 \lambda+4 \lambda-10=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad-5 \lambda=10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda=-2 {/tex}
Now, the lines are
{tex}\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{-4}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{-6}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-6}{-5}{/tex}
The co-ordinates of any point of first line are given by:
{tex}\frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{-4}=\frac{z-3}{2}=\alpha{/tex}
or {tex}x-1=-3 \alpha \Rightarrow x=-3 \alpha+1{/tex}
{tex}y-2=-4 \alpha \Rightarrow y=-4 \alpha+2{/tex}
{tex}z-3=2 \alpha \Rightarrow z=2 \alpha+3{/tex}
So, coordiantes of any point on this line are,
{tex}(-3 \alpha+1,4 \alpha+2,2 \alpha+3){/tex}
The coordinates of any point on second line are given by:
{tex}\frac{x-1}{-6}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-6}{-5}=\beta{/tex}
or {tex}x-1=-6 \beta \Rightarrow x=-6 \beta+1{/tex}
{tex}y-1=2 \beta \Rightarrow y=2 \beta+1{/tex}
{tex}z-6=-5 \beta \Rightarrow z=-5 \beta+6{/tex}
So, co-ordinates of any point on second line are
{tex}(-6 \beta+1,2 \beta+1,-5 \beta+6){/tex}
If lines intersect then they have a common point.
So, for some value of {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex}, we have
{tex}-3 \alpha+1=-6 \beta+1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad-3 \alpha=-6 \beta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \alpha=2 \beta{/tex}
and {tex}-4 \alpha+2=2 \beta+1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad-4 \alpha+1=2 \beta{/tex}
On solving, we have
{tex}\alpha=\frac{1}{5}, and \;\beta=\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
The values of {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} do not satisfy the third equation. Hence, lines do not intersect each other.

question_html: Find the vector equation of the line which passes through the point (3, 4, 5) and is parallel to the vector {tex}2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} Find the equations of the line in the vector as well as Cartesian forms.
question_text: Find the vector equation of the line which passes through the point (3, 4, 5) and is parallel to the vector {tex}2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} Find the equations of the line in the vector as well as Cartesian forms.
explanation: If the coordinates of a point A= (x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ), then the position vector is {tex}\vec{a}=x_{1} \hat{\imath}+y_{1} \hat{\jmath}+z_{1} \hat{k}{/tex} Hence, the position vector of point P = (3,4,5) is {tex}\vec{p}=3 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} If the position vector ( {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} ) of a point on the line and a vector ( {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} ) parallel to the line is given, then the vector equation of a line is given by {tex}\vec{l}=\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{r}{/tex} Here, {tex}\vec{a}=\vec{p}=3 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=2 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} , then {tex}\vec{l}=3 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}+5 \hat{k}+\lambda(2 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=(3+2 \lambda) \hat{\imath}+(4+2 \lambda) \hat{\jmath}+(5-3 \lambda) \hat{k}{/tex}
solution: If the coordinates of a point A= (x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ), then the position vector is {tex}\vec{a}=x_{1} \hat{\imath}+y_{1} \hat{\jmath}+z_{1} \hat{k}{/tex} Hence, the position vector of point P = (3,4,5) is {tex}\vec{p}=3 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} If the position vector ( {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} ) of a point on the line and a vector ( {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} ) parallel to the line is given, then the vector equation of a line is given by {tex}\vec{l}=\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{r}{/tex} Here, {tex}\vec{a}=\vec{p}=3 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=2 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} , then {tex}\vec{l}=3 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}+5 \hat{k}+\lambda(2 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-3 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}=(3+2 \lambda) \hat{\imath}+(4+2 \lambda) \hat{\jmath}+(5-3 \lambda) \hat{k}{/tex}
solution_html:
If the coordinates of a point A= (x1, y1, z1), then the position vector is
{tex}\vec{a}=x_{1} \hat{\imath}+y_{1} \hat{\jmath}+z_{1} \hat{k}{/tex}
Hence, the position vector of point P = (3,4,5) is
{tex}\vec{p}=3 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}+5 \hat{k}{/tex}
If the position vector ({tex}\vec{a}{/tex}) of a point on the line and a vector ({tex}\vec{r}{/tex}) parallel to the line is given, then the vector equation of a line is given by
{tex}\vec{l}=\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{r}{/tex}
Here, {tex}\vec{a}=\vec{p}=3 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=2 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-3 \hat{k}{/tex}, then
{tex}\vec{l}=3 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}+5 \hat{k}+\lambda(2 \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-3 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}=(3+2 \lambda) \hat{\imath}+(4+2 \lambda) \hat{\jmath}+(5-3 \lambda) \hat{k}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the vector equation of a line passing through the point (2, 3, 2) and parallel to the line {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathbf{r}}=(-2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}})+\lambda(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+6 \hat{\mathbf{k}}){/tex}. Also, find the distance between these two lines.
Hint: The given line is {tex}L_{1}: \vec{r}=(-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}){/tex}{tex}+\lambda(2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex}
The required line is {tex}L_{2}: \vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}{tex}+\mu(2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex}
Now, find the distance between the parallel lines L1 and L2.
question_text: Find the vector equation of a line passing through the point (2, 3, 2) and parallel to the line {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathbf{r}}=(-2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}})+\lambda(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+6 \hat{\mathbf{k}}){/tex} . Also, find the distance between these two lines. Hint: The given line is {tex}L_{1}: \vec{r}=(-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}){/tex} {tex}+\lambda(2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} The required line is {tex}L_{2}: \vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\mu(2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} Now, find the distance between the parallel lines L 1 and L 2 .
explanation: The given line is {tex}\vec r{/tex} = {tex}(-2\hat i+3\hat j){/tex} + {tex}\lambda(2 \hat i - 3 \hat j + 6 \hat k){/tex} Directions ratios of this line are 2, -3 and 6. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction ratios of required line are 2, -3, 6 (as it is parallel to given line) Given that required line passes through (2, 3, 2) So, equation of the required line is {tex}\vec r{/tex} = {tex}(2 \hat i + 3 \hat j + 2 \hat i){/tex} + {tex}\lambda(2 \hat i - 3 \hat j + 6 \hat k){/tex} Now, the shortest distance between this lines is given by {tex}\vec d{/tex} = {tex}| \frac{\left.| \vec a_{2}- \vec a_{1}\right) \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec b|}|{/tex} Here, {tex}\vec a_2 - \vec a_1{/tex} = {tex}(2 \hat i + 3 \hat j + 2 \hat k){/tex} - {tex}(-2 \hat i + 3 \hat j){/tex} = {tex}4 \hat i + 2 \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} = {tex}(2 \hat i - 3 \hat j + 6 \hat k){/tex} {tex}|\vec b|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+9+36}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{49}{/tex} = 7 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec d{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(4 \hat i+2 \hat k\right) \cdot\left(2 \hat i-3 \hat j+6 \hat k\right)}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\frac{8+0+12}{7}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{20}{7}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = {tex}\frac{20}{7}{/tex}
solution: The given line is {tex}\vec r{/tex} = {tex}(-2\hat i+3\hat j){/tex} + {tex}\lambda(2 \hat i - 3 \hat j + 6 \hat k){/tex} Directions ratios of this line are 2, -3 and 6. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction ratios of required line are 2, -3, 6 (as it is parallel to given line) Given that required line passes through (2, 3, 2) So, equation of the required line is {tex}\vec r{/tex} = {tex}(2 \hat i + 3 \hat j + 2 \hat i){/tex} + {tex}\lambda(2 \hat i - 3 \hat j + 6 \hat k){/tex} Now, the shortest distance between this lines is given by {tex}\vec d{/tex} = {tex}| \frac{\left.| \vec a_{2}- \vec a_{1}\right) \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec b|}|{/tex} Here, {tex}\vec a_2 - \vec a_1{/tex} = {tex}(2 \hat i + 3 \hat j + 2 \hat k){/tex} - {tex}(-2 \hat i + 3 \hat j){/tex} = {tex}4 \hat i + 2 \hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex} = {tex}(2 \hat i - 3 \hat j + 6 \hat k){/tex} {tex}|\vec b|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+9+36}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{49}{/tex} = 7 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec d{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(4 \hat i+2 \hat k\right) \cdot\left(2 \hat i-3 \hat j+6 \hat k\right)}{7}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\frac{8+0+12}{7}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{20}{7}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = {tex}\frac{20}{7}{/tex}
solution_html:
The given line is
{tex}\vec r{/tex} = {tex}(-2\hat i+3\hat j){/tex} + {tex}\lambda(2 \hat i - 3 \hat j + 6 \hat k){/tex} 
Directions ratios of this line are 2, -3 and 6.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction ratios of required line are 2, -3, 6 (as it is parallel to given line)
Given that required line passes through (2, 3, 2)
So, equation of the required line is
{tex}\vec r{/tex} = {tex}(2 \hat i + 3 \hat j + 2 \hat i){/tex} + {tex}\lambda(2 \hat i - 3 \hat j + 6 \hat k){/tex} 
Now, the shortest distance between this lines is given by
{tex}\vec d{/tex} = {tex}| \frac{\left.| \vec a_{2}- \vec a_{1}\right) \cdot \vec{b}}{|\vec b|}|{/tex} 
Here, {tex}\vec a_2 - \vec a_1{/tex} = {tex}(2 \hat i + 3 \hat j + 2 \hat k){/tex} - {tex}(-2 \hat i + 3 \hat j){/tex} 
= {tex}4 \hat i + 2 \hat k{/tex} 
and {tex}\vec b{/tex} = {tex}(2 \hat i - 3 \hat j + 6 \hat k){/tex} 
{tex}|\vec b|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+9+36}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{49}{/tex} = 7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec d{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(4 \hat i+2 \hat k\right) \cdot\left(2 \hat i-3 \hat j+6 \hat k\right)}{7}{/tex} 
= {tex}\left|\frac{8+0+12}{7}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{20}{7}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} d = {tex}\frac{20}{7}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If a line makes anlges 90o, 135o, 45o with the x, y and z axes respectively, find its direction cosines.
question_text: If a line makes anlges 90 o , 135 o , 45 o with the x, y and z axes respectively, find its direction cosines.
explanation: Direction cosines of a line making angle {tex}\alpha{/tex} with x-axis, {tex}\beta{/tex} with y-axis and {tex}\gamma{/tex} with z-axis are l, m ,n. l = cos {tex}\alpha{/tex} , m = cos {tex}\beta{/tex} , n = cos {tex}\gamma{/tex} Here, {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 90° , {tex}\beta{/tex} = 135° and {tex}\gamma{/tex} = 45° l = cos 90° =0 m = cos 135° = cos(180° - 45°) = -cos 45° {tex}=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = cos 45° {tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
solution: Direction cosines of a line making angle {tex}\alpha{/tex} with x-axis, {tex}\beta{/tex} with y-axis and {tex}\gamma{/tex} with z-axis are l, m ,n. l = cos {tex}\alpha{/tex} , m = cos {tex}\beta{/tex} , n = cos {tex}\gamma{/tex} Here, {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 90° , {tex}\beta{/tex} = 135° and {tex}\gamma{/tex} = 45° l = cos 90° =0 m = cos 135° = cos(180° - 45°) = -cos 45° {tex}=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = cos 45° {tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Direction cosines of a line making angle {tex}\alpha{/tex} with x-axis, {tex}\beta{/tex} with y-axis and {tex}\gamma{/tex} with z-axis are l, m ,n.
l = cos{tex}\alpha{/tex} , m = cos{tex}\beta{/tex} , n = cos{tex}\gamma{/tex}
Here, {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 90° , {tex}\beta{/tex} = 135° and {tex}\gamma{/tex} = 45°
l = cos 90°
=0
m = cos 135°
= cos(180° - 45°)
= -cos 45°
{tex}=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
= cos 45°
{tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A line passes through the point with position vector {tex}2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and is in the direction of the vector {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}. Find the equation of the line in cartesian form.
question_text: A line passes through the point with position vector {tex}2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and is in the direction of the vector {tex}\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} . Find the equation of the line in cartesian form.
explanation: The line passes through a point (2,-1,4) and has direction ratios proportional to (1,1,-2). Cartesian equation of the line {tex}=\frac{x-x_{1}}{a}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{b}=\frac{z-z_{1}}{c}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y+1}{1}=\frac{z-4}{-2}{/tex}
solution: The line passes through a point (2,-1,4) and has direction ratios proportional to (1,1,-2). Cartesian equation of the line {tex}=\frac{x-x_{1}}{a}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{b}=\frac{z-z_{1}}{c}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y+1}{1}=\frac{z-4}{-2}{/tex}
solution_html:
The line passes through a point (2,-1,4) and has direction ratios proportional to (1,1,-2).
Cartesian equation of the line
{tex}=\frac{x-x_{1}}{a}=\frac{y-y_{1}}{b}=\frac{z-z_{1}}{c}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y+1}{1}=\frac{z-4}{-2}{/tex}

question_html: Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} so that the lines {tex}\frac{1-x}{3}=\frac{7 y-14}{\lambda}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{7-7 x}{3 \lambda}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{6-z}{5}{/tex} are at right angles.
question_text: Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} so that the lines {tex}\frac{1-x}{3}=\frac{7 y-14}{\lambda}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{7-7 x}{3 \lambda}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{6-z}{5}{/tex} are at right angles.
explanation: Equation of 1st line, {tex}\frac{1-x}{3}=\frac{7 y-14}{\lambda}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{\lambda / 7}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} Direction Ratios of 1st line are (-3, {tex}\frac{\lambda}{7}{/tex} , 2) and equation of 2nd line, {tex}\frac{7-7 x}{3 \lambda}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{6-z}{5}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x-1}{-3 \lambda / 7}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{z-6}{-5}{/tex} Direction Ratios of 2nd line ( {tex}\frac{-3 \lambda}{7}{/tex} , 1, -5) Since, given lines are at right angles, {tex}\therefore -3\left(\frac{-3 \lambda}{7}\right)+\frac{\lambda}{7}(1)+2(-5)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{9 \lambda}{7}+\frac{\lambda}{7}-10=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 10 \lambda-70=0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 10 \lambda=70{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=7 {/tex}
solution: Equation of 1st line, {tex}\frac{1-x}{3}=\frac{7 y-14}{\lambda}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{\lambda / 7}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} Direction Ratios of 1st line are (-3, {tex}\frac{\lambda}{7}{/tex} , 2) and equation of 2nd line, {tex}\frac{7-7 x}{3 \lambda}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{6-z}{5}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x-1}{-3 \lambda / 7}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{z-6}{-5}{/tex} Direction Ratios of 2nd line ( {tex}\frac{-3 \lambda}{7}{/tex} , 1, -5) Since, given lines are at right angles, {tex}\therefore -3\left(\frac{-3 \lambda}{7}\right)+\frac{\lambda}{7}(1)+2(-5)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{9 \lambda}{7}+\frac{\lambda}{7}-10=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 10 \lambda-70=0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 10 \lambda=70{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=7 {/tex}
solution_html:
Equation of 1st line,
{tex}\frac{1-x}{3}=\frac{7 y-14}{\lambda}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{\lambda / 7}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex}
Direction Ratios of 1st line are (-3, {tex}\frac{\lambda}{7}{/tex}, 2)
and equation of 2nd line,
{tex}\frac{7-7 x}{3 \lambda}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{6-z}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x-1}{-3 \lambda / 7}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{z-6}{-5}{/tex}
Direction Ratios of 2nd line ({tex}\frac{-3 \lambda}{7}{/tex}, 1, -5)
Since, given lines are at right angles,
{tex}\therefore -3\left(\frac{-3 \lambda}{7}\right)+\frac{\lambda}{7}(1)+2(-5)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{9 \lambda}{7}+\frac{\lambda}{7}-10=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 10 \lambda-70=0 {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 10 \lambda=70{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=7 {/tex}

question_html:
Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} for which the following lines are perpendicular to each other:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{x}-5}{5 \lambda+2}=\frac{2-\mathrm{y}}{5}=\frac{1-\mathrm{z}}{-1};{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y+\frac{1}{2}}{2 \lambda}=\frac{z-1}{3}{/tex}
Hence, find whether the lines intersect or not.
question_text: Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} for which the following lines are perpendicular to each other: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{x}-5}{5 \lambda+2}=\frac{2-\mathrm{y}}{5}=\frac{1-\mathrm{z}}{-1};{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y+\frac{1}{2}}{2 \lambda}=\frac{z-1}{3}{/tex} Hence, find whether the lines intersect or not.
explanation: Writing the equations of given lines in standard form, as {tex}\frac{x-5}{5 \lambda+2}=\frac{y-2}{-5}=\frac{z-1}{1} ; \frac{x}{1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{y+\frac{1}{2}}{2 \lambda}=\frac{z-1}{3}{/tex} As lines are perpendicular to each other, {tex}\Rightarrow(5 \lambda+2) \cdot 1+(-5)(2 \lambda)+1(3)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-5 \lambda+5=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} lines are {tex}\frac{x-5}{7}=\frac{y-2}{-5}-\frac{z-1}{1};{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y+\frac{1}{2}}{2}=\frac{z-1}{3}{/tex} Shortest distance between these lines {tex}=\frac{\left|\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right)\right|}{\left|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left|\left(5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{5}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}\right) \cdot(-17 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-20 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+19 \hat{\mathrm{k}})\right|}{\left|\vec{\mathrm{b}}_{1} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}}_{2}\right|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{135}{\left|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right|} \neq 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} lines are not intersecting
solution: Writing the equations of given lines in standard form, as {tex}\frac{x-5}{5 \lambda+2}=\frac{y-2}{-5}=\frac{z-1}{1} ; \frac{x}{1}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{y+\frac{1}{2}}{2 \lambda}=\frac{z-1}{3}{/tex} As lines are perpendicular to each other, {tex}\Rightarrow(5 \lambda+2) \cdot 1+(-5)(2 \lambda)+1(3)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-5 \lambda+5=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} lines are {tex}\frac{x-5}{7}=\frac{y-2}{-5}-\frac{z-1}{1};{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y+\frac{1}{2}}{2}=\frac{z-1}{3}{/tex} Shortest distance between these lines {tex}=\frac{\left|\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right)\right|}{\left|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left|\left(5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{5}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}\right) \cdot(-17 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-20 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+19 \hat{\mathrm{k}})\right|}{\left|\vec{\mathrm{b}}_{1} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}}_{2}\right|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{135}{\left|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right|} \neq 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} lines are not intersecting
solution_html:
Writing the equations of given lines in standard form, as
{tex}\frac{x-5}{5 \lambda+2}=\frac{y-2}{-5}=\frac{z-1}{1} ; \frac{x}{1}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{y+\frac{1}{2}}{2 \lambda}=\frac{z-1}{3}{/tex}
As lines are perpendicular to each other,
{tex}\Rightarrow(5 \lambda+2) \cdot 1+(-5)(2 \lambda)+1(3)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-5 \lambda+5=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} lines are {tex}\frac{x-5}{7}=\frac{y-2}{-5}-\frac{z-1}{1};{/tex}{tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y+\frac{1}{2}}{2}=\frac{z-1}{3}{/tex}
Shortest distance between these lines
{tex}=\frac{\left|\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right)\right|}{\left|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right|}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\left|\left(5 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{5}{2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}\right) \cdot(-17 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-20 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+19 \hat{\mathrm{k}})\right|}{\left|\vec{\mathrm{b}}_{1} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}}_{2}\right|}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{135}{\left|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right|} \neq 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} lines are not intersecting

question_html: Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex}, so that the lines {tex}\frac{1-x}{3}=\frac{7 y-14}{\lambda}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{7-7 x}{3 \lambda}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{6-z}{5}{/tex} are at right angles. Also, find whether the lines are intersecting or not.
question_text: Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} , so that the lines {tex}\frac{1-x}{3}=\frac{7 y-14}{\lambda}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{7-7 x}{3 \lambda}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{6-z}{5}{/tex} are at right angles. Also, find whether the lines are intersecting or not.
explanation: Equation of 1st line, {tex}\frac{1-x}{3}=\frac{7 y-14}{\lambda}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{\lambda / 7}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} Direction Ratios of 1st line are (-3, {tex}\frac{\lambda}{7}{/tex} , 2) and equation of 2nd line, {tex}\frac{7-7 x}{3 \lambda}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{6-z}{5}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x-1}{-3 \lambda / 7}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{z-6}{-5}{/tex} Direction Ratios of 2nd line ( {tex}\frac{-3 \lambda}{7}{/tex} , 1, -5) Since, given lines are at right angles, {tex}\therefore -3\left(\frac{-3 \lambda}{7}\right)+\frac{\lambda}{7}(1)+2(-5)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{9 \lambda}{7}+\frac{\lambda}{7}-10=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 10 \lambda-70=0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 10 \lambda=70{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=7 {/tex}
solution: Equation of 1st line, {tex}\frac{1-x}{3}=\frac{7 y-14}{\lambda}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{\lambda / 7}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex} Direction Ratios of 1st line are (-3, {tex}\frac{\lambda}{7}{/tex} , 2) and equation of 2nd line, {tex}\frac{7-7 x}{3 \lambda}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{6-z}{5}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x-1}{-3 \lambda / 7}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{z-6}{-5}{/tex} Direction Ratios of 2nd line ( {tex}\frac{-3 \lambda}{7}{/tex} , 1, -5) Since, given lines are at right angles, {tex}\therefore -3\left(\frac{-3 \lambda}{7}\right)+\frac{\lambda}{7}(1)+2(-5)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{9 \lambda}{7}+\frac{\lambda}{7}-10=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 10 \lambda-70=0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 10 \lambda=70{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=7 {/tex}
solution_html:
Equation of 1st line,
{tex}\frac{1-x}{3}=\frac{7 y-14}{\lambda}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-1}{-3}=\frac{y-2}{\lambda / 7}=\frac{z-3}{2}{/tex}
Direction Ratios of 1st line are (-3, {tex}\frac{\lambda}{7}{/tex}, 2)
and equation of 2nd line,
{tex}\frac{7-7 x}{3 \lambda}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{6-z}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x-1}{-3 \lambda / 7}=\frac{y-5}{1}=\frac{z-6}{-5}{/tex}
Direction Ratios of 2nd line ({tex}\frac{-3 \lambda}{7}{/tex}, 1, -5)
Since, given lines are at right angles,
{tex}\therefore -3\left(\frac{-3 \lambda}{7}\right)+\frac{\lambda}{7}(1)+2(-5)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{9 \lambda}{7}+\frac{\lambda}{7}-10=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 10 \lambda-70=0 {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 10 \lambda=70{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lambda=7 {/tex}

question_html: Find the vector equation of the line passing through (2, 1, -1) and parallel to the line {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+\hat{j})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}. Also, find the distance between these two lines.
question_text: Find the vector equation of the line passing through (2, 1, -1) and parallel to the line {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+\hat{j})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} . Also, find the distance between these two lines.
explanation: {tex}\vec r{/tex} = {tex}(\hat i + \hat j) + \lambda(2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k){/tex} DR's are (2, -1, 1) {tex}\vec r{/tex} = {tex}\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}(2 \hat i + \hat j - \hat k){/tex} + {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}(2 \hat i - \hat j + \hat k){/tex} d = {tex}\frac{|(\vec a_2 - \vec a_1) \times \vec b)|}{|\vec b|}{/tex} {tex}\vec a_2 - \vec a_1{/tex} = {tex}\hat i - \hat k{/tex} {tex}(\vec a_2 - \vec a_1) \times \vec b{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} i & j & k \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex} (-1) - {tex}\hat j{/tex} (1 + 2) + {tex}\hat k{/tex} (-1) = - {tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} - {tex}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|(\vec a_2 - \vec a_1) \times \vec b|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1+9+1}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{11}{/tex} {tex}|\vec b|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+1+1}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{6}{/tex} d = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{11}}{\sqrt{6}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{11}{6}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\vec r{/tex} = {tex}(\hat i + \hat j) + \lambda(2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k){/tex} DR's are (2, -1, 1) {tex}\vec r{/tex} = {tex}\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}(2 \hat i + \hat j - \hat k){/tex} + {tex}\lambda{/tex} {tex}(2 \hat i - \hat j + \hat k){/tex} d = {tex}\frac{|(\vec a_2 - \vec a_1) \times \vec b)|}{|\vec b|}{/tex} {tex}\vec a_2 - \vec a_1{/tex} = {tex}\hat i - \hat k{/tex} {tex}(\vec a_2 - \vec a_1) \times \vec b{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} i & j & k \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\hat i{/tex} (-1) - {tex}\hat j{/tex} (1 + 2) + {tex}\hat k{/tex} (-1) = - {tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3 {tex}\hat j{/tex} - {tex}\hat k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|(\vec a_2 - \vec a_1) \times \vec b|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1+9+1}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{11}{/tex} {tex}|\vec b|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+1+1}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{6}{/tex} d = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{11}}{\sqrt{6}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{11}{6}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\vec r{/tex} = {tex}(\hat i + \hat j) + \lambda(2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k){/tex} 
DR's are (2, -1, 1)
{tex}\vec r{/tex} = {tex}\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}(2 \hat i + \hat j - \hat k){/tex} + {tex}\lambda{/tex}{tex}(2 \hat i - \hat j + \hat k){/tex} 
d = {tex}\frac{|(\vec a_2 - \vec a_1) \times \vec b)|}{|\vec b|}{/tex} 
{tex}\vec a_2 - \vec a_1{/tex} = {tex}\hat i - \hat k{/tex}  
{tex}(\vec a_2 - \vec a_1) \times \vec b{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} i & j & k \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 2 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} 
= {tex}\hat i{/tex}(-1) - {tex}\hat j{/tex}(1 + 2) + {tex}\hat k{/tex}(-1)
= -{tex}\hat i{/tex} - 3{tex}\hat j{/tex} - {tex}\hat k{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|(\vec a_2 - \vec a_1) \times \vec b|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1+9+1}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{11}{/tex} 
{tex}|\vec b|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+1+1}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{6}{/tex} 
d = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{11}}{\sqrt{6}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{11}{6}}{/tex}

question_html: A line with direction ratios < 2, 2, 1 > intersects the lines {tex}\frac{x-7}{3}=\frac{y-5}{2}=\frac{z-3}{1} \text { and } \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{4}=\frac{z+1}{3}{/tex} at the points P and Q respectively. Find the length and the equation of the intercept PQ.
question_text: A line with direction ratios < 2, 2, 1 > intersects the lines {tex}\frac{x-7}{3}=\frac{y-5}{2}=\frac{z-3}{1} \text { and } \frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{4}=\frac{z+1}{3}{/tex} at the points P and Q respectively. Find the length and the equation of the intercept PQ.
explanation: Let P(3 {tex}\lambda{/tex} + 7, 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} + 5, {tex}\lambda{/tex} + 3) and Q(2 {tex}\mu{/tex} + 1, 4 {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1, 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1) Now, d.r′.s. of PQ = 3 {tex}\lambda{/tex} - 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} + 6, 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} - 4 {tex}\mu{/tex} + 6, {tex}\lambda{/tex} - 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} + 4 According to question, {tex}\frac{3 \lambda-2 \mu+6}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda-4 \mu+6}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda-3 \mu+4}{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} + 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} = 0 and 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} = 2 ⇒ {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -2 {tex}\mu{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(1, 1, 1) and Q(3, 3, 2) PQ = {tex}\sqrt{(3-1)^{2}+(3-1)^{2}+(2-1)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+4+1}{/tex} = 3 therefore length is 3 unit Equation of PQ is {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y-1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{z-1}{1}{/tex}
solution: Let P(3 {tex}\lambda{/tex} + 7, 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} + 5, {tex}\lambda{/tex} + 3) and Q(2 {tex}\mu{/tex} + 1, 4 {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1, 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} - 1) Now, d.r′.s. of PQ = 3 {tex}\lambda{/tex} - 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} + 6, 2 {tex}\lambda{/tex} - 4 {tex}\mu{/tex} + 6, {tex}\lambda{/tex} - 3 {tex}\mu{/tex} + 4 According to question, {tex}\frac{3 \lambda-2 \mu+6}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \lambda-4 \mu+6}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda-3 \mu+4}{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} + 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} = 0 and 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} = 2 ⇒ {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -2 {tex}\mu{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} P(1, 1, 1) and Q(3, 3, 2) PQ = {tex}\sqrt{(3-1)^{2}+(3-1)^{2}+(2-1)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+4+1}{/tex} = 3 therefore length is 3 unit Equation of PQ is {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y-1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{z-1}{1}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let P(3{tex}\lambda{/tex} + 7, 2{tex}\lambda{/tex} + 5, {tex}\lambda{/tex} + 3) and
Q(2{tex}\mu{/tex} + 1, 4{tex}\mu{/tex} - 1, 3{tex}\mu{/tex} - 1)
Now, d.r′.s. of PQ = 3{tex}\lambda{/tex} - 2{tex}\mu{/tex} + 6, 2{tex}\lambda{/tex} - 4{tex}\mu{/tex} + 6, {tex}\lambda{/tex} - 3{tex}\mu{/tex} + 4
According to question,
{tex}\frac{3 \lambda-2 \mu+6}{2}{/tex}={tex}\frac{2 \lambda-4 \mu+6}{2}{/tex}={tex}\frac{\lambda-3 \mu+4}{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} + 2{tex}\mu{/tex} = 0 and 2{tex}\mu{/tex} = 2 ⇒ {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -2{tex}\mu{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} P(1, 1, 1) and Q(3, 3, 2)
PQ = {tex}\sqrt{(3-1)^{2}+(3-1)^{2}+(2-1)^{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{4+4+1}{/tex}
= 3
therefore length is 3 unit
Equation of PQ is {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}{/tex}={tex}\frac{y-1}{2}{/tex}={tex}\frac{z-1}{1}{/tex}

question_html: A line passes through the point with position vector {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} and is in the direction of the vector {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}. Find the equation of the line in cartesian form.
question_text: A line passes through the point with position vector {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} and is in the direction of the vector {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} . Find the equation of the line in cartesian form.
explanation: Equation of a line passing through (x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ) and parallel to line having direction ratios a, b, c is {tex} \frac{x-x_1}{a}=\frac{y-y_1}{b}=\frac{z-z_1}{c} {/tex} Since, the line passes through a point with position vector {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} x 1 = 2, y 1 = -1, z 1 = 4 Also, line is in the direction of {tex}\hat i+\hat j-2\hat k{/tex} Direction ratios: a = 1, b = 1, c = -2 Equation of line in cartesian form is: {tex}\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-(-1)}{1}=\frac{z-4}{(-2)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y+1}{1}=\frac{z-4}{(-2)}{/tex}
solution: Equation of a line passing through (x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ) and parallel to line having direction ratios a, b, c is {tex} \frac{x-x_1}{a}=\frac{y-y_1}{b}=\frac{z-z_1}{c} {/tex} Since, the line passes through a point with position vector {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} x 1 = 2, y 1 = -1, z 1 = 4 Also, line is in the direction of {tex}\hat i+\hat j-2\hat k{/tex} Direction ratios: a = 1, b = 1, c = -2 Equation of line in cartesian form is: {tex}\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-(-1)}{1}=\frac{z-4}{(-2)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y+1}{1}=\frac{z-4}{(-2)}{/tex}
solution_html:
Equation of a line passing through (x1, y1, z1) and parallel to line having direction ratios a, b, c is
{tex} \frac{x-x_1}{a}=\frac{y-y_1}{b}=\frac{z-z_1}{c} {/tex}
Since, the line passes through a point with position vector {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}
 x1 = 2, y1 = -1, z1 = 4
Also, line is in the direction of {tex}\hat i+\hat j-2\hat k{/tex}
Direction ratios: a = 1, b = 1, c = -2
Equation of line in cartesian form is:
{tex}\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y-(-1)}{1}=\frac{z-4}{(-2)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x-2}{1}=\frac{y+1}{1}=\frac{z-4}{(-2)}{/tex}
