question_text: Find the angle between the lines {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-5}{5}{/tex} .
explanation: We know that for the lines {tex}\frac{x-a}{p}=\frac{y-b}{q}=\frac{z-c}{r}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-c}{l}=\frac{y-d}{m}=\frac{z-\epsilon}{n}{/tex} the angle between them ' {tex}\theta{/tex} ' is given by {tex}\theta={\cos ^{-1}} \frac{p l+q m+r n}{\sqrt{p^{2}+q^{2}+r^{2}} \sqrt{l^{2}+m^{2}+n^{2}}}{/tex} The lines are {tex}\frac{x+3}{3}=\frac{y-1}{5}=\frac{z+3}{4} {/tex} and {tex}\frac{x+1}{1}=\frac{y-4}{1}=\frac{z-5}{2}{/tex} Here p = 2, q = 3, r = 4 and l = 3, m = 4, n = 5 {tex}\theta=^{-1} \frac{2(3)+3(4)+4(5)}{\sqrt{2^{2}+3^{2}+4^{2}} \sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}+5^{2}}}=\cos ^{-1} \frac{6+12+20}{\sqrt{4+9+16} \sqrt{9+16+25}}{/tex} {tex}\theta=\cos ^{-1} \frac{6+12+20}{\sqrt{29} \sqrt{50}}=\cos ^{-1} \frac{38}{5 \sqrt{58}}{/tex} {tex}\theta=\cos ^{-1} \frac{38}{5 \sqrt{58}}{/tex} The angle between the {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-5}{5}{/tex} is {tex}\cos ^{-1} \frac{38}{5 \sqrt{58}}{/tex}
solution: We know that for the lines {tex}\frac{x-a}{p}=\frac{y-b}{q}=\frac{z-c}{r}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-c}{l}=\frac{y-d}{m}=\frac{z-\epsilon}{n}{/tex} the angle between them ' {tex}\theta{/tex} ' is given by {tex}\theta={\cos ^{-1}} \frac{p l+q m+r n}{\sqrt{p^{2}+q^{2}+r^{2}} \sqrt{l^{2}+m^{2}+n^{2}}}{/tex} The lines are {tex}\frac{x+3}{3}=\frac{y-1}{5}=\frac{z+3}{4} {/tex} and {tex}\frac{x+1}{1}=\frac{y-4}{1}=\frac{z-5}{2}{/tex} Here p = 2, q = 3, r = 4 and l = 3, m = 4, n = 5 {tex}\theta=^{-1} \frac{2(3)+3(4)+4(5)}{\sqrt{2^{2}+3^{2}+4^{2}} \sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}+5^{2}}}=\cos ^{-1} \frac{6+12+20}{\sqrt{4+9+16} \sqrt{9+16+25}}{/tex} {tex}\theta=\cos ^{-1} \frac{6+12+20}{\sqrt{29} \sqrt{50}}=\cos ^{-1} \frac{38}{5 \sqrt{58}}{/tex} {tex}\theta=\cos ^{-1} \frac{38}{5 \sqrt{58}}{/tex} The angle between the {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-5}{5}{/tex} is {tex}\cos ^{-1} \frac{38}{5 \sqrt{58}}{/tex}
solution_html:
We know that for the lines {tex}\frac{x-a}{p}=\frac{y-b}{q}=\frac{z-c}{r}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-c}{l}=\frac{y-d}{m}=\frac{z-\epsilon}{n}{/tex}
the angle between them '{tex}\theta{/tex}' is given by
{tex}\theta={\cos ^{-1}} \frac{p l+q m+r n}{\sqrt{p^{2}+q^{2}+r^{2}} \sqrt{l^{2}+m^{2}+n^{2}}}{/tex}
The lines are {tex}\frac{x+3}{3}=\frac{y-1}{5}=\frac{z+3}{4} {/tex} and {tex}\frac{x+1}{1}=\frac{y-4}{1}=\frac{z-5}{2}{/tex}
Here p = 2, q = 3, r = 4 and l = 3, m = 4, n = 5
{tex}\theta=^{-1} \frac{2(3)+3(4)+4(5)}{\sqrt{2^{2}+3^{2}+4^{2}} \sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}+5^{2}}}=\cos ^{-1} \frac{6+12+20}{\sqrt{4+9+16} \sqrt{9+16+25}}{/tex}
{tex}\theta=\cos ^{-1} \frac{6+12+20}{\sqrt{29} \sqrt{50}}=\cos ^{-1} \frac{38}{5 \sqrt{58}}{/tex}
{tex}\theta=\cos ^{-1} \frac{38}{5 \sqrt{58}}{/tex}
The angle between the  {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-2}{3}=\frac{y-3}{4}=\frac{z-5}{5}{/tex} is {tex}\cos ^{-1} \frac{38}{5 \sqrt{58}}{/tex}

question_html: Find the shortest distance between the lines {tex}\vec r = \left( {\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {\hat i - 3\hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex}and {tex}\vec r = \left( {4\hat i + 5\hat j + 6\hat k} \right) + \mu \left( {2\hat i + 3\hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex}
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the lines {tex}\vec r = \left( {\hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {\hat i - 3\hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex} and {tex}\vec r = \left( {4\hat i + 5\hat j + 6\hat k} \right) + \mu \left( {2\hat i + 3\hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex}
explanation: {tex}{\vec a_1} = \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} = \hat i - 3\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} = 4\hat i + 5\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_2} = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} - {\vec a_1} = 3\hat i + 3\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} \times {\vec b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&{ - 3}&2 \\ 2&3&1 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = - 9\hat i + 3\hat j + 9\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore |\vec b_1×\vec b_2|=\sqrt {(-9)^2+3^2+9^2}\\ =\sqrt{3^2}\sqrt{3^2+1+3^2}=3\sqrt{19}{/tex} Required shortest distance {tex}\begin{array}{l}=\left|\frac{(a_2-a_1).(b_2-b_1)}{\vert b_1\times b_2\vert}\right|=\left|\frac{-9\times3+3\times3+9\times3}{3\sqrt{19}}\right|=\left|\frac9{3\sqrt{19}}\right|=\frac3{\sqrt{19}}units\\\end{array}{/tex}
solution: {tex}{\vec a_1} = \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} = \hat i - 3\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} = 4\hat i + 5\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_2} = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} - {\vec a_1} = 3\hat i + 3\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} \times {\vec b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&{ - 3}&2 \\ 2&3&1 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = - 9\hat i + 3\hat j + 9\hat k{/tex} {tex}\therefore |\vec b_1×\vec b_2|=\sqrt {(-9)^2+3^2+9^2}\\ =\sqrt{3^2}\sqrt{3^2+1+3^2}=3\sqrt{19}{/tex} Required shortest distance {tex}\begin{array}{l}=\left|\frac{(a_2-a_1).(b_2-b_1)}{\vert b_1\times b_2\vert}\right|=\left|\frac{-9\times3+3\times3+9\times3}{3\sqrt{19}}\right|=\left|\frac9{3\sqrt{19}}\right|=\frac3{\sqrt{19}}units\\\end{array}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{\vec a_1} = \hat i + 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}

{tex}{\vec b_1} = \hat i - 3\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex}

{tex}{\vec a_2} = 4\hat i + 5\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex}

{tex}{\vec b_2} = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + \hat k{/tex}

{tex}{\vec a_2} - {\vec a_1} = 3\hat i + 3\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}

{tex}{\vec b_1} \times {\vec b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&{ - 3}&2 \\ 2&3&1 \end{array}} \right|{/tex}

{tex} = - 9\hat i + 3\hat j + 9\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\therefore |\vec b_1×\vec b_2|=\sqrt {(-9)^2+3^2+9^2}\\ =\sqrt{3^2}\sqrt{3^2+1+3^2}=3\sqrt{19}{/tex}

Required shortest distance

{tex}\begin{array}{l}=\left|\frac{(a_2-a_1).(b_2-b_1)}{\vert b_1\times b_2\vert}\right|=\left|\frac{-9\times3+3\times3+9\times3}{3\sqrt{19}}\right|=\left|\frac9{3\sqrt{19}}\right|=\frac3{\sqrt{19}}units\\\end{array}{/tex}

question_html: Find the equations of the tangent and the normal, to the curve 16x2 + 9y2 = 145 at the point (x1, y1), where x1 = 2 and y1 > 0.
question_text: Find the equations of the tangent and the normal, to the curve 16x 2 + 9y 2 = 145 at the point (x 1 , y 1 ), where x 1 = 2 and y 1 > 0.
explanation: According to the question, equation of curve is 16x 2 + 9y 2 = 145.......(i) Let P(x 1 , y 1 ) be a point on the curve. Given, x 1 = 2 and y 1 > 0. Substitute Point 'P' in the given curve. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 16(2) 2 + 9(y 1 ) 2 = 145 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9(y 1 ) 2 = 145 - 64 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9(y 1 ) 2 = 81 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y 1 ) 2 ​​​​​​​ = 9 {tex}\Rightarrow y _1= \pm 3{/tex} But, y 1 > 0 {tex}\therefore y_1 = 3 {/tex} Hence, P(x 1 , y 1 ) = (2, 3) Now, Differentiate eq.(i) w.r.t x, we get {tex}32x + 18y \frac{dy}{dx}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 18y \frac{dy}{dx}=-32x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{dy}{dx}= -\frac{16}{9}.\frac xy{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Slope of tangent at point P(2, 3) is {tex}= \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{(2, 3)}= -\frac{16}{9}\times \frac 23=\frac{-32}{27}{/tex} Slope of normal at point P(2, 3) is {tex}= \frac{-1}{ \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{(2, 3)} } =\frac{27}{32}{/tex} Now, equation of tangent at point (2, 3) is {tex}( y - 3 ) = \frac { - 32 } { 27 } ( x - 2 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow27 y - 81 = - 32 x + 64{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 32 x + 27 y = 145{/tex} equation of normal at point (2, 3) is {tex}( y - 3 ) = \frac { 27 } { 32 } ( x - 2 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 32 y - 96 = 27 x - 54{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 27 x - 32 y = - 42{/tex}
solution: According to the question, equation of curve is 16x 2 + 9y 2 = 145.......(i) Let P(x 1 , y 1 ) be a point on the curve. Given, x 1 = 2 and y 1 > 0. Substitute Point 'P' in the given curve. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 16(2) 2 + 9(y 1 ) 2 = 145 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9(y 1 ) 2 = 145 - 64 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9(y 1 ) 2 = 81 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y 1 ) 2 ​​​​​​​ = 9 {tex}\Rightarrow y _1= \pm 3{/tex} But, y 1 > 0 {tex}\therefore y_1 = 3 {/tex} Hence, P(x 1 , y 1 ) = (2, 3) Now, Differentiate eq.(i) w.r.t x, we get {tex}32x + 18y \frac{dy}{dx}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 18y \frac{dy}{dx}=-32x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{dy}{dx}= -\frac{16}{9}.\frac xy{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Slope of tangent at point P(2, 3) is {tex}= \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{(2, 3)}= -\frac{16}{9}\times \frac 23=\frac{-32}{27}{/tex} Slope of normal at point P(2, 3) is {tex}= \frac{-1}{ \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{(2, 3)} } =\frac{27}{32}{/tex} Now, equation of tangent at point (2, 3) is {tex}( y - 3 ) = \frac { - 32 } { 27 } ( x - 2 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow27 y - 81 = - 32 x + 64{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 32 x + 27 y = 145{/tex} equation of normal at point (2, 3) is {tex}( y - 3 ) = \frac { 27 } { 32 } ( x - 2 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 32 y - 96 = 27 x - 54{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 27 x - 32 y = - 42{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, equation of curve is 16x2 + 9y2 = 145.......(i)
Let P(x1, y1) be a point on the curve.
Given, x1 = 2 and y1 > 0.
Substitute Point 'P' in the given curve.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}16(2)2 + 9(y1)2 = 145
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9(y1)2 = 145 - 64
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9(y1)2 = 81
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (y1)2​​​​​​​ = 9
{tex}\Rightarrow y _1= \pm 3{/tex}
But, y1 > 0
{tex}\therefore y_1 = 3 {/tex}
Hence, P(x1, y1) = (2, 3)
Now, Differentiate eq.(i) w.r.t x, we get
 {tex}32x + 18y \frac{dy}{dx}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 18y \frac{dy}{dx}=-32x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{dy}{dx}= -\frac{16}{9}.\frac xy{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Slope of tangent at point P(2, 3) is 
{tex}= \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{(2, 3)}= -\frac{16}{9}\times \frac 23=\frac{-32}{27}{/tex}
Slope of normal at point P(2, 3) is
{tex}= \frac{-1}{ \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)_{(2, 3)} } =\frac{27}{32}{/tex}
Now, equation of tangent at point (2, 3) is
{tex}( y - 3 ) = \frac { - 32 } { 27 } ( x - 2 ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow27 y - 81 = - 32 x + 64{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 32 x + 27 y = 145{/tex}
equation of normal at point (2, 3) is
{tex}( y - 3 ) = \frac { 27 } { 32 } ( x - 2 ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 32 y - 96 = 27 x - 54{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 27 x - 32 y = - 42{/tex}

question_html:
Find the shortest distance between the lines 
{tex}\vec{r}=(4 \hat{i}-\hat{j})+\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})+\mu(2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}){/tex}
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the lines {tex}\vec{r}=(4 \hat{i}-\hat{j})+\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})+\mu(2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}){/tex}
explanation: We know that the shortest distance between the lines {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{1}}+\lambda \vec{b_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{2}}+\mu \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} is given by d = {tex}| \frac{(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \cdot(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}})}{|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|}|{/tex} Now we will Comparing the given equations with the equations {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{1}}+\lambda \vec{b_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{2}}+\mu \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} respectively, we get {tex}\vec{a_{1}}=4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{a_{2}}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{b_{1}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b_{2}}=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}-3 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{rrr} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 4 & -5 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+0 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}(\vec{a_{2}}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}) \cdot(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}){/tex} = {tex}(-3 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+2 \hat k) \cdot(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+0 \hat{k}){/tex} = -6 + 0 + 0 = -6 and, {tex}|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|=\sqrt{4+1+0}=\sqrt{5}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Shortest distance = {tex}\left|\frac{(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \cdot(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}})}{|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|}\right| {/tex} = {tex}\left|\frac{-6}{\sqrt{5}}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6}{\sqrt{5}}{/tex}
solution: We know that the shortest distance between the lines {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{1}}+\lambda \vec{b_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{2}}+\mu \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} is given by d = {tex}| \frac{(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \cdot(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}})}{|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|}|{/tex} Now we will Comparing the given equations with the equations {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{1}}+\lambda \vec{b_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{2}}+\mu \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} respectively, we get {tex}\vec{a_{1}}=4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{a_{2}}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} , {tex}\vec{b_{1}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b_{2}}=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}-3 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{rrr} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 4 & -5 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+0 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}(\vec{a_{2}}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}) \cdot(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}){/tex} = {tex}(-3 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+2 \hat k) \cdot(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+0 \hat{k}){/tex} = -6 + 0 + 0 = -6 and, {tex}|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|=\sqrt{4+1+0}=\sqrt{5}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Shortest distance = {tex}\left|\frac{(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \cdot(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}})}{|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|}\right| {/tex} = {tex}\left|\frac{-6}{\sqrt{5}}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6}{\sqrt{5}}{/tex}
solution_html:
We know that the shortest distance between the lines {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{1}}+\lambda \vec{b_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{2}}+\mu \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} is given by
d = {tex}| \frac{(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \cdot(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}})}{|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|}|{/tex} 
Now we will Comparing the given equations with the equations {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{1}}+\lambda \vec{b_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{2}}+\mu \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} respectively, we get
{tex}\vec{a_{1}}=4 \hat{i}-\hat{j}{/tex}, {tex}\vec{a_{2}}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}, {tex}\vec{b_{1}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b_{2}}=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}-3 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} {/tex} and 
{tex}\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{rrr} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 4 & -5 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+0 \hat{k}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}(\vec{a_{2}}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}) \cdot(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}){/tex} = {tex}(-3 \hat{i}+0 \hat{j}+2 \hat k) \cdot(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+0 \hat{k}){/tex} = -6 + 0 + 0 = -6
and, {tex}|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|=\sqrt{4+1+0}=\sqrt{5}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Shortest distance  = {tex}\left|\frac{(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \cdot(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}})}{|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|}\right| {/tex} = {tex}\left|\frac{-6}{\sqrt{5}}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6}{\sqrt{5}}{/tex}

question_html: Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex}, so that the lines {tex} l_1 : \frac { 1 - x } { 3 } = \frac { 7 y - 14 } { \lambda } = \frac { z - 3 } { 2 }{/tex} and {tex}{l_2}:\frac{{7 - 7x}}{{3\lambda}} = \frac{{y - 5}}{1} = \frac{{6 - z}}{5}{/tex} are perpendicular to each other. Also, find the equation of a line passing through a point (3,2,-4) and parallel to line l1.
question_text: Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} , so that the lines {tex} l_1 : \frac { 1 - x } { 3 } = \frac { 7 y - 14 } { \lambda } = \frac { z - 3 } { 2 }{/tex} and {tex}{l_2}:\frac{{7 - 7x}}{{3\lambda}} = \frac{{y - 5}}{1} = \frac{{6 - z}}{5}{/tex} are perpendicular to each other. Also, find the equation of a line passing through a point (3,2,-4) and parallel to line l 1 .
explanation: Firstly, we write the given equations of lines in standard form to determine the direction ratios of both lines by using the condition {tex} a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } = 0{/tex} to get the value of p. Further, we use the formula {tex} \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a _ { 1 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b _ { 1 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c _ { 1 } }{/tex} Now, equation of the given lines can be written in standard form as: {tex} l _ { 1 } : \frac { x - 1 } { - 3 } = \frac { y - 2 } { \lambda / 7 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 2 }{/tex} and {tex} l_2: \frac { x - 1 } { - 3 \lambda / 7 } = \frac { y - 5 } { 1 } = \frac { z - 6 } { - 5 }{/tex} Direction ratios of these lines are {tex} \left( - 3 , \frac { \lambda } { 7 } , 2 \right){/tex} and {tex} \left( - \frac { 3\lambda} { 7 } { } - 1 , - 5 \right){/tex} respectively. We know that, two lines of direction ratios (a 1 , b 2 , c 2 ) and (a 2 , b 2, c 2 ) are perpendicular to each other, if a 1 a 2 + b 1 b 2 + c 1 c 2 = 0 {tex} \therefore \quad ( - 3 ) \left( - \frac { 3 \lambda } { 7 } \right) + \left( \frac { \lambda } { 7 } \right) ( 1 ) + ( 2 ) ( - 9 ) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { \underline { 9\lambda } } { 7 } + \frac { \lambda } { 7 } - 10 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 10 \lambda } { 7 } = 10{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 7{/tex} Hence, the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} is 7. Aso, we know that, the equation of a line which passes through the point (x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ) with direction ratios a, b, c is given by {tex} \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c }{/tex} Since, requied line is parallel to line l 1 . a = -3, {tex}b = \frac { 7 } { 7 }{/tex} and c = 2 Therefore, equation of line passing through the point (3,2,-4) and having direction ratios (-3,1,2) is {tex} \frac { x - 3 } { - 3 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 1 } = \frac { z + 4 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \frac { 3 - x } { 3 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 1 } = \frac { z + 4 } { 2 }{/tex}
solution: Firstly, we write the given equations of lines in standard form to determine the direction ratios of both lines by using the condition {tex} a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } = 0{/tex} to get the value of p. Further, we use the formula {tex} \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a _ { 1 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b _ { 1 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c _ { 1 } }{/tex} Now, equation of the given lines can be written in standard form as: {tex} l _ { 1 } : \frac { x - 1 } { - 3 } = \frac { y - 2 } { \lambda / 7 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 2 }{/tex} and {tex} l_2: \frac { x - 1 } { - 3 \lambda / 7 } = \frac { y - 5 } { 1 } = \frac { z - 6 } { - 5 }{/tex} Direction ratios of these lines are {tex} \left( - 3 , \frac { \lambda } { 7 } , 2 \right){/tex} and {tex} \left( - \frac { 3\lambda} { 7 } { } - 1 , - 5 \right){/tex} respectively. We know that, two lines of direction ratios (a 1 , b 2 , c 2 ) and (a 2 , b 2, c 2 ) are perpendicular to each other, if a 1 a 2 + b 1 b 2 + c 1 c 2 = 0 {tex} \therefore \quad ( - 3 ) \left( - \frac { 3 \lambda } { 7 } \right) + \left( \frac { \lambda } { 7 } \right) ( 1 ) + ( 2 ) ( - 9 ) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { \underline { 9\lambda } } { 7 } + \frac { \lambda } { 7 } - 10 = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 10 \lambda } { 7 } = 10{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 7{/tex} Hence, the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} is 7. Aso, we know that, the equation of a line which passes through the point (x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ) with direction ratios a, b, c is given by {tex} \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c }{/tex} Since, requied line is parallel to line l 1 . a = -3, {tex}b = \frac { 7 } { 7 }{/tex} and c = 2 Therefore, equation of line passing through the point (3,2,-4) and having direction ratios (-3,1,2) is {tex} \frac { x - 3 } { - 3 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 1 } = \frac { z + 4 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \frac { 3 - x } { 3 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 1 } = \frac { z + 4 } { 2 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Firstly, we  write the given equations of lines in standard form to  determine the direction ratios of both lines by  using the condition
{tex} a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } = 0{/tex} to get the value of p.
Further,  we use the formula {tex} \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a _ { 1 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b _ { 1 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c _ { 1 } }{/tex}  
Now, equation of the given lines can be written in standard form as:
{tex} l _ { 1 } : \frac { x - 1 } { - 3 } = \frac { y - 2 } { \lambda / 7 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 2 }{/tex}
and {tex} l_2: \frac { x - 1 } { - 3 \lambda / 7 } = \frac { y - 5 } { 1 } = \frac { z - 6 } { - 5 }{/tex}
Direction ratios of these lines are {tex} \left( - 3 , \frac { \lambda } { 7 } , 2 \right){/tex}and {tex} \left( - \frac { 3\lambda} { 7 } { } - 1 , - 5 \right){/tex}respectively.
We know that, two lines of direction ratios (a1, b2, c2) and (a2, b2, c2) are perpendicular to each other, if
a1a2 + b1b2 + c1c2 = 0
{tex} \therefore \quad ( - 3 ) \left( - \frac { 3 \lambda } { 7 } \right) + \left( \frac { \lambda } { 7 } \right) ( 1 ) + ( 2 ) ( - 9 ) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { \underline { 9\lambda } } { 7 } + \frac { \lambda } { 7 } - 10 = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 10 \lambda } { 7 } = 10{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 7{/tex}
Hence, the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} is 7.
Aso, we know that, the equation of a line which passes through the point (x1, y1, z1) with direction ratios a, b, c is given by
{tex} \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c }{/tex}
Since, requied line is parallel to line l1 .
a = -3, {tex}b = \frac { 7 } { 7 }{/tex} and c = 2
Therefore, equation of line passing through the point (3,2,-4) and having direction ratios (-3,1,2) is
{tex} \frac { x - 3 } { - 3 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 1 } = \frac { z + 4 } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad \frac { 3 - x } { 3 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 1 } = \frac { z + 4 } { 2 }{/tex}

question_html: If a line makes angles 90° and 60° respectively with the positive directions of X and Y-axes, find the angle which it makes with the positive direction of Z-axis.
question_text: If a line makes angles 90° and 60° respectively with the positive directions of X and Y-axes, find the angle which it makes with the positive direction of Z-axis.
explanation: We need to find the angle which the given line makes with the positive direction of Z-axis.Given that the line makes angles {tex}a{/tex} , {tex}\beta{/tex} ( and {tex}\gamma{/tex} with the X-axis, Y-axis and Z-axis, respectively. {tex}\therefore \quad \cos ^2 \alpha + \cos ^2 \beta + \cos^ 2 \gamma = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos ^290 ^ { \circ } + \cos^ 2 \left (6 0 )^ { \circ } + \cos ^ { 2 } \gamma = 1\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 0 + \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \cos ^ { 2 } \gamma = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 4 } + \cos ^ { 2 } \gamma = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos ^ { 2 } \gamma = 1 - \frac { 1 } { 4 } = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \gamma = \pm \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \gamma = 3 0 °{/tex} [ as angle is to be measured with positive direction of Z-axis]
solution: We need to find the angle which the given line makes with the positive direction of Z-axis.Given that the line makes angles {tex}a{/tex} , {tex}\beta{/tex} ( and {tex}\gamma{/tex} with the X-axis, Y-axis and Z-axis, respectively. {tex}\therefore \quad \cos ^2 \alpha + \cos ^2 \beta + \cos^ 2 \gamma = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos ^290 ^ { \circ } + \cos^ 2 \left (6 0 )^ { \circ } + \cos ^ { 2 } \gamma = 1\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 0 + \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \cos ^ { 2 } \gamma = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 4 } + \cos ^ { 2 } \gamma = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos ^ { 2 } \gamma = 1 - \frac { 1 } { 4 } = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \gamma = \pm \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \gamma = 3 0 °{/tex} [ as angle is to be measured with positive direction of Z-axis]
solution_html:
We need to find the angle which the given line makes with the positive direction of Z-axis.Given that  the  line makes angles {tex}a{/tex} ,{tex}\beta{/tex} ( and{tex}\gamma{/tex} with the X-axis, Y-axis and Z-axis, respectively.
{tex}\therefore \quad \cos ^2 \alpha + \cos ^2 \beta + \cos^ 2 \gamma = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos ^290 ^ { \circ } + \cos^ 2 \left (6 0 )^ { \circ } + \cos ^ { 2 } \gamma = 1\right.{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 0 + \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \cos ^ { 2 } \gamma = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 4 } + \cos ^ { 2 } \gamma = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos ^ { 2 } \gamma = 1 - \frac { 1 } { 4 } = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \gamma = \pm \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \gamma = 3 0 °{/tex}[ as angle is to be measured with positive direction of Z-axis]

question_html: Find the vector equation of the line passing through the point A (1, 2, -1) and parallel to the line 5x - 25 = 14 -7y = 35 z.
question_text: Find the vector equation of the line passing through the point A (1, 2, -1) and parallel to the line 5x - 25 = 14 -7y = 35 z.
explanation: According to the question, Line is {tex}5x - 25 = 14 -7y = 35z.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { x - 5 } { 1 / 5 } = \frac { 2 - y } { 1 / 7 } = \frac { z } { 1 / 35 } \Rightarrow \frac { x - 5 } { 1 / 5 } = \frac { y - 2 } { - 1 / 7 } = \frac { z } { 1 / 35 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Direction ratio of the given line = {tex} \frac { 1 } { 5 } , - \frac { 1 } { 7 } , \frac { 1 } { 35 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Direction ratio of a line parallel to the given line= {tex}\frac { 1 } { 5 } , - \frac { 1 } { 7 } , \frac { 1 } { 35 }{/tex} :. The required equation of a line passing through the point {tex}A(1, 2,-1){/tex} and parallel to the given line is {tex}\frac { x - 1 } { 1 / 5 } = \frac { y - 2 } { - 1 / 7 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 1 / 35 }{/tex}
solution: According to the question, Line is {tex}5x - 25 = 14 -7y = 35z.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { x - 5 } { 1 / 5 } = \frac { 2 - y } { 1 / 7 } = \frac { z } { 1 / 35 } \Rightarrow \frac { x - 5 } { 1 / 5 } = \frac { y - 2 } { - 1 / 7 } = \frac { z } { 1 / 35 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Direction ratio of the given line = {tex} \frac { 1 } { 5 } , - \frac { 1 } { 7 } , \frac { 1 } { 35 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Direction ratio of a line parallel to the given line= {tex}\frac { 1 } { 5 } , - \frac { 1 } { 7 } , \frac { 1 } { 35 }{/tex} :. The required equation of a line passing through the point {tex}A(1, 2,-1){/tex} and parallel to the given line is {tex}\frac { x - 1 } { 1 / 5 } = \frac { y - 2 } { - 1 / 7 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 1 / 35 }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,

Line is {tex}5x - 25 = 14 -7y = 35z.{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { x - 5 } { 1 / 5 } = \frac { 2 - y } { 1 / 7 } = \frac { z } { 1 / 35 } \Rightarrow \frac { x - 5 } { 1 / 5 } = \frac { y - 2 } { - 1 / 7 } = \frac { z } { 1 / 35 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Direction ratio of the given line =  {tex} \frac { 1 } { 5 } , - \frac { 1 } { 7 } , \frac { 1 } { 35 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Direction ratio of a line parallel to the given line= {tex}\frac { 1 } { 5 } , - \frac { 1 } { 7 } , \frac { 1 } { 35 }{/tex}
:. The required equation of a line passing through the point {tex}A(1, 2,-1){/tex} and parallel to the given line is
{tex}\frac { x - 1 } { 1 / 5 } = \frac { y - 2 } { - 1 / 7 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 1 / 35 }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If the points with position vectors {tex}10 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}, 12 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda \hat{i}+11 \hat{j}{/tex} are collinear, find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex}.
question_text: If the points with position vectors {tex}10 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}, 12 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda \hat{i}+11 \hat{j}{/tex} are collinear, find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} .
explanation: Let A be {tex}10 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j},{/tex} B be {tex}12 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}{/tex} , C be {tex}\lambda \hat{i}+11 \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} {tex}=2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} {tex}=(\lambda-10) \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}{/tex} As {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} are collinear {tex}\frac{2}{\lambda-10}=\frac{-8}{8}{/tex} So, {tex}\lambda=8{/tex}
solution: Let A be {tex}10 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j},{/tex} B be {tex}12 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}{/tex} , C be {tex}\lambda \hat{i}+11 \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} {tex}=2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} {tex}=(\lambda-10) \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}{/tex} As {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} are collinear {tex}\frac{2}{\lambda-10}=\frac{-8}{8}{/tex} So, {tex}\lambda=8{/tex}
solution_html:
Let A be {tex}10 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j},{/tex} B be {tex}12 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}{/tex}, C be {tex}\lambda \hat{i}+11 \hat{j}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} {tex}=2 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{A C}{/tex}{tex}=(\lambda-10) \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}{/tex}
As {tex}\overrightarrow{A B}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{A C}{/tex} are collinear
{tex}\frac{2}{\lambda-10}=\frac{-8}{8}{/tex}
So, {tex}\lambda=8{/tex}

question_html: Find the vector and cartesian equations of the line through the point (1, 2, - 4) and perpendicular to the two lines {tex} \vec { r } = ( 8 \hat { i } - 19 \hat { j } + 10 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 3 \hat { i } - 16 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ){/tex} and {tex} \vec { r } = ( 15 \hat { i } + 29 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k } ) + \mu ( 3 \hat { i } + 8 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } ){/tex}.
question_text: Find the vector and cartesian equations of the line through the point (1, 2, - 4) and perpendicular to the two lines {tex} \vec { r } = ( 8 \hat { i } - 19 \hat { j } + 10 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 3 \hat { i } - 16 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ){/tex} and {tex} \vec { r } = ( 15 \hat { i } + 29 \hat { j } + 5 \hat { k } ) + \mu ( 3 \hat { i } + 8 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } ){/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, the equations of lines are {tex} \vec { r } = ( 8 \hat { i } - 19 \hat { j} + 10 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 3 \hat { i} - 16 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex} \vec { r } = ( 15 \hat { i } + 29 \hat { j} + 5 \hat { k } ) + \mu ( 3 \hat {i} + 8 \hat { j} - 5 \hat { k } ){/tex} Comparing with vector form of equation of line {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } ,{/tex} we get {tex}\Rightarrow \vec { b _ { 1 } } = 3 \hat { i } - 16 \hat { j} + 7 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec { b _ { 2 } } = 3 \hat { i} + 8 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex} \vec { b } = \vec { b _ { 1 } } \times \vec { b _ { 2 } } = \left| \begin{array} { r r r } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 3 } & { - 16 } & { 7 } \\ { 3 } & { 8 } & { - 5 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \hat { i } ( 80 - 56 ) - \hat { j } ( - 15 - 21 ) + \hat { k } ( 24 + 48 ){/tex} {tex} = 24 \hat { i } + 36 \hat { j } + 72 \hat { k } = 12(2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ) {/tex} The required line is perpendicular to the given lines. So, it is parallel to {tex}\vec { b } _ { 1 } \times \vec { b } _ { 2 }{/tex} Now, the equation of a line passing through the point {tex}(1, 2, -4){/tex} and parallel to {tex}24 \hat { i } + 36 \hat {j} + 72 \hat { k }{/tex} or {tex}( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} is {tex}\vec { r } = ( \hat i + 2\hat { j} - 4 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 2 \hat {j} + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} which is required vector equation of a line. For cartesian equation, put {tex}\vec { r } = x \hat { i } +y \hat { j } + z \hat { k } ,{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow { x } \hat{i}+ { y }\hat {j}+ z \hat { k } = ( 1 + 2 \lambda ) \hat { i } + ( 2 + 3 \lambda ) \hat { j }{/tex} {tex}+ ( - 4 + 6 \lambda ) \hat { k }{/tex} Comparing the coefficients of {tex}\hat i{/tex} , {tex}\hat j{/tex} and {tex}\hat k{/tex} , {tex}\Rightarrow x = 1 + 2 \lambda , y = 2 + 3 \lambda \text { and } z = - 4 + 6 \lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - 1 } { 2 } = \lambda , \frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \lambda \text { and } \frac { z + 4 } { 6 } = \lambda{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { x - 1 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \frac { z + 4 } { 6 }{/tex} which is the required cartesian equation of a line.
solution: According to the question, the equations of lines are {tex} \vec { r } = ( 8 \hat { i } - 19 \hat { j} + 10 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 3 \hat { i} - 16 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex} \vec { r } = ( 15 \hat { i } + 29 \hat { j} + 5 \hat { k } ) + \mu ( 3 \hat {i} + 8 \hat { j} - 5 \hat { k } ){/tex} Comparing with vector form of equation of line {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } ,{/tex} we get {tex}\Rightarrow \vec { b _ { 1 } } = 3 \hat { i } - 16 \hat { j} + 7 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec { b _ { 2 } } = 3 \hat { i} + 8 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex} {tex} \vec { b } = \vec { b _ { 1 } } \times \vec { b _ { 2 } } = \left| \begin{array} { r r r } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 3 } & { - 16 } & { 7 } \\ { 3 } & { 8 } & { - 5 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \hat { i } ( 80 - 56 ) - \hat { j } ( - 15 - 21 ) + \hat { k } ( 24 + 48 ){/tex} {tex} = 24 \hat { i } + 36 \hat { j } + 72 \hat { k } = 12(2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ) {/tex} The required line is perpendicular to the given lines. So, it is parallel to {tex}\vec { b } _ { 1 } \times \vec { b } _ { 2 }{/tex} Now, the equation of a line passing through the point {tex}(1, 2, -4){/tex} and parallel to {tex}24 \hat { i } + 36 \hat {j} + 72 \hat { k }{/tex} or {tex}( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} is {tex}\vec { r } = ( \hat i + 2\hat { j} - 4 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 2 \hat {j} + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} which is required vector equation of a line. For cartesian equation, put {tex}\vec { r } = x \hat { i } +y \hat { j } + z \hat { k } ,{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow { x } \hat{i}+ { y }\hat {j}+ z \hat { k } = ( 1 + 2 \lambda ) \hat { i } + ( 2 + 3 \lambda ) \hat { j }{/tex} {tex}+ ( - 4 + 6 \lambda ) \hat { k }{/tex} Comparing the coefficients of {tex}\hat i{/tex} , {tex}\hat j{/tex} and {tex}\hat k{/tex} , {tex}\Rightarrow x = 1 + 2 \lambda , y = 2 + 3 \lambda \text { and } z = - 4 + 6 \lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - 1 } { 2 } = \lambda , \frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \lambda \text { and } \frac { z + 4 } { 6 } = \lambda{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { x - 1 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \frac { z + 4 } { 6 }{/tex} which is the required cartesian equation of a line.
solution_html:
According to the question, the equations of lines are
{tex} \vec { r } = ( 8 \hat { i } - 19 \hat { j} + 10 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 3 \hat { i} - 16 \hat { j } + 7 \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex} \vec { r } = ( 15 \hat { i } + 29 \hat { j} + 5 \hat { k } ) + \mu ( 3 \hat {i} + 8 \hat { j} - 5 \hat { k } ){/tex}
Comparing with vector form of equation of line {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } ,{/tex} we get
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec { b _ { 1 } } = 3 \hat { i } - 16 \hat { j} + 7 \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec { b _ { 2 } } = 3 \hat { i} + 8 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex} \vec { b } = \vec { b _ { 1 } } \times \vec { b _ { 2 } } = \left| \begin{array} { r r r } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 3 } & { - 16 } & { 7 } \\ { 3 } & { 8 } & { - 5 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \hat { i } ( 80 - 56 ) - \hat { j } ( - 15 - 21 ) + \hat { k } ( 24 + 48 ){/tex}
{tex} = 24 \hat { i } + 36 \hat { j } + 72 \hat { k } = 12(2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ) {/tex}
The required line is perpendicular to the given lines.
So, it is parallel to {tex}\vec { b } _ { 1 } \times \vec { b } _ { 2 }{/tex} Now, the equation of a line passing through the point {tex}(1, 2, -4){/tex} and parallel to  {tex}24 \hat { i } + 36 \hat {j} + 72 \hat { k }{/tex} or {tex}( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} is
{tex}\vec { r } = ( \hat i + 2\hat { j} - 4 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 2 \hat {j} + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex}
which is required vector equation of a line.
For cartesian equation, put {tex}\vec { r } = x \hat { i } +y \hat { j } + z \hat { k } ,{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow { x } \hat{i}+ { y }\hat {j}+ z \hat { k } = ( 1 + 2 \lambda ) \hat { i } + ( 2 + 3 \lambda ) \hat { j }{/tex}{tex}+ ( - 4 + 6 \lambda ) \hat { k }{/tex}
Comparing the coefficients of {tex}\hat i{/tex}, {tex}\hat j{/tex} and {tex}\hat k{/tex}, 
{tex}\Rightarrow x = 1 + 2 \lambda , y = 2 + 3 \lambda \text { and } z = - 4 + 6 \lambda{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - 1 } { 2 } = \lambda , \frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \lambda \text { and } \frac { z + 4 } { 6 } = \lambda{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { x - 1 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \frac { z + 4 } { 6 }{/tex}
which is the required cartesian equation of a line.

question_text: A line passes through the point with position vector {tex}2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} and makes angles {tex}60^{\circ}, 120^{\circ}{/tex} , and {tex}45^{\circ}{/tex} with X, Y and Z-axis respectively. Find the equation of the line in the Cartesian form.
explanation: D-Cosines of line are {tex}\frac{1}{2},-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Equation of line is: {tex}\frac{x-2}{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{y+3}{\frac{-1}{2}}=\frac{z-4}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}{/tex} or 2x - 4 = -2y - 6 = {tex}\sqrt{2}(z-4){/tex}
solution: D-Cosines of line are {tex}\frac{1}{2},-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Equation of line is: {tex}\frac{x-2}{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{y+3}{\frac{-1}{2}}=\frac{z-4}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}{/tex} or 2x - 4 = -2y - 6 = {tex}\sqrt{2}(z-4){/tex}
solution_html:
D-Cosines of line are {tex}\frac{1}{2},-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
Equation of line is:
{tex}\frac{x-2}{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{y+3}{\frac{-1}{2}}=\frac{z-4}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}{/tex}
or 2x - 4 = -2y - 6 = {tex}\sqrt{2}(z-4){/tex}

question_html:
Find the shortest distance between the lines

{tex}\vec r = \left( {\hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex}

{tex}\vec r = \left( {2\hat i - \hat j - \hat k} \right) + \mu \left( {2\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex}
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the lines {tex}\vec r = \left( {\hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {\hat i - \hat j + \hat k} \right){/tex} {tex}\vec r = \left( {2\hat i - \hat j - \hat k} \right) + \mu \left( {2\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex}
explanation: {tex}{\vec a_1} = \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k,{\vec b_1} = \hat i - \hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} = 2\hat i - \hat j - \hat k,{\vec b_2} = 2\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}d = \left| {\frac{{\left( {{{\vec a}_2} - {{\vec a}_1}} \right).\left( {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right)}}{{\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} - \vec a{_1} = \hat i - 3\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} \times {\vec b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&{ - 1}&1 \\ 2&1&2 \end{array}} \right|\\ {/tex} {tex}=\hat i(-2-1)-\hat j(2-2)+\hat k(1+2){/tex} {tex} = - 3\hat i + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}d = \left| {\frac{{\left( {\hat i - 3\hat j - 2\hat k} \right).\left( { - 3\hat i + 3\hat k} \right)}}{{\left| { - 3\hat i + 3\hat k} \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left| {\frac{{ - 3 - 6}}{{\sqrt {9 + 9} }}} \right| = \left| {\frac{9}{{3\sqrt 2 }}} \right| = \frac{3}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex}
solution: {tex}{\vec a_1} = \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k,{\vec b_1} = \hat i - \hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} = 2\hat i - \hat j - \hat k,{\vec b_2} = 2\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}d = \left| {\frac{{\left( {{{\vec a}_2} - {{\vec a}_1}} \right).\left( {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right)}}{{\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} - \vec a{_1} = \hat i - 3\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} \times {\vec b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&{ - 1}&1 \\ 2&1&2 \end{array}} \right|\\ {/tex} {tex}=\hat i(-2-1)-\hat j(2-2)+\hat k(1+2){/tex} {tex} = - 3\hat i + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}d = \left| {\frac{{\left( {\hat i - 3\hat j - 2\hat k} \right).\left( { - 3\hat i + 3\hat k} \right)}}{{\left| { - 3\hat i + 3\hat k} \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left| {\frac{{ - 3 - 6}}{{\sqrt {9 + 9} }}} \right| = \left| {\frac{9}{{3\sqrt 2 }}} \right| = \frac{3}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{\vec a_1} = \hat i + 2\hat j + \hat k,{\vec b_1} = \hat i - \hat j + \hat k{/tex}

{tex}{\vec a_2} = 2\hat i - \hat j - \hat k,{\vec b_2} = 2\hat i + \hat j + 2\hat k{/tex}

{tex}d = \left| {\frac{{\left( {{{\vec a}_2} - {{\vec a}_1}} \right).\left( {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right)}}{{\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right|}}} \right|{/tex}

{tex}{\vec a_2} - \vec a{_1} = \hat i - 3\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex}

{tex}{\vec b_1} \times {\vec b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&{ - 1}&1 \\ 2&1&2 \end{array}} \right|\\ {/tex}

{tex}=\hat i(-2-1)-\hat j(2-2)+\hat k(1+2){/tex}

{tex} = - 3\hat i + 3\hat k{/tex}

{tex}d = \left| {\frac{{\left( {\hat i - 3\hat j - 2\hat k} \right).\left( { - 3\hat i + 3\hat k} \right)}}{{\left| { - 3\hat i + 3\hat k} \right|}}} \right|{/tex}

{tex} = \left| {\frac{{ - 3 - 6}}{{\sqrt {9 + 9} }}} \right| = \left| {\frac{9}{{3\sqrt 2 }}} \right| = \frac{3}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the shortest distance between the lines whose vector equations are
{tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})+\mu(4 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}){/tex}
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the lines whose vector equations are {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})+\mu(4 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}){/tex}
explanation: Given that The vector equations of given lines are {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} .....(i) and {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})+2 \mu(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} .....(ii) Equation (ii) can re-written as {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})+\mu^{\prime}(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} .....(iii) where {tex}\mu^{\prime}=2 \mu{/tex} These two lines passes through the points having position vectors {tex}\vec{a_{1}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a_{2}}=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively and both are parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} . So, the shortest distance between them is given by Shortest Distance = {tex}\frac{|(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \times \vec{b}|}{|\vec{b}|}{/tex} ....(iv) Now, {tex}(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \times \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \times(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}2 \hat{i}-0 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}|(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \times \vec{b}|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+0+1}=\sqrt{5}{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{4+9+16}=\sqrt{29}{/tex} Substituting the values of {tex}|(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \times \vec{b}|{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{b}|{/tex} in (iv), we obtain Shortest distance = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}}{\sqrt{29}}{/tex}
solution: Given that The vector equations of given lines are {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} .....(i) and {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})+2 \mu(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} .....(ii) Equation (ii) can re-written as {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})+\mu^{\prime}(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} .....(iii) where {tex}\mu^{\prime}=2 \mu{/tex} These two lines passes through the points having position vectors {tex}\vec{a_{1}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a_{2}}=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively and both are parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} . So, the shortest distance between them is given by Shortest Distance = {tex}\frac{|(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \times \vec{b}|}{|\vec{b}|}{/tex} ....(iv) Now, {tex}(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \times \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \times(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}2 \hat{i}-0 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}|(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \times \vec{b}|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+0+1}=\sqrt{5}{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{4+9+16}=\sqrt{29}{/tex} Substituting the values of {tex}|(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \times \vec{b}|{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{b}|{/tex} in (iv), we obtain Shortest distance = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}}{\sqrt{29}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that The vector equations of given lines are
{tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex}  .....(i)
and {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})+2 \mu(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} .....(ii)
Equation (ii) can re-written as
{tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k})+\mu^{\prime}(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex}  .....(iii)
where {tex}\mu^{\prime}=2 \mu{/tex} 
These two lines passes through the points having position vectors {tex}\vec{a_{1}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a_{2}}=2 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively
and both are parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}. 
So, the shortest distance between them is given by
Shortest Distance = {tex}\frac{|(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \times \vec{b}|}{|\vec{b}|}{/tex}  ....(iv)
Now, {tex}(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \times \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \times(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}){/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}2 \hat{i}-0 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}|(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \times \vec{b}|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{4+0+1}=\sqrt{5}{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{b}|=\sqrt{4+9+16}=\sqrt{29}{/tex} 
Substituting the values of {tex}|(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \times \vec{b}|{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{b}|{/tex} in (iv), we obtain
Shortest distance = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{5}}{\sqrt{29}}{/tex}

question_html: If a line makes angles 90°, 60° and {tex}\theta {/tex} with X, Y and Z-axes respectively, where {tex}\theta {/tex} is acute angle, then find {tex}\theta {/tex}.
question_text: If a line makes angles 90°, 60° and {tex}\theta {/tex} with X, Y and Z-axes respectively, where {tex}\theta {/tex} is acute angle, then find {tex}\theta {/tex} .
explanation: We need to find the value of angle {tex}\theta {/tex} . Now,let I, m and n be the direction cosines of the given line Therefore, we have , {tex}l = \cos 90 ^ { \circ } = 0{/tex} {tex}m = \cos 6 0^ { \circ } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\quad n = \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\because \quad l ^ { 2 } + m^ 2 + n ^ { 2 } = 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore 0 + \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \cos ^ { 2 } \theta = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos^{ 2} \theta = 1 - \frac { 1 } { 4 } = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 }{/tex} [·: cos {tex}\theta {/tex} cannot be negative as {tex}\theta {/tex} is an acute angle] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \cos 3 0°{/tex} {tex}\theta = 30°{/tex}
solution: We need to find the value of angle {tex}\theta {/tex} . Now,let I, m and n be the direction cosines of the given line Therefore, we have , {tex}l = \cos 90 ^ { \circ } = 0{/tex} {tex}m = \cos 6 0^ { \circ } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\quad n = \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\because \quad l ^ { 2 } + m^ 2 + n ^ { 2 } = 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore 0 + \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \cos ^ { 2 } \theta = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos^{ 2} \theta = 1 - \frac { 1 } { 4 } = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 }{/tex} [·: cos {tex}\theta {/tex} cannot be negative as {tex}\theta {/tex} is an acute angle] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \cos 3 0°{/tex} {tex}\theta = 30°{/tex}
solution_html:
We need to find the value of angle  {tex}\theta {/tex}. Now,let I, m and n be the direction cosines of the given line Therefore, we have ,
{tex}l = \cos 90 ^ { \circ } = 0{/tex}
{tex}m = \cos 6 0^ { \circ } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\quad n = \cos \theta{/tex}
{tex}\because \quad l ^ { 2 } + m^ 2 + n ^ { 2 } = 1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore 0 + \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \cos ^ { 2 } \theta = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos^{ 2} \theta = 1 - \frac { 1 } { 4 } = \frac { 3 } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 }{/tex}
[·: cos{tex}\theta {/tex} cannot be negative as {tex}\theta {/tex} is an acute angle]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \cos 3 0°{/tex}
{tex}\theta = 30°{/tex}

question_html: The equation of a line is 5x-3= 15y+7=3-10z. Write the direction cosines of the line.
question_text: The equation of a line is 5x-3= 15y+7=3-10z. Write the direction cosines of the line.
explanation: We need to find the direction cosines of the line. Here, we are given equation of a line in the following form.Now, we have, 5x - 3 = 15y + 7 = 3 - 10z ..... ..................(i) Let us first convert the equation in standard form {tex} \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c }{/tex} ....................... (ii) Let us divide Eq. (i) by LCM (coefficients of x, y and z). i.e. LCM (5, 15, -10) = 30 Now, the Eq. (i) becomes {tex} \frac { 5 x - 3 } { 30 } = \frac { 15 y + 7 } { 3 0 } = \frac { 3 - 10 z } { 3 0}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - \frac { 3 } { 5 } } { 6 } = \frac { y + \frac { 7 } { 15 } } { 2 } = \frac { z - \frac { 3 } { 10 } } { - 3 }{/tex} On comparing the above equation with Eq.(ii), we get 6, 2, -3 are the direction ratios of the given line. Now, the direction cosines of given line are {tex} \frac { 6 } { \sqrt { 6 ^ { 2 } + 2 ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } } } \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 6 ^ { 2 } + 2 ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} and {tex} \frac { - 3 } { \sqrt { 6 ^ { 2 } + 2 ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} i.e, {tex} \left( \frac { 6 } { 7 } , \frac { 2 } { 7 } , \frac { - 3 } { 7 } \right){/tex}
solution: We need to find the direction cosines of the line. Here, we are given equation of a line in the following form.Now, we have, 5x - 3 = 15y + 7 = 3 - 10z ..... ..................(i) Let us first convert the equation in standard form {tex} \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c }{/tex} ....................... (ii) Let us divide Eq. (i) by LCM (coefficients of x, y and z). i.e. LCM (5, 15, -10) = 30 Now, the Eq. (i) becomes {tex} \frac { 5 x - 3 } { 30 } = \frac { 15 y + 7 } { 3 0 } = \frac { 3 - 10 z } { 3 0}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - \frac { 3 } { 5 } } { 6 } = \frac { y + \frac { 7 } { 15 } } { 2 } = \frac { z - \frac { 3 } { 10 } } { - 3 }{/tex} On comparing the above equation with Eq.(ii), we get 6, 2, -3 are the direction ratios of the given line. Now, the direction cosines of given line are {tex} \frac { 6 } { \sqrt { 6 ^ { 2 } + 2 ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } } } \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 6 ^ { 2 } + 2 ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} and {tex} \frac { - 3 } { \sqrt { 6 ^ { 2 } + 2 ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} i.e, {tex} \left( \frac { 6 } { 7 } , \frac { 2 } { 7 } , \frac { - 3 } { 7 } \right){/tex}
solution_html:
We need to find the direction cosines of the line. Here, we are given equation of a line in the following form.Now, we have,
5x - 3 = 15y + 7 = 3 - 10z ..... ..................(i)
Let us first convert the equation in standard form
{tex} \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c }{/tex} ....................... (ii)
Let us divide Eq. (i) by LCM (coefficients of x, y and z). i.e. LCM (5, 15, -10) = 30
Now, the Eq. (i) becomes
{tex} \frac { 5 x - 3 } { 30 } = \frac { 15 y + 7 } { 3 0 } = \frac { 3 - 10 z } { 3 0}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - \frac { 3 } { 5 } } { 6 } = \frac { y + \frac { 7 } { 15 } } { 2 } = \frac { z - \frac { 3 } { 10 } } { - 3 }{/tex}
On comparing the above equation with Eq.(ii), we get 6, 2, -3 are the direction ratios of the given line.
Now, the direction cosines of given line are
{tex} \frac { 6 } { \sqrt { 6 ^ { 2 } + 2 ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } } } \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 6 ^ { 2 } + 2 ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} and {tex} \frac { - 3 } { \sqrt { 6 ^ { 2 } + 2 ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}i.e,{tex} \left( \frac { 6 } { 7 } , \frac { 2 } { 7 } , \frac { - 3 } { 7 } \right){/tex}

question_html:
Find the shortest distance between the following lines : 
{tex}\vec{r}=2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+\hat{k}+\lambda(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} and  {tex}\vec{r}=7 \hat{i}-6 \hat{k}+\mu(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the following lines : {tex}\vec{r}=2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+\hat{k}+\lambda(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=7 \hat{i}-6 \hat{k}+\mu(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}
explanation: Given lines are: {tex}\vec{r}=2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+\hat{k}+\lambda(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=7 \hat{i}-6 \hat{k}+\mu(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} Let L 1 : {tex}\vec{r}=2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+\hat{k}+\lambda(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} and L 2 = {tex}\vec{r}=7 \hat{i}-6 \hat{k}+\mu(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{a_{1}}=2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{b_{1}}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{a_{2}}=7 \hat{i}-6 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{b_{2}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} So, {tex}\overrightarrow{a_{2}}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}=5 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{b_{1}} \times \overrightarrow{b_{2}}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & 2 & 6 \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}-8 \hat{i}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} Therefore, Shortest distance = {tex}\frac{\left|\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}\right) \cdot(\overrightarrow{b_{1}} \times \overrightarrow{b_{2}})\right|}{|\overrightarrow{b_{1}} \times \overrightarrow{b_{2}}|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{|-40-28|}{\sqrt{64+16}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{68}{\sqrt{80}}=\frac{17}{\sqrt{5}}=\frac{17 \sqrt{5}}{5}{/tex}
solution: Given lines are: {tex}\vec{r}=2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+\hat{k}+\lambda(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\vec{r}=7 \hat{i}-6 \hat{k}+\mu(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} Let L 1 : {tex}\vec{r}=2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+\hat{k}+\lambda(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} and L 2 = {tex}\vec{r}=7 \hat{i}-6 \hat{k}+\mu(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{a_{1}}=2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{b_{1}}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{a_{2}}=7 \hat{i}-6 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{b_{2}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} So, {tex}\overrightarrow{a_{2}}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}=5 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{b_{1}} \times \overrightarrow{b_{2}}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & 2 & 6 \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}-8 \hat{i}+4 \hat{k}{/tex} Therefore, Shortest distance = {tex}\frac{\left|\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}\right) \cdot(\overrightarrow{b_{1}} \times \overrightarrow{b_{2}})\right|}{|\overrightarrow{b_{1}} \times \overrightarrow{b_{2}}|}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{|-40-28|}{\sqrt{64+16}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{68}{\sqrt{80}}=\frac{17}{\sqrt{5}}=\frac{17 \sqrt{5}}{5}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given lines are:
{tex}\vec{r}=2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+\hat{k}+\lambda(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{r}=7 \hat{i}-6 \hat{k}+\mu(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}
Let L1 :{tex}\vec{r}=2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+\hat{k}+\lambda(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex}
and L2 = {tex}\vec{r}=7 \hat{i}-6 \hat{k}+\mu(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow{a_{1}}=2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{b_{1}}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex}
and {tex}\overrightarrow{a_{2}}=7 \hat{i}-6 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{b_{2}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
So, {tex}\overrightarrow{a_{2}}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}=5 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-7 \hat{k}{/tex}
and {tex}\overrightarrow{b_{1}} \times \overrightarrow{b_{2}}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 3 & 2 & 6 \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
= {tex}-8 \hat{i}+4 \hat{k}{/tex}
Therefore, Shortest distance = {tex}\frac{\left|\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\overrightarrow{a_{1}}\right) \cdot(\overrightarrow{b_{1}} \times \overrightarrow{b_{2}})\right|}{|\overrightarrow{b_{1}} \times \overrightarrow{b_{2}}|}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{|-40-28|}{\sqrt{64+16}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{68}{\sqrt{80}}=\frac{17}{\sqrt{5}}=\frac{17 \sqrt{5}}{5}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the shortest distance between the lines x + 1 = 2y = -12z and x = y + 2 = 6z - 6
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the lines x + 1 = 2y = -12z and x = y + 2 = 6z - 6
explanation: Given lines are x = y + 2 = 6z - 6 and x + 1 = 2y = 12z these can be written as {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-(-2)}{1}=\frac{z-1}{1 / 6}{/tex} ....(1) and {tex}\frac{x+1}{1}=\frac{y}{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{a}{\frac{-1}{12}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{x+1}{12}= \frac {y}{6}=\frac{z}{-1}{/tex} .... (2) D, ratios of the lines are {tex}\left|\begin{array}{rr} 6 & 1 \\ 6 & -1 \end{array}\right| , \left|\begin{array}{rr} 1 & 6 \\ -1 & 12 \end{array}\right|,\left|\begin{array}{ll} 6 & 6 \\ 12 & 6 \end{array}\right|{/tex} (by cm mehod) i.e. (-12, 18, -36) or (-2, 3, -6) {tex}\therefore{/tex} D ratio of the line of the shortest distance are {tex}\left(-\frac{2}{7}, \frac{3}{7}, \frac{-6}{7}\right)=(l, m, n){/tex} the shortest distance = (x 2 - x 1 ) (l) + (y 2 - y 1 )(m) + (z 2 - z 1 ) (X) = (-1 - 0) {tex}\left(-\frac{2}{7}\right)+(0+2)\left(\frac{3}{7}\right)+(0-1) (\frac {-6}{7}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{7}+\frac{6}{7}+\frac{6}{7}=2 units{/tex}
solution: Given lines are x = y + 2 = 6z - 6 and x + 1 = 2y = 12z these can be written as {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-(-2)}{1}=\frac{z-1}{1 / 6}{/tex} ....(1) and {tex}\frac{x+1}{1}=\frac{y}{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{a}{\frac{-1}{12}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{x+1}{12}= \frac {y}{6}=\frac{z}{-1}{/tex} .... (2) D, ratios of the lines are {tex}\left|\begin{array}{rr} 6 & 1 \\ 6 & -1 \end{array}\right| , \left|\begin{array}{rr} 1 & 6 \\ -1 & 12 \end{array}\right|,\left|\begin{array}{ll} 6 & 6 \\ 12 & 6 \end{array}\right|{/tex} (by cm mehod) i.e. (-12, 18, -36) or (-2, 3, -6) {tex}\therefore{/tex} D ratio of the line of the shortest distance are {tex}\left(-\frac{2}{7}, \frac{3}{7}, \frac{-6}{7}\right)=(l, m, n){/tex} the shortest distance = (x 2 - x 1 ) (l) + (y 2 - y 1 )(m) + (z 2 - z 1 ) (X) = (-1 - 0) {tex}\left(-\frac{2}{7}\right)+(0+2)\left(\frac{3}{7}\right)+(0-1) (\frac {-6}{7}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{7}+\frac{6}{7}+\frac{6}{7}=2 units{/tex}
solution_html:
Given lines are
x = y + 2 = 6z - 6 and
x + 1 = 2y = 12z
these can be written as
{tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-(-2)}{1}=\frac{z-1}{1 / 6}{/tex} ....(1)
and {tex}\frac{x+1}{1}=\frac{y}{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{a}{\frac{-1}{12}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{x+1}{12}= \frac {y}{6}=\frac{z}{-1}{/tex} .... (2)
D, ratios of the lines are
{tex}\left|\begin{array}{rr} 6 & 1 \\ 6 & -1 \end{array}\right| , \left|\begin{array}{rr} 1 & 6 \\ -1 & 12 \end{array}\right|,\left|\begin{array}{ll} 6 & 6 \\ 12 & 6 \end{array}\right|{/tex} (by cm mehod)
i.e. (-12, 18, -36)
or (-2, 3, -6)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} D ratio of the line of the shortest distance are
{tex}\left(-\frac{2}{7}, \frac{3}{7}, \frac{-6}{7}\right)=(l, m, n){/tex}
the shortest distance
= (x2 - x1) (l) + (y2 - y1)(m) + (z2 - z1) (X)
= (-1 - 0) {tex}\left(-\frac{2}{7}\right)+(0+2)\left(\frac{3}{7}\right)+(0-1) (\frac {-6}{7}){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{7}+\frac{6}{7}+\frac{6}{7}=2 units{/tex}

question_html: Show that the lines {tex}\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y-1}{-1}{/tex}, z + 1 = 0 and {tex}\frac{x-4}{2}=\frac{z+1}{3}{/tex}, y = 0 intersect each other. Also find their point of intersection.
question_text: Show that the lines {tex}\frac{x-1}{3}=\frac{y-1}{-1}{/tex} , z + 1 = 0 and {tex}\frac{x-4}{2}=\frac{z+1}{3}{/tex} , y = 0 intersect each other. Also find their point of intersection.
explanation: x - 13 = y - 1 - 1x - 13 = y -1 - 1, z + 1 = 0, z + 1 = 0 ..... (1) Let x - 13 = y - 1 - 1x - 13 = y - 1 - 1 = k = k x = 3k + 1, y = -k + 1x = 3k + 1, y = -k + 1 and z = -1z = -1 x - 42 = z + 13x - 42 = z + 13y = 0, y = 0 .... question_html:
Find the value of k for which the following lines are perpendicular to each other: 
{tex}\frac{x+3}{k-5}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{5-z}{-2 k-1}{/tex}; {tex}\frac{x+2}{-1}=\frac{2-y}{-k}=\frac{z}{5}{/tex} 
Hence find the equation of the plane containing the above lines.
question_text: Find the value of k for which the following lines are perpendicular to each other: {tex}\frac{x+3}{k-5}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{5-z}{-2 k-1}{/tex} ; {tex}\frac{x+2}{-1}=\frac{2-y}{-k}=\frac{z}{5}{/tex} Hence find the equation of the plane containing the above lines.
explanation: Here the equation of the given lines are: {tex}\frac{x+3}{k-5}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{5-z}{2 k-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-(-3)}{k-5}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{z-5}{1-2 k}{/tex} ........ (i) and {tex}\frac{x+2}{-1}=\frac{2-y}{-k}=\frac{z}{5}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-(-2)}{-1}=\frac{y-2}{k}=\frac{z}{5}{/tex} ............ (ii) Comparing (i) and (ii) with standard equations, we get {tex}\frac{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}_{1}}{\mathrm{a}_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{y}_{1}}{\mathrm{b}_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-\mathrm{z}_{1}}{\mathrm{c}_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-x_{2}}{a_{2}}=\frac{y-y_{2}}{b_{2}}=\frac{z-z_{2}}{c_{2}}{/tex} We get: {tex}<a_{1}, b_{1}, c_{1}>\equiv<(k-5), 1,(1-2 k)>{/tex} {tex}<a_{2}, b_{2}, c_{2}>\equiv<-1, k, 5>{/tex} Since the lines (i) and (ii) are {tex}\perp{/tex} to each other, so a 1 a 2 + b 1 b 2 + c 1 c 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow-1(\mathrm{k}-5)+1 \times \mathrm{k}+5(1-2 \mathrm{k})=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -k + 5 + k + 5 - 10k = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -10k + 10 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -10k = -10 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 1
solution: Here the equation of the given lines are: {tex}\frac{x+3}{k-5}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{5-z}{2 k-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-(-3)}{k-5}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{z-5}{1-2 k}{/tex} ........ (i) and {tex}\frac{x+2}{-1}=\frac{2-y}{-k}=\frac{z}{5}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-(-2)}{-1}=\frac{y-2}{k}=\frac{z}{5}{/tex} ............ (ii) Comparing (i) and (ii) with standard equations, we get {tex}\frac{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}_{1}}{\mathrm{a}_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{y}_{1}}{\mathrm{b}_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-\mathrm{z}_{1}}{\mathrm{c}_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-x_{2}}{a_{2}}=\frac{y-y_{2}}{b_{2}}=\frac{z-z_{2}}{c_{2}}{/tex} We get: {tex}<a_{1}, b_{1}, c_{1}>\equiv<(k-5), 1,(1-2 k)>{/tex} {tex}<a_{2}, b_{2}, c_{2}>\equiv<-1, k, 5>{/tex} Since the lines (i) and (ii) are {tex}\perp{/tex} to each other, so a 1 a 2 + b 1 b 2 + c 1 c 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow-1(\mathrm{k}-5)+1 \times \mathrm{k}+5(1-2 \mathrm{k})=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -k + 5 + k + 5 - 10k = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -10k + 10 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -10k = -10 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 1
solution_html:
Here the equation of the given lines are:
{tex}\frac{x+3}{k-5}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{5-z}{2 k-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-(-3)}{k-5}=\frac{y-1}{1}=\frac{z-5}{1-2 k}{/tex} ........ (i)
and {tex}\frac{x+2}{-1}=\frac{2-y}{-k}=\frac{z}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-(-2)}{-1}=\frac{y-2}{k}=\frac{z}{5}{/tex} ............ (ii)
Comparing (i) and (ii) with standard equations, we get
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}_{1}}{\mathrm{a}_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{y}_{1}}{\mathrm{b}_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{z}-\mathrm{z}_{1}}{\mathrm{c}_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-x_{2}}{a_{2}}=\frac{y-y_{2}}{b_{2}}=\frac{z-z_{2}}{c_{2}}{/tex}
We get:
{tex}<a_{1}, b_{1}, c_{1}>\equiv<(k-5), 1,(1-2 k)>{/tex}
{tex}<a_{2}, b_{2}, c_{2}>\equiv<-1, k, 5>{/tex}
Since the lines (i) and (ii) are {tex}\perp{/tex} to each other, so
a1a2 + b1b2 + c1c2 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow-1(\mathrm{k}-5)+1 \times \mathrm{k}+5(1-2 \mathrm{k})=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -k + 5 + k + 5 - 10k = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -10k + 10 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -10k = -10
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 1

question_html:
Find the shortest distance between the lines whose vector equations are 
{tex} \vec { r } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \lambda ( 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex}and {tex} \vec { r } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } + \mu ( 3 \hat { i } - 5 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ){/tex}.
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the lines whose vector equations are {tex} \vec { r } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } + \lambda ( 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex} and {tex} \vec { r } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } + \mu ( 3 \hat { i } - 5 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ){/tex} .
explanation: Given equations of lines are, {tex} \vec { r } = ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } ) + \lambda ( 2 \hat { i} - \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex} .................(i) and {tex} \vec { r } = ( 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) + \mu ( 3 \hat { i } - 5 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ){/tex} ......................(ii) On comparing above equations with vector equation {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } ,{/tex} we get {tex} \vec { a _ { 1 } } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } _ { } { _ { } } = 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} , {tex}\vec b_1=2\hat i-\hat j+\hat k{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a _ { 2 } } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } , \vec { b _ { 2 } } = \hat { 3 i } - 5 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k }{/tex} We know that, the shortest distance between two lines is given by {tex} d = \left| {\frac{{\left( {\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} } \right)\cdot\left( {\overrightarrow {{a_2}} - \overrightarrow {{a_1}} } \right)}}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} } \right|}}} \right|{/tex} .............................(iii) Now, {tex} \vec { b _ { 1 } } \times \vec { b _ { 2 } } = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 2 } & { - 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 3 } & { - 5 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \hat { i } ( - 2 + 5 ) - \hat { j } ( 4 - 3 ) + \hat { k } ( - 10 + 3 ){/tex} ...(iv) and {tex} \left| \vec { b _ { 1 } } \times \vec { b _ { 2 } } \right| = \sqrt { ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 7 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \sqrt { 9 + 1 + 49 } = \sqrt { 59 }{/tex} ...................(v) Also, {tex} \vec { a _ { 2 } } - \vec { a _ { 1 } } = ( 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) - \hat { ( i } + \hat { j }){/tex} {tex} \begin{aligned} & = \hat { i } - \hat { k } \end{aligned}{/tex} ...(vi) From Eqs. (iii), (iv), (v) and (vi), we get {tex} d = \left| \frac { ( { 3 } \hat { i } - \hat { j } - 7 \hat { k } ) \cdot ( \hat { i } - \hat { k } ) } { \sqrt { 59 } } \right|{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad d = \left| \frac { 3 - 0 + 7 } { \sqrt { 5 } \overline { 9 } } \right| = \frac { 10 } { \sqrt { 59 } }{/tex} Hence, required shortest distance is {tex} \frac { 10 } { \sqrt { 59 } }{/tex} units.
solution: Given equations of lines are, {tex} \vec { r } = ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } ) + \lambda ( 2 \hat { i} - \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex} .................(i) and {tex} \vec { r } = ( 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) + \mu ( 3 \hat { i } - 5 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ){/tex} ......................(ii) On comparing above equations with vector equation {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } ,{/tex} we get {tex} \vec { a _ { 1 } } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } _ { } { _ { } } = 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex} , {tex}\vec b_1=2\hat i-\hat j+\hat k{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a _ { 2 } } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } , \vec { b _ { 2 } } = \hat { 3 i } - 5 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k }{/tex} We know that, the shortest distance between two lines is given by {tex} d = \left| {\frac{{\left( {\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} } \right)\cdot\left( {\overrightarrow {{a_2}} - \overrightarrow {{a_1}} } \right)}}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} } \right|}}} \right|{/tex} .............................(iii) Now, {tex} \vec { b _ { 1 } } \times \vec { b _ { 2 } } = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 2 } & { - 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 3 } & { - 5 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \hat { i } ( - 2 + 5 ) - \hat { j } ( 4 - 3 ) + \hat { k } ( - 10 + 3 ){/tex} ...(iv) and {tex} \left| \vec { b _ { 1 } } \times \vec { b _ { 2 } } \right| = \sqrt { ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 7 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \sqrt { 9 + 1 + 49 } = \sqrt { 59 }{/tex} ...................(v) Also, {tex} \vec { a _ { 2 } } - \vec { a _ { 1 } } = ( 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) - \hat { ( i } + \hat { j }){/tex} {tex} \begin{aligned} & = \hat { i } - \hat { k } \end{aligned}{/tex} ...(vi) From Eqs. (iii), (iv), (v) and (vi), we get {tex} d = \left| \frac { ( { 3 } \hat { i } - \hat { j } - 7 \hat { k } ) \cdot ( \hat { i } - \hat { k } ) } { \sqrt { 59 } } \right|{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad d = \left| \frac { 3 - 0 + 7 } { \sqrt { 5 } \overline { 9 } } \right| = \frac { 10 } { \sqrt { 59 } }{/tex} Hence, required shortest distance is {tex} \frac { 10 } { \sqrt { 59 } }{/tex} units.
solution_html:
Given equations of lines are,

{tex} \vec { r } = ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } ) + \lambda ( 2 \hat { i} - \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex}.................(i)
and {tex} \vec { r } = ( 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) + \mu ( 3 \hat { i } - 5 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ){/tex}......................(ii)
On comparing above equations with vector
equation {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } ,{/tex}we get 
{tex} \vec { a _ { 1 } } = \hat { i } + \hat { j } _ { } { _ { } } = 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } + \hat { k }{/tex}, {tex}\vec b_1=2\hat i-\hat j+\hat k{/tex}
and {tex} \vec { a _ { 2 } } = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } , \vec { b _ { 2 } } = \hat { 3 i } - 5 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k }{/tex}
We know that, the shortest distance between two lines is given by
{tex} d = \left| {\frac{{\left( {\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} } \right)\cdot\left( {\overrightarrow {{a_2}} - \overrightarrow {{a_1}} } \right)}}{{\left| {\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times \overrightarrow {{b_2}} } \right|}}} \right|{/tex}.............................(iii)
Now, {tex} \vec { b _ { 1 } } \times \vec { b _ { 2 } } = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 2 } & { - 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 3 } & { - 5 } & { 2 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \hat { i } ( - 2 + 5 ) - \hat { j } ( 4 - 3 ) + \hat { k } ( - 10 + 3 ){/tex}...(iv)
and {tex} \left| \vec { b _ { 1 } } \times \vec { b _ { 2 } } \right| = \sqrt { ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 7 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt { 9 + 1 + 49 } = \sqrt { 59 }{/tex}...................(v)
Also, {tex} \vec { a _ { 2 } } - \vec { a _ { 1 } } = ( 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) - \hat { ( i } + \hat { j }){/tex}
{tex} \begin{aligned} & = \hat { i } - \hat { k } \end{aligned}{/tex}...(vi)
From Eqs. (iii), (iv), (v) and (vi), we get
{tex} d = \left| \frac { ( { 3 } \hat { i } - \hat { j } - 7 \hat { k } ) \cdot ( \hat { i } - \hat { k } ) } { \sqrt { 59 } } \right|{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad d = \left| \frac { 3 - 0 + 7 } { \sqrt { 5 } \overline { 9 } } \right| = \frac { 10 } { \sqrt { 59 } }{/tex}
Hence, required shortest distance is {tex} \frac { 10 } { \sqrt { 59 } }{/tex}units.
