question_html: Find the angle between the lines {tex} \vec { r } = 2 \hat { i } - 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k } + \lambda ( 3 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex}and {tex} \vec { r } = 7 \hat { i } - 6 \hat { j } - 6 \hat { k } + \mu ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ).{/tex}
question_text: Find the angle between the lines {tex} \vec { r } = 2 \hat { i } - 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k } + \lambda ( 3 \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} and {tex} \vec { r } = 7 \hat { i } - 6 \hat { j } - 6 \hat { k } + \mu ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ).{/tex}
explanation: If vector form of lines are {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a _ { 1 } } + \lambda \vec { b _ { 1 } }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a _ { 2 } } + \lambda \vec { b _ { 2 } }{/tex} then angle between them is {tex} \cos \theta = \frac { \left| \vec { b _ { 1 } } .\vec { b _ { 2 } } \right| } { \left| \vec { b _ { 1 } } \right| \left| \vec { b _ { 2 } } \right| }{/tex} Given equations of lines are {tex} \vec { r } = ( 2 \hat { i } - 5 \hat { j} + \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 3\hat i + 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} ......(i) and {tex} \vec { r } = ( 7 \hat { i } - 6 \hat { j } - 6 \hat { k } ) + \mu ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ){/tex} ........................................(ii) On comparing Equations , (i) and (ii) with vector form of equation of line i.e. {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } ,{/tex} we get, {tex} \vec { a _ { 1 } } = 2 \hat { i } - 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b _ { 1 } } = \hat { 3 } \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a _ { 2 } } = \hat { 7 } \hat { i} - 6 \hat { j } - 6 \hat { k } , \vec { b } _ { 2 } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k }{/tex} We know that, the angle between two lines is given by {tex} \cos \theta = \frac { \left| \vec { b _ { 1 } } \cdot \vec { b _ { 2 } } \right| } { \left| \vec { b _ { 1 } } \right| \left| \vec { b _ { 2 } } \right| }{/tex} {tex} \cos \theta = \frac { ( { 3 } \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j} + 6 \hat { k }) \cdot ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ) } { \sqrt { ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } } \cdot \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \left| \frac { 4 + 12 } { 3 \sqrt { 49 } \times \sqrt { 9 } } \right|{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \left| \frac { 19 } { 7 \times 3 } \right| \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac { 19 } { 21 }{/tex} Hence, the angle between given two lies is {tex} \theta = \cos ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 1 9 }{ 2 1 } \right){/tex}
solution: If vector form of lines are {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a _ { 1 } } + \lambda \vec { b _ { 1 } }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a _ { 2 } } + \lambda \vec { b _ { 2 } }{/tex} then angle between them is {tex} \cos \theta = \frac { \left| \vec { b _ { 1 } } .\vec { b _ { 2 } } \right| } { \left| \vec { b _ { 1 } } \right| \left| \vec { b _ { 2 } } \right| }{/tex} Given equations of lines are {tex} \vec { r } = ( 2 \hat { i } - 5 \hat { j} + \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 3\hat i + 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} ......(i) and {tex} \vec { r } = ( 7 \hat { i } - 6 \hat { j } - 6 \hat { k } ) + \mu ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ){/tex} ........................................(ii) On comparing Equations , (i) and (ii) with vector form of equation of line i.e. {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } ,{/tex} we get, {tex} \vec { a _ { 1 } } = 2 \hat { i } - 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b _ { 1 } } = \hat { 3 } \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a _ { 2 } } = \hat { 7 } \hat { i} - 6 \hat { j } - 6 \hat { k } , \vec { b } _ { 2 } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k }{/tex} We know that, the angle between two lines is given by {tex} \cos \theta = \frac { \left| \vec { b _ { 1 } } \cdot \vec { b _ { 2 } } \right| } { \left| \vec { b _ { 1 } } \right| \left| \vec { b _ { 2 } } \right| }{/tex} {tex} \cos \theta = \frac { ( { 3 } \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j} + 6 \hat { k }) \cdot ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ) } { \sqrt { ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } } \cdot \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \left| \frac { 4 + 12 } { 3 \sqrt { 49 } \times \sqrt { 9 } } \right|{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \left| \frac { 19 } { 7 \times 3 } \right| \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac { 19 } { 21 }{/tex} Hence, the angle between given two lies is {tex} \theta = \cos ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 1 9 }{ 2 1 } \right){/tex}
solution_html:
If vector form of lines are {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a _ { 1 } } + \lambda \vec { b _ { 1 } }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a _ { 2 } } + \lambda \vec { b _ { 2 } }{/tex} then angle between them is 
{tex} \cos \theta = \frac { \left| \vec { b _ { 1 } } .\vec { b _ { 2 } } \right| } { \left| \vec { b _ { 1 } } \right| \left| \vec { b _ { 2 } } \right| }{/tex}
Given equations of lines are
{tex} \vec { r } = ( 2 \hat { i } - 5 \hat { j} + \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 3\hat i + 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex}......(i)
and {tex} \vec { r } = ( 7 \hat { i } - 6 \hat { j } - 6 \hat { k } ) + \mu ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ){/tex}........................................(ii)
On comparing Equations , (i) and (ii) with vector form of equation of line i.e.
{tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } ,{/tex} we get,
{tex} \vec { a _ { 1 } } = 2 \hat { i } - 5 \hat { j } + \hat { k } , \vec { b _ { 1 } } = \hat { 3 } \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex}
and {tex} \vec { a _ { 2 } } = \hat { 7 } \hat { i} - 6 \hat { j } - 6 \hat { k } , \vec { b } _ { 2 } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k }{/tex}
We know that, the angle between two lines is given by
{tex} \cos \theta = \frac { \left| \vec { b _ { 1 } } \cdot \vec { b _ { 2 } } \right| } { \left| \vec { b _ { 1 } } \right| \left| \vec { b _ { 2 } } \right| }{/tex}

{tex} \cos \theta = \frac { ( { 3 } \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j} + 6 \hat { k }) \cdot ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ) } { \sqrt { ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } } \cdot \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \left| \frac { 4 + 12 } { 3 \sqrt { 49 } \times \sqrt { 9 } } \right|{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \left| \frac { 19 } { 7 \times 3 } \right| \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac { 19 } { 21 }{/tex}
Hence, the angle between given two lies is
{tex} \theta = \cos ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 1 9 }{ 2 1 } \right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the shortest distance between the following lines.
{tex} \frac { x - 3 } { 1 } = \frac { y - 5 } { - 2 } =\frac { z - 7 } { - 1 } , \frac { x + 1 } { 7 } = \frac { y + 1 } { - 6 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 1 }{/tex}
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the following lines. {tex} \frac { x - 3 } { 1 } = \frac { y - 5 } { - 2 } =\frac { z - 7 } { - 1 } , \frac { x + 1 } { 7 } = \frac { y + 1 } { - 6 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 1 }{/tex}
explanation: The given equations of lines are {tex} \frac { x - 3 } { 1 } = \frac { y - 5 } { - 2 } = \frac { z - 7 } { 1 }{/tex} .........................(i) and {tex} \frac { x + 1 } { 7 } = \frac { y + 1 } { - 6 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 1 }{/tex} .................(ii) On comparing above equations with one point form of equation of line, i.e. {tex} \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c }{/tex} , we get a 1 = 1, b 1 = -2,c 1 = 1, x 1 = 3, y 1 = 5, z 1 = 7 and a 2 = 7, b 2 = -6, c 2 = 1, x 2 = -1 y 2 = -1, z 2 = -1 We know that, the shortest distance between two lines is given by {tex} d = \frac{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{x_2} - {x_1}}&{{y_2} - {y_1}}&{{z_2} - {z_1}} \\ {{a_1}}&{{b_1}}&{{c_1}} \\ {{a_2}}&{{b_2}}&{{c_2}} \end{array}} \right|}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {{b_1}{c_2} - {b_2}{c_1}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {{c_1}{a_2} - {c_2}{a_1}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {{a_1}{b_2} - {a_2}{b_1}} \right)}^2}} }}{/tex} {tex} d = \frac { \left| \begin{array} { r r r } { - 1 - 3 } & { - 1 - 5 } & { - 1 - 7 } \\ { 1 } & { - 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 7 } & { - 6 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| } { \sqrt { ( - 2 + 6 ) ^2 + ( 7 - 1 ) ^2 + ( - 6 + 14 ) ^2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \left| \begin{array} { r r r } { - 4 } & { - 6 } & { - 8 } \\ { 1 } & { - 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 7 } & { - 6 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| } { \sqrt { ( 4 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 8 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} = \left| \frac { - 4 ( - 2 + 6 ) + 6 ( 1 - 7 ) - 8 ( - 6 + 14 ) } { \sqrt { ( 4 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 8 ) ^ { 2 } } } \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left| \frac { - 4 ( 4 ) + 6 ( - 6 ) - 8 ( 8 ) } { \sqrt { 16 + 36 + 64 } } \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left| \frac { - 16 - 36 - 64 } { \sqrt { 116 } } \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left| \frac { - 116 } { \sqrt { 116 } } \right| = \frac { 116 } { \sqrt { 116 } } = \sqrt { 116 }{/tex} Hence, the required shortest distance is {tex} \sqrt { 116 }{/tex} units.
solution: The given equations of lines are {tex} \frac { x - 3 } { 1 } = \frac { y - 5 } { - 2 } = \frac { z - 7 } { 1 }{/tex} .........................(i) and {tex} \frac { x + 1 } { 7 } = \frac { y + 1 } { - 6 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 1 }{/tex} .................(ii) On comparing above equations with one point form of equation of line, i.e. {tex} \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c }{/tex} , we get a 1 = 1, b 1 = -2,c 1 = 1, x 1 = 3, y 1 = 5, z 1 = 7 and a 2 = 7, b 2 = -6, c 2 = 1, x 2 = -1 y 2 = -1, z 2 = -1 We know that, the shortest distance between two lines is given by {tex} d = \frac{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{x_2} - {x_1}}&{{y_2} - {y_1}}&{{z_2} - {z_1}} \\ {{a_1}}&{{b_1}}&{{c_1}} \\ {{a_2}}&{{b_2}}&{{c_2}} \end{array}} \right|}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {{b_1}{c_2} - {b_2}{c_1}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {{c_1}{a_2} - {c_2}{a_1}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {{a_1}{b_2} - {a_2}{b_1}} \right)}^2}} }}{/tex} {tex} d = \frac { \left| \begin{array} { r r r } { - 1 - 3 } & { - 1 - 5 } & { - 1 - 7 } \\ { 1 } & { - 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 7 } & { - 6 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| } { \sqrt { ( - 2 + 6 ) ^2 + ( 7 - 1 ) ^2 + ( - 6 + 14 ) ^2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \left| \begin{array} { r r r } { - 4 } & { - 6 } & { - 8 } \\ { 1 } & { - 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 7 } & { - 6 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| } { \sqrt { ( 4 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 8 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} = \left| \frac { - 4 ( - 2 + 6 ) + 6 ( 1 - 7 ) - 8 ( - 6 + 14 ) } { \sqrt { ( 4 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 8 ) ^ { 2 } } } \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left| \frac { - 4 ( 4 ) + 6 ( - 6 ) - 8 ( 8 ) } { \sqrt { 16 + 36 + 64 } } \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left| \frac { - 16 - 36 - 64 } { \sqrt { 116 } } \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left| \frac { - 116 } { \sqrt { 116 } } \right| = \frac { 116 } { \sqrt { 116 } } = \sqrt { 116 }{/tex} Hence, the required shortest distance is {tex} \sqrt { 116 }{/tex} units.
solution_html:
The given equations of lines are

{tex} \frac { x - 3 } { 1 } = \frac { y - 5 } { - 2 } = \frac { z - 7 } { 1 }{/tex}.........................(i)
and {tex} \frac { x + 1 } { 7 } = \frac { y + 1 } { - 6 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 1 }{/tex}.................(ii)
On comparing above equations with one point form  of equation of line, i.e.
{tex} \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c }{/tex}, we get 
a1 = 1, b1 = -2,c1 = 1, x1 = 3, y1 = 5, z1 = 7
and a2 = 7, b2 = -6, c2 = 1, x2 = -1
y2 = -1, z2 = -1
We know that, the shortest distance between two lines is given by
{tex} d = \frac{{\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{x_2} - {x_1}}&{{y_2} - {y_1}}&{{z_2} - {z_1}} \\ {{a_1}}&{{b_1}}&{{c_1}} \\ {{a_2}}&{{b_2}}&{{c_2}} \end{array}} \right|}}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {{b_1}{c_2} - {b_2}{c_1}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {{c_1}{a_2} - {c_2}{a_1}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {{a_1}{b_2} - {a_2}{b_1}} \right)}^2}} }}{/tex}
{tex} d = \frac { \left| \begin{array} { r r r } { - 1 - 3 } & { - 1 - 5 } & { - 1 - 7 } \\ { 1 } & { - 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 7 } & { - 6 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| } { \sqrt { ( - 2 + 6 ) ^2 + ( 7 - 1 ) ^2 + ( - 6 + 14 ) ^2 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { \left| \begin{array} { r r r } { - 4 } & { - 6 } & { - 8 } \\ { 1 } & { - 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 7 } & { - 6 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| } { \sqrt { ( 4 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 8 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
{tex} = \left| \frac { - 4 ( - 2 + 6 ) + 6 ( 1 - 7 ) - 8 ( - 6 + 14 ) } { \sqrt { ( 4 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 8 ) ^ { 2 } } } \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \left| \frac { - 4 ( 4 ) + 6 ( - 6 ) - 8 ( 8 ) } { \sqrt { 16 + 36 + 64 } } \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \left| \frac { - 16 - 36 - 64 } { \sqrt { 116 } } \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \left| \frac { - 116 } { \sqrt { 116 } } \right| = \frac { 116 } { \sqrt { 116 } } = \sqrt { 116 }{/tex}
Hence, the required shortest distance is {tex} \sqrt { 116 }{/tex} units.

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the shortest distance between the pairs of lines whose Cartesian equations are:
{tex}\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y-5}{-2}=\frac{z-7}{1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x+1}{7}=\frac{y+1}{-6}=\frac{z+1}{1}{/tex}
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the pairs of lines whose Cartesian equations are: {tex}\frac{x-3}{1}=\frac{y-5}{-2}=\frac{z-7}{1}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x+1}{7}=\frac{y+1}{-6}=\frac{z+1}{1}{/tex}
explanation: {tex}{\overrightarrow a _1} = - \hat i - \hat j - \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\overrightarrow a _2} = 3\hat i + 5\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\overrightarrow b _1} = 7\hat i - 6\hat j + 1\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\overrightarrow b _2} = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\overrightarrow a _2} - {\overrightarrow a_1} = 4\hat i + 6\hat j + 8\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\overrightarrow b _1} \times {\overrightarrow b _2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 7&{ - 6}&1 \\ 1&{ - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} =- 4\hat i - 6\hat j - 8\hat k{/tex} {tex}(\vec a_2-\vec a_1).(\vec b_1×\vec b_2){/tex} {tex}=(4\hat i +6\hat j+8\hat k ).(-4\hat i-6\hat j-8\hat k){/tex} = -16 - 36 - 64 = -116 {tex}\left| {{{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2}} \right| = \sqrt {{{( - 4)}^2} + {{( - 6)}^2} + {{( - 8)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {116} {/tex} {tex} = 2\sqrt {29} {/tex} {tex}d = \left| {\frac{{({{\overrightarrow a }_2} - {{\overrightarrow a }_1}).({b_1} \times {b_2})}}{{\left| {{{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2}} \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left| {\frac{{ - 116}}{{2\sqrt {29} }}} \right| = \frac{{4×29}}{{2\sqrt {29} }}{/tex} {tex} = 2\sqrt {29} {/tex}
solution: {tex}{\overrightarrow a _1} = - \hat i - \hat j - \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\overrightarrow a _2} = 3\hat i + 5\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\overrightarrow b _1} = 7\hat i - 6\hat j + 1\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\overrightarrow b _2} = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\overrightarrow a _2} - {\overrightarrow a_1} = 4\hat i + 6\hat j + 8\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\overrightarrow b _1} \times {\overrightarrow b _2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 7&{ - 6}&1 \\ 1&{ - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} =- 4\hat i - 6\hat j - 8\hat k{/tex} {tex}(\vec a_2-\vec a_1).(\vec b_1×\vec b_2){/tex} {tex}=(4\hat i +6\hat j+8\hat k ).(-4\hat i-6\hat j-8\hat k){/tex} = -16 - 36 - 64 = -116 {tex}\left| {{{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2}} \right| = \sqrt {{{( - 4)}^2} + {{( - 6)}^2} + {{( - 8)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {116} {/tex} {tex} = 2\sqrt {29} {/tex} {tex}d = \left| {\frac{{({{\overrightarrow a }_2} - {{\overrightarrow a }_1}).({b_1} \times {b_2})}}{{\left| {{{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2}} \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left| {\frac{{ - 116}}{{2\sqrt {29} }}} \right| = \frac{{4×29}}{{2\sqrt {29} }}{/tex} {tex} = 2\sqrt {29} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{\overrightarrow a _1} = - \hat i - \hat j - \hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\overrightarrow a _2} = 3\hat i + 5\hat j + 7\hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\overrightarrow b _1} = 7\hat i - 6\hat j + 1\hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\overrightarrow b _2} = \hat i - 2\hat j + \hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\overrightarrow a _2} - {\overrightarrow a_1} = 4\hat i + 6\hat j + 8\hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\overrightarrow b _1} \times {\overrightarrow b _2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 7&{ - 6}&1 \\ 1&{ - 2}&1 \end{array}} \right|{/tex}
{tex} =- 4\hat i - 6\hat j - 8\hat k{/tex}
{tex}(\vec a_2-\vec a_1).(\vec b_1×\vec b_2){/tex} {tex}=(4\hat i +6\hat j+8\hat k ).(-4\hat i-6\hat j-8\hat k){/tex}  = -16 - 36 - 64 = -116
{tex}\left| {{{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2}} \right| = \sqrt {{{( - 4)}^2} + {{( - 6)}^2} + {{( - 8)}^2}} {/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {116} {/tex}
{tex} = 2\sqrt {29} {/tex}
{tex}d = \left| {\frac{{({{\overrightarrow a }_2} - {{\overrightarrow a }_1}).({b_1} \times {b_2})}}{{\left| {{{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2}} \right|}}} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \left| {\frac{{ - 116}}{{2\sqrt {29} }}} \right| = \frac{{4×29}}{{2\sqrt {29} }}{/tex}
{tex} = 2\sqrt {29} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the shortest distance between the lines l1 and l2 whose vector equations are
{tex}\vec r = \hat i + \hat j + \lambda (2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k){/tex} ...(1)  
and {tex}\vec r = 2\hat i + \hat j - \hat k + \mu (3\hat i - 5\hat j + 2\hat k){/tex} ...(2)
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the lines l 1 and l 2 whose vector equations are {tex}\vec r = \hat i + \hat j + \lambda (2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k){/tex} ...(1) and {tex}\vec r = 2\hat i + \hat j - \hat k + \mu (3\hat i - 5\hat j + 2\hat k){/tex} ...(2)
explanation: {tex}{\vec a_1} = \hat i + \hat j,{\vec b_1} = 2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} = 2\hat i + \hat j - \hat k,{\vec b_2} = 3\hat i - 5\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} - {\vec a_1} = \hat i - \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} \times {\vec b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&{ - 1}&1 \\ 3&{ - 5}&2 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex}=\hat i (-2+5)-\hat j(4-3)+\hat k(-10+3){/tex} {tex} = 3\hat i - \hat j - 7\hat k{/tex} {tex}\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right|=\sqrt {9+1+49}= \sqrt {59} {/tex} Also, {tex}(\vec b_1×\vec b_2).(\vec a_2-\vec a_1)=(3\hat i-\hat j-7 \hat k)(\hat i-\hat k)=3+7+0=10{/tex} {tex}d=\left|\frac{(\vec b_1×\vec b_2).(\vec a_2-\vec a_1)}{|\vec b_1×\vec b_2|}\right|=\frac{10}{\sqrt{59}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}{\vec a_1} = \hat i + \hat j,{\vec b_1} = 2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} = 2\hat i + \hat j - \hat k,{\vec b_2} = 3\hat i - 5\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} - {\vec a_1} = \hat i - \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} \times {\vec b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&{ - 1}&1 \\ 3&{ - 5}&2 \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex}=\hat i (-2+5)-\hat j(4-3)+\hat k(-10+3){/tex} {tex} = 3\hat i - \hat j - 7\hat k{/tex} {tex}\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right|=\sqrt {9+1+49}= \sqrt {59} {/tex} Also, {tex}(\vec b_1×\vec b_2).(\vec a_2-\vec a_1)=(3\hat i-\hat j-7 \hat k)(\hat i-\hat k)=3+7+0=10{/tex} {tex}d=\left|\frac{(\vec b_1×\vec b_2).(\vec a_2-\vec a_1)}{|\vec b_1×\vec b_2|}\right|=\frac{10}{\sqrt{59}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{\vec a_1} = \hat i + \hat j,{\vec b_1} = 2\hat i - \hat j + \hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\vec a_2} = 2\hat i + \hat j - \hat k,{\vec b_2} = 3\hat i - 5\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\vec a_2} - {\vec a_1} = \hat i - \hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\vec b_1} \times {\vec b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&{ - 1}&1 \\ 3&{ - 5}&2 \end{array}} \right|{/tex}
{tex}=\hat i (-2+5)-\hat j(4-3)+\hat k(-10+3){/tex}
{tex} = 3\hat i - \hat j - 7\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right|=\sqrt {9+1+49}= \sqrt {59} {/tex}
Also, {tex}(\vec b_1×\vec b_2).(\vec a_2-\vec a_1)=(3\hat i-\hat j-7 \hat k)(\hat i-\hat k)=3+7+0=10{/tex}
{tex}d=\left|\frac{(\vec b_1×\vec b_2).(\vec a_2-\vec a_1)}{|\vec b_1×\vec b_2|}\right|=\frac{10}{\sqrt{59}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: A line passes through (2, - 1, 3) and is perpendicular to the line {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} + {tex}\lambda(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-3 \hat{k})+\mu(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} . Obtain its equation.
question_text: A line passes through (2, - 1, 3) and is perpendicular to the line {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} + {tex}\lambda(2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-3 \hat{k})+\mu(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} . Obtain its equation.
explanation: The required line is perpendicular to the lines which are parallel to vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{b_{1}}=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b_{2}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively. So, it is parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} . Now {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}-6 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} Thus, the required line passes through the point (2, - 1, 3) and is parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}=-6 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} . So, its vector equation is {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(-6 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} [Using {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{b}{/tex} ] or {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\mu(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex} where {tex}\mu=-3 \lambda{/tex}
solution: The required line is perpendicular to the lines which are parallel to vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{b_{1}}=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b_{2}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} respectively. So, it is parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} . Now {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}-6 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} Thus, the required line passes through the point (2, - 1, 3) and is parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}=-6 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} . So, its vector equation is {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(-6 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} [Using {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{b}{/tex} ] or {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\mu(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex} where {tex}\mu=-3 \lambda{/tex}
solution_html:
The required line is perpendicular to the lines which are parallel to vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{b_{1}}=2 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b_{2}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}  respectively. So, it is parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}{/tex}.
Now {tex}\vec{b}=\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}-6 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex} 
Thus, the required line passes through the point (2, - 1, 3) and is parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}=-6 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}{/tex}. So, its vector equation is 
{tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\lambda(-6 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} [Using {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{b}{/tex}]
or {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k})+\mu(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}){/tex} where {tex}\mu=-3 \lambda{/tex}

question_html: Show that the lines {tex}\vec { r } = ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 3 \hat { i } - \hat { j } ){/tex} and {tex}\vec { r } = ( 4 \hat { i } - \hat { k } ) + \mu ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex}intersect. Find their point of intersection.
question_text: Show that the lines {tex}\vec { r } = ( \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 3 \hat { i } - \hat { j } ){/tex} and {tex}\vec { r } = ( 4 \hat { i } - \hat { k } ) + \mu ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex} intersect. Find their point of intersection.
explanation: The given lines can be rewritten as {tex}\vec { r } = ( 3 \lambda + 1 ) \hat { i } + \left( 1 - \lambda _ { } \right) \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex} .................(i) and {tex}\vec { r } = ( 4 + 2 \mu ) \hat { i } + 0 \hat { j } + ( \beta \mu - 1 ) \hat { k }{/tex} .......(ii) Clearly, any point on line (i) is of the form {tex}P ( 3 \lambda + 1,1 - \lambda , - 1 ){/tex} and any point on line (ii) is of the form {tex}Q ( 4 + 2 \mu , 0,3 \mu - 1 ){/tex} If lines (i) and (ii) intersect, then these points must coincide for some {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}\mu{/tex} Consider, {tex}3 \lambda + 1 = 4 + 2 \mu{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 \lambda - 2 \mu = 3{/tex} n...................(iii) {tex}1 - \lambda = 0{/tex} ....................................(iv) and {tex} \quad 3 \mu - 1 = - 1{/tex} .....................(v) From Equations, (iv), we get {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1 and put the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} in Equation, (i), we get {tex}3 ( 1 ) - 2 \mu = 3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 2 u = 3 - 3 \Rightarrow \mu = 0{/tex} On putting the value of µ in Equation, (v), we get 3 (0) - 1 = - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0 - 1 = - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -1 = -1, which is true Hence, both lines intersect each other. The point of intersection of both lines can be obtained by putting {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1 in coordinates of P. So, the point of intersection is (3 + 1, 1 - 1, -1), i.e. (4,0,-1).
solution: The given lines can be rewritten as {tex}\vec { r } = ( 3 \lambda + 1 ) \hat { i } + \left( 1 - \lambda _ { } \right) \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex} .................(i) and {tex}\vec { r } = ( 4 + 2 \mu ) \hat { i } + 0 \hat { j } + ( \beta \mu - 1 ) \hat { k }{/tex} .......(ii) Clearly, any point on line (i) is of the form {tex}P ( 3 \lambda + 1,1 - \lambda , - 1 ){/tex} and any point on line (ii) is of the form {tex}Q ( 4 + 2 \mu , 0,3 \mu - 1 ){/tex} If lines (i) and (ii) intersect, then these points must coincide for some {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}\mu{/tex} Consider, {tex}3 \lambda + 1 = 4 + 2 \mu{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 \lambda - 2 \mu = 3{/tex} n...................(iii) {tex}1 - \lambda = 0{/tex} ....................................(iv) and {tex} \quad 3 \mu - 1 = - 1{/tex} .....................(v) From Equations, (iv), we get {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1 and put the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} in Equation, (i), we get {tex}3 ( 1 ) - 2 \mu = 3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 2 u = 3 - 3 \Rightarrow \mu = 0{/tex} On putting the value of µ in Equation, (v), we get 3 (0) - 1 = - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0 - 1 = - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -1 = -1, which is true Hence, both lines intersect each other. The point of intersection of both lines can be obtained by putting {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1 in coordinates of P. So, the point of intersection is (3 + 1, 1 - 1, -1), i.e. (4,0,-1).
solution_html:
The given lines can be rewritten as
{tex}\vec { r } = ( 3 \lambda + 1 ) \hat { i } + \left( 1 - \lambda _ { } \right) \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex}.................(i)
and {tex}\vec { r } = ( 4 + 2 \mu ) \hat { i } + 0 \hat { j } + ( \beta \mu - 1 ) \hat { k }{/tex}.......(ii)
Clearly, any point on line (i) is of the form {tex}P ( 3 \lambda + 1,1 - \lambda , - 1 ){/tex} and any point on line (ii) is of the form  {tex}Q ( 4 + 2 \mu , 0,3 \mu - 1 ){/tex}
If lines (i) and (ii) intersect, then these points must coincide for some {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}\mu{/tex}
Consider, {tex}3 \lambda + 1 = 4 + 2 \mu{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 \lambda - 2 \mu = 3{/tex} n...................(iii)
{tex}1 - \lambda = 0{/tex} ....................................(iv)

and {tex} \quad 3 \mu - 1 = - 1{/tex}.....................(v)
From Equations, (iv), we get {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1 and put the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex}  in Equation, (i), we get
{tex}3 ( 1 ) - 2 \mu = 3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 2 u = 3 - 3 \Rightarrow \mu = 0{/tex}
On putting the value of µ in Equation, (v), we get
3 (0) - 1 = - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0 - 1 = - 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -1 = -1, which is true
Hence, both lines intersect each other.
The point of intersection of both lines can be obtained by putting {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1 in coordinates of P. So, the point of intersection is (3 + 1, 1 - 1, -1), i.e. (4,0,-1).

question_html: Find the distance between the lines l1 and l2 given by {tex} l \mathrm { _1: } \vec { r } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } + \lambda ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ),{/tex}{tex} l _2 :\vec { r } = 3 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } + \mu ( 4 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 12 \hat { k } ).{/tex}
question_text: Find the distance between the lines l 1 and l 2 given by {tex} l \mathrm { _1: } \vec { r } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } + \lambda ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ),{/tex} {tex} l _2 :\vec { r } = 3 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } + \mu ( 4 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 12 \hat { k } ).{/tex}
explanation: We are required to find the distance between the two lines whose equations are given as: {tex} \vec { r } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } + \lambda ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} and {tex} \vec { r } = 3 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } + \mu ( 4 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 12 \hat { k } ){/tex} On comparing with {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b }{/tex} , we get {tex} a _ { 1 } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } , \vec { b } _ { 1 } = 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a } _ { 2 } = \hat { 3 } \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} - 5 \hat { k } \;\; \vec { b } _ { 2 } = 4 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j} + 12 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, {tex} \vec { a } _ { 2 } - \vec { a } _ { 1 } = ( 3\hat i+ 3 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } ) - ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= { 2 } \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } _ { 1 } \times \vec { b } _ { 2 } = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 6 } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 12 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \hat { i } ( 36 - 36 ) - \hat { j } ( 24 - 24 ) + \hat { k } ( 12 - 12 ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex} So, both given lines are parallel and {tex}\vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} Then, {tex}\vec { b } \times \left( \vec { a _ { 2 } } - \vec { a _ { 1 } } \right) = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 6 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \hat { i } ( - 3 - 6 ) - \hat { j } ( - 2 - 12 ) + \hat { k } ( 2 - \boldsymbol { 6 }){/tex} {tex}= { 9 } \hat { i } + 14 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, required distance between given lines is {tex} d = \frac { \left| \vec { b } \times \left( \vec { a } _ { 1 } - \vec { a } _ { 2 } \right) \right| } { | \vec { b } | } = \left| \frac { - 9 \hat { i } + 14 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } } } \right|{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \sqrt { 81 + 196 - 16 } } { \sqrt { 4 + 9 + 36 } } = \frac { \sqrt { 261 } } { \sqrt { 49 } } = \frac { \sqrt { 261 } } { 7 }{/tex} units
solution: We are required to find the distance between the two lines whose equations are given as: {tex} \vec { r } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } + \lambda ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} and {tex} \vec { r } = 3 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } + \mu ( 4 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 12 \hat { k } ){/tex} On comparing with {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b }{/tex} , we get {tex} a _ { 1 } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } , \vec { b } _ { 1 } = 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a } _ { 2 } = \hat { 3 } \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} - 5 \hat { k } \;\; \vec { b } _ { 2 } = 4 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j} + 12 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, {tex} \vec { a } _ { 2 } - \vec { a } _ { 1 } = ( 3\hat i+ 3 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } ) - ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= { 2 } \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex}\vec { b } _ { 1 } \times \vec { b } _ { 2 } = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 6 } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 12 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \hat { i } ( 36 - 36 ) - \hat { j } ( 24 - 24 ) + \hat { k } ( 12 - 12 ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex} So, both given lines are parallel and {tex}\vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} Then, {tex}\vec { b } \times \left( \vec { a _ { 2 } } - \vec { a _ { 1 } } \right) = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 6 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \hat { i } ( - 3 - 6 ) - \hat { j } ( - 2 - 12 ) + \hat { k } ( 2 - \boldsymbol { 6 }){/tex} {tex}= { 9 } \hat { i } + 14 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, required distance between given lines is {tex} d = \frac { \left| \vec { b } \times \left( \vec { a } _ { 1 } - \vec { a } _ { 2 } \right) \right| } { | \vec { b } | } = \left| \frac { - 9 \hat { i } + 14 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } } } \right|{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \sqrt { 81 + 196 - 16 } } { \sqrt { 4 + 9 + 36 } } = \frac { \sqrt { 261 } } { \sqrt { 49 } } = \frac { \sqrt { 261 } } { 7 }{/tex} units
solution_html:
We are required to find the distance between the two lines whose  equations are given as:
{tex} \vec { r } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } + \lambda ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex}
and {tex} \vec { r } = 3 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } + \mu ( 4 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 12 \hat { k } ){/tex}
On comparing with {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b }{/tex}, we get
{tex} a _ { 1 } = \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } , \vec { b } _ { 1 } = 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex}
and {tex} \vec { a } _ { 2 } = \hat { 3 } \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} - 5 \hat { k } \;\; \vec { b } _ { 2 } = 4 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j} + 12 \hat { k }{/tex}
Now, {tex} \vec { a } _ { 2 } - \vec { a } _ { 1 } = ( 3\hat i+ 3 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } ) - ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex}= { 2 } \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex}
and {tex}\vec { b } _ { 1 } \times \vec { b } _ { 2 } = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 6 } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 12 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex}= \hat { i } ( 36 - 36 ) - \hat { j } ( 24 - 24 ) + \hat { k } ( 12 - 12 ) = \vec { 0 }{/tex}
So, both given lines are parallel and
{tex}\vec { b } = 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex}
Then, {tex}\vec { b } \times \left( \vec { a _ { 2 } } - \vec { a _ { 1 } } \right) = \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 6 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex}= \hat { i } ( - 3 - 6 ) - \hat { j } ( - 2 - 12 ) + \hat { k } ( 2 - \boldsymbol { 6 }){/tex}
{tex}= { 9 } \hat { i } + 14 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k }{/tex}
Now, required distance between given lines is
{tex} d = \frac { \left| \vec { b } \times \left( \vec { a } _ { 1 } - \vec { a } _ { 2 } \right) \right| } { | \vec { b } | } = \left| \frac { - 9 \hat { i } + 14 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } } } \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { \sqrt { 81 + 196 - 16 } } { \sqrt { 4 + 9 + 36 } } = \frac { \sqrt { 261 } } { \sqrt { 49 } } = \frac { \sqrt { 261 } } { 7 }{/tex}units

question_html: Write the equation of the straight line through the point {tex} ( \alpha , \beta , \gamma ){/tex} and parallel to Z-axis.
question_text: Write the equation of the straight line through the point {tex} ( \alpha , \beta , \gamma ){/tex} and parallel to Z-axis.
explanation: According to the question, the required line is parallel to z-axis. The vector equation of a line parallel to Z-axis is {tex} \vec { m } = { 0 } \hat { i } + 0 \hat { i } + \hat { k }{/tex} Then, the required line passes through the point {tex} A ( \alpha , \beta , \gamma ){/tex} whose position vector is {tex} \vec { r } _ { 1 } = \alpha \hat { i } + \hat { \beta } \hat { j } + \gamma \hat { j }{/tex} and is parallel to the vector {tex} \vec { m } = ( 0 \hat { i } + 0 \hat { \jmath } + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} The equation is {tex}\vec { r } = \vec { r } _ { 1 } + \lambda \vec { m }{/tex} {tex} = ( \alpha \hat { i } + \beta \hat { j } + \gamma \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 0 \hat { i } + 0 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex} = ( \hat { \alpha } \hat { i } + \beta \hat { j } + \gamma \hat { k } ) + \lambda \hat { k } {/tex}
solution: According to the question, the required line is parallel to z-axis. The vector equation of a line parallel to Z-axis is {tex} \vec { m } = { 0 } \hat { i } + 0 \hat { i } + \hat { k }{/tex} Then, the required line passes through the point {tex} A ( \alpha , \beta , \gamma ){/tex} whose position vector is {tex} \vec { r } _ { 1 } = \alpha \hat { i } + \hat { \beta } \hat { j } + \gamma \hat { j }{/tex} and is parallel to the vector {tex} \vec { m } = ( 0 \hat { i } + 0 \hat { \jmath } + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} The equation is {tex}\vec { r } = \vec { r } _ { 1 } + \lambda \vec { m }{/tex} {tex} = ( \alpha \hat { i } + \beta \hat { j } + \gamma \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 0 \hat { i } + 0 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex} = ( \hat { \alpha } \hat { i } + \beta \hat { j } + \gamma \hat { k } ) + \lambda \hat { k } {/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, the required line is parallel to z-axis.

The vector equation of a line parallel to Z-axis is {tex} \vec { m } = { 0 } \hat { i } + 0 \hat { i } + \hat { k }{/tex}

Then, the required line passes through the point {tex} A ( \alpha , \beta , \gamma ){/tex} whose position vector is {tex} \vec { r } _ { 1 } = \alpha \hat { i } + \hat { \beta } \hat { j } + \gamma \hat { j }{/tex} and is parallel to the vector {tex} \vec { m } = ( 0 \hat { i } + 0 \hat { \jmath } + \hat { k } ){/tex}
 {tex}\therefore {/tex} The equation is  {tex}\vec { r } = \vec { r } _ { 1 } + \lambda \vec { m }{/tex}
{tex} = ( \alpha \hat { i } + \beta \hat { j } + \gamma \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 0 \hat { i } + 0 \hat { j } + \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex} = ( \hat { \alpha } \hat { i } + \beta \hat { j } + \gamma \hat { k } ) + \lambda \hat { k } {/tex}

question_html: Find the direction cosines of the line {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { 2 } = \frac { 2 y - 7 } { 6 } = \frac { 5 - z } { 6 }{/tex}. Also, find the vector equation of the line through the point A(-1,2,3) and parallel to the given line.
question_text: Find the direction cosines of the line {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { 2 } = \frac { 2 y - 7 } { 6 } = \frac { 5 - z } { 6 }{/tex} . Also, find the vector equation of the line through the point A(-1,2,3) and parallel to the given line.
explanation: Given equation of line is {tex}\frac { x + 2 } { 2 } = \frac { 2 y - 7 } { 6 } = \frac { 5 - z } { 6 }{/tex} This equation can be written as {tex}\frac { x + 2 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 7 / 2 } { 3 } = \frac { z - 5 } { - 6 }{/tex} So, direction ratios of line are ( 2, 3,- 6). Now, direction cosines of a line are {tex}\left( {l = \frac{2}{{\sqrt {{2^2} + {3^2} + {{( - 6)}^2}} }},m = \frac{3}{{\sqrt {{2^2} + {3^2} + {{( - 6)}^2}} }},n = \frac{{ - 6}} {{\sqrt {{2^2} + {3^2} + {{( - 6)}^2}} }}} \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because l = \frac{{ \pm a}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }},m = \frac{{ \pm b}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }},n = \frac{{ \pm c}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad l = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 49 } } , m = \frac { 3 } { \sqrt { 49 } } , n = - \frac { 6 } { \sqrt { 49 } }{/tex} So, direction cosines of given line are {tex}\left( \frac { 2 } { 7 } , \frac { 3 } { 7 } , \frac { - 6 } { 7 } \right){/tex} Here, DR's of a line parallel to given line are (2, 3, -6). Therefore, the required equation of line passes through the point A(-1,2,3) and parallel to given line is {tex} \frac { x + 1 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \frac { z - 3 } { - 6 }{/tex}
solution: Given equation of line is {tex}\frac { x + 2 } { 2 } = \frac { 2 y - 7 } { 6 } = \frac { 5 - z } { 6 }{/tex} This equation can be written as {tex}\frac { x + 2 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 7 / 2 } { 3 } = \frac { z - 5 } { - 6 }{/tex} So, direction ratios of line are ( 2, 3,- 6). Now, direction cosines of a line are {tex}\left( {l = \frac{2}{{\sqrt {{2^2} + {3^2} + {{( - 6)}^2}} }},m = \frac{3}{{\sqrt {{2^2} + {3^2} + {{( - 6)}^2}} }},n = \frac{{ - 6}} {{\sqrt {{2^2} + {3^2} + {{( - 6)}^2}} }}} \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because l = \frac{{ \pm a}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }},m = \frac{{ \pm b}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }},n = \frac{{ \pm c}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad l = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 49 } } , m = \frac { 3 } { \sqrt { 49 } } , n = - \frac { 6 } { \sqrt { 49 } }{/tex} So, direction cosines of given line are {tex}\left( \frac { 2 } { 7 } , \frac { 3 } { 7 } , \frac { - 6 } { 7 } \right){/tex} Here, DR's of a line parallel to given line are (2, 3, -6). Therefore, the required equation of line passes through the point A(-1,2,3) and parallel to given line is {tex} \frac { x + 1 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \frac { z - 3 } { - 6 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given equation of line is
{tex}\frac { x + 2 } { 2 } = \frac { 2 y - 7 } { 6 } = \frac { 5 - z } { 6 }{/tex}
This equation can be written as
{tex}\frac { x + 2 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 7 / 2 } { 3 } = \frac { z - 5 } { - 6 }{/tex}
So, direction ratios of line are ( 2, 3,- 6).
Now, direction cosines of a line are 
{tex}\left( {l = \frac{2}{{\sqrt {{2^2} + {3^2} + {{( - 6)}^2}} }},m = \frac{3}{{\sqrt {{2^2} + {3^2} + {{( - 6)}^2}} }},n = \frac{{ - 6}} {{\sqrt {{2^2} + {3^2} + {{( - 6)}^2}} }}} \right){/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\because l = \frac{{ \pm a}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }},m = \frac{{ \pm b}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }},n = \frac{{ \pm c}}{{\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2}} }}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad l = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 49 } } , m = \frac { 3 } { \sqrt { 49 } } , n = - \frac { 6 } { \sqrt { 49 } }{/tex}
So, direction cosines of given line are {tex}\left( \frac { 2 } { 7 } , \frac { 3 } { 7 } , \frac { - 6 } { 7 } \right){/tex}
Here, DR's of a line parallel to given line are (2, 3, -6). Therefore, the required equation of line passes through the point A(-1,2,3) and parallel  to given line is 
{tex} \frac { x + 1 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \frac { z - 3 } { - 6 }{/tex}

question_html: If the Cartesian equation of a line is {tex}\frac { 3 - x } { 5 } = \frac { y + 4 } { 7 } = \frac { 2 z - 6 } { 4 }{/tex}, then write the vector equation for the line.
question_text: If the Cartesian equation of a line is {tex}\frac { 3 - x } { 5 } = \frac { y + 4 } { 7 } = \frac { 2 z - 6 } { 4 }{/tex} , then write the vector equation for the line.
explanation: According to the question,Given cartesian equation of a line is {tex}\frac { 3 - x } { 5 } = \frac { y + 4 } { 7 } = \frac { 2 z - 6 } { 4 }{/tex} On rewriting the given equation, we get {tex}\frac { x - 3 } { - 5 } = \frac { y + 4 } { 7 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 2 } = \lambda \ \ \ ( \text { let } ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = - 5 \lambda + 3 , \ y = 7 \lambda - 4 {/tex} and {tex}\ z = 2 \lambda + 3{/tex} Required equation of line in vector form is given by , {tex}\vec {r}=x\hat { i } +y \hat { j } + z \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} = ( - 5 \lambda + 3) \hat { i } + ( 7 \lambda & - 4 ) \hat { j } + ( 2 \lambda + 3 ) \hat { k } \end{aligned}{/tex} {tex}= 3 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } + \lambda ( - 5 \hat { i} + 7 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \vec { r } = ( { 3 } \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( - 5 \hat { i } + 7 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ){/tex}
solution: According to the question,Given cartesian equation of a line is {tex}\frac { 3 - x } { 5 } = \frac { y + 4 } { 7 } = \frac { 2 z - 6 } { 4 }{/tex} On rewriting the given equation, we get {tex}\frac { x - 3 } { - 5 } = \frac { y + 4 } { 7 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 2 } = \lambda \ \ \ ( \text { let } ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = - 5 \lambda + 3 , \ y = 7 \lambda - 4 {/tex} and {tex}\ z = 2 \lambda + 3{/tex} Required equation of line in vector form is given by , {tex}\vec {r}=x\hat { i } +y \hat { j } + z \hat { k }{/tex} {tex}\begin{aligned} = ( - 5 \lambda + 3) \hat { i } + ( 7 \lambda & - 4 ) \hat { j } + ( 2 \lambda + 3 ) \hat { k } \end{aligned}{/tex} {tex}= 3 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } + \lambda ( - 5 \hat { i} + 7 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \vec { r } = ( { 3 } \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( - 5 \hat { i } + 7 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ){/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,Given cartesian equation of a line is
{tex}\frac { 3 - x } { 5 } = \frac { y + 4 } { 7 } = \frac { 2 z - 6 } { 4 }{/tex}
On rewriting the given equation, we get
{tex}\frac { x - 3 } { - 5 } = \frac { y + 4 } { 7 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 2 } = \lambda \ \ \ ( \text { let } ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = - 5 \lambda + 3 , \ y = 7 \lambda - 4 {/tex} and {tex}\ z = 2 \lambda + 3{/tex}
Required equation of line in vector form is given by ,  {tex}\vec {r}=x\hat { i } +y \hat { j } + z \hat { k }{/tex}
{tex}\begin{aligned} = ( - 5 \lambda + 3) \hat { i } + ( 7 \lambda & - 4 ) \hat { j } + ( 2 \lambda + 3 ) \hat { k } \end{aligned}{/tex}
{tex}= 3 \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } + \lambda ( - 5 \hat { i} + 7 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \vec { r } = ( { 3 } \hat { i } - 4 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( - 5 \hat { i } + 7 \hat { j } + 2 \hat { k } ){/tex}

question_html:
Find the distance between the lines l1 and l2 given by
{tex}\vec r = \hat i + 2\hat j - 4\hat k + \lambda (2\hat i + 3\hat j + 6\hat k){/tex}
and {tex}\vec r = 3\hat i + 3\hat j - 5\hat k + \mu (2\hat i + 3\hat j + 6\hat k){/tex}
question_text: Find the distance between the lines l 1 and l 2 given by {tex}\vec r = \hat i + 2\hat j - 4\hat k + \lambda (2\hat i + 3\hat j + 6\hat k){/tex} and {tex}\vec r = 3\hat i + 3\hat j - 5\hat k + \mu (2\hat i + 3\hat j + 6\hat k){/tex}
explanation: {tex}{\vec a_1} = \hat i + 2\hat j - 4\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} = 3\hat i + 3\hat j - 5\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} = {\vec b_2}=\vec b{/tex} {tex}=2\hat i+3\hat j+6\hat k{/tex} {tex}|\vec b|=\sqrt{4+9+36}=\sqrt {49}{/tex} Hence lines are parallel. {tex}{\vec a_2} - {\vec a_1} = 2\hat i + \hat i - \hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec b \times \left( {{{\vec a}_2} - {{\vec a}_1}} \right) = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&3&6 \\ 2&1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex}=-9\vec i+14\vec j-4\vec k{/tex} {tex}d = \left| {\frac{{\vec b \times \left( {{{\vec a}_2} - {{\vec a}_1}} \right)}}{{\left| {\vec b} \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left| {\frac{{ - 9\hat i + 14\hat j - 4\hat k}}{{\sqrt {49} }}} \right|{/tex} {tex}=\frac{{\sqrt {81+196+16} }}{{\sqrt {49} }} = \frac{{\sqrt {293} }}{7}{/tex}
solution: {tex}{\vec a_1} = \hat i + 2\hat j - 4\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} = 3\hat i + 3\hat j - 5\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} = {\vec b_2}=\vec b{/tex} {tex}=2\hat i+3\hat j+6\hat k{/tex} {tex}|\vec b|=\sqrt{4+9+36}=\sqrt {49}{/tex} Hence lines are parallel. {tex}{\vec a_2} - {\vec a_1} = 2\hat i + \hat i - \hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec b \times \left( {{{\vec a}_2} - {{\vec a}_1}} \right) = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&3&6 \\ 2&1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex}=-9\vec i+14\vec j-4\vec k{/tex} {tex}d = \left| {\frac{{\vec b \times \left( {{{\vec a}_2} - {{\vec a}_1}} \right)}}{{\left| {\vec b} \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \left| {\frac{{ - 9\hat i + 14\hat j - 4\hat k}}{{\sqrt {49} }}} \right|{/tex} {tex}=\frac{{\sqrt {81+196+16} }}{{\sqrt {49} }} = \frac{{\sqrt {293} }}{7}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{\vec a_1} = \hat i + 2\hat j - 4\hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\vec a_2} = 3\hat i + 3\hat j - 5\hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\vec b_1} = {\vec b_2}=\vec b{/tex}{tex}=2\hat i+3\hat j+6\hat k{/tex}
{tex}|\vec b|=\sqrt{4+9+36}=\sqrt {49}{/tex}
Hence lines are parallel.
{tex}{\vec a_2} - {\vec a_1} = 2\hat i + \hat i - \hat k{/tex}
{tex}\vec b \times \left( {{{\vec a}_2} - {{\vec a}_1}} \right) = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 2&3&6 \\ 2&1&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right|{/tex}
{tex}=-9\vec i+14\vec j-4\vec k{/tex}
{tex}d = \left| {\frac{{\vec b \times \left( {{{\vec a}_2} - {{\vec a}_1}} \right)}}{{\left| {\vec b} \right|}}} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \left| {\frac{{ - 9\hat i + 14\hat j - 4\hat k}}{{\sqrt {49} }}} \right|{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{{\sqrt {81+196+16} }}{{\sqrt {49} }} = \frac{{\sqrt {293} }}{7}{/tex}

question_text: Find the direction cosines of the line {tex}\frac { 4 - x } { 2 } = \frac { y } { 6 } = \frac { 1 - z } { 3 }{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, equation of line is {tex}\frac { 4 - x } { 2 } = \frac { y } { 6 } = \frac { 1 - z } { 3 }{/tex} It can be rewritten as {tex}\frac { x - 4 } { - 2 } = \frac { y } { 6 } = \frac { z - 1 } { - 3 }{/tex} Here, Direction ratios of the line are (-2, 6, -3). :. Direction cosines of the line are {tex}\frac { - 2 } { \sqrt { ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + 6 ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } } } , \frac { 6 } { \sqrt { ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + 6 ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex}\frac { - 3 } { \sqrt { ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + 6 ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } } } \text { i.e. } \frac { - 2 } { \sqrt { 49 } } , \frac { 6 } { \sqrt { 49 } } \text { and } \frac { - 3 } { \sqrt { 49 } }{/tex} Thus, Direction cosines of line are {tex}\left( - \frac { 2 } { 7 } , \frac { 6 } { 7 } , - \frac { 3 } { 7 } \right){/tex}
solution: According to the question, equation of line is {tex}\frac { 4 - x } { 2 } = \frac { y } { 6 } = \frac { 1 - z } { 3 }{/tex} It can be rewritten as {tex}\frac { x - 4 } { - 2 } = \frac { y } { 6 } = \frac { z - 1 } { - 3 }{/tex} Here, Direction ratios of the line are (-2, 6, -3). :. Direction cosines of the line are {tex}\frac { - 2 } { \sqrt { ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + 6 ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } } } , \frac { 6 } { \sqrt { ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + 6 ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex}\frac { - 3 } { \sqrt { ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + 6 ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } } } \text { i.e. } \frac { - 2 } { \sqrt { 49 } } , \frac { 6 } { \sqrt { 49 } } \text { and } \frac { - 3 } { \sqrt { 49 } }{/tex} Thus, Direction cosines of line are {tex}\left( - \frac { 2 } { 7 } , \frac { 6 } { 7 } , - \frac { 3 } { 7 } \right){/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, equation of line is
{tex}\frac { 4 - x } { 2 } = \frac { y } { 6 } = \frac { 1 - z } { 3 }{/tex}
It can be rewritten as
{tex}\frac { x - 4 } { - 2 } = \frac { y } { 6 } = \frac { z - 1 } { - 3 }{/tex}
Here, Direction ratios of the line are (-2, 6, -3).
:. Direction cosines of the line are

{tex}\frac { - 3 } { \sqrt { ( - 2 ) ^ { 2 } + 6 ^ { 2 } + ( - 3 ) ^ { 2 } } } \text { i.e. } \frac { - 2 } { \sqrt { 49 } } , \frac { 6 } { \sqrt { 49 } } \text { and } \frac { - 3 } { \sqrt { 49 } }{/tex}
Thus, Direction cosines of line are{tex}\left( - \frac { 2 } { 7 } , \frac { 6 } { 7 } , - \frac { 3 } { 7 } \right){/tex}

question_html: The cartesian equation of a line is 6x -2 = 3y+1 = 2z-2 . Find the direction cosines of the line. Write down the cartesian and vector equations of a line passing through (2, -1, -1) which is parallel to the given line?
question_text: The cartesian equation of a line is 6x -2 = 3y+1 = 2z-2 . Find the direction cosines of the line. Write down the cartesian and vector equations of a line passing through (2, -1, -1) which is parallel to the given line?
explanation: Given equation of line is 6x - 2= 3y + 1 = 2z -2 or {tex} \frac { x - 2 / 6 } { 1 / 6 } = \frac { y + 1 / 3 } { 1 / 3 } = \frac { z - 2 / 2 } { 1 / 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - 1 / 3 } { 1 / 6 } = \frac { y + 1 / 3 } { 1 / 3 } = \frac { z - 1 } { 1 / 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - 1 / 3 } { 1 } = \frac { y + 1 / 3 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 1 } { 3 }{/tex} [on dividing each by 6] Here, DR's of the line are ( 1, 2, 3). {tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction cosines of the line are {tex} \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } } } \, ,\frac { 2 } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } } } \,,\frac { 3 } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 14 } } , \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 14 } } , \frac { 3 } { \sqrt { 14 } }{/tex} The equation of a line passing through (2 -1, -1) and parallel to the given line is {tex} \frac { x - 2 } { 1 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 2 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 3 } = \lambda ( \text { say } ){/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 2 + \lambda , y = - 1 + 2 \lambda {/tex} and {tex}\ z = - 1 + 3 \lambda{/tex} Now, {tex} x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k = (2 + \lambda )\hat i + ( - 1 + 2\lambda )\hat j + ( - 1 + 3\lambda )\hat k{/tex} {tex} \therefore \vec { r } = ( 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex} which is the required equation of line in vector form.
solution: Given equation of line is 6x - 2= 3y + 1 = 2z -2 or {tex} \frac { x - 2 / 6 } { 1 / 6 } = \frac { y + 1 / 3 } { 1 / 3 } = \frac { z - 2 / 2 } { 1 / 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - 1 / 3 } { 1 / 6 } = \frac { y + 1 / 3 } { 1 / 3 } = \frac { z - 1 } { 1 / 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - 1 / 3 } { 1 } = \frac { y + 1 / 3 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 1 } { 3 }{/tex} [on dividing each by 6] Here, DR's of the line are ( 1, 2, 3). {tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction cosines of the line are {tex} \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } } } \, ,\frac { 2 } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } } } \,,\frac { 3 } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 14 } } , \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 14 } } , \frac { 3 } { \sqrt { 14 } }{/tex} The equation of a line passing through (2 -1, -1) and parallel to the given line is {tex} \frac { x - 2 } { 1 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 2 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 3 } = \lambda ( \text { say } ){/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c } \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 2 + \lambda , y = - 1 + 2 \lambda {/tex} and {tex}\ z = - 1 + 3 \lambda{/tex} Now, {tex} x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k = (2 + \lambda )\hat i + ( - 1 + 2\lambda )\hat j + ( - 1 + 3\lambda )\hat k{/tex} {tex} \therefore \vec { r } = ( 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex} which is the required equation of line in vector form.
solution_html:
Given equation of line is
6x - 2= 3y + 1 = 2z -2
or {tex} \frac { x - 2 / 6 } { 1 / 6 } = \frac { y + 1 / 3 } { 1 / 3 } = \frac { z - 2 / 2 } { 1 / 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - 1 / 3 } { 1 / 6 } = \frac { y + 1 / 3 } { 1 / 3 } = \frac { z - 1 } { 1 / 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - 1 / 3 } { 1 } = \frac { y + 1 / 3 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 1 } { 3 }{/tex}
[on dividing each by 6]
Here, DR's of the line are ( 1, 2, 3).
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction cosines of the line are
{tex} \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } } } \, ,\frac { 2 } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } } } \,,\frac { 3 } { \sqrt { ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 14 } } , \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 14 } } , \frac { 3 } { \sqrt { 14 } }{/tex}
The equation of a line passing through (2 -1, -1) and parallel to the given line is
{tex} \frac { x - 2 } { 1 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 2 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 3 } = \lambda ( \text { say } ){/tex}
{tex} \left[ \because \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c } \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 2 + \lambda , y = - 1 + 2 \lambda {/tex} and {tex}\ z = - 1 + 3 \lambda{/tex}
Now, {tex} x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k = (2 + \lambda )\hat i + ( - 1 + 2\lambda )\hat j + ( - 1 + 3\lambda )\hat k{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \vec { r } = ( 2 \hat { i } - \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } + 3 \hat { k } ){/tex} which is the required equation of line in vector form.

question_html: Write the vector equation of the line passing through the point (1, -2, - 3) and normal to the plane {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})=5{/tex}
question_text: Write the vector equation of the line passing through the point (1, -2, - 3) and normal to the plane {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})=5{/tex}
explanation: The required line is normal to the plane {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})=5{/tex} and it is parallel to the normal vector of the plane. Since, the required line is parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} It is given that the line passes through the point (1, -2, 3) whose position vector is given by {tex}\vec{a}-\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} We know that the equation of the line passing through the point whose position vector {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} is given by {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{b}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{r}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}
solution: The required line is normal to the plane {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})=5{/tex} and it is parallel to the normal vector of the plane. Since, the required line is parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} It is given that the line passes through the point (1, -2, 3) whose position vector is given by {tex}\vec{a}-\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} We know that the equation of the line passing through the point whose position vector {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} is given by {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{b}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{r}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}
solution_html:
The required line is normal to the plane {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})=5{/tex} and it is parallel to the normal vector of the plane.
Since, the required line is parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
It is given that the line passes through the point (1, -2, 3) whose position vector is given by {tex}\vec{a}-\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex}
We know that the equation of the line passing through the point whose position vector {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} is given by {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{b}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{r}=(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex}

question_html: Show that the lines {tex}\vec{r}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}+\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=5 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\mu(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} are intersecting. Hence, find their point of intersection.
question_text: Show that the lines {tex}\vec{r}=3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}+\lambda(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=5 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\mu(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} are intersecting. Hence, find their point of intersection.
explanation: The position vectors of two arbitrary points on the given lines are {tex}3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}+\lambda(i+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} = {tex}(3+\lambda) \hat{i}+(2+2 \lambda) \hat{j}+(2 \lambda-4) \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}5 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\mu(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} = {tex}(5+3 \mu) \hat{i}+(-2+2 \mu) \hat{j}+6 \mu \hat{k}{/tex} If the lines intersect, then they have a common point. So, for some values of {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}\mu{/tex} we must have, {tex}3+\lambda=5+3 \mu{/tex} .....(i) {tex}2+2 \lambda=-2+2 \mu{/tex} .....(ii) {tex}2 \lambda-4=6 \mu{/tex} .....(iii) Solving (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -4 {tex}\mu{/tex} = -2 Substituting the values {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -4 and {tex}\mu{/tex} = -2 in (iii), we get LHS = {tex}2 \lambda-4{/tex} = 2(-4) - 4 = -12 RHS = {tex}6 \mu{/tex} = 6(-2) = -12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} LHS = RHS Since {tex}\lambda=-4{/tex} and {tex}\mu=-2{/tex} satisfy (iii), the lines intersect. {tex}\mu=-2{/tex} in the second line, we get {tex}\vec{r}=5 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-12 \hat{k}{/tex} = {tex}-\hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-12 \hat{k}{/tex} as the position vector of the point of intersection. Thus, the coordinates of the point of intersection are (-1, -6, -12).
solution: The position vectors of two arbitrary points on the given lines are {tex}3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}+\lambda(i+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} = {tex}(3+\lambda) \hat{i}+(2+2 \lambda) \hat{j}+(2 \lambda-4) \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}5 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\mu(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} = {tex}(5+3 \mu) \hat{i}+(-2+2 \mu) \hat{j}+6 \mu \hat{k}{/tex} If the lines intersect, then they have a common point. So, for some values of {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}\mu{/tex} we must have, {tex}3+\lambda=5+3 \mu{/tex} .....(i) {tex}2+2 \lambda=-2+2 \mu{/tex} .....(ii) {tex}2 \lambda-4=6 \mu{/tex} .....(iii) Solving (i) and (ii), we get {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -4 {tex}\mu{/tex} = -2 Substituting the values {tex}\lambda{/tex} = -4 and {tex}\mu{/tex} = -2 in (iii), we get LHS = {tex}2 \lambda-4{/tex} = 2(-4) - 4 = -12 RHS = {tex}6 \mu{/tex} = 6(-2) = -12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} LHS = RHS Since {tex}\lambda=-4{/tex} and {tex}\mu=-2{/tex} satisfy (iii), the lines intersect. {tex}\mu=-2{/tex} in the second line, we get {tex}\vec{r}=5 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-12 \hat{k}{/tex} = {tex}-\hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-12 \hat{k}{/tex} as the position vector of the point of intersection. Thus, the coordinates of the point of intersection are (-1, -6, -12).
solution_html:
The position vectors of two arbitrary points on the given lines are
{tex}3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-4 \hat{k}+\lambda(i+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} = {tex}(3+\lambda) \hat{i}+(2+2 \lambda) \hat{j}+(2 \lambda-4) \hat{k}{/tex} and
{tex}5 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+\mu(3 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex} = {tex}(5+3 \mu) \hat{i}+(-2+2 \mu) \hat{j}+6 \mu \hat{k}{/tex} 
If the lines intersect, then they have a common point. So, for some values of {tex}\lambda{/tex} and {tex}\mu{/tex} we must have,
{tex}3+\lambda=5+3 \mu{/tex} .....(i)
{tex}2+2 \lambda=-2+2 \mu{/tex} .....(ii)
{tex}2 \lambda-4=6 \mu{/tex} .....(iii)
Solving (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = -4
{tex}\mu{/tex} = -2
Substituting the values
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = -4 and {tex}\mu{/tex} = -2 in (iii), we get
LHS = {tex}2 \lambda-4{/tex} 
= 2(-4) - 4
= -12
RHS = {tex}6 \mu{/tex} 
= 6(-2)
= -12
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} LHS = RHS 
Since {tex}\lambda=-4{/tex} and {tex}\mu=-2{/tex} satisfy (iii), the lines intersect.
{tex}\mu=-2{/tex} in the second line, we get
{tex}\vec{r}=5 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-12 \hat{k}{/tex} = {tex}-\hat{i}-6 \hat{j}-12 \hat{k}{/tex} as the position vector of the point of intersection.
Thus, the coordinates of the point of intersection are (-1, -6, -12).

question_html: If the line has direction ratios 2, -1, -2 determine its direction Cosines.
question_text: If the line has direction ratios 2, -1, -2 determine its direction Cosines.
explanation: a = 2, b = -1, c = -2 {tex}\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2} = \sqrt{2^2+(-1)^2+(-2)^2}=\sqrt{4+1+4}=3{/tex} Therefore, direction cosines are, {tex}\frac{2}{3},\frac{-1}{3},\frac{-2}{3}{/tex}
solution: a = 2, b = -1, c = -2 {tex}\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2} = \sqrt{2^2+(-1)^2+(-2)^2}=\sqrt{4+1+4}=3{/tex} Therefore, direction cosines are, {tex}\frac{2}{3},\frac{-1}{3},\frac{-2}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
a = 2, b = -1, c = -2

{tex}\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2} = \sqrt{2^2+(-1)^2+(-2)^2}=\sqrt{4+1+4}=3{/tex}

Therefore, direction cosines are, {tex}\frac{2}{3},\frac{-1}{3},\frac{-2}{3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The equations of a line are given by {tex}\frac{4-x}{3}=\frac{y+3}{3}=\frac{z+2}{6}{/tex}. Write the direction cosines of a line parallel to this line.
question_text: The equations of a line are given by {tex}\frac{4-x}{3}=\frac{y+3}{3}=\frac{z+2}{6}{/tex} . Write the direction cosines of a line parallel to this line.
explanation: We have {tex}\frac{4-x}{3}=\frac{y+3}{3}=\frac{z+2}{6}{/tex} The equation of the given line can be re-written as {tex}\frac{x-4}{-3}=\frac{y+3}{3}=\frac{z+2}{6}{/tex} The direction ratios of the line parallel to the given line are proportional to -3, 3, 6. Hence, the direction cosines of the line parallel to the given line are proportional to {tex}\frac{-3}{\sqrt{(-3)^{2}+3^{2}+6^{2}}}, \frac{3}{\sqrt{(-3)^{2}+3^{2}+6^{2}}}, \frac{6}{\sqrt{(-3)^{2}+3^{2}+6^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{\sqrt{6}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}, \frac{2}{\sqrt{6}}{/tex}
solution: We have {tex}\frac{4-x}{3}=\frac{y+3}{3}=\frac{z+2}{6}{/tex} The equation of the given line can be re-written as {tex}\frac{x-4}{-3}=\frac{y+3}{3}=\frac{z+2}{6}{/tex} The direction ratios of the line parallel to the given line are proportional to -3, 3, 6. Hence, the direction cosines of the line parallel to the given line are proportional to {tex}\frac{-3}{\sqrt{(-3)^{2}+3^{2}+6^{2}}}, \frac{3}{\sqrt{(-3)^{2}+3^{2}+6^{2}}}, \frac{6}{\sqrt{(-3)^{2}+3^{2}+6^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{\sqrt{6}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}, \frac{2}{\sqrt{6}}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have
{tex}\frac{4-x}{3}=\frac{y+3}{3}=\frac{z+2}{6}{/tex} 
The equation of the given line can be re-written as
{tex}\frac{x-4}{-3}=\frac{y+3}{3}=\frac{z+2}{6}{/tex} 
The direction ratios of the line parallel to the given line are proportional to -3, 3, 6.
Hence, the direction cosines of the line parallel to the given line are proportional to
{tex}\frac{-3}{\sqrt{(-3)^{2}+3^{2}+6^{2}}}, \frac{3}{\sqrt{(-3)^{2}+3^{2}+6^{2}}}, \frac{6}{\sqrt{(-3)^{2}+3^{2}+6^{2}}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{-1}{\sqrt{6}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}, \frac{2}{\sqrt{6}}{/tex}

question_html:
By computing the shortest distance determine whether the pairs of lines intersect or not:
{tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+\hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j})+\mu(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}
question_text: By computing the shortest distance determine whether the pairs of lines intersect or not: {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}-\hat{j})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+\hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j})+\mu(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}
explanation: Equation of line in vector form Line I: {tex}\vec{\mathbf{r}}=(\hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda(2 \hat{\imath}+0 \hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}){/tex} Line II: {tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{f}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}})+\mu(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} Here, {tex}\vec{a_{1}}=\hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}=2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+0 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} We know that the shortest distance between lines is d = {tex}\frac{|(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}})(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}})|}{|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|}{/tex} {tex}(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}){/tex} = {tex}(2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath})-(\hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}})=\hat{\imath}+0 \hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{1} & \hat{\jmath} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}=(0-1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}-(-2-1) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2-0) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}=-\hat{1}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|=\sqrt{(-1)^{2}+3^{2}+2^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}|=\sqrt{14}{/tex} {tex}|(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}})(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}})|{/tex} = {tex}|(\hat{\imath}+0 \hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{\mathrm{k}})(-\hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}})(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}})|=1{/tex} Substituting these values in the expression, d = {tex}\frac{|(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1})(\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2})|}{|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}|}{/tex} d = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}{/tex} d = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}{/tex} units Shortest distance d between the lines is not 0. Hence the given lines are not intersecting.
solution: Equation of line in vector form Line I: {tex}\vec{\mathbf{r}}=(\hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda(2 \hat{\imath}+0 \hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}){/tex} Line II: {tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{f}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}})+\mu(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} Here, {tex}\vec{a_{1}}=\hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}=2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+0 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} We know that the shortest distance between lines is d = {tex}\frac{|(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}})(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}})|}{|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|}{/tex} {tex}(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}){/tex} = {tex}(2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath})-(\hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{k}){/tex} {tex}(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}})=\hat{\imath}+0 \hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{1} & \hat{\jmath} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}=(0-1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}-(-2-1) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2-0) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}=-\hat{1}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|=\sqrt{(-1)^{2}+3^{2}+2^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}|=\sqrt{14}{/tex} {tex}|(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}})(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}})|{/tex} = {tex}|(\hat{\imath}+0 \hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{\mathrm{k}})(-\hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}})(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}})|=1{/tex} Substituting these values in the expression, d = {tex}\frac{|(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1})(\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2})|}{|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}|}{/tex} d = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}{/tex} d = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}{/tex} units Shortest distance d between the lines is not 0. Hence the given lines are not intersecting.
solution_html:
Equation of line in vector form
Line I: {tex}\vec{\mathbf{r}}=(\hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda(2 \hat{\imath}+0 \hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}){/tex} 
Line II: {tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{f}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}})+\mu(\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} 
Here,
{tex}\vec{a_{1}}=\hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{k}{/tex} 
{tex}\vec{a_{2}}=2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}{/tex} 
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+0 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} 
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}+\hat{\mathrm{j}}-\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} 
We know that the shortest distance between lines is
d = {tex}\frac{|(\vec{\mathrm{a}_{2}}-\vec{\mathrm{a}_{1}})(\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}})|}{|\vec{\mathrm{b}_{1}} \times \vec{\mathrm{b}_{2}}|}{/tex} 
{tex}(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}){/tex} = {tex}(2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath})-(\hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{k}){/tex} 
{tex}(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}})=\hat{\imath}+0 \hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{k}{/tex} 
{tex}\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{1} & \hat{\jmath} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & -1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} 
{tex}\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}=(0-1) \hat{\mathrm{i}}-(-2-1) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2-0) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}=-\hat{1}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} 
{tex}|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|=\sqrt{(-1)^{2}+3^{2}+2^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}|=\sqrt{14}{/tex} 
{tex}|(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}})(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}})|{/tex} = {tex}|(\hat{\imath}+0 \hat{\jmath}+0 \hat{\mathrm{k}})(-\hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow|(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}})(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}})|=1{/tex} 
Substituting these values in the expression,
d = {tex}\frac{|(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1})(\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2})|}{|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}|}{/tex} 
d = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}{/tex} 
d = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{14}}{/tex} units
Shortest distance d between the lines is not 0. Hence the given lines are not intersecting.

question_html: Find the equations of the line passing through the point (3, 0, 1) and parallel to the planes x + 2y = 0 and 3y - z = 0.
question_text: Find the equations of the line passing through the point (3, 0, 1) and parallel to the planes x + 2y = 0 and 3y - z = 0.
explanation: Equation of the two planes are x + 2y = 0 and 3y - z = 0. Let {tex}{\overrightarrow n _1}{/tex} and {tex}{\overrightarrow n _2}{/tex} be the normal to the two planes, respectively. {tex}\therefore {\overrightarrow n _1} = \hat i + 2\hat j{/tex} and {tex}{\overrightarrow n _2} = 3\hat j - \hat k{/tex} Since required line is parallel to the given two planes. Therefore, {tex}\overrightarrow b = {\overrightarrow n _1} \times {\overrightarrow n _2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&2&0 \\ 0&3&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \hat i( - 2) - \hat j( - 1) + \hat k(3){/tex} {tex} = - 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} So, the equation of the line through the point (3, 0, 1) and parallel to the given two planes is {tex}(x - 3)\hat i + (y - 0)\hat j + (z - 1)\hat k + \lambda ( - 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k){/tex} {tex}=(x - 3)\hat i + y\hat j + (z - 1)\hat k + \lambda ( - 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k){/tex}
solution: Equation of the two planes are x + 2y = 0 and 3y - z = 0. Let {tex}{\overrightarrow n _1}{/tex} and {tex}{\overrightarrow n _2}{/tex} be the normal to the two planes, respectively. {tex}\therefore {\overrightarrow n _1} = \hat i + 2\hat j{/tex} and {tex}{\overrightarrow n _2} = 3\hat j - \hat k{/tex} Since required line is parallel to the given two planes. Therefore, {tex}\overrightarrow b = {\overrightarrow n _1} \times {\overrightarrow n _2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&2&0 \\ 0&3&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \hat i( - 2) - \hat j( - 1) + \hat k(3){/tex} {tex} = - 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} So, the equation of the line through the point (3, 0, 1) and parallel to the given two planes is {tex}(x - 3)\hat i + (y - 0)\hat j + (z - 1)\hat k + \lambda ( - 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k){/tex} {tex}=(x - 3)\hat i + y\hat j + (z - 1)\hat k + \lambda ( - 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k){/tex}
solution_html:
Equation of the two planes are x + 2y = 0 and 3y - z = 0.
Let {tex}{\overrightarrow n _1}{/tex} and {tex}{\overrightarrow n _2}{/tex} be  the normal to the two planes, respectively.
{tex}\therefore {\overrightarrow n _1} = \hat i + 2\hat j{/tex} and {tex}{\overrightarrow n _2} = 3\hat j - \hat k{/tex}
Since required line is parallel to the given two planes.
Therefore, {tex}\overrightarrow b = {\overrightarrow n _1} \times {\overrightarrow n _2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ 1&2&0 \\ 0&3&{ - 1} \end{array}} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \hat i( - 2) - \hat j( - 1) + \hat k(3){/tex}
{tex} = - 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}
So, the equation of the line through the point (3, 0, 1) and parallel to the given two planes is 
{tex}(x - 3)\hat i + (y - 0)\hat j + (z - 1)\hat k + \lambda ( - 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k){/tex}
{tex}=(x - 3)\hat i + y\hat j + (z - 1)\hat k + \lambda ( - 2\hat i + \hat j + 3\hat k){/tex}

solution: Let {tex}\frac{x-9}{C_{1}}=\frac{y-6}{C_{2}}=\frac{z-6}{C_{3}}=t{/tex} x = a + c 1 t = a y = b + c 2 t = b c 1 = 0,c 2 = 0,c 3 = k {tex}\sqrt{c_{1}^{2}+c_{2}^{2}+c_{3}^{2}=1}{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{0+0+k^{2}}=1{/tex} k = 1 The direction cosines of a line parallel to z-axis(0, 0, 1).

x = a + c1t = a

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If the equations of a line are {tex}\frac{3-x}{-3}=\frac{y+2}{-2}=\frac{z+2}{6}{/tex}, find the direction cosines of a line parallel to the given line.
question_text: If the equations of a line are {tex}\frac{3-x}{-3}=\frac{y+2}{-2}=\frac{z+2}{6}{/tex} , find the direction cosines of a line parallel to the given line.
explanation: Here,it is given that the line is {tex}\frac{x-3}{3}=\frac{y+2}{-2}=\frac{z+2}{6}{/tex} Parallel lines have same direction ratios and direction cosines Direction cosines of the line {tex}\frac{3}{\sqrt{(3)^{2}+(-2)^{2}+(6)^{2}}}, \frac{-2}{\sqrt{(3)^{2}+(-2)^{2}+(6)^{2}}}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{6}{\sqrt{(3)^{2}+(-2)^{2}+(6)^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{9+4+36}}, \frac{-2}{\sqrt{9+4+36}}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{6}{\sqrt{9+4+36}}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{49}}, \frac{-2}{\sqrt{49}}, \frac{6}{\sqrt{49}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{7}, \frac{-2}{7}, \frac{6}{7}{/tex} Therefore,Direction cosines of the line parallel to the line {tex}\frac{x-3}{-3}=\frac{y+2}{-2}=\frac{z+2}{6}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{3}{7}, \frac{-2}{7}, \frac{6}{7}{/tex}
solution: Here,it is given that the line is {tex}\frac{x-3}{3}=\frac{y+2}{-2}=\frac{z+2}{6}{/tex} Parallel lines have same direction ratios and direction cosines Direction cosines of the line {tex}\frac{3}{\sqrt{(3)^{2}+(-2)^{2}+(6)^{2}}}, \frac{-2}{\sqrt{(3)^{2}+(-2)^{2}+(6)^{2}}}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{6}{\sqrt{(3)^{2}+(-2)^{2}+(6)^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{9+4+36}}, \frac{-2}{\sqrt{9+4+36}}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{6}{\sqrt{9+4+36}}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{49}}, \frac{-2}{\sqrt{49}}, \frac{6}{\sqrt{49}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3}{7}, \frac{-2}{7}, \frac{6}{7}{/tex} Therefore,Direction cosines of the line parallel to the line {tex}\frac{x-3}{-3}=\frac{y+2}{-2}=\frac{z+2}{6}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{3}{7}, \frac{-2}{7}, \frac{6}{7}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here,it is given that the line is {tex}\frac{x-3}{3}=\frac{y+2}{-2}=\frac{z+2}{6}{/tex}
Parallel lines have same direction ratios and direction cosines
Direction cosines of the line 
{tex}\frac{3}{\sqrt{(3)^{2}+(-2)^{2}+(6)^{2}}}, \frac{-2}{\sqrt{(3)^{2}+(-2)^{2}+(6)^{2}}}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{6}{\sqrt{(3)^{2}+(-2)^{2}+(6)^{2}}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{9+4+36}}, \frac{-2}{\sqrt{9+4+36}}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{6}{\sqrt{9+4+36}}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{49}}, \frac{-2}{\sqrt{49}}, \frac{6}{\sqrt{49}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{3}{7}, \frac{-2}{7}, \frac{6}{7}{/tex}
Therefore,Direction cosines of the line parallel to the line {tex}\frac{x-3}{-3}=\frac{y+2}{-2}=\frac{z+2}{6}{/tex} is
{tex}\frac{3}{7}, \frac{-2}{7}, \frac{6}{7}{/tex}
