question_html: Find the coordinates of the foot of perpendicular and the length of the perpendicular drawn from the point P(5, 4, 2) to the line {tex}\vec{r}=(-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}{tex}+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}. Also, find the image of P in this line.
question_text: Find the coordinates of the foot of perpendicular and the length of the perpendicular drawn from the point P(5, 4, 2) to the line {tex}\vec{r}=(-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} {tex}+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} . Also, find the image of P in this line.
explanation: According to question , the vector equation of the given line is {tex}\vec{r}=(-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} Clearly, it passes through the point (-1, 3, 1) and it has direction ratios 2, 3, -1. So, its Cartesian equations are {tex}\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y-3}{3}=\frac{z-1}{-1}=r{/tex} (say) The general point on this line is (2r -1, 3r + 3, - r +1) Suppose N be the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point P(5, 4, 2) on the given line. Then, this point is N(2r -1, 3r + 3, - r +1) for some fixed value of r. D.r.'s of PN are(2r - 6, 3r -1, - r -1). Direction ratios of the given line are 2, 3, -1. Since PN is perpendicular to the given line (i), we have 2(2r - 6) + 3(3r -1) -1 • (-r -1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 14r = 14 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 1 So, the point N is given by N(l, 6, 0). Hence, the foot of the perpendicular from the given point P(5,4,2) on the given line is N( 1, 6,0). Suppose Q {tex}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma){/tex} be the image of P(5,4, 2) in the given line. Then, N( 1, 6, 0) is the midpoint of PQ {tex}\therefore \frac{5+\alpha}{2}=1, \frac{4+\beta}{2}=6{/tex} and {tex}\frac{2+y}{2}=0 \Rightarrow \alpha=-3{/tex} , {tex}\beta=8{/tex} and {tex}\gamma=-2{/tex}
solution: According to question , the vector equation of the given line is {tex}\vec{r}=(-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex} Clearly, it passes through the point (-1, 3, 1) and it has direction ratios 2, 3, -1. So, its Cartesian equations are {tex}\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y-3}{3}=\frac{z-1}{-1}=r{/tex} (say) The general point on this line is (2r -1, 3r + 3, - r +1) Suppose N be the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point P(5, 4, 2) on the given line. Then, this point is N(2r -1, 3r + 3, - r +1) for some fixed value of r. D.r.'s of PN are(2r - 6, 3r -1, - r -1). Direction ratios of the given line are 2, 3, -1. Since PN is perpendicular to the given line (i), we have 2(2r - 6) + 3(3r -1) -1 • (-r -1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 14r = 14 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 1 So, the point N is given by N(l, 6, 0). Hence, the foot of the perpendicular from the given point P(5,4,2) on the given line is N( 1, 6,0). Suppose Q {tex}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma){/tex} be the image of P(5,4, 2) in the given line. Then, N( 1, 6, 0) is the midpoint of PQ {tex}\therefore \frac{5+\alpha}{2}=1, \frac{4+\beta}{2}=6{/tex} and {tex}\frac{2+y}{2}=0 \Rightarrow \alpha=-3{/tex} , {tex}\beta=8{/tex} and {tex}\gamma=-2{/tex}
solution_html:
According to question , the vector equation of the given line is
{tex}\vec{r}=(-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}
Clearly, it passes through the point  (-1, 3, 1) and it has direction ratios 2, 3, -1.
So, its Cartesian equations are
{tex}\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y-3}{3}=\frac{z-1}{-1}=r{/tex} (say)
The general point on this line is (2r -1, 3r + 3, - r +1)
Suppose N be the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point P(5, 4, 2) on the given line.
Then, this point is N(2r -1, 3r + 3, - r +1) for some fixed value of r. D.r.'s of PN are(2r - 6, 3r -1, - r -1).
Direction ratios of the given line are 2, 3, -1.
Since PN is perpendicular to the given line (i), we have
2(2r - 6) + 3(3r -1) -1 • (-r -1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 14r = 14 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 1
So, the point N is given by N(l, 6, 0).
Hence, the foot of the perpendicular from the given point P(5,4,2) on the given line is N( 1, 6,0).
Suppose Q {tex}(\alpha, \beta, \gamma){/tex} be the image of P(5,4, 2) in the given line.
Then, N( 1, 6, 0) is the midpoint of PQ
{tex}\therefore \frac{5+\alpha}{2}=1, \frac{4+\beta}{2}=6{/tex} and {tex}\frac{2+y}{2}=0 \Rightarrow \alpha=-3{/tex},{tex}\beta=8{/tex} and {tex}\gamma=-2{/tex}

question_html: The Cartesian equation of a line is {tex}\frac{{x - 5}}{3} = \frac{{y + 4}}{7} = \frac{{z - 6}}{2}{/tex}. Write its vector form.
question_text: The Cartesian equation of a line is {tex}\frac{{x - 5}}{3} = \frac{{y + 4}}{7} = \frac{{z - 6}}{2}{/tex} . Write its vector form.
explanation: {tex}\vec r = \vec a + \lambda \vec b{/tex} {tex}\vec a = 5\hat i - 4\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec b = 3\hat i + 7\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec r = \left( {5\hat i - 4\hat j + 6\hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {3\hat i + 7\hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex}
solution: {tex}\vec r = \vec a + \lambda \vec b{/tex} {tex}\vec a = 5\hat i - 4\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec b = 3\hat i + 7\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}\vec r = \left( {5\hat i - 4\hat j + 6\hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {3\hat i + 7\hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\vec r = \vec a + \lambda \vec b{/tex}

{tex}\vec a = 5\hat i - 4\hat j + 6\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\vec b = 3\hat i + 7\hat j + 2\hat k{/tex}

{tex}\vec r = \left( {5\hat i - 4\hat j + 6\hat k} \right) + \lambda \left( {3\hat i + 7\hat j + 2\hat k} \right){/tex}

question_html:
Find the shortest distance between the lines whose vector equations  are
{tex}\overrightarrow r = (1 - t)\hat i + (t - 2)\hat j + (3 - 2t)\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow r = (s + 1)\hat i + (2s - 1)\hat j - (2s + 1)\hat k{/tex}
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the lines whose vector equations are {tex}\overrightarrow r = (1 - t)\hat i + (t - 2)\hat j + (3 - 2t)\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow r = (s + 1)\hat i + (2s - 1)\hat j - (2s + 1)\hat k{/tex}
explanation: {tex}\vec r=\hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k + t( - \hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k){/tex} {tex}\vec r = \hat i - \hat j - \hat k + s(\hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {{a_1}} = \hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {{b_1}} = - \hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\overrightarrow a _2} = \hat i - \hat j - \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\overrightarrow b _2} = \hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\overrightarrow a _2} - \overrightarrow {{a_1}} = \hat j - 4\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times {\hat b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ { - 1}&1&{ - 2} \\ 1&2&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = 2\hat i - 4\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}(\vec a_2-\vec a_1).(\vec b_1×\vec b_2)=(0\vec i+\vec j-4\vec k).(2\vec i-4\vec j-3\vec k)=0-4+12=8{/tex} {tex}\left| {{{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2}} \right| = \sqrt {{{(2)}^2} + {{( - 4)}^2} + {{( - 3)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {29} {/tex} {tex}d = \left| {\frac{{({{\overrightarrow a }_2} - {{\overrightarrow a }_1})({{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2})}}{{\left| {{{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2}} \right|}}} \right| = \frac{8}{{\sqrt {29} }}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\vec r=\hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k + t( - \hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k){/tex} {tex}\vec r = \hat i - \hat j - \hat k + s(\hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k){/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {{a_1}} = \hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {{b_1}} = - \hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\overrightarrow a _2} = \hat i - \hat j - \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\overrightarrow b _2} = \hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\overrightarrow a _2} - \overrightarrow {{a_1}} = \hat j - 4\hat k{/tex} {tex}\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times {\hat b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ { - 1}&1&{ - 2} \\ 1&2&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = 2\hat i - 4\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}(\vec a_2-\vec a_1).(\vec b_1×\vec b_2)=(0\vec i+\vec j-4\vec k).(2\vec i-4\vec j-3\vec k)=0-4+12=8{/tex} {tex}\left| {{{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2}} \right| = \sqrt {{{(2)}^2} + {{( - 4)}^2} + {{( - 3)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {29} {/tex} {tex}d = \left| {\frac{{({{\overrightarrow a }_2} - {{\overrightarrow a }_1})({{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2})}}{{\left| {{{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2}} \right|}}} \right| = \frac{8}{{\sqrt {29} }}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\vec r=\hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k + t( - \hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k){/tex}
{tex}\vec r = \hat i - \hat j - \hat k + s(\hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k){/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow {{a_1}} = \hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow {{b_1}} = - \hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\overrightarrow a _2} = \hat i - \hat j - \hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\overrightarrow b _2} = \hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\overrightarrow a _2} - \overrightarrow {{a_1}} = \hat j - 4\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow {{b_1}} \times {\hat b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ { - 1}&1&{ - 2} \\ 1&2&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = 2\hat i - 4\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex}

{tex}(\vec a_2-\vec a_1).(\vec b_1×\vec b_2)=(0\vec i+\vec j-4\vec k).(2\vec i-4\vec j-3\vec k)=0-4+12=8{/tex}
{tex}\left| {{{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2}} \right| = \sqrt {{{(2)}^2} + {{( - 4)}^2} + {{( - 3)}^2}} {/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {29} {/tex}
{tex}d = \left| {\frac{{({{\overrightarrow a }_2} - {{\overrightarrow a }_1})({{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2})}}{{\left| {{{\overrightarrow b }_1} \times {{\overrightarrow b }_2}} \right|}}} \right| = \frac{8}{{\sqrt {29} }}{/tex}

question_html: Find the angle between following pair of lines. {tex} \frac { - x + 2 } { - 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { 7 } = \frac { z + 3 } { - 3 }{/tex} and {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { - 1 } = \frac { 2 y - 8 } { 4 } = \frac { z - 5 } { 4 }{/tex} and check whether the lines are parallel or perpendicular.
question_text: Find the angle between following pair of lines. {tex} \frac { - x + 2 } { - 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { 7 } = \frac { z + 3 } { - 3 }{/tex} and {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { - 1 } = \frac { 2 y - 8 } { 4 } = \frac { z - 5 } { 4 }{/tex} and check whether the lines are parallel or perpendicular.
explanation: We have to find the angle between following pair of lines. {tex} \frac { - x + 2 } { - 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { 7 } = \frac { z + 3 } { - 3 }{/tex} and {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { - 1 } = \frac { 2 y - 8 } { 4 } = \frac { z - 5 } { 4 }{/tex} . Also,we are required to check whether the lines are parallel or perpendicular to each other. Firstly, we convert the given lines in standard form and then use the formula {tex} \cos \theta = \frac { a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } } { \sqrt { a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \sqrt { a _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} to get the required angle Now, given equations of two lines are {tex} \frac { - x + 2 } { - 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { 7 } = \frac { z + 3 } { - 3 }{/tex} and {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { - 1 } = \frac { 2 y - 8 } { 4 } = \frac { z - 5 } { 4 }{/tex} Above equations can be written as {tex} \frac { x - 2 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { 7 } = \frac { z + 3 } { - 3 }{/tex} ....................(i) and {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { - 1 } = \frac { y - 4 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 5 } { 4 }{/tex} ............(ii) On comparing Equations. (i) and (ii) with one point form of equation of line {tex} \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c } ,{/tex} we get a 1 = 2, b 1 = 7, c 1 = -3 and a 2 = -1, b 2 = 2, c 2 = 4 We know that, angle between two lines is given by {tex} \cos \theta = \frac { a a _ { 2 } + b b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } } { \sqrt { a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \cdot \sqrt { a _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \cos \theta = \frac{{(2)( - 1) + (7)(2) + ( - 3)(4)}}{{\left[ {\sqrt {{{(2)}^2} + {{(7)}^2} + {{( - 3)}^2}} \cdot\sqrt {{{( - 1)}^2} + {{(2)}^2} + {{(4)}^2}} } \right]}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { - 2 + 14 - 12 } { \sqrt { 62 } \times \sqrt { 21 } } = \frac { 0 } { \sqrt { 62 } \times \sqrt { 21 } } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because 0 = \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right]{/tex} Hence, the angle between them is {tex} \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} . Therefore, the given pair of lines are perpendicular to each other.

Therefore, the given pair of lines are perpendicular to each other.

Above equations can be written as
{tex} \frac { x - 2 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { 7 } = \frac { z + 3 } { - 3 }{/tex}....................(i)
and {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { - 1 } = \frac { y - 4 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 5 } { 4 }{/tex}............(ii)
On comparing Equations. (i) and (ii) with one point form of equation of line
{tex} \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c } ,{/tex}we get
a1 = 2, b1 = 7, c1 = -3
and  a2 = -1, b2 = 2, c2 = 4
We know that, angle between two lines is given 
by {tex} \cos \theta = \frac { a a _ { 2 } + b b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } } { \sqrt { a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \cdot \sqrt { a _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad \cos \theta = \frac{{(2)( - 1) + (7)(2) + ( - 3)(4)}}{{\left[ {\sqrt {{{(2)}^2} + {{(7)}^2} + {{( - 3)}^2}} \cdot\sqrt {{{( - 1)}^2} + {{(2)}^2} + {{(4)}^2}} } \right]}}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \frac { - 2 + 14 - 12 } { \sqrt { 62 } \times \sqrt { 21 } } = \frac { 0 } { \sqrt { 62 } \times \sqrt { 21 } } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta = \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because 0 = \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right]{/tex}
Hence, the angle between them is {tex} \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}.
Therefore, the given pair of lines are perpendicular to each other.

question_html: Find the equation of the perpendicular from point (3, -1,11) to line {tex} \frac { x } { 2 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 4 }{/tex} . Also, find the coordinates of foot of perpendicular and the length of perpendicular.
question_text: Find the equation of the perpendicular from point (3, -1,11) to line {tex} \frac { x } { 2 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 4 }{/tex} . Also, find the coordinates of foot of perpendicular and the length of perpendicular.
explanation: Firstly, determine any point P on the given line and DR's between given point Q and P, using the relation a 1 a 2 +b 1 b 2 +c 1 c 2 =0, where (a 1 , b 1, c 1 ) and (a 2 , b 2 , c 2 ) and Direction ratio's of PQ and given line. Given equation of line AB is {tex} \frac { x } { 2 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 4 } = \lambda ( \text { (say) }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { x } { 2 } = \lambda ,\frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \lambda{/tex} and {tex} \quad \frac { z - 3 } { 4 } = \lambda{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 2 \lambda , y = 3 \lambda + 2{/tex} and {tex} z = 4 \lambda + 3{/tex} :. Any point P on the given line {tex} = ( 2 \lambda , 3\lambda + 2,4 \lambda + 3 ){/tex} Let P be the foot of perpendicular drawn from point Q(3, -1,11) on line AB Now, DR's of line {tex}Q P = ( 2 \lambda - 3,3 \lambda + 2 + 1,4 \lambda + 3 - 11 ){/tex} {tex}= ( 2 \lambda - 3,3 \lambda + 3,4 \lambda - 8 ){/tex} Here, {tex}a _ { 1 } = 2 \lambda - 3 , b _ { 1 } = 3 \lambda + 3 , c _ { 1 } = 4 \lambda - 8{/tex} and {tex}a _ { 2 } = 2, b _ { 2 } = 3 , c _ { 2 } = 4{/tex} Since, {tex}Q P \perp A B{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} We have, a 1 a 2 + b 1 b 2 + c 1 c 2 = 0 ............(i) {tex}\Rightarrow 2 ( 2 \lambda - 3 ) + 3 ( 3 \lambda + 3 ) + 4 ( 4 \lambda - 8 ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 \lambda - 6 + 9 \lambda + 9 + 16 \lambda - 32 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 29 \lambda - 29 = 0 \Rightarrow 29 \lambda = 29 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Foot of perpendicular is {tex}P = ( 2,3 + 2,4 + 3 ){/tex} {tex}= ( 2,5,7 ){/tex} Now, equation of perpendicular QP, where Q(3, -1, 11) and P(2, 5, 7), is {tex}\frac { x - 3 } { 2 - 3 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 5 + 1 } = \frac { z - 11 } { 7 - 11 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c } { \text { using two point form of equation of line, } } \\ { \text { i.e. } \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { z _ { 2 } - z _ { 1 } } } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - 3 } { - 1 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 6 } = \frac { z - 11 } { - 4 }{/tex} Now, length of perpendicular QP = distance between points Q(3, -1, 11) and P (2, 5, 7) {tex}= \sqrt { ( 2 - 3 )^ 2 + ( 5 + 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 7 - 11 ) ^2 }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 1 + 36 + 16 } = \sqrt { 53 }{/tex} Hence, the length of perpendicular is {tex}\sqrt { 53 }.{/tex}
solution: Firstly, determine any point P on the given line and DR's between given point Q and P, using the relation a 1 a 2 +b 1 b 2 +c 1 c 2 =0, where (a 1 , b 1, c 1 ) and (a 2 , b 2 , c 2 ) and Direction ratio's of PQ and given line. Given equation of line AB is {tex} \frac { x } { 2 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 4 } = \lambda ( \text { (say) }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { x } { 2 } = \lambda ,\frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \lambda{/tex} and {tex} \quad \frac { z - 3 } { 4 } = \lambda{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 2 \lambda , y = 3 \lambda + 2{/tex} and {tex} z = 4 \lambda + 3{/tex} :. Any point P on the given line {tex} = ( 2 \lambda , 3\lambda + 2,4 \lambda + 3 ){/tex} Let P be the foot of perpendicular drawn from point Q(3, -1,11) on line AB Now, DR's of line {tex}Q P = ( 2 \lambda - 3,3 \lambda + 2 + 1,4 \lambda + 3 - 11 ){/tex} {tex}= ( 2 \lambda - 3,3 \lambda + 3,4 \lambda - 8 ){/tex} Here, {tex}a _ { 1 } = 2 \lambda - 3 , b _ { 1 } = 3 \lambda + 3 , c _ { 1 } = 4 \lambda - 8{/tex} and {tex}a _ { 2 } = 2, b _ { 2 } = 3 , c _ { 2 } = 4{/tex} Since, {tex}Q P \perp A B{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} We have, a 1 a 2 + b 1 b 2 + c 1 c 2 = 0 ............(i) {tex}\Rightarrow 2 ( 2 \lambda - 3 ) + 3 ( 3 \lambda + 3 ) + 4 ( 4 \lambda - 8 ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 \lambda - 6 + 9 \lambda + 9 + 16 \lambda - 32 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 29 \lambda - 29 = 0 \Rightarrow 29 \lambda = 29 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Foot of perpendicular is {tex}P = ( 2,3 + 2,4 + 3 ){/tex} {tex}= ( 2,5,7 ){/tex} Now, equation of perpendicular QP, where Q(3, -1, 11) and P(2, 5, 7), is {tex}\frac { x - 3 } { 2 - 3 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 5 + 1 } = \frac { z - 11 } { 7 - 11 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c } { \text { using two point form of equation of line, } } \\ { \text { i.e. } \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { z _ { 2 } - z _ { 1 } } } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - 3 } { - 1 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 6 } = \frac { z - 11 } { - 4 }{/tex} Now, length of perpendicular QP = distance between points Q(3, -1, 11) and P (2, 5, 7) {tex}= \sqrt { ( 2 - 3 )^ 2 + ( 5 + 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 7 - 11 ) ^2 }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 1 + 36 + 16 } = \sqrt { 53 }{/tex} Hence, the length of perpendicular is {tex}\sqrt { 53 }.{/tex}
solution_html:
Firstly, determine any point P on the given line and DR's between given point Q and P,
using the relation a1a2+b1 b2+c1 c2=0, where (a1, b1, c1) and (a2, b2, c2) and Direction ratio's of PQ and given line.
Given equation of line AB is
{tex} \frac { x } { 2 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \frac { z - 3 } { 4 } = \lambda ( \text { (say) }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { x } { 2 } = \lambda ,\frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \lambda{/tex}
and {tex} \quad \frac { z - 3 } { 4 } = \lambda{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x = 2 \lambda , y = 3 \lambda + 2{/tex}
and {tex} z = 4 \lambda + 3{/tex}
:. Any point P on the given line
{tex} = ( 2 \lambda , 3\lambda + 2,4 \lambda + 3 ){/tex}

Let P be the foot of perpendicular drawn from point Q(3, -1,11) on line AB Now, DR's of line
{tex}Q P = ( 2 \lambda - 3,3 \lambda + 2 + 1,4 \lambda + 3 - 11 ){/tex}
{tex}= ( 2 \lambda - 3,3 \lambda + 3,4 \lambda - 8 ){/tex}
Here, {tex}a _ { 1 } = 2 \lambda - 3 , b _ { 1 } = 3 \lambda + 3 , c _ { 1 } = 4 \lambda - 8{/tex}
and {tex}a _ { 2 } = 2, b _ { 2 } = 3 , c _ { 2 } = 4{/tex}
Since, {tex}Q P \perp A B{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} We have, a1a2 + b1b2 + c1c2 = 0 ............(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 ( 2 \lambda - 3 ) + 3 ( 3 \lambda + 3 ) + 4 ( 4 \lambda - 8 ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 \lambda - 6 + 9 \lambda + 9 + 16 \lambda - 32 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 29 \lambda - 29 = 0 \Rightarrow 29 \lambda = 29 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Foot of perpendicular  is {tex}P = ( 2,3 + 2,4 + 3 ){/tex}
{tex}= ( 2,5,7 ){/tex}
Now, equation of perpendicular QP, where Q(3, -1, 11) and P(2, 5, 7), is
{tex}\frac { x - 3 } { 2 - 3 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 5 + 1 } = \frac { z - 11 } { 7 - 11 }{/tex}
{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c } { \text { using two point form of equation of line, } } \\ { \text { i.e. } \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { z _ { 2 } - z _ { 1 } } } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - 3 } { - 1 } = \frac { y + 1 } { 6 } = \frac { z - 11 } { - 4 }{/tex}
Now, length of perpendicular QP = distance between points Q(3, -1, 11) and P (2, 5, 7)
{tex}= \sqrt { ( 2 - 3 )^ 2 + ( 5 + 1 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 7 - 11 ) ^2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { 1 + 36 + 16 } = \sqrt { 53 }{/tex}
Hence, the length of perpendicular is {tex}\sqrt { 53 }.{/tex}

question_html: Find the direction ratios and the direction cosines of the line segment joining the points: A(1, 0, 0) and B(0, 1,1).
question_text: Find the direction ratios and the direction cosines of the line segment joining the points: A(1, 0, 0) and B(0, 1,1).
explanation: According to the question the direction ratios of line joining the points {tex}(1, 0, 0) and (0, 1, 1){/tex} are {tex}(0 -1 , 1 -0\ and\ 1 -0){/tex} , i.e. -1, 1 and 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction cosines are {tex}\frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } } } {/tex} and {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} i.e., {tex}\frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } {/tex} and {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex}
solution: According to the question the direction ratios of line joining the points {tex}(1, 0, 0) and (0, 1, 1){/tex} are {tex}(0 -1 , 1 -0\ and\ 1 -0){/tex} , i.e. -1, 1 and 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction cosines are {tex}\frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } } } {/tex} and {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} i.e., {tex}\frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } {/tex} and {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question the direction ratios of line joining the points {tex}(1, 0, 0) and (0, 1, 1){/tex} are {tex}(0 -1 , 1 -0\ and\ 1 -0){/tex},
i.e. -1, 1 and 1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Direction cosines are{tex}\frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
{tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } } } {/tex} and {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { ( - 1 ) ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } + 1 ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
i.e.,{tex}\frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } {/tex} and {tex}\frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } }{/tex}

question_html: Equation of line is {tex} \frac { 4 - x } { 2 } = \frac { y + 3 } { 2 } = \frac { z + 2 } { 1 }{/tex}. Find the direction cosines of a line parallel to above line.
question_text: Equation of line is {tex} \frac { 4 - x } { 2 } = \frac { y + 3 } { 2 } = \frac { z + 2 } { 1 }{/tex} . Find the direction cosines of a line parallel to above line.
explanation: According to the question, equation of line can be written as {tex} \frac { x - 4 } { - 2 } = \frac { y + 3 } { 2 } = \frac { z + 2 } { 1 }{/tex} Here, Direction ratios of a line are (-2,2,1). Direction cosines of a line parallel to above line are given by {tex} \left( {\frac{{ - 2}}{{\sqrt {{{( - 2)}^2} + {{(2)}^2} + {{(1)}^2}} }},\frac{2}{{\sqrt {{{( - 2)}^2} + {{(2)}^2} + {{(1)}^2}} }},\frac{1}{{\sqrt {{{( - 2)}^2} + {{(2)}^2} + {{(1)}^2}} }}} \right){/tex} {tex} = \left( \frac { - 2 } { \sqrt { 4 + 4 + 1 } } , \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 4 + 4 + 1 } } , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 4 + 4 + 1 } } \right){/tex} {tex} = \left( \frac { - 2 } { \sqrt { 9 } } , \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 9 } } , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 9 } } \right) = \left( - \frac { 2 } { 3 } , \frac { 2 } { 3 } , \frac { 1 } { 3 } \right){/tex} Required Direction cosines of a line parallel to the given line are {tex} \left( -\frac { 2 } { 3 } , \frac { 2 } { 3 } , \frac { 1 } { 3 } \right){/tex} .
solution: According to the question, equation of line can be written as {tex} \frac { x - 4 } { - 2 } = \frac { y + 3 } { 2 } = \frac { z + 2 } { 1 }{/tex} Here, Direction ratios of a line are (-2,2,1). Direction cosines of a line parallel to above line are given by {tex} \left( {\frac{{ - 2}}{{\sqrt {{{( - 2)}^2} + {{(2)}^2} + {{(1)}^2}} }},\frac{2}{{\sqrt {{{( - 2)}^2} + {{(2)}^2} + {{(1)}^2}} }},\frac{1}{{\sqrt {{{( - 2)}^2} + {{(2)}^2} + {{(1)}^2}} }}} \right){/tex} {tex} = \left( \frac { - 2 } { \sqrt { 4 + 4 + 1 } } , \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 4 + 4 + 1 } } , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 4 + 4 + 1 } } \right){/tex} {tex} = \left( \frac { - 2 } { \sqrt { 9 } } , \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 9 } } , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 9 } } \right) = \left( - \frac { 2 } { 3 } , \frac { 2 } { 3 } , \frac { 1 } { 3 } \right){/tex} Required Direction cosines of a line parallel to the given line are {tex} \left( -\frac { 2 } { 3 } , \frac { 2 } { 3 } , \frac { 1 } { 3 } \right){/tex} .
solution_html:
According to the question, equation of line can be written as
{tex} \frac { x - 4 } { - 2 } = \frac { y + 3 } { 2 } = \frac { z + 2 } { 1 }{/tex}
Here, Direction ratios of a line are (-2,2,1).
Direction cosines of a line parallel to above line are given by 
{tex} \left( {\frac{{ - 2}}{{\sqrt {{{( - 2)}^2} + {{(2)}^2} + {{(1)}^2}} }},\frac{2}{{\sqrt {{{( - 2)}^2} + {{(2)}^2} + {{(1)}^2}} }},\frac{1}{{\sqrt {{{( - 2)}^2} + {{(2)}^2} + {{(1)}^2}} }}} \right){/tex} 
{tex} = \left( \frac { - 2 } { \sqrt { 4 + 4 + 1 } } , \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 4 + 4 + 1 } } , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 4 + 4 + 1 } } \right){/tex}
{tex} = \left( \frac { - 2 } { \sqrt { 9 } } , \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 9 } } , \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 9 } } \right) = \left( - \frac { 2 } { 3 } , \frac { 2 } { 3 } , \frac { 1 } { 3 } \right){/tex}
Required Direction cosines of a line parallel to the given line are {tex} \left( -\frac { 2 } { 3 } , \frac { 2 } { 3 } , \frac { 1 } { 3 } \right){/tex}.

question_text: If the equations of a line AB are {tex}\frac{3-x}{1}=\frac{y+2}{-2}=\frac{z-5}{4}{/tex} , write the direction ratios of a line parallel to AB.

{tex}\therefore{/tex} direction ratios of the line parallel to the given line are (-1, -2, 4).

question_html: Find the perpendicular distance of the point (1, 0, 0) from the line {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{-3}=\frac{z+10}{8}{/tex}. Also, find the coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular and the equation of the perpendicular.
question_text: Find the perpendicular distance of the point (1, 0, 0) from the line {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{-3}=\frac{z+10}{8}{/tex} . Also, find the coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular and the equation of the perpendicular.
explanation: Suppose the point (1, 0, 0) be P and the point through which the line passes be Q(1,-1,-10). The line is parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec{P Q}=0 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-10 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{b} \times \overrightarrow{P Q}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -3 & 8 \\ 0 & -1 & -10 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}38 \hat{i}+20 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{b} \times \vec{P Q}|=\sqrt{38^{2}+20^{2}+2^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1444+400+4}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1848}{/tex} d = {tex}\frac{|\vec{b} \times \vec{P Q}|}{|\vec{b}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{1848}}{\sqrt{77}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{24}{/tex} = {tex}2 \sqrt{6}{/tex} Suppose L be the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point P(1,0,0) to the given line- The coordinates of a general point on the line {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{-3}=\frac{z+10}{8}{/tex} are given by {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{-3}=\frac{z+10}{8}=\lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=2 \lambda+1{/tex} {tex}y=-3 \lambda-1{/tex} {tex}z=8 \lambda-10{/tex} Suppose the coordinates of L be {tex}(2 \lambda+1,-3 \lambda-1,8 \lambda-10){/tex} Since,The direction ratios of PL are proportional to, {tex}2 \lambda+1-1,-3 \lambda-1-0{/tex} , {tex}8 \lambda-10-0{/tex} , i.e., {tex}2 \lambda,-3 \lambda-1,8 \lambda-10{/tex} Since,The direction ratios of the given line are proportional to 2, -3, 8, but PL is perpendicular to the given line. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2(2 \lambda)-3(-3 \lambda-1)+8(8 \lambda-10){/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1 Substituting {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1 in {tex}(2 \lambda+1,-3 \lambda-1,8 \lambda-10){/tex} we get the coordinates of L as (3, -4, -2). Equation of the line PL is given by {tex}\frac{x-1}{3-1}=\frac{y-0}{-4-0}=\frac{z-0}{-2-0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y}{-2}=\frac{z}{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{r}=\hat{i}+\lambda(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}
solution: Suppose the point (1, 0, 0) be P and the point through which the line passes be Q(1,-1,-10). The line is parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex} Now, {tex}\vec{P Q}=0 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-10 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{b} \times \overrightarrow{P Q}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -3 & 8 \\ 0 & -1 & -10 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}38 \hat{i}+20 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{b} \times \vec{P Q}|=\sqrt{38^{2}+20^{2}+2^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1444+400+4}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{1848}{/tex} d = {tex}\frac{|\vec{b} \times \vec{P Q}|}{|\vec{b}|}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{1848}}{\sqrt{77}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{24}{/tex} = {tex}2 \sqrt{6}{/tex} Suppose L be the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point P(1,0,0) to the given line- The coordinates of a general point on the line {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{-3}=\frac{z+10}{8}{/tex} are given by {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{-3}=\frac{z+10}{8}=\lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=2 \lambda+1{/tex} {tex}y=-3 \lambda-1{/tex} {tex}z=8 \lambda-10{/tex} Suppose the coordinates of L be {tex}(2 \lambda+1,-3 \lambda-1,8 \lambda-10){/tex} Since,The direction ratios of PL are proportional to, {tex}2 \lambda+1-1,-3 \lambda-1-0{/tex} , {tex}8 \lambda-10-0{/tex} , i.e., {tex}2 \lambda,-3 \lambda-1,8 \lambda-10{/tex} Since,The direction ratios of the given line are proportional to 2, -3, 8, but PL is perpendicular to the given line. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2(2 \lambda)-3(-3 \lambda-1)+8(8 \lambda-10){/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1 Substituting {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1 in {tex}(2 \lambda+1,-3 \lambda-1,8 \lambda-10){/tex} we get the coordinates of L as (3, -4, -2). Equation of the line PL is given by {tex}\frac{x-1}{3-1}=\frac{y-0}{-4-0}=\frac{z-0}{-2-0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y}{-2}=\frac{z}{-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \vec{r}=\hat{i}+\lambda(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}
solution_html:
Suppose the point (1, 0, 0) be P and the point through which the line passes be Q(1,-1,-10). The line is parallel to the vector {tex}\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}{/tex} 
Now, 
{tex}\vec{P Q}=0 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-10 \hat{k}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \vec{b} \times \overrightarrow{P Q}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -3 & 8 \\ 0 & -1 & -10 \end{array}\right|{/tex} 
= {tex}38 \hat{i}+20 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{b} \times \vec{P Q}|=\sqrt{38^{2}+20^{2}+2^{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt{1444+400+4}{/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt{1848}{/tex} 
d = {tex}\frac{|\vec{b} \times \vec{P Q}|}{|\vec{b}|}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\sqrt{1848}}{\sqrt{77}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt{24}{/tex} 
= {tex}2 \sqrt{6}{/tex} 
Suppose L be the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point P(1,0,0) to the given line-

The coordinates of a general point on the line
{tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{-3}=\frac{z+10}{8}{/tex} are given by
{tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+1}{-3}=\frac{z+10}{8}=\lambda{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow x=2 \lambda+1{/tex} 
{tex}y=-3 \lambda-1{/tex} 
{tex}z=8 \lambda-10{/tex} 
Suppose the coordinates of L be
{tex}(2 \lambda+1,-3 \lambda-1,8 \lambda-10){/tex} 
Since,The direction ratios of PL are proportional to,
{tex}2 \lambda+1-1,-3 \lambda-1-0{/tex}, {tex}8 \lambda-10-0{/tex}, i.e., {tex}2 \lambda,-3 \lambda-1,8 \lambda-10{/tex} 
Since,The direction ratios of the given line are proportional to 2, -3, 8, but PL is perpendicular to the given line.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2(2 \lambda)-3(-3 \lambda-1)+8(8 \lambda-10){/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1 Substituting {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1 in {tex}(2 \lambda+1,-3 \lambda-1,8 \lambda-10){/tex} we get the coordinates of L as (3, -4, -2). Equation of the line PL is given by
{tex}\frac{x-1}{3-1}=\frac{y-0}{-4-0}=\frac{z-0}{-2-0}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y}{-2}=\frac{z}{-1}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \vec{r}=\hat{i}+\lambda(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}){/tex}

question_html:
Find the angle between the pairs of lines: 
{tex}\frac{-x+2}{-2}=\frac{y-1}{7}=\frac{z+3}{-3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x+2}{-1}=\frac{2 y-8}{4}=\frac{z-5}{4}{/tex}
question_text: Find the angle between the pairs of lines: {tex}\frac{-x+2}{-2}=\frac{y-1}{7}=\frac{z+3}{-3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x+2}{-1}=\frac{2 y-8}{4}=\frac{z-5}{4}{/tex}
explanation: We have to find the angle between following pair of lines. {tex} \frac { - x + 2 } { - 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { 7 } = \frac { z + 3 } { - 3 }{/tex} and {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { - 1 } = \frac { 2 y - 8 } { 4 } = \frac { z - 5 } { 4 }{/tex} . Also,we are required to check whether the lines are parallel or perpendicular to each other. Firstly, we convert the given lines in standard form and then use the formula {tex} \cos \theta = \frac { a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } } { \sqrt { a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \sqrt { a _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} to get the required angle Now, given equations of two lines are {tex} \frac { - x + 2 } { - 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { 7 } = \frac { z + 3 } { - 3 }{/tex} and {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { - 1 } = \frac { 2 y - 8 } { 4 } = \frac { z - 5 } { 4 }{/tex} Above equations can be written as {tex} \frac { x - 2 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { 7 } = \frac { z + 3 } { - 3 }{/tex} ...(i) and {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { - 1 } = \frac { y - 4 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 5 } { 4 }{/tex} ...(ii) On comparing Equations. (i) and (ii) with one point form of equation of line {tex} \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c } ,{/tex} we get a 1 = 2, b 1 = 7, c 1 = -3 and a 2 = -1, b 2 = 2, c 2 = 4 We know that, angle between two lines is given by {tex} \cos \theta = \frac { a_1 a _ { 2 } + b_1 b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } } { \sqrt { a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \cdot \sqrt { a _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \cos \theta = \frac{{(2)( - 1) + (7)(2) + ( - 3)(4)}}{{\left[ {\sqrt {{{(2)}^2} + {{(7)}^2} + {{( - 3)}^2}} \cdot\sqrt {{{( - 1)}^2} + {{(2)}^2} + {{(4)}^2}} } \right]}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac { - 2 + 14 - 12 } { \sqrt { 62 } \times \sqrt { 21 } } = \frac { 0 } { \sqrt { 62 } \times \sqrt { 21 } } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \theta = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} ... {tex} \left[ \because 0 = \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right]{/tex} Which is the required angle between the lines.
solution: We have to find the angle between following pair of lines. {tex} \frac { - x + 2 } { - 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { 7 } = \frac { z + 3 } { - 3 }{/tex} and {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { - 1 } = \frac { 2 y - 8 } { 4 } = \frac { z - 5 } { 4 }{/tex} . Also,we are required to check whether the lines are parallel or perpendicular to each other. Firstly, we convert the given lines in standard form and then use the formula {tex} \cos \theta = \frac { a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } } { \sqrt { a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \sqrt { a _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} to get the required angle Now, given equations of two lines are {tex} \frac { - x + 2 } { - 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { 7 } = \frac { z + 3 } { - 3 }{/tex} and {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { - 1 } = \frac { 2 y - 8 } { 4 } = \frac { z - 5 } { 4 }{/tex} Above equations can be written as {tex} \frac { x - 2 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { 7 } = \frac { z + 3 } { - 3 }{/tex} ...(i) and {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { - 1 } = \frac { y - 4 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 5 } { 4 }{/tex} ...(ii) On comparing Equations. (i) and (ii) with one point form of equation of line {tex} \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c } ,{/tex} we get a 1 = 2, b 1 = 7, c 1 = -3 and a 2 = -1, b 2 = 2, c 2 = 4 We know that, angle between two lines is given by {tex} \cos \theta = \frac { a_1 a _ { 2 } + b_1 b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } } { \sqrt { a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \cdot \sqrt { a _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \cos \theta = \frac{{(2)( - 1) + (7)(2) + ( - 3)(4)}}{{\left[ {\sqrt {{{(2)}^2} + {{(7)}^2} + {{( - 3)}^2}} \cdot\sqrt {{{( - 1)}^2} + {{(2)}^2} + {{(4)}^2}} } \right]}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac { - 2 + 14 - 12 } { \sqrt { 62 } \times \sqrt { 21 } } = \frac { 0 } { \sqrt { 62 } \times \sqrt { 21 } } = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \theta = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} ... {tex} \left[ \because 0 = \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right]{/tex} Which is the required angle between the lines.
solution_html:
We have to find the angle between following pair of lines. {tex} \frac { - x + 2 } { - 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { 7 } = \frac { z + 3 } { - 3 }{/tex} and {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { - 1 } = \frac { 2 y - 8 } { 4 } = \frac { z - 5 } { 4 }{/tex}. Also,we are required to check whether the lines are parallel or perpendicular to each other.
Firstly, we convert the given lines in standard form and then use the formula
{tex} \cos \theta = \frac { a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } } { \sqrt { a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \sqrt { a _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} to get the required angle
Now, given equations of two lines are
{tex} \frac { - x + 2 } { - 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { 7 } = \frac { z + 3 } { - 3 }{/tex}
and {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { - 1 } = \frac { 2 y - 8 } { 4 } = \frac { z - 5 } { 4 }{/tex}
Above equations can be written as
{tex} \frac { x - 2 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 1 } { 7 } = \frac { z + 3 } { - 3 }{/tex}   ...(i)
and {tex} \frac { x + 2 } { - 1 } = \frac { y - 4 } { 2 } = \frac { z - 5 } { 4 }{/tex}   ...(ii)
On comparing Equations. (i) and (ii) with one point form of equation of line
{tex} \frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c } ,{/tex}we get
a1 = 2, b1 = 7, c1 = -3
and  a2 = -1, b2 = 2, c2 = 4
We know that, angle between two lines is given 
by {tex} \cos \theta = \frac { a_1 a _ { 2 } + b_1 b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } } { \sqrt { a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \cdot \sqrt { a _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \cos \theta = \frac{{(2)( - 1) + (7)(2) + ( - 3)(4)}}{{\left[ {\sqrt {{{(2)}^2} + {{(7)}^2} + {{( - 3)}^2}} \cdot\sqrt {{{( - 1)}^2} + {{(2)}^2} + {{(4)}^2}} } \right]}}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac { - 2 + 14 - 12 } { \sqrt { 62 } \times \sqrt { 21 } } = \frac { 0 } { \sqrt { 62 } \times \sqrt { 21 } } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow \theta = \frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}    ...{tex} \left[ \because 0 = \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right]{/tex}
Which is the required angle between the lines.

question_html: Find the distance of the point with position vector {tex}-\hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-10 \hat{k}{/tex} from the point of intersection of the line {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})+\lambda(3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+12 \hat{k}){/tex} with the plane {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})=5{/tex}
question_text: Find the distance of the point with position vector {tex}-\hat{i}-5 \hat{j}-10 \hat{k}{/tex} from the point of intersection of the line {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k})+\lambda(3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}+12 \hat{k}){/tex} with the plane {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})=5{/tex}
explanation: Let P be the point with position vector {tex}\vec{p}=-\hat{\imath}-5 \hat{\jmath}-10 \hat{k}{/tex} and Q be the point of intersection of the given line and the plane. We have the line equation as {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda(3 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}+12 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(2+3 \lambda) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(-1+4 \lambda) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2+12 \lambda) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Let the position vector of Q be {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}{/tex} . As Q is a point on this line, for some scalar {tex}\alpha{/tex} , we have {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=(2+3 \alpha) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(-1+4 \alpha) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2+12 \alpha) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} This point Q also lies on the given plane, which means this point satisfies the plane equation {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})=5{/tex} . {tex}\Rightarrow[(2+3 \alpha) \hat{\mathbf{i}}+(-1+4 \alpha) \hat{\mathbf{j}}+(2+12 \alpha) \hat{\mathbf{k}}] \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}})=5{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2 + 3 {tex}\alpha{/tex} )(1) + (–1 + 4 {tex}\alpha{/tex} )(–1) + (2 + 12 {tex}\alpha{/tex} )(1) = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 + 3 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + 1 – 4 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + 2 + 12 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 11 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + 5 = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 11 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0 We have {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=(2+3 \alpha) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(-1+4 \alpha) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2+12 \alpha) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=[2+3(0)] \hat{\mathrm{i}}+[-1+4(0)] \hat{\mathrm{j}}+[2+12(0)] \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Using the distance formula, we have {tex}P Q=\sqrt{(2-(-1))^{2}+((-1)-(-5))^{2}+(2-(-10))^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{PQ}=\sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}+12^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{PQ}=\sqrt{9+16+144}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm{PQ}=\sqrt{169}=13{/tex} Thus, the required distance is 13 units.
solution: Let P be the point with position vector {tex}\vec{p}=-\hat{\imath}-5 \hat{\jmath}-10 \hat{k}{/tex} and Q be the point of intersection of the given line and the plane. We have the line equation as {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda(3 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}+12 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(2+3 \lambda) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(-1+4 \lambda) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2+12 \lambda) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Let the position vector of Q be {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}{/tex} . As Q is a point on this line, for some scalar {tex}\alpha{/tex} , we have {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=(2+3 \alpha) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(-1+4 \alpha) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2+12 \alpha) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} This point Q also lies on the given plane, which means this point satisfies the plane equation {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})=5{/tex} . {tex}\Rightarrow[(2+3 \alpha) \hat{\mathbf{i}}+(-1+4 \alpha) \hat{\mathbf{j}}+(2+12 \alpha) \hat{\mathbf{k}}] \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}})=5{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2 + 3 {tex}\alpha{/tex} )(1) + (–1 + 4 {tex}\alpha{/tex} )(–1) + (2 + 12 {tex}\alpha{/tex} )(1) = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 + 3 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + 1 – 4 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + 2 + 12 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 11 {tex}\alpha{/tex} + 5 = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 11 {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0 We have {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=(2+3 \alpha) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(-1+4 \alpha) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2+12 \alpha) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=[2+3(0)] \hat{\mathrm{i}}+[-1+4(0)] \hat{\mathrm{j}}+[2+12(0)] \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} Using the distance formula, we have {tex}P Q=\sqrt{(2-(-1))^{2}+((-1)-(-5))^{2}+(2-(-10))^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{PQ}=\sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}+12^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{PQ}=\sqrt{9+16+144}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm{PQ}=\sqrt{169}=13{/tex} Thus, the required distance is 13 units.
solution_html:
Let P be the point with position vector{tex}\vec{p}=-\hat{\imath}-5 \hat{\jmath}-10 \hat{k}{/tex} and Q be the point of intersection of the given line and the plane.
We have the line equation as
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(2 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}})+\lambda(3 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}+12 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(2+3 \lambda) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(-1+4 \lambda) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2+12 \lambda) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
Let the position vector of Q be{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}{/tex}. As Q is a point on this line, for some scalar {tex}\alpha{/tex}, we have
{tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=(2+3 \alpha) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(-1+4 \alpha) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2+12 \alpha) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
This point Q also lies on the given plane, which means this point satisfies the plane equation{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(\hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}})=5{/tex}.
{tex}\Rightarrow[(2+3 \alpha) \hat{\mathbf{i}}+(-1+4 \alpha) \hat{\mathbf{j}}+(2+12 \alpha) \hat{\mathbf{k}}] \cdot(\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}})=5{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2 + 3{tex}\alpha{/tex})(1) + (–1 + 4{tex}\alpha{/tex})(–1) + (2 + 12{tex}\alpha{/tex})(1) = 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 + 3{tex}\alpha{/tex} + 1 – 4{tex}\alpha{/tex} + 2 + 12{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 11{tex}\alpha{/tex} + 5 = 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 11{tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0
We have{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=(2+3 \alpha) \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(-1+4 \alpha) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(2+12 \alpha) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=[2+3(0)] \hat{\mathrm{i}}+[-1+4(0)] \hat{\mathrm{j}}+[2+12(0)] \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \overrightarrow{\mathrm{q}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
Using the distance formula, we have
{tex}P Q=\sqrt{(2-(-1))^{2}+((-1)-(-5))^{2}+(2-(-10))^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{PQ}=\sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}+12^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{PQ}=\sqrt{9+16+144}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{PQ}=\sqrt{169}=13{/tex}
Thus, the required distance is 13 units.

question_html: Find the shortest distance between the lines whose vector equations are {tex} \vec r = \left( {1 - t} \right)\hat i + (t - 2)\hat j + (3 - 2t)\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec r = \left( {s + 1} \right)\hat i + (2s - 1)\hat j - (2s + 1)\hat k{/tex}
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the lines whose vector equations are {tex} \vec r = \left( {1 - t} \right)\hat i + (t - 2)\hat j + (3 - 2t)\hat k{/tex} and {tex}\vec r = \left( {s + 1} \right)\hat i + (2s - 1)\hat j - (2s + 1)\hat k{/tex}
explanation: We have, {tex}\vec r=\hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k + t( - \hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k){/tex} {tex}\vec r = \hat i - \hat j - \hat k + s(\hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k){/tex} {tex}{\vec a_1} = \hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} = - \hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} = \hat i - \hat j - \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_2} = \hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} - {\vec a_1} = \hat j - 4\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} \times {\hat b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ { - 1}&1&{ - 2} \\ 1&2&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = 2\hat i - 4\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( 2 \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 4} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 3} \right)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {29} {/tex} {tex}d = \left| {\frac{{\left( {{{\vec a}_2} - {{\vec a}_1}} \right)\left( {{b_1} \times {b_2}} \right)}}{{\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \frac{8}{{\sqrt {29} }}{/tex}
solution: We have, {tex}\vec r=\hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k + t( - \hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k){/tex} {tex}\vec r = \hat i - \hat j - \hat k + s(\hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k){/tex} {tex}{\vec a_1} = \hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} = - \hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} = \hat i - \hat j - \hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_2} = \hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec a_2} - {\vec a_1} = \hat j - 4\hat k{/tex} {tex}{\vec b_1} \times {\hat b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ { - 1}&1&{ - 2} \\ 1&2&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = 2\hat i - 4\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex} {tex}\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( 2 \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 4} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 3} \right)}^2}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {29} {/tex} {tex}d = \left| {\frac{{\left( {{{\vec a}_2} - {{\vec a}_1}} \right)\left( {{b_1} \times {b_2}} \right)}}{{\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right|}}} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \frac{8}{{\sqrt {29} }}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have,
{tex}\vec r=\hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k + t( - \hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k){/tex}
{tex}\vec r = \hat i - \hat j - \hat k + s(\hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k){/tex}
{tex}{\vec a_1} = \hat i - 2\hat j + 3\hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\vec b_1} = - \hat i + \hat j - 2\hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\vec a_2} = \hat i - \hat j - \hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\vec b_2} = \hat i + 2\hat j - 2\hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\vec a_2} - {\vec a_1} = \hat j - 4\hat k{/tex}
{tex}{\vec b_1} \times {\hat b_2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\ { - 1}&1&{ - 2} \\ 1&2&{ - 2} \end{array}} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = 2\hat i - 4\hat j - 3\hat k{/tex}
{tex}\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( 2 \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 4} \right)}^2} + {{\left( { - 3} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {29} {/tex}
{tex}d = \left| {\frac{{\left( {{{\vec a}_2} - {{\vec a}_1}} \right)\left( {{b_1} \times {b_2}} \right)}}{{\left| {{{\vec b}_1} \times {{\vec b}_2}} \right|}}} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{8}{{\sqrt {29} }}{/tex}

question_html: Find the image of the point (1, 6, 3) in the line {tex}\frac{x}{1} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z - 2}}{3}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the image of the point (1, 6, 3) in the line {tex}\frac{x}{1} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z - 2}}{3}{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}P\left( {1,6,3} \right){/tex} be the given point and let L be the foot of perpendicular from P to the given line. The coordinates of a general point on the given line are {tex}\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z - 2}}{3} = \lambda {/tex} i.e., {tex}x = \lambda ,y = 2\lambda + 1,z = 3\lambda + 2{/tex} If the coordinates of L are {tex}\left( {\lambda ,2\lambda + 1,3\lambda + 2} \right){/tex} , then the direction ratios of PL are {tex}\lambda - 1,2\lambda - 5,3\lambda - 1{/tex} . But the direction ratios of given line which is perpendicular to PL are 1,2,3. Therefore, {tex}\left( {\lambda - 1} \right) + \left( {2\lambda - 5} \right)2 + \left( {3\lambda - 1} \right)3 = 0{/tex} which gives {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex} . Hence coordinate of L are (1, 3, 5). Let {tex}Q\left( {{x_1},{y_1},{z_1}} \right){/tex} be the image of {tex}P\left( {1,6,3} \right){/tex} in the given line. Then L is the mid-point of PQ. Therefore, {tex}\frac{{{x_1} + 1}}{2} = 1,\frac{{{y_1} + 6}}{2} = 3,\frac{{{z_1} + 3}}{2} = 5{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {x_1} = 1,{y_1} = 0,{z_1} = 7{/tex} Hence, the image of (1, 6, 3) in the given line is (1, 0, 7).

solution_html:
Let {tex}P\left( {1,6,3} \right){/tex}be the given point and let L be the foot of perpendicular from P to the given line.

The coordinates of a general point on the given line are

{tex}\frac{{x - 1}}{1} = \frac{{y - 1}}{2} = \frac{{z - 2}}{3} = \lambda {/tex} i.e., {tex}x = \lambda ,y = 2\lambda + 1,z = 3\lambda + 2{/tex}

If the coordinates of L are {tex}\left( {\lambda ,2\lambda + 1,3\lambda + 2} \right){/tex}, then the direction ratios of PL are {tex}\lambda - 1,2\lambda - 5,3\lambda - 1{/tex}.
But the direction ratios of given line which is perpendicular to PL are 1,2,3. Therefore, {tex}\left( {\lambda - 1} \right) + \left( {2\lambda - 5} \right)2 + \left( {3\lambda - 1} \right)3 = 0{/tex}which gives {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex}. Hence coordinate of L are (1, 3, 5).
Let {tex}Q\left( {{x_1},{y_1},{z_1}} \right){/tex}be the image of {tex}P\left( {1,6,3} \right){/tex} in the given line. Then L is the mid-point of PQ. Therefore, {tex}\frac{{{x_1} + 1}}{2} = 1,\frac{{{y_1} + 6}}{2} = 3,\frac{{{z_1} + 3}}{2} = 5{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {x_1} = 1,{y_1} = 0,{z_1} = 7{/tex}
Hence, the image of (1, 6, 3) in the given line is (1, 0, 7).

Write the vector equations of the following lines and hence determine the distance between them
{tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z+4}{6}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-3}{4}=\frac{y-3}{6}=\frac{z+5}{12}{/tex}
question_text: Write the vector equations of the following lines and hence determine the distance between them {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y-2}{3}=\frac{z+4}{6}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-3}{4}=\frac{y-3}{6}=\frac{z+5}{12}{/tex}
explanation: The given equations of lines are {tex} \frac { x - 1 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \frac { z + 4 } { 6 }{/tex} and {tex} \frac { x - 3 } { 4 } = \frac { y - 3 } { 6 } = \frac { z + 5 } { 12 }{/tex} Now, the vector equation of given lines are {tex} \vec { r } = ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 2\hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} ......(i) [ {tex}\because{/tex} vector form of equation of line is {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } ]{/tex} and {tex} \vec { r } = ( 3 i + 3 \hat { j} - 5 \hat { k } ) + \mu ( 4 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 12 \hat { k } ){/tex} ...................(ii) Here, {tex} \vec { a _ { 1 } } = \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } , \vec { b _ { 1 } } = 2 \hat { i} + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex} and {tex} \vec { a _ { 2 } } = 3 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } , \vec { b _ { 2 } } = 4 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 12 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, {tex} \vec { a _ { 2 } } - \vec { a _ { 1 } } = ( 3 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } ) - ( \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex} = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex} .................(iii) and {tex} \vec { b _ { 1 } } \times \vec { b _ { 2 } } = \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 6 } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 12 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex} = \hat { i } ( 36 - 36 ) - \hat { j } ( 24 - 24 ) + \hat { k } ( 12 - 12 ){/tex} {tex} = 0 \hat { i} - \hat { 0 } \hat { j } + 0 \hat { k } = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { b } _ { 1 } \times \vec { b } _ { 2 } = \vec { 0 },{/tex} i.e. Vector b 1 is parallel to {tex}\vec { b } _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \text { if } \vec { a } \times \vec { b } = \vec { 0 } , \text { then } \vec { a } \| \vec { b } ]{/tex} Thus, two lines are parallel. {tex} \therefore \quad \vec { b } = ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} ...................(iv) [since, DR's of given lines are proportional] Since, the two lines are parallel, we use the formula for shortest distance between two parallel lines {tex} d = \left| \frac { \vec { b } \times \left( \vec { a } _ { 2 } - \vec { a _ { 1 } } \right) } { | \vec { b } | } \right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad d = \left| \frac { ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} + 6 \hat { k } ) \times ( 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) } { \sqrt { ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } }} \right|{/tex} ..............(v) [from Eqs. (iii) and (iv) ] Now, {tex}( \hat { 2 } i + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ) \times ( 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 6 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \hat { i } ( - 3 - 6 ) - \hat { j } ( - 2 - 12 ) + \hat { k } ( 2 -6 ){/tex} {tex}= -9 \hat { i } + 14 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k }{/tex} From Eq, (v), we get {tex} d = \left| \frac { - 9 \hat { i } + 14 \hat { j} - 4 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 49 } } \right| = \frac { \sqrt { ( - 9 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 14 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 4 ) ^ { 2 } } } { 7 }{/tex} {tex} \therefore d = \frac { \sqrt { 81 + 196 + 16 } } { 7 } = \frac { \sqrt { 293 } } { 7 }{/tex} units

4 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 2\hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} ......(i) [ {tex}\because{/tex} vector form of equation of line is {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } ]{/tex} and {tex} \vec { r } = ( 3 i + 3 \hat { j} - 5 \hat { k } , \vec { b _ { 2 } } = 4 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 12 \hat { k }{/tex} Now, {tex} \vec { a _ { 2 } } - \vec { a _ { 1 } } = ( 3 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } ) - ( \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j } - 36 ) - \hat { j } ( 24 - 24 ) + \hat { k } ( 12 - 12 ){/tex} {tex} = 0 \hat { i} - \hat { 0 } \hat { j } + 0 \hat { k } = \vec { 0 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { b } _ { 1 } \times \vec { b } _ { 2 } = \vec { 0 },{/tex} i.e. Vector b 1 is parallel to {tex}\vec { b } _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \text { if } \vec { a } \times \vec { b } = \vec { 0 } , \text { then } \vec { a } \| \vec { b } ]{/tex} Thus, two lines are parallel. {tex} \therefore \quad \vec { b } = ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex} ...................(iv) [since, DR's of given lines are proportional] Since, the two lines are parallel, we use the formula for shortest distance between two parallel lines {tex} d = \left| \frac { \vec { b } \times \left( \vec { a } _ { 2 } - \vec { a _ { 1 } } \right) } { | \vec { b } | } \right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad d = \left| \frac { ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} + 6 \hat { k } ) \times ( 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) } { \sqrt { ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } }} \right|{/tex} ..............(v) [from Eqs. (iii) and (iv) ] Now, {tex}( \hat { 2 } i + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ) \times ( 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } ){/tex} {tex}= \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 6 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right|{/tex} {tex}= \hat { i } ( - 3 - 6 ) - \hat { j } ( - 2 - 12 ) + \hat { k } ( 2 -6 ){/tex} {tex}= -9 \hat { i } + 14 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k }{/tex} From Eq, (v), we get {tex} d = \left| \frac { - 9 \hat { i } + 14 \hat { j} - 4 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 49 } } \right| = \frac { \sqrt { ( - 9 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 14 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 4 ) ^ { 2 } } } { 7 }{/tex} {tex} \therefore d = \frac { \sqrt { 81 + 196 + 16 } } { 7 } = \frac { \sqrt { 293 } } { 7 }{/tex} units
solution_html:
The given equations of lines are

{tex} \frac { x - 1 } { 2 } = \frac { y - 2 } { 3 } = \frac { z + 4 } { 6 }{/tex}
and {tex} \frac { x - 3 } { 4 } = \frac { y - 3 } { 6 } = \frac { z + 5 } { 12 }{/tex}
Now, the vector equation of given lines are
{tex} \vec { r } = ( \hat { i } + 2 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } ) + \lambda ( 2\hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex}......(i)
[{tex}\because{/tex} vector form of equation of line is {tex} \vec { r } = \vec { a } + \lambda \vec { b } ]{/tex}
and {tex} \vec { r } = ( 3 i + 3 \hat { j} - 5 \hat { k } ) + \mu ( 4 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 12 \hat { k } ){/tex}...................(ii)
Here, {tex} \vec { a _ { 1 } } = \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } , \vec { b _ { 1 } } = 2 \hat { i} + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k }{/tex}
and {tex} \vec { a _ { 2 } } = 3 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } , \vec { b _ { 2 } } = 4 \hat { i } + 6 \hat { j } + 12 \hat { k }{/tex}
 Now, {tex} \vec { a _ { 2 } } - \vec { a _ { 1 } } = ( 3 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } - 5 \hat { k } ) - ( \hat { i} + 2 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex} = 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k }{/tex}.................(iii)
and {tex} \vec { b _ { 1 } } \times \vec { b _ { 2 } } = \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 6 } \\ { 4 } & { 6 } & { 12 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex} = \hat { i } ( 36 - 36 ) - \hat { j } ( 24 - 24 ) + \hat { k } ( 12 - 12 ){/tex}
{tex} = 0 \hat { i} - \hat { 0 } \hat { j } + 0 \hat { k } = \vec { 0 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \vec { b } _ { 1 } \times \vec { b } _ { 2 } = \vec { 0 },{/tex}
i.e. Vector  b1 is parallel to {tex}\vec { b } _ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}[ \because \text { if } \vec { a } \times \vec { b } = \vec { 0 } , \text { then } \vec { a } \| \vec { b } ]{/tex}
Thus, two lines are parallel.
{tex} \therefore \quad \vec { b } = ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ){/tex}...................(iv)
[since, DR's of given lines are proportional]
Since, the two lines are parallel, we use the formula for shortest distance between two parallel lines
{tex} d = \left| \frac { \vec { b } \times \left( \vec { a } _ { 2 } - \vec { a _ { 1 } } \right) } { | \vec { b } | } \right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad d = \left| \frac { ( 2 \hat { i } + 3 \hat { j} + 6 \hat { k } ) \times ( 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } ) } { \sqrt { ( 2 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 3 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 6 ) ^ { 2 } }} \right|{/tex}..............(v)
[from Eqs. (iii) and (iv) ]
Now, {tex}( \hat { 2 } i + 3 \hat { j } + 6 \hat { k } ) \times ( 2 \hat { i } + \hat { j } - \hat { k } ){/tex}
{tex}= \left| \begin{array} { c c c } { \hat { i } } & { \hat { j } } & { \hat { k } } \\ { 2 } & { 3 } & { 6 } \\ { 2 } & { 1 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right|{/tex}
{tex}= \hat { i } ( - 3 - 6 ) - \hat { j } ( - 2 - 12 ) + \hat { k } ( 2 -6 ){/tex}
{tex}= -9 \hat { i } + 14 \hat { j } - 4 \hat { k }{/tex}
From Eq, (v), we get
{tex} d = \left| \frac { - 9 \hat { i } + 14 \hat { j} - 4 \hat { k } } { \sqrt { 49 } } \right| = \frac { \sqrt { ( - 9 ) ^ { 2 } + ( 14 ) ^ { 2 } + ( - 4 ) ^ { 2 } } } { 7 }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore d = \frac { \sqrt { 81 + 196 + 16 } } { 7 } = \frac { \sqrt { 293 } } { 7 }{/tex}units

question_html: Find the equation of line passing through points A (0,6,-9) and B(-3,-6,3). If D is the foot of perpendicular drawn from the point C (7,4,-1) on the line AB, then find the coordinates of point D and equation of line CD.
question_text: Find the equation of line passing through points A (0,6,-9) and B(-3,-6,3). If D is the foot of perpendicular drawn from the point C (7,4,-1) on the line AB, then find the coordinates of point D and equation of line CD.
explanation: We have to find the equation of line passing through points A (0,6,-9) and B(-3,-6,3). If D is the foot of perpendicular drawn from the point C (7,4,-1) on the line AB, then we have to find the coordinates of point D and equation of line CD. We know that, equation of line passing through the points {tex}\left( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } , z _ { 1 } \right){/tex} and {tex}\left( x _ { 2 , } y _ { 2 , } z _ { 2 ) }\right.{/tex} is given by {tex}\frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { z _ { 2 } - z _ { 1 } }{/tex} .......(i) Here, A(x 1, y 1 ,z 1 )=(0,6,-9) and B(x 2, y 2, z 2 )=(-3,-6,3) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of line AB is given by, {tex}\frac { x - 0 } { - 3 - 0 } = \frac { y - 6 } { - 6 - 6 } = \frac { z + 9 } { 3 + 9 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x } { - 3 } = \frac { y - 6 } { - 12 } = \frac { z + 9 } { 12 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x } { - 1 } = \frac { y - 6 } { - 4 } = \frac { z + 9 } { 4 }{/tex} [dividing denominator by 3] Next, we have to find coordinates of foot of perpendicular D. Now, let {tex}\frac { x } { - 1 } = \frac { y - 6 } { - 4 } = \frac { z + 9 } { 4 } = \lambda{/tex} (say) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = - \lambda,{/tex} {tex}y - 6 = - 4 \lambda {/tex} and {tex}\ z + 9 = 4 \lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = - \lambda , y = - 4 \lambda + 6{/tex} and {tex} \ z = 4 \lambda - 9{/tex} Since CD lies on line AB ,so coordinates of , {tex}D = ( - \lambda , - 4 \lambda + 6,4 \lambda - 9){/tex} ....(ii) Now, DR's of line CD are {tex}( - \lambda - 7 , - 4 \lambda + 6 - 4,4 \lambda - 9 + 1){/tex} {tex}= ( - \lambda - 7 , - 4 \lambda + 2,4 \lambda - 8 ){/tex} Now, CD {tex}\perp {/tex} AB {tex}\therefore{/tex} a 1 a 2 + b 1 b 2 + c 1 c 2 = 0 Where, {tex}a _ { 1 } = - \lambda - 7 , b _ { 1 } = - 4 \lambda + 2{/tex} , {tex}c _ { 1 } = 4 \lambda - 8{/tex} [DR's of line CD] and a 2 = -1, b 2 = -4, c 2 = 4 [DR's of line AB] {tex}\Rightarrow ( - \lambda - 7 ) ( - 1 ) + ( - 4 \lambda + 2 ) ( - 4 ) + ( 4 \lambda - 8 ) 4 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda + 7 + 16 \lambda - 8 + 16 \lambda - 32 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 33 \lambda - 33 = 0{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex} On putting {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex} in Eq. (ii), we get required foot of perpendicular, D = (-1,2,5) Also, we have to find equation of line CD, where C(7,4,-1) and D(-1,2,-5). {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required equation of line is {tex}\frac { x - 7 } { - 1 - 7 } = \frac { y - 4 } { 2 - 4 } = \frac { z + 1 } { - 5 + 1 }{/tex} [using Eq. (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - 7 } { - 8 } = \frac { y - 4 } { - 2 } = \frac { z + 1 } { - 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - 7 } { 4 } = \frac { y - 4 } { 1 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 2 }{/tex} [dividing denominator by -2]
solution: We have to find the equation of line passing through points A (0,6,-9) and B(-3,-6,3). If D is the foot of perpendicular drawn from the point C (7,4,-1) on the line AB, then we have to find the coordinates of point D and equation of line CD. We know that, equation of line passing through the points {tex}\left( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } , z _ { 1 } \right){/tex} and {tex}\left( x _ { 2 , } y _ { 2 , } z _ { 2 ) }\right.{/tex} is given by {tex}\frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { z _ { 2 } - z _ { 1 } }{/tex} .......(i) Here, A(x 1, y 1 ,z 1 )=(0,6,-9) and B(x 2, y 2, z 2 )=(-3,-6,3) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of line AB is given by, {tex}\frac { x - 0 } { - 3 - 0 } = \frac { y - 6 } { - 6 - 6 } = \frac { z + 9 } { 3 + 9 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x } { - 3 } = \frac { y - 6 } { - 12 } = \frac { z + 9 } { 12 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x } { - 1 } = \frac { y - 6 } { - 4 } = \frac { z + 9 } { 4 }{/tex} [dividing denominator by 3] Next, we have to find coordinates of foot of perpendicular D. Now, let {tex}\frac { x } { - 1 } = \frac { y - 6 } { - 4 } = \frac { z + 9 } { 4 } = \lambda{/tex} (say) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = - \lambda,{/tex} {tex}y - 6 = - 4 \lambda {/tex} and {tex}\ z + 9 = 4 \lambda{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = - \lambda , y = - 4 \lambda + 6{/tex} and {tex} \ z = 4 \lambda - 9{/tex} Since CD lies on line AB ,so coordinates of , {tex}D = ( - \lambda , - 4 \lambda + 6,4 \lambda - 9){/tex} ....(ii) Now, DR's of line CD are {tex}( - \lambda - 7 , - 4 \lambda + 6 - 4,4 \lambda - 9 + 1){/tex} {tex}= ( - \lambda - 7 , - 4 \lambda + 2,4 \lambda - 8 ){/tex} Now, CD {tex}\perp {/tex} AB {tex}\therefore{/tex} a 1 a 2 + b 1 b 2 + c 1 c 2 = 0 Where, {tex}a _ { 1 } = - \lambda - 7 , b _ { 1 } = - 4 \lambda + 2{/tex} , {tex}c _ { 1 } = 4 \lambda - 8{/tex} [DR's of line CD] and a 2 = -1, b 2 = -4, c 2 = 4 [DR's of line AB] {tex}\Rightarrow ( - \lambda - 7 ) ( - 1 ) + ( - 4 \lambda + 2 ) ( - 4 ) + ( 4 \lambda - 8 ) 4 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda + 7 + 16 \lambda - 8 + 16 \lambda - 32 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 33 \lambda - 33 = 0{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex} On putting {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex} in Eq. (ii), we get required foot of perpendicular, D = (-1,2,5) Also, we have to find equation of line CD, where C(7,4,-1) and D(-1,2,-5). {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required equation of line is {tex}\frac { x - 7 } { - 1 - 7 } = \frac { y - 4 } { 2 - 4 } = \frac { z + 1 } { - 5 + 1 }{/tex} [using Eq. (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - 7 } { - 8 } = \frac { y - 4 } { - 2 } = \frac { z + 1 } { - 4 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - 7 } { 4 } = \frac { y - 4 } { 1 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 2 }{/tex} [dividing denominator by -2]
solution_html:
We have  to find the equation of line passing through points A (0,6,-9) and B(-3,-6,3). If D is the foot of perpendicular drawn from the point C (7,4,-1) on the line AB, then we have to find  the coordinates of point D and equation of line CD.

We know that, equation of line passing through the points {tex}\left( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } , z _ { 1 } \right){/tex}and {tex}\left( x _ { 2 , } y _ { 2 , } z _ { 2 ) }\right.{/tex}is given by
{tex}\frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { y _ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { z _ { 2 } - z _ { 1 } }{/tex}.......(i)
Here,  A(x1,y1,z1)=(0,6,-9)
and  B(x2,y2,z2)=(-3,-6,3)

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of line AB is given by,
{tex}\frac { x - 0 } { - 3 - 0 } = \frac { y - 6 } { - 6 - 6 } = \frac { z + 9 } { 3 + 9 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x } { - 3 } = \frac { y - 6 } { - 12 } = \frac { z + 9 } { 12 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x } { - 1 } = \frac { y - 6 } { - 4 } = \frac { z + 9 } { 4 }{/tex} [dividing denominator by 3]
Next, we have to find coordinates of foot of perpendicular D.
Now, let {tex}\frac { x } { - 1 } = \frac { y - 6 } { - 4 } = \frac { z + 9 } { 4 } = \lambda{/tex}(say)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = - \lambda,{/tex}
{tex}y - 6 = - 4 \lambda {/tex} and {tex}\ z + 9 = 4 \lambda{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = - \lambda , y = - 4 \lambda + 6{/tex} and {tex} \ z = 4 \lambda - 9{/tex}
Since CD lies on line AB ,so coordinates of ,
{tex}D = ( - \lambda , - 4 \lambda + 6,4 \lambda - 9){/tex}....(ii)
Now, DR's of line CD are
{tex}( - \lambda - 7 , - 4 \lambda + 6 - 4,4 \lambda - 9 + 1){/tex}
{tex}= ( - \lambda - 7 , - 4 \lambda + 2,4 \lambda - 8 ){/tex}
Now, CD{tex}\perp {/tex}AB
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a1a2 + b1b2 + c1c2 = 0
Where, {tex}a _ { 1 } = - \lambda - 7 , b _ { 1 } = - 4 \lambda + 2{/tex}, {tex}c _ { 1 } = 4 \lambda - 8{/tex} [DR's of line CD]
and  a2 = -1, b2 = -4, c2 = 4 [DR's of line AB]
{tex}\Rightarrow ( - \lambda - 7 ) ( - 1 ) + ( - 4 \lambda + 2 ) ( - 4 ) + ( 4 \lambda - 8 ) 4 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda + 7 + 16 \lambda - 8 + 16 \lambda - 32 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 33 \lambda - 33 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\lambda = 1{/tex}
On putting {tex}\lambda = 1{/tex} in Eq. (ii), we get required foot of perpendicular,
D = (-1,2,5)
Also, we have to find equation of line CD, where
C(7,4,-1) and D(-1,2,-5).
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required equation of line is
{tex}\frac { x - 7 } { - 1 - 7 } = \frac { y - 4 } { 2 - 4 } = \frac { z + 1 } { - 5 + 1 }{/tex} [using Eq. (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - 7 } { - 8 } = \frac { y - 4 } { - 2 } = \frac { z + 1 } { - 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x - 7 } { 4 } = \frac { y - 4 } { 1 } = \frac { z + 1 } { 2 }{/tex} [dividing denominator by -2]

question_html: Find the image of the point (1, 6, 3) in the line {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{3}{/tex}. Also, write the equation of the line joining the given point and its image and find the length of the segment joining the given point and its image.
question_text: Find the image of the point (1, 6, 3) in the line {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{3}{/tex} . Also, write the equation of the line joining the given point and its image and find the length of the segment joining the given point and its image.
explanation: Suppose Q be the image of point P (1, 6, 3) in the line {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{3}{/tex} and M be the foot of perpendicular drawn from P to this line. Then, PM = MQ. Suppose the coordinates of M be given by {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{3}=r{/tex} Suppose the coordinates of M be (r, 2r + 1, 3r + 2). Since The direction ratios of PM are proportional to r - 1, 2r - 5, 3r - 1. Since PM is pei'pendicular to the given line. Therefore, 1(r - 1) + 2 (2r - 5) + 3 (3r -1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 14r - 14 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 1. So, the coordinates of M are (1, 3,5). Let (x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ) be the coordinates of Q. Since M is the mid-point of PQ. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{x_{1}+1}{2}=1, \frac{y_{1}+6}{2}=3, \frac{z_{1}+3}{2}=5{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 = 1, y 1 = 0, z 1 = 7 Hence, the coordinates point of Q are (1, 0, 7). So, the cartesian equations of PQ are {tex}\frac{x-1}{1-1}=\frac{y-6}{0-6}=\frac{z-3}{7-3}{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{x-1}{0}=\frac{y-6}{-6}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} and, PQ = {tex}\sqrt{(1-1)^{2}+(6-0)^{2}+(3-7)^{2}}=2 \sqrt{13}{/tex}
solution: Suppose Q be the image of point P (1, 6, 3) in the line {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{3}{/tex} and M be the foot of perpendicular drawn from P to this line. Then, PM = MQ. Suppose the coordinates of M be given by {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{3}=r{/tex} Suppose the coordinates of M be (r, 2r + 1, 3r + 2). Since The direction ratios of PM are proportional to r - 1, 2r - 5, 3r - 1. Since PM is pei'pendicular to the given line. Therefore, 1(r - 1) + 2 (2r - 5) + 3 (3r -1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 14r - 14 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 1. So, the coordinates of M are (1, 3,5). Let (x 1 , y 1 , z 1 ) be the coordinates of Q. Since M is the mid-point of PQ. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{x_{1}+1}{2}=1, \frac{y_{1}+6}{2}=3, \frac{z_{1}+3}{2}=5{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 1 = 1, y 1 = 0, z 1 = 7 Hence, the coordinates point of Q are (1, 0, 7). So, the cartesian equations of PQ are {tex}\frac{x-1}{1-1}=\frac{y-6}{0-6}=\frac{z-3}{7-3}{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{x-1}{0}=\frac{y-6}{-6}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} and, PQ = {tex}\sqrt{(1-1)^{2}+(6-0)^{2}+(3-7)^{2}}=2 \sqrt{13}{/tex}
solution_html:
Suppose Q be the image of point P (1, 6, 3) in the line {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{3}{/tex} and M be the foot of perpendicular drawn from P to this line. Then, PM = MQ.

Suppose the coordinates of M be given by {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{3}=r{/tex} 
Suppose the coordinates of M be (r, 2r + 1, 3r + 2).
Since The direction ratios of PM are proportional to r - 1, 2r - 5, 3r - 1.
Since PM is pei'pendicular to the given line. Therefore,
1(r - 1) + 2 (2r - 5) + 3 (3r -1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 14r - 14 = 0 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 1.
So, the coordinates of M are (1, 3,5).
Let (x1, y1, z1) be the coordinates of Q.
Since M is the mid-point of PQ.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{x_{1}+1}{2}=1, \frac{y_{1}+6}{2}=3, \frac{z_{1}+3}{2}=5{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x1 = 1, y1 = 0, z1 = 7
Hence, the coordinates point of Q are (1, 0, 7). So, the cartesian equations of PQ are
{tex}\frac{x-1}{1-1}=\frac{y-6}{0-6}=\frac{z-3}{7-3}{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{x-1}{0}=\frac{y-6}{-6}=\frac{z-3}{4}{/tex} 
and, PQ = {tex}\sqrt{(1-1)^{2}+(6-0)^{2}+(3-7)^{2}}=2 \sqrt{13}{/tex}
