question_html: Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex}, so that following lines are perpendicular to each other {tex}\frac { x + 5 } { 5 \lambda + 2 } = \frac { 2 - y } { 5 } = \frac { 1 - z } { - 1 }{/tex}and {tex}\frac { x } { 1 } = \frac { 2 y + 1 } { 4 \lambda } = \frac { 1 - z } { - 3 }.{/tex}
question_text: Find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} , so that following lines are perpendicular to each other {tex}\frac { x + 5 } { 5 \lambda + 2 } = \frac { 2 - y } { 5 } = \frac { 1 - z } { - 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { x } { 1 } = \frac { 2 y + 1 } { 4 \lambda } = \frac { 1 - z } { - 3 }.{/tex}
explanation: We have to find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} , so that following lines are perpendicular to each other {tex}\frac { x + 5 } { 5 \lambda + 2 } = \frac { 2 - y } { 5 } = \frac { 1 - z } { - 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { x } { 1 } = \frac { 2 y + 1 } { 4 \lambda } = \frac { 1 - z } { - 3 }.{/tex} Firstly, convert the given equations of lines into one point form of the line, which is of form {tex}\frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c }{/tex} and then use the condition a 1 a 2 +b 1 b 2 +c 1 c 2 =0 for perpendicularity of two lines and get value of {tex}\lambda.{/tex} Given equation of lines are {tex}\frac { x + 5 } { 5 \lambda + 2 } = \frac { 2 - y } { 5 } = \frac { 1 - z } { - 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { x } { 1 } = \frac { 2 y + 1 } { 4 \lambda } = \frac { 1 - z } { - 3 }{/tex} Above equations can be written as {tex}\frac { x + 5 } { 5 \lambda + 2 } = \frac { y - 2 } { - 5 } = \frac { z - 1 } { 1 }{/tex} ...........................(i) and {tex}\frac { x } { 1 } = \frac { 2 \left( y + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) } { 4 \lambda } = \frac { z - 1 } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x } { 1 } = \frac { y + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { 2 \lambda } = \frac { z - 1 } { 3 }{/tex} ...................(ii) On comparing Equations. (i) and (ii) with one point form of line {tex}\frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c },{/tex} we get {tex}a _ { 1 } = 5 \lambda + 2 , b _ { 1 } = - 5 , c _ { 1 } = 1{/tex} and {tex}a _ { 2 } = 1 , b _ { 2 } = 2 \lambda , c _ { 2 } = 3{/tex} Since, the two lines are perpendicular. {tex}\therefore \quad a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( 5 \lambda + 2 ) 1 + ( - 5 ) 2 \lambda + ( 1 ) 3 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 5 \lambda + 2 - 10 \lambda + 3 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 5 \lambda + 5 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 5 \lambda = 5{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \lambda = 1{/tex}
solution: We have to find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} , so that following lines are perpendicular to each other {tex}\frac { x + 5 } { 5 \lambda + 2 } = \frac { 2 - y } { 5 } = \frac { 1 - z } { - 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { x } { 1 } = \frac { 2 y + 1 } { 4 \lambda } = \frac { 1 - z } { - 3 }.{/tex} Firstly, convert the given equations of lines into one point form of the line, which is of form {tex}\frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c }{/tex} and then use the condition a 1 a 2 +b 1 b 2 +c 1 c 2 =0 for perpendicularity of two lines and get value of {tex}\lambda.{/tex} Given equation of lines are {tex}\frac { x + 5 } { 5 \lambda + 2 } = \frac { 2 - y } { 5 } = \frac { 1 - z } { - 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { x } { 1 } = \frac { 2 y + 1 } { 4 \lambda } = \frac { 1 - z } { - 3 }{/tex} Above equations can be written as {tex}\frac { x + 5 } { 5 \lambda + 2 } = \frac { y - 2 } { - 5 } = \frac { z - 1 } { 1 }{/tex} ...........................(i) and {tex}\frac { x } { 1 } = \frac { 2 \left( y + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) } { 4 \lambda } = \frac { z - 1 } { 3 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x } { 1 } = \frac { y + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { 2 \lambda } = \frac { z - 1 } { 3 }{/tex} ...................(ii) On comparing Equations. (i) and (ii) with one point form of line {tex}\frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c },{/tex} we get {tex}a _ { 1 } = 5 \lambda + 2 , b _ { 1 } = - 5 , c _ { 1 } = 1{/tex} and {tex}a _ { 2 } = 1 , b _ { 2 } = 2 \lambda , c _ { 2 } = 3{/tex} Since, the two lines are perpendicular. {tex}\therefore \quad a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( 5 \lambda + 2 ) 1 + ( - 5 ) 2 \lambda + ( 1 ) 3 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 5 \lambda + 2 - 10 \lambda + 3 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 5 \lambda + 5 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 5 \lambda = 5{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \lambda = 1{/tex}
solution_html:
We have to find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex}, so that following lines are perpendicular to each other {tex}\frac { x + 5 } { 5 \lambda + 2 } = \frac { 2 - y } { 5 } = \frac { 1 - z } { - 1 }{/tex}and {tex}\frac { x } { 1 } = \frac { 2 y + 1 } { 4 \lambda } = \frac { 1 - z } { - 3 }.{/tex}

Firstly, convert the given equations of lines into one point form of the line, which is of form {tex}\frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c }{/tex}and then use the condition a1a2+b1b2+c1c2=0 for perpendicularity of two lines and get value of {tex}\lambda.{/tex}
Given equation of lines are
{tex}\frac { x + 5 } { 5 \lambda + 2 } = \frac { 2 - y } { 5 } = \frac { 1 - z } { - 1 }{/tex}
and {tex}\frac { x } { 1 } = \frac { 2 y + 1 } { 4 \lambda } = \frac { 1 - z } { - 3 }{/tex}
Above equations can be written as
{tex}\frac { x + 5 } { 5 \lambda + 2 } = \frac { y - 2 } { - 5 } = \frac { z - 1 } { 1 }{/tex}...........................(i)

and {tex}\frac { x } { 1 } = \frac { 2 \left( y + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) } { 4 \lambda } = \frac { z - 1 } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { x } { 1 } = \frac { y + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { 2 \lambda } = \frac { z - 1 } { 3 }{/tex}...................(ii)

On comparing Equations. (i) and (ii) with one point form of line
{tex}\frac { x - x _ { 1 } } { a } = \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { b } = \frac { z - z _ { 1 } } { c },{/tex}we get
{tex}a _ { 1 } = 5 \lambda + 2 , b _ { 1 } = - 5 , c _ { 1 } = 1{/tex}
and {tex}a _ { 2 } = 1 , b _ { 2 } = 2 \lambda , c _ { 2 } = 3{/tex}
Since, the two lines are perpendicular.
{tex}\therefore \quad a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } b _ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad ( 5 \lambda + 2 ) 1 + ( - 5 ) 2 \lambda + ( 1 ) 3 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 5 \lambda + 2 - 10 \lambda + 3 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad - 5 \lambda + 5 = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 5 \lambda = 5{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \lambda = 1{/tex}

question_html: The cartesian equations of a line AB are {tex}\frac{2 x-1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-3}{3}{/tex}. Find the direction cosines of a line parallel to AB.
question_text: The cartesian equations of a line AB are {tex}\frac{2 x-1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-3}{3}{/tex} . Find the direction cosines of a line parallel to AB.
explanation: Here, {tex}\frac{2 x-1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-3}{3}{/tex} The equation of the line AB can be re-written as {tex}=\frac{x-\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-3}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x-\frac{1}{2}}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{y+2}{4}=\frac{z-3}{6}{/tex} Therefore, the direction ratios of the line parallel to AB are proportional to {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} , 4, 6. Therefore, the direction cosines of the line parallel to AB are proportional to {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{(\sqrt{3})^{2}+4^{2}+6^{2}}}, \frac{4}{\sqrt{(\sqrt{3})^{2}+4^{2}+6^{2}}}, \frac{6}{\sqrt{(\sqrt{3})^{2}+4^{2}+6^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{55}}, \frac{4}{\sqrt{55}}, \frac{6}{\sqrt{55}}{/tex}
solution: Here, {tex}\frac{2 x-1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-3}{3}{/tex} The equation of the line AB can be re-written as {tex}=\frac{x-\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-3}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x-\frac{1}{2}}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{y+2}{4}=\frac{z-3}{6}{/tex} Therefore, the direction ratios of the line parallel to AB are proportional to {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} , 4, 6. Therefore, the direction cosines of the line parallel to AB are proportional to {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{(\sqrt{3})^{2}+4^{2}+6^{2}}}, \frac{4}{\sqrt{(\sqrt{3})^{2}+4^{2}+6^{2}}}, \frac{6}{\sqrt{(\sqrt{3})^{2}+4^{2}+6^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{55}}, \frac{4}{\sqrt{55}}, \frac{6}{\sqrt{55}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here,
{tex}\frac{2 x-1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-3}{3}{/tex} 
The equation of the line AB can be re-written as
{tex}=\frac{x-\frac{1}{2}}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}=\frac{y+2}{2}=\frac{z-3}{3}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{x-\frac{1}{2}}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{y+2}{4}=\frac{z-3}{6}{/tex} 
Therefore, the direction ratios of the line parallel to AB are proportional to {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}, 4, 6.
Therefore, the direction cosines of the line parallel to AB are proportional to
{tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{(\sqrt{3})^{2}+4^{2}+6^{2}}}, \frac{4}{\sqrt{(\sqrt{3})^{2}+4^{2}+6^{2}}}, \frac{6}{\sqrt{(\sqrt{3})^{2}+4^{2}+6^{2}}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{55}}, \frac{4}{\sqrt{55}}, \frac{6}{\sqrt{55}}{/tex}

question_html:
Find the shortest distance between the pairs of lines whose vector equations are:
{tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\mu(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}
question_text: Find the shortest distance between the pairs of lines whose vector equations are: {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\mu(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex}
explanation: We know that {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\mu(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} Comparing the given equations with the equations {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{1}}+\lambda \vec{b_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{2}}+\mu \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} We get, {tex}\vec{a_{1}}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b_{1}}=2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b_{2}}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -5 & 2 \\ 1 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}-3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{(-3)^{2}+3^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{9+9}{/tex} = {tex}3 \sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \cdot(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}){/tex} = {tex}(-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \cdot(-3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} = 3 + 6 = 9 Hence the shortest distance between the lines {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{1}}+\lambda \vec{b_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{2}}+\mu \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} is given by d = {tex}|\frac{(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \cdot(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}})}{|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|}|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\frac{9}{3 \sqrt{2}}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
solution: We know that {tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\mu(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} Comparing the given equations with the equations {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{1}}+\lambda \vec{b_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{2}}+\mu \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} We get, {tex}\vec{a_{1}}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{a_{2}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b_{1}}=2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{b_{2}}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -5 & 2 \\ 1 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}-3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{(-3)^{2}+3^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{9+9}{/tex} = {tex}3 \sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \cdot(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}){/tex} = {tex}(-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \cdot(-3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} = 3 + 6 = 9 Hence the shortest distance between the lines {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{1}}+\lambda \vec{b_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{2}}+\mu \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} is given by d = {tex}|\frac{(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \cdot(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}})}{|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|}|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\frac{9}{3 \sqrt{2}}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
We know that{tex}\vec{r}=(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}){/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\mu(\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}){/tex} 
Comparing the given equations with the equations
{tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{1}}+\lambda \vec{b_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{2}}+\mu \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} 
We get,
{tex}\vec{a_{1}}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} 
{tex}\vec{a_{2}}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} 
{tex}\vec{b_{1}}=2 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} 
{tex}\vec{b_{2}}=\hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}=-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex} 
and {tex}|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -5 & 2 \\ 1 & -1 & 1 \end{array}\right|{/tex} 
= {tex}-3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{(-3)^{2}+3^{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt{9+9}{/tex} 
= {tex}3 \sqrt{2}{/tex} 
{tex}(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \cdot(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}){/tex} = {tex}(-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \cdot(-3 \hat{i}+3 \hat{k}){/tex} 
= 3 + 6 = 9
Hence the shortest distance between the lines
{tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{1}}+\lambda \vec{b_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a_{2}}+\mu \vec{b_{2}}{/tex} is given by
d = {tex}|\frac{(\vec{a_{2}}-\vec{a_{1}}) \cdot(\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}})}{|\vec{b_{1}} \times \vec{b_{2}}|}|{/tex} 
= {tex}\left|\frac{9}{3 \sqrt{2}}\right|{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
